quote: Who is an Addict. “We use to live and live to use. Very simply an addict is a man or woman whose life is controlled by drugs.”
source: Memphis Review Form - page 1, first paragraph
body:
We become responsible members of society with integrity, freedom, joy and serenity as we work our 12 Steps. The Steps give us the creative freedom to become the very best people we want to be: respected, responsible, productive members of society. People we are proud to be recovering addicts living clean and free.

We are all the same, victims of the disease of addiction. We all want the same thing, an end to pain and recovery from our disease. Although our stories may be different, the feelings and difficulties brought about by this disease are the same: worthlessness, abject misery, regret, hopelessness, remorse, resentment, fear, anger, grief, shame and self-pity. We have all done things we would take back if we could. Some things we may never want to admit. By the same token, if we have a deep enough desire and we are willing to make the effort, we can be given hope. We can use the principles of honesty, willingness and open-mindedness. We develop our own acceptance, courage, strength and perseverance. By working Step Three, we are blessed with power and wisdom by the God of our understanding.

Just saying no is not an option for addicts who are hooked. The drugs control our actions. Until we have the NA program, the disease is driving the bus. We just hang on for dear life while we watch ourselves going down. Surrender is the way out. Through surrender, we starve our addiction. We may feel like using but just bide our time as the desire to use is replaced by the desire for recovery.

[bs/jf]

quote: “We regretted the past; we feared the future.”
body:
This is the way we lived in our addiction. We fretted over the past or projected possible futures. We were never focused in the present instead we were using to escape our feelings. Perhaps we mistakenly thought our using made our present bearable. In reality, we were obliterating our present, then we could regret it in the future! We were slaves to our addiction. Very few of us were able to live in the present - except perhaps when calamity or disaster overcame us. Then, of course, we had to use again. Through working the Steps, we begin to live in the present. The present is the only time we can change the future.

Active addiction made us slaves. Developing a deep sense of self-honesty is essential to our recovery. Giving ourselves progressively to the program of Narcotics Anonymous by working the Steps, we begin to live by spiritual principles. As we progress through the Steps we clean up our past and acquire the skills necessary to live in the present. We begin doing the right thing for the right reason. This brings us the great freedom of being able to focus our attention on the present, right here, right now. When we can live in the present, we become aware of the great joy and beauty surrounding us. We see how much we have missed in the past. Our lives are much richer now. We are grateful to live in the present.

quote: “Drugs could no longer hide the pain. We just wanted an easy way out. Suicide was on many of our minds.”
body:
We used to mask our pain from our consciousness. With or without drugs, we lost the ability to remember what was making us feel so badly. It was all we could do just to get through the day! People yelling at us or putting pressure on us kept us even more distracted. Is it any wonder we became isolated? Eventually we became so dysfunctional even other addicts couldn’t stand our company.

We seek personality change for the better through the N A 12 Steps. Spiritual growth replaces the broken parts of our personality with things that work for us. A day at a time recovery gives us a brand new life without all the self-inflicted wounds and unnecessary habits. While recovery takes place on a day by day basis, the changes are at a level so fundamental that they touch every part of our lives.

Frequently we had to force ourselves to endure feeling good. Many of us were afraid to get our hopes up. Helping others put us in situations where we forget ourselves while seeing very clearly where someone else is going wrong. Then we apply the lessons we learn to our own lives.

quote: “We can die from untreated addiction. But before we die, the disease takes from us our pride, our self-esteem, our families and loved ones. And finally, it takes our very will to live.”
body:
The pain and confusion of active addiction makes it hard to realize we are slipping into a hole until it is too late. Actually, there is no such thing as ‘too late.’ Most of us arrive to a N A meeting feeling hopeless and expecting things to get worse. The lessening of despair and the early glimmers of hope come as a surprise! Listening to other members share, we slowly realize we are not alone. Eventually we hear someone share an experience that seems to apply to what we are going through. We try out what worked for them and find it working for us.

In our recovery program, we do not dwell on the horrors of active addiction but rather share and emphasize the processes of recovery. We surrender, and this allows us to end the struggle to deny our addiction but we may persist in our efforts to control our lives. When we discover our unmanageability, we can work the second half of the First Step. We find just by not using, going to meetings and reading N A literature we become focused on a new way to live. Hope is given to us and therefore grows in us.

We hear others share their pain. At other times, they share the joy of being liberated from active addiction. One day we look up and realize the `12 Steps are working for us. Our identification with the members in the rooms of NA allows us to rejoin the human race. Recovery becomes an adventure!

quote: “After attending a few meetings, we began to feel like we were finally getting somewhere. It was in these meetings that we were first introduced to the Twelve Steps of NA.”
body:
Surrender seems like the last thing that would help us dispel the negative feelings and emptiness we feel inside. But if we are powerless, we get instant relief when we stop fighting. Life is not our adversary; addiction is our problem. We find ways to substitute real life and solutions instead of using drugs and their parallel behaviours. We deal with things as they come up.

Sometimes, when we are listening to someone share about what happened when they stopped fighting and began looking for new answers, we have a flash of insight. We Identify with the speaker. An idea catches hold and suddenly we change our minds. Through the miracle of identification, we no longer see change as a threat. It is a jump ahead! We become winners when we can accept life as it is. At last we can learn ways that were blocked by our ego and self-will.

In the Second Step, we go looking for a belief we can trust. We enjoy great freedom in N A because we are free to find our own path, the life of the spirit. We read, discuss and study whatever written materials we can find and if we are dissatisfied we can move on to another belief until we are satisfied and feel ourselves being restored to sanity. This is usually called a peaceful, easy feeling.

The Third Step completes our entry into a new way of life. We find it natural to turn our will and our lives over to the God of our understanding. Many just call their God “Higher Power.” It is the word ‘care’ that is important in this Step. Only a loving H P can restore us to sanity by helping us through the next six Steps!

quote: “Go off by yourself and say silently, ‘God, I’ve made a mess of my life. I can’t solve my problems and I ask you to take care of me and show me how to live.’”
body:
This simple prayer has changed countless lives for the better. These few words show self- awareness, surrender, acceptance, humility, willingness to change, honesty, hope, trust, faith, simplicity, dedication and devotion. When said with sincerity from the heart, we are no longer spiritually bankrupt.

As we grow in recovery, we acquire the personal resources that paint a beautiful picture of spiritual growth and insight. This is true for all addicts who follow our way of life. Rich or poor, educated or not, skilled or rough labor - all take on a spiritual quality. No matter what our social standing, we all seem to shine when filled with the spiritual light! Our growing interest in the well being of others has many positive side effects.

Helping others helps us overcome our selfishness. We see people struggling and want to help. Our sincere desire to help suggests solutions which might not have occurred to us on our own. This insight shows the way we might change ourselves! These solutions may ‘fly in under the radar’ because our ego is out of the way.

quote: “We became capable of depravity--of betrayal to ourselves and to others. Temporarily clean, we ran around trying to get it all squared away before our next spree.”
body:
Waking up can be a painful process when we recall what we did last night or in the recent past. Befuddled and cloudy from mood and mind altering drugs, we suspended good sense and found misbehavior funny. Angry or resentful, we felt liberated when released from unwanted pressure from authority figures whom we did not respect. Sometimes our friends egged us on. Our whole way of thinking became “us” and “them.”

Depravity involves immoral and unacceptable behavior that can create shame and guilt. We know we will feel badly about what we are doing. We find it impossible to stop. Subjecting others to embarrassing treatment can be exciting or titillating. Bad behavior raises questions of correctness and propriety. Our misbehavior becomes worse as we continue.

With the clarity and the focus that comes with recovery, we can see what is happening is not right, and stop it before it goes any further. We can also see other ways to do things that are even more exciting and choose to do them instead, bypassing the wrongdoings. Loving and caring behavior replaces doing what will embarrass us later on. We want to hurry up and clean up the mess because we do not have much time to set things straight

quote: “Our program is in fact a way of life. We learn the value of principles such as humility, surrender, and service. The idea that we have to do it alone is obsolete.”
body:
As a way of life, we enjoy the company of others who are in the same process we are engaged in ourselves. We become so closely associated with fellow members, we really enjoy the type of closeness otherwise found only in families. This is a very natural way to obtain feedback that is simple and honest. Many times their answers turn out to be our answers.

Humility is simply honestly setting aside our egotistical ideas and being open to the experience and thoughts of others. Surrender gets us out of ourselves and frees us to shake off many of the chains that used to bind us. Service involves extending ourselves for others. We can practice projecting our feelings onto those in need and help them find solutions.

The energy and power we get from others bring alternatives into view. We find different ways of looking at things and develop approaches that never occurred to us before. What we do leads to other positive moves and doorways swing open for us.

For example, my first sponsor would say “Ask yourself, what can I do to improve my situation.” I had never thought of looking at things this way before.

Another member told me when we were in the midst of a car problem, “I don’t see these things as problems, I see them as experiences”. This really helped me to avoid the negative when situations went bad for me.”

Old ideas got us into trouble and crippled us to a low standard of behavior. You can’t get a new life with old ideas. As we grow in recovery, we find others sharing about the very problems that plague (plaguing) our lives! Their answers may become our answers. This successful pattern of growing takes hold of us and creates a momentum that feels good while getting good results. Getting to know other addicts seeking recovery gives us answers, identification and a joyous spirit long missing in our lives! [jf/bs]

quote: “When we admit our powerlessness and inability to manage our own lives, we open the door to a power greater than ourselves.”
body:
Belief is about our relationship with a power greater than ourselves. Our belief clarifies our lives because at last we find a pathway to something bigger, smarter and lovingly powerful that has the answers we need. The belief a higher power exists and can help us get results where we used to have this naked, lost feeling. It is a powerful bringer of hope. Praying for guidance, reading, listening to others share their experience and feelings gives us courage to approach our goals. Even our wildest goals can awaken dreams long lost to the insanity of active addiction.

Clinging to the notion we can manage our lives is a control game. By the time we reach the N A program, control is something we choose to leave behind. “If it isn’t practical, it isn’t spiritual.” reminds us to keep our feet on the ground and use spiritual principles to get what we want or need.

This higher power is an endless source of alternative viewpoints and ideas. The energy flows into us as soon as we are open to it. Conscious contact reveals an endless supply of suggestions and approaches that were out of reach before we started working the NA Twelve Steps! It is so powerful some members get carried away on a pink cloud. This is why we talk about working our Steps on a ‘daily basis.’ Yesterday is forever gone and tomorrow never quite gets here. Today is the only day we can make a difference, one tiny act of faith at a time.

[jf/bs]

quote: “Part of ourselves could see what was happening; another part would not accept it. We were caught in an illusion of ‘what if,’ ‘if only’ and ‘just one more try.’”
body:
During our using, the part of us that could see what was happening got ignored or discounted so often, we stopped believing it. Our illusion of “what if,” “if only” and “just one more try” got us building walls of denial so thick, the truth could not be seen (experienced fully). The Steps and the Program gives us (offers) the tools to break down our walls of denial (DENIAL - Don’t Even Notice I Am Lying) with honesty, open-mindedness and willingness. Our insane illusions are replaced with sanity. Sanity is living in harmony with reality. We learn to be fully focused in the present, observing all the joy and beauty that we used to miss in our search for “just one more.”

Not thinking clearly, we sought easy answers that turned out to be substitutes for real action. We were bumping into walls without really getting anywhere. Even in recovery we can find ourselves coasting without thinking things through. It helps to review our goals and methods every once in awhile. The drug induced fog can become so familiar that we live there. Snapping out of habitual ways of doing things gives us a break from our insanity. Going easy on ourselves and developing a watchful attitude towards our inventory gives us time to prepare for the changes ahead.

[jf/bs]

quote: “In the Third Step, we simply recognize that there is a force for good in the world and we cooperate with that force. We let good things happen to us.”
body:
This incredibly beautiful and inclusive observation opens the doorway to a spiritual life. In N A, we need to get on a good footing with the God of our understanding. To get out of the ditch, we have to have more power and this involves finding a belief we can live with. G-O-D is good, orderly direction. Like many things in life, it doesn’t matter where we are, it matters where we’re going. One Step at a time, we find ourselves setting new goals. The road never ends and each break-through leads to another.

Putting what we learn into practice turns up new items to think about and leads us to even brighter horizons of discovery and application. Even dead-ends and apparent failures will often lead us to solutions we could find no other way. Life becomes full of learning experiences. Our goal is not to become perfect. Recovery is ongoing discovery. We just want happiness of the sort we can live with.

Spirituality involves states and experiences that go beyond what words can easily express. Persons going through spiritual growth have trouble sharing with someone just starting out. “When we feel far from God, God seems to be our greatest enemy. The closer we get to God, the more we realize God was our only true friend all along.” Meditation can seem a big waste of time until we realize we are noticing things that have slipped our minds. The original meaning of the Summerian word for prayer was ‘to work for.’

[jf/bs]

quote: “All of us have this one thing in common -- we put the disease that kills us and, one way or another, we usually pay for it, a bag at a time, a few pills at a time, or a bottle at a time until we die.”
body:
One of the benefits of recovery is choice. We can see better, hear better and process information better. This gives our choices the power to draw better conclusions and pick better ways to go forward in our lives. We find better ways to express ourselves and so present more accurately who we are and what we care about. This way we attract people into our lives and we grow in the process. Our living reflects our truth and our feelings.

Putting our energy into the things we really care about or blindly following the obsession of the moment without considering the cost is a choice we have today. Taking life a day at a time helps us reach escape velocity from our old habits and preferences we developed when we were out of our minds. Being in the present is an important tool for finding a new way of living.

Stopping now and then to make a written list of all we have to be grateful for is a good way to improve our mood and raise our energy for the day to come. Saying ‘’thank you” and asking permission before forcing an issue or getting someone’s help smooths out our relations with everyone we encounter.

We may stop and wonder that this way of simply living according to our inner beliefs and genuine desires was not taught to us in early life. The dysfunctions we grew up in confused the better part of us and generates all the problems that come with active addiction. The NA 12 Steps guide us gently into a whole new way of living.

[bs]

quote: “If we have come this far in the N.A. program, we have already noticed some change in our lives. However, the change may not be as fast nor as dramatic as we wish.”
body:
One of the things about recovery we have to learn is we see the world through damaged equipment. Our associations are messed up; we jump to conclusions too quickly. We cobble together a few facts and in our hurry we have yet to establish a habit of calming our minds and bodies so as to achieve a clear state of attention. This is like the need for putting on goggles to see underwater. Without the goggles, we get blurs and debris instead of a clear vision. We move from desperation to inspiration.

Getting in a hurry, we miss things and leave things out. Slow down! Be careful! Build your life up on a firm foundation. You’re not in danger like it used to be. Really, the clean life is based on achieving and practicing good mental habits. We learn to look at things as they really are. If what we are hearing is unclear, we ask questions and don’t let ourselves go off half cocked. That’s what our NA friends are for.

Astonishingly, almost everyone of us is surprised to find out that gratitude is an internal resource instead of an exterior force. We exercise gratitude and find we are often more able to become consciously grateful. This brings us back to consider our need to adjust to positive changes without getting in a hurry! If we want the changes to stick, we give them time to replace old habits and ways of doing things. Again and again, we surrender to our Higher Power.

[bs]

quote: “We find that we will continue to have living problems.”
body:
In time we may see where these ‘problems’ are part of our learning curve and lead us to understandings we could get no other way. We may even reach the point where they are no longer problems but simply challenges we take in stride as we continue to practice living in the solution. Our attitude is to see problems in a different light. If we slow down and do things more carefully, many skills come to us naturally. If we re-read the directions of ask for help from those who have experience with what we are doing, it makes all the difference in the world!

Many times, what we see as problems are just doing things that were impossible or out of reach in active addiction. Many are not that hard, they are just new. Sometimes our members tell us they develop a habit of immediately looking for a solution rather than getting stymied. New things require us to stare at the ‘problem’ area until we notice things that might go together and make connections that lead to a solution.

Assuming we will fail before we even try may be based on our former failures. Calming down, taking a deep breath, making a prayer for our HP to help us, we may find ourselves getting curious and seeing differently. I knew a diesel mechanic who worked for years with a buddy and when one of them was having trouble, they would stop and ask one another for help. The ‘asking’ put them in a state of finding the solution. For one thing, they weren’t alone. Later when his friend got a job in another state, even though he was working alone, when faced with a problem, he would ask himself what would Charlie do about this. Frequently, a solution jumped into his mind out of thin air!

In recovery, we learn that what used to block our way often turns out to be a doorway to a new understanding. When we move from fear-based living to faith-based living we are living in a new existence where every ‘problem’ is just a new doorway about to open.

[bs]

quote: “We examine in depth our relationships with people, places and situations asking ourselves what we have demanded of these relationships. Often the answers will show that we are placing unreasonable demands on reality. WE find that we are demanding other people to stop being who they are.”
body:
As our minds and bodies settle down in recovery, our perceptions become more clear. When we hear, see and think better, much of the world around us becomes clearer. We lose our anger and frustration, one piece at a time. Resolving our lives around our true feelings and accurate conclusions enable us to live with fewer contradictions, so we are not at odds with ourselves.

We find using past behavior as a guide may not contain a skill set that fits our new way of living life. Patience, tolerance and humility get better results because they have worked for many others. Patience gives us time to wait for another person to finish what they have already committed to doing. It is their competence makes them popular.

Tolerance cushions personal interaction where we have to give respect to get respect. People are often willing to match us if we are considerate. Humility diminishes the blockage set in place by our pride and ego. Even if we get someone to do what we want them to do, they will remember being poorly treated and seek to ‘get even’ with us!

What we demand of others, we need to be willing to give ourselves. Setting ourselves above those we claim to care for is not only bad manners, it angers people because it shows they are not being honest. You can’t treat someone badly if we care for them. Giving opens the doorway to receiving and as our demands lessen, our happiness increases.

(bs)

quote: “It doesn’t matter whether we just took a few pills, fixed eight times a day, suck on a pipe, drink bottles of cough remedy behind drug stores, or have one tranquilizer with our first martini each day….. we have certain things in common, no matter to what degree or what kind of addict we are.”
body:
The obsession and compulsion were two things we had in common. We were also all familiar with the feelings: guilt, remorse, fear, self-loathing, anger, desperation, self-pity, inadequacy. The twin attributes of obsession and compulsion just didn’t work. It is like an animal stuck in a laboratory that keeps mashing a button without getting the food. Obsession is losing control of our minds. Compulsion is losing control of our actions. Between bouts of using, we begin to realize it just gets worse and there seems to be no way out. That’s why NA is called “The Last House on the Block.”

Pain is our common denominator. It can actually be a comfort to find there are others who hurt like we do. Just going to meetings with fellow addicts we slowly discover what works for them, works for us. When we find ourselves passing along what we have found to others, the spiritual Program of NA really wakes up for us. We realize there is a network of love, peace and understanding worldwide.

Many members believe when we act as a conduit of love and gratitude the energy that passes through us energizes us in passing. As recovering addicts, the good feelings we enjoy are part of what keeps us coming back. It seems like all people take a moment to reflect on how their lives are going. If you are clean and working the NA 12 Steps, the answer is generally the same: we are steadily getting better.

(bs)

quote: “We quit fighting. We accept our addiction and life the way it is.”
body:
When we forget to work our recovery Steps, we find ourselves slipping back into our old habit patterns. After all, those habit patterns were formed over months or years of active addiction and dysfunctional thinking. When we quit fighting recovery, we can start our days on a whole new platform.

We can learn to start our days being grateful and surrendered. Praying for our HP to help us stay in touch with our surrender, faith and active gratitude, everything in our live’s changes. It is funny how we find ways to get along with others when we are mindful of our release from our addictive prisons. Pain can make us real open-minded! Gratitude can make us more open to what others are saying or going through. When we pay attention, we get attention.

Our disease tells us we can never win and no one really understands or cares. We sink into the old grooves of our past behavior. We catch ourselves sinking and suddenly we reach up, practicing what we have learned in recovery. As we learn to live clean, we fail less and discover more. We begin to see the principles work together: honesty, open-mindedness and the willingness to try. If we are willing, we frequently don’t have to!

Honesty puts us in touch with reality. It feels spooky. Being open, we are free to entertain notions without feeling trapped. We have a choice. The willingness to try often frees us from having to do something. All three of these may seem illogical at times because we realize they are rooted in a higher realm. They are uplifting us into a new life.

(bs)

quote: “This Fourth Step can be a wonderful adventure, reviewing our past performance and our present behavior to see what we want to keep and what we want to be rid of.”
body:
The ‘rubber hits the road’ when we work our Fourth Step. Steps Four through Nine make real the personality changes we have been seeking. The journey of a thousand miles begins with that one simple step forward. Steps Four, Six and Eight prepare us to move forward. Steps Five, Seven and Nine are when we actually make our move!

The cost of freedom is getting used to living without our chains. The old boundaries no longer apply in our new lives. As clear headed-ness and good karma work together, new rules apply. When we stop using drugs, we can go to a family picnic without getting beat up when when we got angry when people didn’t agree with us. We find many family members really like us when we drop some of our old habits. Playing mind games to distract family members from our past behavior doesn’t really work, does it?

Instead of stewing in the past with negative expectations for the future, we can pick and choose what our life experiences will become. We find we can have almost any good thing if we are willing to pay the price and make a little effort.

(bs)

quote: “The thing that will make us better is to clean up our lives by relieving ourselves of guilt. The Eighth Step is a mighty stride away from a lie dominated by guilt and remorse.”
body:
The willingness to admit fault releases the chains that have held us back from re-experiencing painful guilt and shame. Life no longer has to be the way it has been. It is one thing to think about freedom and it is quite another to walk down new roads in our lives. Keeping our wits about us and staying with the ‘one day at a time’ approach to the new life is a real good idea. If we move too fast, we make it easy to take a fall. Slower is quicker.

Looking at our lives in a new light, we realize some to the things that bothered us were not real. Others we skipped over had to be resolved and dealt with. Recovery has a way of changing our pasts and present. That’s how we get a new future! Frequently when we review things that bother us from back when, it occurs that is was the situation that was so painful. There is almost no mean or hurtful thing that could have done worse or more often.

One of the things that may surprise us is when we visit past events and feelings with our current resources: we are not afraid and can see what really took place. As reality sets in, amends become a way out of the endless pain and punishment of guilt. Many times we find our future has been blocked by our lack of understanding and inability to see or hear clearly. Why do so many of us call it ‘messed up?’

(bs)

quote: “We had used all sorts of drugs over the years and experienced numerous living problems as the result of our using and yet did not consider ourselves addicted.”
body:
It usually takes a total catastrophe or disaster to penetrate our walls of denial. Denial is so all pervasive, we actually come to believe our own lies. The acronym “Don’t Even Notice I Am Lying” is such a wonderful description of D-E-N-I-A-L. Spinning stories and then believing them is an ability we have all become so skilled at, we often are not aware we are doing it we exaggerate how bad we are to keep from surfacing what’s really bothering us. If something has been bothering us a long time, we may be surprised with we open up and write about it. It is like another hand is writing the truth from our inner being, without the hedges and blocks held in our minds.

When we hit our bottoms, when our lives are so unmanageable and out of control that we can’t believe our own lies anymore, our addiction becomes apparent. Even then, it often takes a long time before we are ready to do anything about it. Fortunately, recovery gives us that time! Our NA Program is designed by fellow addicts who love and understand what we are going through.

When we finally, truly, accept that we are addicts and can’t live this life of addiction anymore, we can make the admission and surrender required by Step One. Then we begin to recover. We must start developing a relationship with honesty, not just cash register honesty, but the deeper level of self- honesty required to look at ourselves. It takes courage and faith to take a deep look inside and see all the mistakes we have made over the years. Often our loving God releases this information to us over a long period of time. It seems God takes us through some preparation inside before it comes up in our conscious minds.

We need to realize that we are not evil, malicious horrible monsters, but we are simply human beings who made mistakes and poor choices. It a way it is to our credit that we experience guilt and shame. Often situations had more control over what happened than we did. We are capable of learning to make better choices and fewer mistakes. This is where the Fellowship, the Program, a Sponsor and a strong relationship with our Higher Power can help us. There is indeed hope for all of us. We can learn to love ourselves, have lives filled with joy and happiness and become people we are proud to be.

(bs)

quote: “We have surrendered our will and our lives to the care of a power greater than ourselves. We are now a part of the Ultimate Reality which has brought Order out of Chaos.”
body:
As long as your belief works for you, we are happy for you. We are serious about finding a loving Higher Power. There are many equal and lower powers.

Our Program only asks that this Power being loving, caring and greater than we are. Ascribing the characteristics of loving and caring is not difficult for us because we certainly had surrendered our lives to a vengeful harmful, even killing, power greater than we are in our active addiction. Surrendering to our growing understanding of a Higher Power is easy. Holding on to the pain and indecision is hard. Our HP will not only do us no harm but actually help us to relieve our pain.

As the drug induced fog lifts, we discover how many of the parts fit in an order that can only be called divine. We advise members to ‘take it easy’ so the changes stick and the broken parts can be replaced or restructured. Clean, time is on our side.

(bs)

quote: “Upon working Step One, we affirmed our surrender to the principles of N.A, only then did we overcome the alienation of addiction.”
body:
From the very beginning, we are members of NA. For many of us this is our first experience with the life saving principle of vulnerability. Our unconditional surrender is the key that will unlock the door and allow others into our lives and us a way out! This provides the help we need and helps us avert our tendency to continue in self will.

The pain of being clean and having the realization that we cannot successfully use drugs, not use others, and control situations through manipulation and control. When we find that we are unable to control life and see how our lives are unmanageable, we can free ourselves through surrender. Surrender becomes a positive move away from things that no longer work for us. We find it helps to bring ourselves to full consciousness whenever we need to make a new surrender.

Hope for something better helps us surrender and we find there are many like us. We are never alone. Identification lets us see up close how many of our defects are on the surface and it doesn’t take much to pull them out by the roots! Sometimes there is a moment of fear when we do this but it always results in feeling better almost right away.

(bs)

quote: “We look at our actions during the day and we practice the art of looking at where we were wrong, considering what we could have done differently, and the amends we need to make.”
body:
Step Ten helps us keep recovery new and fresh by sweeping the debris of our life before we can trip over it. It is also vital to our ongoing recovery because it keeps us from making up things under the guise of prayer and meditation. The old phrase, “if it ain’t practical, it ain’t spiritual” has served many a member well in recovery. A spiritual life requires cleaning and grooming to stay in adjustment with reality.

Jimmy K, one of our early members, called NA the self-adjusting program. Every member cleans their own spot. If we see someone else making a mistake, we learn to be gentle and respectful just as we find it works better if we are gentle and respectful with our own faults and errors. Life happens and we go along with it, doing our best as we go, making amends as needed and helping others wherever we can.

Amends are not as bad as trying to leave them undone. After we can clearly see what went wrong, our personal sense of right and wrong will give us all the prompting we need. If we really care about people, we don’t want to be hurting them in any way. Ongoing inventory is also a vital part of the prayer and meditation in Step Eleven. Without spiritual inventory, we are subject to mistaking strength and guidance for wishful thinking and self-service!

One of the reasons we, as a Fellowship, settled on the words ‘clean’ and ‘clean time.’ is that it suggests getting rid accumulated dirt and debris as we go forward. It is said that big airliners are off course 90% of the time and have self-correcting electronics to keep them on course. Recovering addicts also need self-correction to stay on course!

(bs)

quote: “We are not going to be perfect. If we were perfect, we would not be human.”
body:
Perfection may simply be a fear-based notion on the mental plane. Our intuition and spiritual sensitivity go way beyond the mental plane! There is no one grading our papers! Recovery is rejoining a process begun millions of years ago. It’s all about being a human being. In recovery, we regain our humanness. Humans make mistakes, correct the mistakes and learn from them as we go. Our learning spreads to address all aspects of a subject. When our understanding contains most of the pertinent aspects of a subject, we are said to be informed. Slowing down and developing the habit of thinking through our understanding is part of the 12 Step recovery process.

In the states of confusion associated with using drugs, we are too busy, to hurried. We don’t feel we have time to think things through clearly. The Buddha said, “May who lives in house that is burning down is always in a hurry.’ This results in about ninety percent of our problems! Proceeding without fully seeing the landscape before us, we can only stumble blindly ahead. Surrendering and connecting with a power greater than ourselves, we find the time to do things once, instead of doing them a dozen times. There is always time to do it over but there is never time to do it right in the first place.

Spending time with clean addicts gives us a chance to review our mental habits and our ways of communicating. Going to meetings and spending time with members is essential to recovery. Otherwise, we begin to float off on our own cloud. Reading recovery literature written by addicts, for addicts also helps put us in a ‘recovery state of mind.’

Perfection may be approached gradually. At any point, there is much to be done! Many of us had to just go to meetings and not use drugs for a while until we began to clear up. Keeping a recovery journal is another good approach for some members. The point is we have to keep varying our approach until we get the results we want. Sometimes all we have to do is just turn up the volume by going to more meetings, reading more material and listening more attentively to our fellow members share their experience, strength and hope.

(jf/bs)

quote: “Day by day we discover the magnitude of the Third Step.”
body:
If we put pennies in a jar every day we are clean, the jar would fill to overflowing, we would have to get another jar! After some time, we would have a row of jars. We have countless opportunities to make bad choices and regrettable decisions each and every day. Not making these poor choices becomes a tidal wave of goodness touching every current and ripple of our lives. Recovery gives us back our losses while eliminating new mistakes as we go.

If we are really surrendered to being an addict and if we have found a belief in a higher power, turning our life and will over should be easy. The test will be seeing how things work out with our HP in charge! We will always be learning more about the God of our understanding. Again and again we remind ourselves our HP is not a lesser or equal source of strength and guidance, it is on a higher plane of reality! When we share in meetings, many times we voice things that were going on inside that may not have yet surfaced in our minds. When we listen to others share, we often find clues and hints that apply in our own lives. In this way, we find answers popping up on a daily basis. It is important that we have the time and desire for recovery to bring these resources into our daily practices.

Usually, every recovery speaker I listen to there is one “sound bite” that really hits home. One example is a lady I heard say, “I have seem better people than me relapse.” At the time, that really got me! It’s not about subjugating others, it is about subjugating our own egos.

(jf/bs)

quote: “We needed proof. In that N.A. meeting we found people like ourselves with the same, or worse patterns of of drug dependency and failure, yet they were clean. They smiled. Their eyes were clear. They cared for each other.”
body:
Patterning after happy people seems to be an innate quality in human beings. Before we had the NA program in our lives, we had a real shortage of ‘happy people.’ We can pick up good vibes and without much preparation, find ourselves looking, feeling and sounding better. As our internal state changes from a fear-based outlook over to a positive setting where good things not only possible, they are common. Spiritual growth is practicing being happy, being fulfilled. Before we got into the 12 Steps, spirituality seemed like something distant and well out of reach. Recovery brings it into our day to day lives. We see and hear what works for others and try out their solutions for ourselves!

Some members use the phrase, ‘acquired learnings’ to refer to things that may not yet be part of our personal experience. As more and more is added to our internal resources, we find ourselves repeating the things we have heard from other members to newcomers and we get to see simple ideas and phrases help jar a person out of a negative view.

The NA program is full of people who had no hope and no way out of their addiction. Bit by bit our habits and mental processes are reformed into working parts of our lives. We may find ourselves thinking, “Gee, if this is what works for me, why didn’t someone tell me?” Miracles are so commonplace in NA, that we soon take them for granted. This is where recalling past pain can help us continue up recovery road!

The old saying, “Everything we needed to learn to get clean, we learned in the first ninety days.” We keep forgetting and often remind ourselves by helping others. A visitor said in a meeting, “If I’m not happy, I’m not working my program!”

(jf/bs)

quote: “We make our list, or take it from our Fourth Step and add to it any more people we can think of and we face that list honestly and openly and examine our fault.”
body:
In working our program, we make lists so that our goals are attainable and not insurmountable. There is a simple way to look at Step Nine. There are stories like the one about a princess who could feel a pea beneath a bunch of mattresses. Another phrase refers to a burr under the saddle that gives your horse the jitters . If these things didn’t bother us, they probably wouldn’t be on our Eighth Step list! When we reach way down and wiggle them loose, we have settled old issues and are free to find different ways to spend our time and energy. Otherwise, they each drain tiny bits on a daily basis until we are empty.

Putting closure on things from the past frees up time and attention for our future. Clean is not only measured in time, it is also the freed up energy. We are sometimes surprised to find new ways to live without endlessly repeating what doesn’t work for us! We need to remind ourselves that working the 12 Steps is how we crawl up out of the endless pit of addictive pain and confusion. It is not something we do for our spouses, parents or other family members and friends. We are the forst beneficiaries of our recovery.

When it’s the proper time for the amends, the means appears. People we have wronged show up. Money suddenly appears to settle past accounts. Our willingness surprisingly rises up to the occasion. The Ninth Step is a gift we give to ourselves like no other.

One of the bonuses of recovery is we realize how useless it is to lie to ourselves! Honestly looking at our inventory is the only way it will work for us! The whole idea is self-betterment. Our happiness depends on it!

(jf/bs)

quote: “A problem many of us seem to have with the Eighth Step and the admission of the harm we did is the belief we were victims, not victimizers, in our addiction.”
body:
The endless quest to look good and sound innocent avails us nothing here. Getting real and finding the source of our pain is worth the tiny jet of honesty it takes to really look at what we have been up to in active addiction and early recovery. But instead of increasing our feelings of doom, we experience relief and joy. Whatever it was, the main thing is to stop feeding the thinking and habits that produce such results.

When we stagger out of a drug induced fog, we often get things turned around and fail to realize how much of a part we played in painful memories of the past. After all, we are the main actors in our own movie! If we felt good about the things we said and did, we wouldn’t need the NA program. That quiet little voice down inside us is available to help as needed. When we ask for God’s help, surprising answers occur out of thin air!

The Fourth Step can be fodder for the items on the Eighth Step. The people we harmed, the money we owe, the loose ends to be dealt with. Facing these things with a good attitude and appropriate action, we become free to enjoy today and look forward to tomorrow.

We put ourselves on the list as being harmed. When I was new, one oldtimer told me “As an addict, you have a right to be angry at what happened to you out there”. I remember one newcomer who was straight but let guys have sex with him in caskets for money for his habit and caused him to get aids. He had a right to be angry at what happened to him out there. In recovery, he asked himself, “Was he running from his situation or compromising on a daily basis.” In recovery we slowly learn to not compromise.

(bs/jf)

quote: “We think of love as a shield against the attacks of other people, and as a weapon to blast through walls of hate, and to open closed hearts and closed minds. We feel protected by our love, through adversity, discouragement, anger, and insecurity.”
body:
Love is a word that takes on many new meanings in recovery. We find that we even use love to justify anger or jealousy. We never had time to think through our feelings before. We were too busy making others happy! Maybe we thought we did but the ‘drug induced fog’ scrambled what happened and why it upset us. The love and caring in Step 12 has to do with something more soulful. We not only mean we appreciate and want to see someone we love happy and doing well, recovery furnishes us ways to help make that difference in their lives. Unconditional love also means we don’t put expectations or make deals like, “I love you so you have to satisfy my needs and wishes!” That’s not love at all, it is a justification for manipulation and control! Subjugation of others is not a worthwhile goal. Participation gives us a voice without the need to control.

Love becomes an active principle for living in a new way. Real love is healing and goes way beyond ‘intending’ goodness, it can provide that goodness. When we spend part of our time thinking about how we can be of service to others, we have less time to be mean and selfish. The activities of loving and caring enrich our lives. We often find we have more after giving than we had before!

When others are unfair or deceitful, we begin to realize how understand the anger raised by others we tried to dominate in the past.

(jf/bs)

quote: “We forgive more easily, anger more slowly, expect less, and give more to our brothers and sisters.”
body:
My Father’s sister once told me, “You never get back all you put out, so if you want a lot, you have to give more than you want to get.” While there are other famous beliefs, I never forgot this one from my Aunt. When I become a ‘giver’, my ‘getting’ goes up. It takes time and energy away from my ‘selfishness.’

Life is not for ‘getting’ things, life is for ‘giving’ to others. Following the NA 12 Steps, I reconcile the parts of me that thwart each other and settle down to my real self. From this ‘seat of power’ I am less easily knocked off balance and less threatened by what others say, think and do.

These aspects of recovery are not to be forgotten or pushed aside. They are a few of the aspects of recovering addicts different from the way we were before. We find ourselves giving real love and caring and feeling ok about it. Since we learn to not put strings on our love, we don’t have to go through the expectations that complicate a simple act of kindness. As we give this sort of love, we also discover we can accept love like this. Perhaps by giving it, we realize the feeling experienced by those who express a willingness to help us.

People are the most important things on the planet. Clear-headed and with no selfish goals in mind, we begin to see how each part affects the whole. So, cutting corners and forcing things into the narrow confines of our minds loses it power to hold us back.

(bs/ye)

quote: “Often, when we get home, write out a resentment, explaining how we feel and how we became angry, and the part we played. We find out how to restrain ourselves later so we don’t repeat that action.”
body:
The freedom of surrender eliminates excuse making and dodging our responsibility. Things we could not see clearly jump out at us on closer examination. Having a clear head free from the issues brought up by fear of repeating past mistakes and situations not likely to occur now that we are clean. Having N A friends and a sponsor to talk over what’s happening with us makes a big difference. This is how human contact makes on the spot re-evaluations - the way they do in a healthy family. People close to us help us see ourselves through their eyes. The time and energy they take with us helps create a better future for us. The love of a brother or a sister creates space in our hearts that opens us to gives and receiving help in our future. This is one of the parts we have to discover if we grew up in a dysfunctional family. Gress, what? We all grew up in dysfunctional families.

We couldn’t see it and neither could our families since they probably reflect the world they grew up in. Optimize the goodness and mimimize the downside. Pile up the good stuff until it overshadows the field of view and keep going, giving and learning every step of the way.

Many parts of our program of recovery consist of repairing mishaps resulting from simply taking things the wrong way or poor thinking. Emotions should not keep us from thinking clearly. Just because a feeling is strong does not make it real, right or justified! We addicts often get it wrong and recovery helps us reconsider before we regret what we say or do. Patience and understanding work better than being right or putting others at a disadvantage.

Making gratitude lists, recovery journals and the discoveries we experience in meetings all work towards actualizing the ‘real self’ within each of us. These home made tools help us get a grip on our recovery and accumulate a written record of what we learn in our own words.! Taking ideas and spiritual concepts and making them into real, working parts of our life helps our personal program grow from small seeds of desire into large trees of new experience!

(bs/ye)

quote: “We do however, remember that God not us is responsible for our change.”
body:
If it were up to us, we would be able to have someone do it for us. In reality, we do have something else helping us change, God. When we finally decide to get clean, get involved in a home group, find a sponsor, and begin working the steps, we let go of our self-centered, self- righteous ways. We let go completely and find that we are being changed and many of us realize we are not doing it. We find that a power greater than ourselves is taking hold and we are allowing ourselves to be transformed into the person we always wanted to be and never knew how. This is how surrender works to set us free!

The importance of this distinction lies down the timeline when we look back and realize how much we have changed. If we imagine it is we ourselves that accounts for the changes, it can easily lead us back into self-will. Attributing the changes to our Higher Power frees us from ego and gives us a path to continue the process of seeking recovery and freedom through inventory, prayer and meditation.

It may help to try conceiving of God as what is to come as well as what has already manifested. Of course, from God’s Viewpoint, each part of reality is part of something whole and complete and we are like ant’s making our way on a huge anthill of all there is! With the help of a loving God, we can slow down for the curves and speed up for the straight aways.

It is said, NA is a self-correcting program. We surrender, come to believe and turn our lives over to a loving Higher Power. The ‘we’ in the NA Twelve Steps is ‘us.’

(bs)

quote: “Ask anyone who has some time on the program and who has the kind of life you want for yourself, they will tell you that the Fourth Step was a turning point in their lives.”
body:
In our early days, many members viewed the Fourth Step as upsetting and many felt pain and upset. The reality is that Step Four takes us out of our old habits and ways of dealing with life. We are free to examine and evaluate what we want to keep and what no longer fits our life. This is the mechanism of personality change where our surrender opens the way to escape our old ways of dealing with life.

When addicts let go of things of the past, they clear the way for future growth. What used to be habitual and we considered to be a genuine ‘part’ of our being, falls away and does not come back - so long as we stay on the 12 Step path. In some periods of confusion, not all members understand or learn to see recovery this way. Like an old timer said, “Nobody likes to admit complete defeat! “

The turning point can best be scene when we look back on it. Clean time gives us a wider view from which to see ourselves. When we look back, our minds and memories are more clear and accurate. Our willingness to make amends or just laugh at ourselves is greater. We are reminded to match up the changes we experience with our willingness to go along with our HP.

(bs)

quote: “Many of us, before we take Step Five, fear that God will turn away from us, when we reveal ourselves to Him.”
body:
There are plenty of curves our disease will throw at us to keep us worried and fearful. We have to look to our old-timers to see what they have learned. They share that the process of spiritual growth is never ending. The higher we climb the mountain of spiritual growth and recovery the more mountain appears before us.

As soon as we think we are approaching the top of the mountain, we see a little more mountain appear before our very eyes! We see not only what’s in front of us, we also see beyond! And this process teaches us to expect more to come into view as we rise step by step.

When we share our innermost feelings about the painful areas of our personal past, there is nothing left to fear. Taking Step Five requires us to put our Faith into action. Of course, we all have the lurking awareness that our Higher Power already knows this stuff but coming out in the open and sharing our Fourth Step with God, ourselves and another human being puts it all on the line, in black and white.

No longer fearing divine retribution over our past lives frees us to continue our journey on recovery road. It is like an athlete in training surpassing a goal that lets them know they can run in the race and have a chance of winning. It gives wings to our hope of ongoing recovery. Let it be said that the list of past wrongs is a list of what has been bothering us for so long - and upsetting us whenever we begin to get ahead in life. The peace this gives us doesn’t go away.

(bs)

quote: “We no longer wish to participate in the problem. We now serve God. In this manner of service, we renew our vow to turn our will and lives over to the care of God.”
body:
The brightness and pain of active addiction does more that make us say, “Ouch.” Hey, it really hurts. With the pain comes the willingness. Most people realize there are ups and downs in life. If you have drifted away from the things that brought you freedom and relief in the beginning of recovery, you do not have to take a fall to get back with the program, just do what it says here: “Renew your vow to turn your will and life over to the care of God.” That’s all it takes. Just say the words to yourself a few times. Like a sticky light switch, keep flipping it until the light comes on!

It may help to put the Third Step prayer in terms of the First Step surrender. Giving up and taking back our ego and self-will is not going to work for us any better than it did before... It was our ability to give up absolutely that empowered our recovery at the very beginning. It is a constant source of amazement to watch newer members surrender and begin their recovery the way many of us did: just accept our unmanageability and take recovery one day at a time.

Complicating things and trying to make recovery harder will not work! Simple surrender and faith works best.

Renewal gives us hope afresh. Positive expectation allows us to surface feelings of optimism and gratitude that we have made it this far. It is rational to think about how dependent recovery is on our desire and belief that it is possible and others have done what we are doing with good results. Actually terrific results in most cases. Some have trouble keeping their faith bright and taking the actions that give life to our daily adventure in the clean life!

(bs)

quote: “Step One means that we don’t have to make excuses for the way we are, and that is a great freedom.”
body:
Our freedom results in more energy and open-mindedness. This is the raft we ride into the Second Step where we find connections to a power that can help us. Surrender would not work out on the streets or places where we have all been. In the loving, caring circle of friends in an N A meeting we find a protected environment made just for us. As we grow in recovery, our need for and ability to surrender grows as well.

Along with our own experiences, we see the Program work for others. It is a funny thing, the people we may see as hopeless or least likely to stay clean and find recovery are often the ones who had really had enough.

The gifts of a good mind and many of the good things that so many take for granted often keep us from really giving up the process of rationalizing and minimizing our need for help! “Yeah, I messed up. I reached the point where I just couldn’t make excuses any more. That’s when I asked for help and meant it.” Painful as it may be to reach this level of despair and desolation, the joy that follows surrender is all the greater!

(bs)

quote: “Trusting others doesn’t come naturally to us, but we must learn to trust because an addict alone is in bad company. The needed strength wasn’t there until we found N.A., which showed us how to make faith work for us.”
body:
What a statement! Faith does work for us but we have to learn a little to get it right. Faith is real evidence of things unseen. The difference shows in how we make new choices and how we react to events and circumstances. Positive expectation may be a big part of it. Expecting good things to happen Keeps our minds open to positive outcomes. If we let ourselves believe our situation is hopeless, it is hard to see good opportunities. Watching others get clean and stay clean, it is easy to see how the evidence of recovery working for us. Asking questions and responding to the answers opens many doors.

Reading recovery literature, sharing and listening in meetings, it feels right and many of the topics we learn more about are things we stumbled onto in the past but were unable to follow through on. Now we can read, listen in meetings and then ask questions to clarify our understanding.

Keeping a recovery journal lets us mark down breakthroughs in our understanding as they occur. It matters not if we ever go over them later, just the physical act of writing down things that are important seems to imbed it in our minds! Written affirmations embed positive images into our minds and helps lead us deeper into our faith and belief.

(bs)

quote: “You may approach the Fourth Step in a number of ways. It is advisable that before you start, go over the first Three Steps with your sponsor.”
body:
We need our surrender, belief and our decision to let our Higher Power care for us - lest we try to take control and work our Fourth Step on self-will! If this happens to you, just take a break and think about how it feels to push harder on something that is not working. Slow down and think about your HP until you get that ‘cared for feeling.’ Remember, we don’t want to lose our assets, just the attributes and habits of mind and action that make trouble for us.

Sharing our perceptions with a trusted friend or sponsor gives us a chance to catch ourselves so we don’t slip into the limitations that used to pin us down. The Third Step makes it clear our relationship with our Higher Power is positive, God will supply us with energy and ideas that open the way before us. Many members conclude that God, our High Power of choice, works the next six Steps for us.

We bring everything we have learned to the fore and turn our life and will over to the care of a loving God. Not the tyranny or the confinement but more of the good feelings that helped us get with the NA Program in the first place.

(bs)

quote: “Surrender means not having to fight anymore. It took a while, for some of us, to realize how unmanageable our lives had become; for others, this was about the only thing of which we could be sure.”
body:
Surrender becomes one of the most powerful weapons in our arsenal. As we truly accept our powerlessness over just about everything but ourselves, we find our power over ourselves is growing. We can change our attitudes, our beliefs, our feelings, our actions and our responses. We learn to take a moment to think about our actions in accordance with our growing belief system and then respond instead of just blindly going into action!

When we fight, we wear ourselves out! With our energy expended, we thrash about like an animal in a trap. Surrendered, we just stop and see what happens. Admitting our powerlessness helps put an end to futile attempts to keep up a good front while crashing and burning inside. Unmanageability is a good big word to cover what we are admitting to. When we were kids on the playground at school fighting with another kid, giving up meant the other child had to get off us and we could stand up and dust our cloths off. It gave us a chance to go on with our play!

Looking back over our past lives, we don’t want to repeat the error of fighting only to find ourselves creating situations that trap us in situations that require even more surrenders in the future. So, instead of fighting, we surrender each day anew and always hope for the best. It is easier to do life once the right way than to do something a dozen times wrong.

(bs)

quote: “In the old days we knew, deep down inside, that we were faking it whenever we indulged in our delusions of grandeur and self-importance. Now, because we were beginning to love ourselves unashamedly, we can love other people more completely, because we no longer feel like we have anything to hide from anyone.”
body:
These are priceless blessings that money cannot buy. Many people live out their lifetimes without activating these aspects from the list of available potentials. We each progress from one area of concern to the next one that comes into view as a result of our growth. Ongoing recovery, one day at a time, is ongoing growth and discovery.

Our membership in N A gives us a new social setting that allows for special people like us, people are living a spiritual recovery program on a daily basis. We can self- correct as we go because when we live spiritual principles, we don’t have to fight life. Helping others gives us insight into how others feel when they are helping us. They’re helping us, not hurting! Because of identification hurting one another in N A would not make sense.

As real talent and ability replace a sick need for egotism, we let go of our fear- based habits and attitudes. We see others floundering to hold on to their controlled lives and we no longer want to embarrass ourselves that way.

(bs)

quote: “Without N A, few of us would have survived, and fewer still would have found recovery.”
body:
We are still constructing N A as we know it. Our experienced members are often appalled at the members who seem to think we just make up things and do not realize we learned from those who have gone before.

Our written literature helps our members get in touch with the reality of passing on experience that has proven itself in the lives of many addicts. Those who have a vested interest in N A want to continue growing and better carry a message of recovery based on the actual experience of our members. The scars of our addiction and the host of ideas carried in the minds and hearts of our members in different stages of recovery make an interesting mix.

Keeping up appearances has to be balanced with the truth of inner experience. Financial issues and concerns need to balance with the love and concern we have for others. The things we gather in recovery should never take precedence over our need for our meetings and the others who walk our path.

People sort out at different levels of understanding and behavior. Nobody’s perfect and no one is so knocked back they can’t get better. We are always on our way up from who we were to something better. Spirituality stabilizes us at a certain point of clarity and it gets easier from there. But we have to keep surrendering and seeking help if we are to continue growing.

(bs)

quote: “As long as we preserve our clean time we enjoy the greatest possible advantage over our disease. For this we are grateful. The first thing to do is get clean. This makes the other stages of recovery possible. Learn that addiction is a disease. Try to forgive past behavior and realize it was caused by our disease. Go to meetings and study the program for your own personal growth.”
body:
This amazing paragraph is supremely important for it contains basic information that has worked for literally countless addicts all over the planet Earth. All faiths are welcome to N A ! All nationalities, languages, national historical settings fit in with us. Addicts are the same wherever they come from and so is recovery!

While there is no magical cure for addiction, it can be arrested. We stop the disease in its tracks when we stop using. Our using ways and character defects may roll on for a while but the root is cut.

Like a man said in a Southern California prison, “If I want to feel good, I have to stop doing the things that make me feel bad.” Like a shoe that fits all feet, N A seems to work for all types of people, all educational levels, etc. The principles are universal and work for all people regardless of their state, status or situation.

(bs)

quote: “Obsessiveness is really common to all the addicts we have met or heard of. Our ego tells us we can do it on our own, then loneliness and paranoia return.”
body:
Obsession is losing control of our thinking. We get afraid and rush ahead to regain control, at least in our imagination. Body drugs may fuel our desperation. Our ego is our false sense of self. It erects barriers that keep others out and keeps us lonely within.

Obsession on the physical level is compulsion, losing control of our our actions. Both work together in active addictions or the early stages of relapse. Spiritual principles start with surrender and getting help. Working on self-improvement and helping others keeps us out of trouble while we are getting with the program.

Isn’t it funny that when we stop flopping around and acting out of desperation, our lives get better. Then when we think we are ready to take control again, we begin to have trouble again. That’s why recovery is surrender on a daily basis!

(bs)

quote:
“Our egos used to control us in all kinds of subtle ways. For one thing, it seemed important for us to compete with others in almost all of our endeavors. Some of us even refused to try something that we might not be the best at. We watched other people closely , not with any concern for their wellbeing, but to check and see if we were measuring up to their standards.

“With all this negative expectation, when we did well, we were only confused. Everyone we met was sized up in terms of how they could be useful to us or a threat to our self-esteem.”
body:
These are the old rules of our lives. It wasn’t our fault, it is stuff we learned in our families, in our schools and among all the people we interacted with. You might call them, “The ways of the world.” When we get physically sick, we are put in a hospital where we can get well. In recovery, we find a need to be among clean addicts so we can get well. Fear-based thinking and acting only made us sick again. Many say, “We don’t just work the Steps, we live the Steps.”

Finding NA literature and other clean addicts, we let the NA ways into our lives. Mimicry let us take in new ways of seeing life and doing things differently. At times it was scary but when something works, it is attractive!

(bs)

quote: “Remember, we didn’t become addicted in one day. We can’t possibly solve all our problems at once, no matter how much energy we have. Take it easy! The only way we recover is to apply what we learn from each other, on a daily basis.”
body:
When we get away from the simple, true, proven program of N A, we can drift into endless complication. Keep it simple is more that just a neat phrase! The N A Program is assembled out of the combined experiences of men and women who found answers in the 12 Steps and work within the boundaries and principles of the 12 Traditions. To let our minds get fuzzy and drift away from what works for us, oftentimes the only thing that works for us, is insanity rejoined!

Years ago, when N A started growing, it seemed to take a long time for people to get with the program. Nowadays, it seems we are adjusted to the deep changes required for spiritual growth and personality change. When you have a disease that can kill you, people seem more willing to take the time!

We are continuing to learn as breakthroughs in neuroscience, general medicine and individual recovery push back the relapse line. Oh, you can still relapse but there is much more information on recovery you have to avoid or push aside than we had back when N A started growing.

(bs)

quote: “We pray for willingness and humility and finally get honest about our mistake in judgment or bad decision. We tell those who were hurt that we were to blame, ask them to forgive us and make whatever amends necessary. Now we’re in the solution again.”
body:
When we are lonely and scared, we are full of negative expectation. Willingness can seem threaten because we are accustomed to failure. The amazing thing is that in many cases, the embarrassment and feeling of not being able to perform well evaporates and we don’t have to do much of anything hard. We often find ourselves slowly down and just doing the next right thing. This is the miracle of ‘willingness. When we are willing, we usually find we don’t have to…

When there are amends to be made, we experience joy being able to set things right. Usually, there are some people we owe amends that we can’t locate and so we have to give what we have, the willingness to make amends when we can. Often writing a letter to the person and filing it with our personal papers can help.

(bs)

quote: “We think that the Tenth Step helps prevent relapse.”
body:
Ongoing inventory allows us to see and react without all the confusion and fear that kept us from being able to deal with life on life’s terms. Inventory gives us momentary pauses to look around and see what’s happening while we are focused on a task at hand. Just doing this prevents minor problems growing whereas it only takes a moment to correct. When our life was confused and desperation prevented us from making a plan and working the plan, many of our efforts fell short of satisfaction. Now, we begin our life anew!

From the beginning, we are able to find new resources that come with membership starting when we surrender to our addiction and our need for help. Going slow and getting it right helps up break up old thinking habits and the messes we make when we get in a hurry. Resources refer to things that have worked for us in the past. They are also people we can turn to for help. Calmly thinking things through makes a big difference.

The Tenth Step readies us for what is to come next in our recovery. As we grow we need to inventory what comes through prayer and meditation. There is great energy in these two aspects of our Tenth Step. Through being clear headed and generally in better working order, we will spot items of inventory we missed in our Fourth Step. But how about when we want something on the spiritual level after we have learned how the spirit within controls what happens in our daily lives. Prayer lets us reach out and meditation lets us take in new perceptions and ideas that do not come to a careless person. (bs)

quote: “There is something in our self-destructive personality’s cries for failure. It permeates our total being – “You do not deserve to succeed.” We have all experienced this and without the knowledge that we finally have earned the right to be responsible, respectable members of society. The manner in which we returned to our addiction is not important. What is essential to each of us is that we have the choice not to continue.”
body:
Written recovery presents assets found by members of N A who have gone the way before you and wrote down their thoughts and feelings to make your way easier. We did not come to the program with a great deal of self-worth and self-esteem. In fact, we felt lower than an ant’s belly before most of us became willing to try a new way of life. When we were beaten we became willing as the old saying goes. It is the deep inner sense of unworthiness that kept most of us In the downward spiral of active addiction as we used, made more messes In our lives then had to use some more to live with the guilt and anguish of what we did while on our last run.

We get off this merry-go-round by surrendering. We surrender control of our lives to the program of Narcotics Anonymous. For most of us this was a huge relief just to be able to let go of control of an unmanageable life, what a relief! Once we get even a little bit of clean time, we begin to see more clearly how the drugs had been managing our lives for quite some time now. As we have small success in recovery like not using just for today or even for a whole month, we begin to see a glimmer of self-esteem, maybe for the first time, In a long time, In our own eyes. Our friends in the fellowship will give us the hope we no longer have as they applaud each new key tag we pick up, yes! N.A. gives us hope. If it can work for some of these crazy lunatics, maybe, just maybe it can work for me. As we work steps with our sponsor, we begin to grow in recovery which as they say happens over time not overnight. As weeks turn into months and months to years, we begin to see a new vision of ourselves as basically happy responsible people. As our insides are healed, we tend to do much less self-destructive stuff because we feel worthy of much more We are recovering!
moment: Just for tonight; I will trust the process of recovery and hold onto the new truth that more will be revealed and a new me is shining through. (ko)

quote: “When we pray and take action it always goes better for us.”
body:
The difference between my will and God’s will is results! When it’s my will, the results are good for me and no one else. They are short-lived and only work for a little while, usually at someone else’s expense. When it’s God’s will the results are good for me and everyone else! It’s like a missing piece in a puzzle, it fits just right.

Turning one’s will and life over to our Higher Power grants us access to great power and tremendous vision. In recovery we can use this Power to build our new lives. Every day is a new chance to practice our new life and do things differently knowing we are acting in the best interest of all and not just ourselves.
moment:
Just For Tonight: As we become more God-Centered and less Self-Centered people, we seek to do our Higher Power’s will above our own. We have found that God’s will is usually what we really want and need.

Ko March 15, 2016

quote: “Attending meetings daily, living a day at a time, and reading the literature seems to send our mental attitude towards the positive. Willingness to try what has worked for others is vital.”
body:
A sponsor shared a simple concept with his sponsee. “If you live in the problem, the problem gets bigger and if you live in the solution, the problem goes away” If we are wondering what the right thing to do is when life crashes in on us, we simply do the very things that got us clean. We call our sponsor, go to meetings regularly, raise our hand and share. We stick with the winners, pray and dive into Step work.

We are reminded that the very things we did to get clean will most certainly help us walk through the difficult and troubling times in our lives. By working a strong program, we most certainly are living in the solution and not the problem today. Just as it paid off when we were new by getting and keeping us clean, so too will our recovery effort keep us centered and grounded in times of woe.
moment:
Just for tonight; I will get out of the problem and get into the solution by working my program like I did when my ass was falling off in my first few weeks clean!

ko March 15, 2016

quote: “We found that we could no longer control our using, nor could we manage our own lives. This surrender was the starting point of our recovery, and is a primary point of unity for the Fellowship.”
body:
Without surrender we find it hard or impossible to admit our need for help or express our need to those who might have our answers. As one member put it; “First I surrendered to recovery and did not use for just one day. The next morning when I awoke, I surrendered to the 2nd half of step one… THAT MY LIFE WAS NONE OF MY BUSINESS!”

When we surrender, we surrender it all. If we have learned one thing in hitting bottom it was that our lives could not be managed by us. We managed to lose just about everything that was near and dear to us. For most we managed to live on the outskirts of life, either by choice or by necessity avoiding something we had screwed up royally. We may be wanted by the law, or hiding from angry creditors, in laws, outlaws.

We need to fully conceive in our inner most selves that we need direction and guidance by others who have been where we came from and have found a new way to live. In time we will learn to incorporate that new way of living by spiritual principles. Only then does our lives become manageable. But for now, it is suggested that we stop trying to run the show as all we end up doing is running it into the ground.
moment:
Just for tonight; I will let go of my life and trust that the fellowship can show me a new way to live. What have I got to lose?

[ko]
