quote: “Our purpose is to achieve freedom from the guilt we have carried so far, with so much pain, so that we can look the world in the eye with neither aggressiveness nor fear.”
body:
In the 9th step in the Basic Text it clearly states the purpose of working the steps up to that point “we forgive others, possibly we are forgiven and eventually forgive ourselves.” This is the main purpose of working the 12 steps so we can finally, at long last, we can get to a place where we forgive ourselves. Once we reach this part of the steps we are truly a free and open channel to love others.

One of the questions a sponsor asked his sponsee about his past ways was.. “could the person you are today do all that negative selfish stuff to the ones you love?” The sponsee thought for just a second and then answered of course not! The sponsor suggested the principle of anonymity. Can’t you grant yourself your own anonymity, you give it to newcomers and other members freely. If we can let go of all we know about ourselves from the past and grant ourselves anonymity by seeing only the new person we have become, we surely know in our heart of hearts that we would not cheat on our spouses, leave our children with no food and use the money for drugs, or steal our children’s Christmas toys for just one more! Of course we would not today!

This type of thinking does not mean we don’t have to hold ourselves accountable for those past mistakes, it just means we need to give ourselves a break on the emotional level and stop drowning ourselves in self-pity for what rotten so-and-so we were. This type of self-abuse will not heal the situation nor move us closer to HP and recovery but keep us and other stuck in the pain of the past.

Just for tonight, I will grant myself some anonymity and free myself to become the person God wants me to be, clean and kind and courageous.

Mar 23, 2016 ko

quote: “For years, we avoided seeing ourselves as we are.”
body:
One member shared that he walked into his first N A meeting in blue hospital pajamas, skinny as a rail from years or heroin use, chipped teeth, matted hair, yellow skin, thinking; “These women must be checkin me out” Our world become so crazy that we had to build a world around us that we could survive in! We had stopped looking the mirror for real as we did not want to see who was looking back for all the stuff we had screwed up in our lives and the lives of those we once loved and for many of us lost to our addiction.

After years of masking our feelings, we begin the program scattered and hurting. Our beginnings are full of anxiety, fear and apprehension. We may search for a reason to continue staying clean at all. If we stay clean, we began hearing stories we can relate to. Seeing members looking healthier than we’ve have felt in many years, we start to believe in the hope we are starting to experience in the rooms of N A.

We learn to get out of ourselves by helping the next newcomer who walks in the door. We show him where the pamphlets are, we suggest that he take a seat next to us and offer them a cup of coffee. As we stay clean a day at a time, we begin to have small successes in our lives that give us just a bit of self-esteem and self-worth. As one member shared, I came here with no self-esteem. I worked a few steps and stayed clean then I developed “low self-esteem” …. Smile.
moment:
Just for tonight: I am grateful for the self-esteem and self-worth I am developing through working the steps and participating in my recovery today.

Mar 23, 2016 ko

quote: “Obviously our way did not work. In utter desperation, we looked for another way. In Narcotics Anonymous, we are told that we can turn our will and our lives over to a care of a God of our own understanding.”
body:
A sponsor shared with his sponsee; “It’s your thinking that’s messed up” The sponsee asked his sponsor; “how much of my thinking?” The sponsor replied… “We usually start with all of it! If there’s any good, we will let you know!”

The one thing most of us grasp fairly easily when we truly surrender in our inner most selves that addiction has kicked our ass is that the only place our thinking got us was jails, institutions and a whole lot of trouble with any and all that loved us. For once in our lives we saw our big egos for what they truly where a bunch of hyped up phony lies to keep us from the truth of what a mess we had made of our lives and the lives of those we held dearest.

It is from this place of what appears to be weakness, comes our true strength, humility. We have learned that if we are not humble we are often humiliated. In recovery we choose to accept ourselves with all our flaws. Once we do so, they have no power over us anymore. We do this by working the steps so we can be freed from our self-made prisons and learn, over time, a simple way to live that works in harmony with God and others. We replace or old thinking with living by spiritual principles.
moment:
Just for tonight; I will replace the code of the street with the 12 steps so my life can be one of love and not hate.

Mar 23, 2016 ko

quote: “Some of us sought spiritual guidance through churches, different religions, meditation and cultism. Some of us sought cure by geographical changes, blaming our surroundings and living situations for our problem. This attempt just gave us new people to take advantage of.”
body:
What we failed to realize is that wherever we are, we bring the problem with us, ourselves. As the cartoon character Pogo Possum so aptly said; “We have met the enemy and they is us!”. We blamed people, places and situations for all our problems. We barely, if it all, took responsibility for most of the bad situations in our lives. If that cop had not been there, if she had only said yes, if they had not walked in on us when we had that needle in our arm, we would not have lost that job. The world is out to get us, we often moaned and groaned.

Solutions to our problems cannot come from outside sources, they must come from within. The Steps are the answer to our problems if we can find the courage, willingness and honesty to use the Steps to look inside, see who we are, accept ourselves and seek change. First we must come to a point of surrender. We give up trying to run the show. We let go and let recovery work in our lives as it has for so many before us.

Finding and developing a relationship with a Higher Power gives us the spiritual power to accomplish great changes we would otherwise be incapable of accomplishing on our own. We start to gain self-acceptance and self-love by realizing we are no longer the despicable depraved people we used to be. That we are becoming capable of giving hope and love to those coming in after us and most importantly to ourselves at long last.
moment:
Just for tonight; I will surrender to the truth that it was not the world that was after me. I was the maker of most all of my problems and with the help of others, I can be part of the solution today.

Mar 23, 2016 ko

quote: “There is a spirit or an energy that can be felt in the meetings, and it is sometimes the newcomer’s first perception of God.”
body:
Recalling our feelings and impressions from our initial contact with N A helps us keep our minds open to new ideas and target what we really want and expect from recovery. That little lift in the chest and sitting up straight expresses the openness and attention to learn more about the N A 12 Steps and Traditions. We may not even have words for it yet, but it is this desire for self- betterment that drives all our recovery.

One member who had just come in from his 3rd relapse in 2 years of trying to get clean, got to hear a story of a member who had tried to get clean 40 times! After the member shared their story at the treatment center H and I meeting the damnedest thing happened. They asked the speaker to come to the front of the room and they gave him his 1 year medallion! The newcomer thought to himself, if that crazy son of a bitch can stay clean a whole year so can I.

As one member shared; “N A gave me hope.” This was probably something that was sorely lacking in our lives for a long, long time. The only hope we had was that we hoped the dope was good this time. We hoped the dealer would get back soon so we could get the sick off. We hoped we wouldn’t get ripped off this time. But hope for freedom, for a better life, for an answer to why we kept sinking lower and lower with no hope of a way out. For many of us that was how we felt at the beginning, hopeless.
moment:
Just for tonight; I am grateful for the hope I have today and if I don’t have much hope today I will call my sponsor and get to a meeting to get some.

Mar 23, 2016 ko

quote: “We convinced ourselves that we could make it alone and proceeded to live life on that basis. The results were disastrous, and, in the end, each of us had to admit that our self-sufficiency was a lie.”
body:
A sponsor told his sponsee; “I will tell you the one thing, that if it doesn’t change, will keep you from getting everything God has intended for his children.. and that is… when you know, you know, you’ll never know! But when you begin to understand that you don’t understand, then you will have understanding. When you stop trying to understand, then you will enjoy life.” The sponsee said back “Hell, I don’t understand a word your saying!” The sponsor responded “and that’s it and don’t you ever forgot it!” The sponsee looked at him dumb-founded … “hell you’re crazy!” The sponsor replied “I know!”

The biggest problem most of us had when we got here was being know-it-alls. We were so smart we just knew we had a better grasp on life than just about anyone. For most of us at some very early age we were abused or abandoned or betrayed by our care-givers. We chose to do life all by ourselves, thank you very much. We lost faith in the world and knew in our heart- of-hearts that if we trusted others, eventually someone would screw us over.

Or we thought that we would never let anyone use us as the brunt of a joke so we became very wise in the ways of the world. We most certainly would not be made a fool of by others. We got real smart and in our people-pleasing obsession we constantly worked at out-smarting others first. The problem was that this became a full-time 24/7 job. It was exhausting. It may have brought us some notoriety. Some folks actually looked up to us but we traded off this small bit of ego boost by a life of always being on guard so we could be whoever you needed us to be to like us or at least tolerate our often bizarre behavior.
moment: Just for tonight; I will find joy in not knowing it all so I can be opened minded enough to learn new things and grow. [ko]

quote: “Another desirable quality is love. We love the ambitious for they can inspire us; we love the failures for they can teach us; we love the kings for they are but human; we love the meek for they are divine. We love the poor for they are so many. We love the rich for they are lonely. We love the young for the faith they hold; we love the old for the wisdom they share. We love the beautiful for their eyes of sadness’ we love the ugly for their souls of peace.”
body: Love feels good whether we are giving love or receiving love. Learning to look for love changes the way we see, hear and feel life.

quote: “Some of us had no idea who we were, or who we wanted to be. We only knew we didn’t want to be ourselves.”
body:
For many of us, we did not surrender to recovery at first, we surrender to the truth that we could not live one more day in the hell of our active addiction! We did not know what this recovery stuff was all about but we had a glimmer of hope that it had to be better than the self-destructive life we had been living for so long.

Once we can surrender our old ways, it is a lot easier to live in the solution of recovery. We are firm believers in “being done”. We were sick and tired of being sick and tired as the literature says. For some it is not until we put it all down on paper in a thorough 1st step, that reality rings true as a bell. One member shared, I just could not use again once I had written about the last 15 years of failed drug usage and the devastation this had been to my wife, my son and those that I loved. If I knew anything for certain it was that I could not use successfully.
moment:
Just for tonight: If I have any reservations about being able to use safely I will share them in a meeting or with my sponsor and Higher-Power. I may even choose to write another 1st step so I don’t do something that I may regret for the rest of my life.

Mar 27, 2016 ko

quote: “We were sick and tired of pain and trouble. We were frightened and ran from the fear, but no matter how far we ran, the fear followed us. We were hopeless, useless and lost. Feelings of worthlessness overcame us.”
body:
Nobody knows the trouble I’ve been. That was the song many of us would keep singing to our dying day if we had not found recovery in Narcotics Anonymous. Our self-esteem was in the toilet for many of us. There were no longer any “good” days, just tolerable ones. We had so much remorse, guilt and shame from the destruction we caused in our active addiction. Was there any help for addicts as bad as us?

As one sponsor told his sponsee: “If you always think what you always thought, then you will always feel what you always felt and if you always feel what you always felt, you will always do what you’ve always done and if you always do what you’ve always done, you will always get what you always got!”

We can get lost in our self-absorbed negative thoughts and never move forward in our lives. We get stuck in feeling sorry for ourselves or wallowing in how bad we are and never have a desire to move. We often find others who will feel sorry for us and this only feeds our desire to be sad or down. We get a pay-off for living in this negative self-destructive state.

For many of us we need to surrender to KISS, keep it simple, smile! If we write down 24 things we have to be grateful for, we can often move into a more positive mind frame and from there we can get back to our willingness to live in the solution today.
moment:
Just for tonight: I will remember that gratitude and self-pity are mutually exclusive. I know that I cannot be grateful and feel sorry for myself. I will write 24 things I have to be grateful for and share them with my sponsor.

March 27, 2016 ko

quote: “Progression of recovery is a continuous journey uphill with loving effort. Without love or effort, we start the downhill run. The progression of the disease is an ongoing process, even during abstinence, no matter how long.”
body:
We have learned, over time, that the road gets a lot less winding but it becomes a lot narrower! When we first come to recovery our feelings and mode go through huge swings, like a road that goes up and down steep hills and winds to and fro. As we work steps and get some time away from our last high, life becomes more peaceful. The road unwinds and the ups and downs aren’t so drastic. We are recovering!

We come to understand that the peace we have found is from doing the right things for the right reasons as we begin to live a life of integrity. We are consistent in our actions and we have chosen to take 100% responsibility for lives. This takes discipline which, for most of us, is something we develop over time as we work the steps.

As one member shared; “I go to sleep at peace each night because I have taken care of all of my responsibilities that I could that day. I have returned the phone calls, shipped the packages, paid the bills or spoken with the creditors. I have spent time with my spouse, hugged my children etc etc etc. I would not feel comfortable going to bed any other way today. What a miracle from the person I was when I got here, wow!”
moment:
Just for tonight; I will stay on the path of recovery as I know that my life and peace of mind depend on it.

[March 27th, 2016 ko]

quote: “We had to feel better internally before we could even bear to think about whom we had harmed and how we had harmed them, and exactly what was the way we perceived ourselves.”
body:
Newcomers want to do three steps, 1, 9 and 12… I surrender, I’m sorry, I’ll help you! Not! Step 9 is at the end of the steps for a very sound reason. It takes a great deal of healing and insight to get to a place where we can make living amends and be consistent with them. It is one thing to say I’m sorry for not being there for you Dad and quite another to commit to be there for our parents for the rest of their days.

For many of us, we have not even a clue as to the exact nature of our wrongs. For many we just think stealing bad… but if we do a searching inventory we may come to the deeper truth that not only did we steal from our loved ones but we betrayed them and their trust over and over again. We took advantage of their kindnesses and saw them as weakness to be exploited. Or at worse, we did this with little or no remorse as the exact nature may have been our total self- centeredness and greed.

With so many levels of wrong to get to, it probably would work best for us first to find out how we deceived and cheated ourselves by continuing to use drugs when everything in our being and those around us kept telling us we were slowly committing suicide. It would probably serve us better in early recovery to find out why we hated ourselves so much that we would continue poisoning ourselves just to avoid feelings of guilt and shame.

There is a lot of love and sensitivity built into our N A Twelve Steps. They come in a given order that helps insure we are ready for the Step we are working at each step forward. It is very supportive and caring that along with each Step we can see how others have gone through the same things. So, as we progress, we are also woven into a Fellowship of men and women just like us, all on the road to a new life clean.
moment:
Just for tonight; I will trust my sponsor and the fellowship and work the steps in order knowing there is wise and sound reasons to do just that. I will trust that those who have come before me have learned the easiest softest way.

Mar 27, 2016 ko

quote: “The Twelve Steps to recovery, that N.A. outlines for us, hold the answer for all of our ego-trips and insecurities. They seem to hold the only answers for us that we can really use. In living these Steps, we first begin to let go of old egotism. We then open up to a Higher Power, so that we will lose all fear of facing ourselves and of facing other people.”
body:
For most of us, we have lived a life of people pleasing, at best and manipulating others at worst. We conned our fellows into thinking we really cared when inside we were merely plotting ways to get over or use them for funds for our next round. Maybe we just wanted folks to like us so we could get away with bloody murder and be forgiven for our transgressions as we continued to screw over our closest friends and family one more time.

We may have been or felt abandoned at a very early age when our caretakers were lost in their addiction or had turned on us by sexually abusing us or worse. We learned to make our caretakers happy at all cost as it would have meant we would be abandoned and die. This type of fear has run with us all our lives so we put up with people who abused us over and over and over again as we dreaded the thought of being abandoned one more time.

In recovery we find one good person to trust, a sponsor. With this small leap of faith, we begin to get honest with another human being, possible for the first time in decades. We share our innermost thoughts with our sponsor and we take direction from someone who has been where we were and has made it through ok. By opening up to this small bit of faith, which for many maybe a huge leap of faith, we can build an entire recovery on this simple step of surrender.

Just for tonight: As a sponsor said to his sponsee “If there isn’t a power greater then you, we are all in a world of trouble!”.
moment:
Just For Tonight; I will trust in just one person besides myself, I will trust my sponsor for they are fairly trustworthy folks.

Mar 27, 2016 ko

quote: “When we came to the Fellowship of Narcotics Anonymous, we faced many disturbing realizations.”
body:
We realized we had run from our feelings, we had lost control of our lives and become victims of the disease of addiction. The compulsion and obsession that had ruled our lives had forced us to take many actions that were against our will. We have lied, stolen, cheated, sold ourselves and many other vile things all in the name of getting one more. Now faced with the total unmanageability of our lives, we were filled with guilt, remorse, shame and sorrow. We couldn’t see a way out. Going to meetings started to give us hope. We heard others share about how their lives got better. We started to learn there was a better way.

Learning is an ongoing process in recovery. Rethinking and sharing with others one on one or in meetings, we wake up to where we have been and what we have been doing! Living a day at a time frees us from repeating things we no longer approve of or care for. Each day is a blank slate. Hope and the freedom that comes from surrender and belief enables us to take a good look and review our lives as casually as we comb our hair. We can substitute actions and viewpoints until we are satisfied. Even then, when they stand for inspection, we are free to make other improvements. Our progress encourages us to continue the process.
moment:
Just for tonight: Instead of feeling lost when something negative comes to mind, we just think, “I’ll share about this at the meetings tonight. Surely I am not the only one who has this problem.” No longer are we trapped and out of touch. With supportive people we can move forward as our lives are not stuck in concrete shoes!

March 27, 2016 ko

quote: “We no longer blame God and others for our own problems, and see that our problems have been of our own making.”
body:
As our notions about God and spirituality change in the light of a new understanding, most of our members see where God is in control but we are responsible for making our own choices. God takes care of the cosmos, the weather, politics and getting us out of our worse messes, when we ask for help. We are in charge of turning our life and will over to the care of a loving God who we have found in our 2nd Step! We also generally have fairly good sense and aren’t afraid of admitting fault whenever we can, allowing that we find that relieves most of the tension and stress in our daily lives.

As we grow in recovery, we grow in internal resources, the tools and abilities to handle most day to day concerns and a few real upsets. Clean, real upsets don’t occur that often but when they do, we are surprised how well honesty, openmindedness and the willingness to try eases the tension and gives us the ability to puzzle out what is going on and what needs to be done. If we remember to pray for help from our Higher Power, it can be like someone remembered to turn on a light!

The insanity mentioned in our 2nd Step is the root of most mishaps and once that is admitted, life is different. We relinquish fear and the need to find fault and lay blame on someone else’s doorstep.

(bs)

quote: “We must carefully choose the person who is to hear our Fifth Step.”
body:
Of course it is most practical to share our 5th step with our sponsors as hopefully they both know us best and will be able to help us see the true nature of our defects in the upcoming steps once he or she hears our life story. We suggest that the person listening to our step know a good bit about us in advance whenever possible so they can listen for what we are not saying, as much as hearing clearly what we are saying.

Like all things spiritual, our Higher Power gives us lots of helpful guidance in this area. We pray for our HP to guide us to the right person! We want someone who cares about us and yet is able to be honest with us. We find someone we trust will keep our Fourth Step secrets confidential. Also, sharing with God, ourselves and another human being does not mean we give that person the right to judge us one way or the other. It helps to have someone listen to our step who has worked all 12 of the steps themselves. The process of working the steps helps remove such defects as judgmentalness, ego, fear, jealousy etc.

We don’t need that when opening our heart and our souls in a thorough Fifth Step. We needed someone who is non-judgmental, who will listen without passing pronouncements on our inappropriate behaviours with friends and loved ones. Usually another recovering addict who has worked through Step Five will certainly have empathy and compassion. They will have had their blinders opened and will have a clear idea of the kinds of denial we need to get past in this and the next few steps. They can share with us, gently, the areas we may need to expand our answers on or get honest about.
moment: Just for tonight: I will trust the process and if I don’t trust the person who is to hear my Fifth Step, I will take the time to choose someone I do so I can open up and free myself from my past once and for all. March 31st ko

quote: “In Step Ten we strive for genuine humility. In this humility we can better interact with others.”
body:
A sponsor told his sponsee; “There are two truths that you need to surrender to… 1st, there is a God and 2nd you’re not it!” When we make our Higher Power the center of our lives, we tend to live more in humility than not. We are filled with a deep sense of trust that the universe and our lives are playing out just the way they are supposed to at any given moment in time. We have no desire or need to fix things, make things go “our way”, heal others, right some great wrongs. We have no white horse to ride off in the sunset today. We can be filled with gratitude and that is the best message we will ever be able to carry to the still suffering addict.

Humility let’s us show our heart and inner being to other human beings. This open-heart calls forth the openness in others. It minimizes or eliminates the fear of being subjugated by others or the need to manipulate and control others. It let’s us just ‘be’ ourselves. It lets us drop the games and be real. When we are open to others, they can relax and be open with us.
moment:
Just For Tonight: As the Basic Text reminds us: “Humility is as much a part of staying clean as food and water is to staying alive.” We can take a moment and think where our lives would be if we had no food and water. This will give us some idea of just how vital living in humility is for a successful recovering life.

Apr 8th, 2016 ko

quote: “There will be times, however, when we feel like getting off. We want to run, we feel lousy, we need to be reminded of where we came from and that it will be worse this time. This is when we need to Fellowship most.”
body:
As the Text says; “Honest sharing is the antidote to our diseased thinking” We have learned that it is wise to “rat” on our disease. We tell others what we are thinking and the desire to use diminishes. This is one of the wonderful concepts of recovery. Just by seeking help from others by honestly sharing, our disease no longer has power over us just for today.

If we use during times of stress and hardship or even to celebrate some life event, we don’t learn anything and we often have a ton more problems when we come down. Distorted reality is not a good guide to living! If we reach out, follow direction and not use, just for today, we gain tools that we can use for the rest of our lives. We can ask ourselves, which do I want, more pain, misery, guilt and shame or a killer new recovery tool that will work over and over again in many problem areas of my life? If we can get honest, it’s not a hard choice.
moment:
Just for tonight; If I have a desire to act out in unhealthy ways, I will call my sponsor, share it with a recovery friend or take it to a meeting and leave it there. I know I will feel much better if I do that simple thing.

Apr 8th, 2016 k0

quote: “We hate to be wrong. It is hard to believe that now in our recovery somehow self-will leads us to make decisions based on manipulation, ego, lust or false pride but it happens . . . often.”
body:
We never outgrow our need for personal inventory. One member shared, “Hell, I was willing to go to any lengths just to be right! Well, old Bob killed himself, yes but he was right.” sigh. Sounds pretty stupid eh? But the sad part is most of us lived our lives with such thoughts and actions. For many of us, we were shamed a great deal in early adolescence. We told ourselves, once we found out how to be cool, that we would never let anyone get us like that ever again grrrr!

As the literature so aptly states: “We no longer need to stand up for non-existent virtues”. When we came to recovery our insides were so raw from years of unhealthy living that we had to put up a huge front so at least “we” wouldn’t feel the pain of being less-than everyone else. We had often destroyed all our relationships and pushed most all our loved ones out of our lives. Reality was not looking very pretty to us so we built a world in which we could feel comfortable in. We where Billy Bad-Ass, Mr. Handsome or the Princess. For some, we were just happy, as one member said it, to be “the man” who sits next to “The Man!” don’t you know.

By the time we get to recovery, for most of us, we are just too tired to play anymore. We become willing to admit that we don’t know it all and in fact we no longer have a desire to anymore. We are content with learning a new way of life that does not rely on what others think of us. We are set free. Many of us go looking for a new set of chains straight away! Humility, patience and tolerance are still important tools once we get on our feet!
moment:
Just for tonight; In the words of a spiritual leader of our times “free at last, free at last, thank God almighty we are free at last!”

Apr 8th, 2016 ko

quote: “The Twelve Steps led us to a point of recovery that seemed to make the world change before our eyes, for the better.”
body:
As one member shared: “I stopped using and my family got better, imagine that?” As the literature states: “Everything we know is subject to revision, especially what we know about the truth!” One of the first persons to die from AIDS in our fellowship that we all knew wrote that now familiar line. She understood that it is not life that changes but our perceptions of life.

As we work the steps, we face our resentments in our Fourth Step and probably for the first time in our lives, get honest about our part in the messes we saw and felt that have weighed us down for so long. If we have a resentment, it is coloring our perception of that situation and probably our perception on life in general. If we have the courage to get honest and own our part, we will surely feel a great sense of relief as we let go of issues and positions that have kept us stuck, often for decades. What a relief to be able to face our issues, come clean and get past them to inner peace and true freedom. For most of us, after Step Five, we feel a freedom we had only dreamed possible before working the steps.

Our Twelve Steps have purpose and function after we think we are getting better. We always have room for growth. Deeper surrender and stronger belief will always work to our betterment. Our inventory process along with prayer and meditation will take us further into the world and life of the spirit. There is no end!
moment:
Just for tonight: As the old adage goes; “The truth will set you free but first it will piss you off!” I am willing to walk through my old angers and hurts so I can be set free.

Apr 8th, 2016 ko

quote: “We are not readily angered, frightened, or maddened by greed or lust. “We remember our part in the divine partnership with God and we are more tolerant and patient with other people.”
body:
Once we find recovery and are no longer the center of our universe, we begin to understand that life is not out to get us. That no one is doing us wrong, so to speak. If we can grasp the concept that “life is hard” then we begin to understand that life takes work. As the text says “the sooner we accept our personal responsibilities, just that much faster do we become responsible, productive members of that society. If we stop coming from a place of being a victim, we quickly realize that taking responsibility in every area of our lives brings us the true freedom we always longed for but could never quite achieve.

As one member said: “I just wanted to shoot heroin and have respect.” I always wondered why I felt less than and not good enough so I chose to hang with those who lived outside of respectability and called responsible people fools and idiots! I looked down my nose at all those I inwardly admired. Hell, I couldn’t hold a job, get up on time each day, raise a family and so on. I told myself that was for squares.

Today we are perfectly ok with being a square. We find enjoyment at being a member of society, something we had dreaded just a few months or years ago. We no longer need to hide in plain site by wearing strange attire or acting like we had inside knowledge and push folks away with ugly stares and mean looks. All we really wanted was to feel a part of and not apart from others and society as a whole.
moment:
Just for tonight; I have learned that the road to recovery is paved with personal responsibility in every area of my life with the pay-off as being peace of mind and peace in my heart.

Apr 8th, 2016 ko

quote: “Freedom to change seems to come mainly after our acceptance of ourselves.”
body:
“Enforced morality lacks the power that comes from a spiritual awakening” as our literature says. For years, family members and loved ones have tried in vain to get us to grow up or to get clean or to stop the insanity once and for all. We even wanted to do these things ourselves but for some reason we lacked the power to do so. Our every attempt at getting our lives together once more was usually short-lived or a complete disaster when we picked up, often completely against our wills. We would scratch our heads in wonder as did those around us who had not completely given up on us like most sane people would after several failed attempts at helping the helpless. We were often just as baffled as they were as we did not understand the true nature of the disease of addiction and that we were caught in its grip.

When we found recovery and began to surrender to the simple suggestions the program has to offer, miracles began to happen in our lives. We began with simple successes like staying clean a day at a time. These days began to turn into months and we began to truly want recovery for ourselves and not just to people-please our spouses or significant others. One day we wake up and find that the promises of recovery have become realities in our lives. What a joy to wake one day and realize that we truly have stopped using, lost the desire to use and have found a new way of life, wow!
moment:
Just for tonight: I will trust the process and surrender my old ways as they got me nowhere. As one member said; “I threw out the code of the streets and exchanged the for the 12 steps and 12 traditions and my life got better!”

Apr 8th, 2016 ko

quote: “By recognizing the defects in our characters, and letting go of them spiritually, we were ready to have sanity restored to us.”
body:
Someone once said; “Hell is being separated from our Higher-Power and heaven is when we are connected”. For most of us, our insanity was not so much being out of our minds, it meant we thought the world revolved around us. That it all began and ended with us. We watched the whole world trying desperately in an instant to be what we thought everyone wanted us to be. Now that’s crazy.

What energy it took to be on guard 24/7 so “they” won’t get us. We knew who “they” were but hell we were ready, by God! A little old lady on a bus would sneer at us or even in our direction and we would have a bad day trying to figure out what we did wrong. As one member said: “When folks sneer at me, my first thought is “Heck, I must not know who you think you are, lol, lol, lol”; He is free from others needing to be superior. That is all that is about.

We need not buy into others ill feelings we are free to live in the here and now and don’t have to spend our days wishing we were someone else other than who we are. Once we become ok with ourselves, there are no more buttons for others to push for it is our guilt and shame that allow others to twist us to their liking's or needs.
moment:
Just for tonight; As an older member said; “you can’t please all of the people all of the time and if you are, you are probably doing something wrong!”

Apr 8th, 2016 ko

quote: “After we accept that we created our own hell and that there is a God that wants to help us, we begin to make progress in solving our problems.”
body:
An older member shared her favorite prayer “whatever is going on in my life, God has it already taken care of.” There is a Japanese Haiku which means a short meaningful poem which says “in the beginner’s mind there are many questions, in the Masters but few.” In other words; if God is the answer, what’s the question.

As we begin to rely on the God of our understanding to meet our needs each day, we begin to find true peace in our lives. When we hit a brick wall in our lives we don’t have to keep banging away at it trying in vain to move it as we are just sure the answer is right on the other side. In recovery we can surrender, knowing the God has a much better and easier solution to each situation. If we but let go of our notions of how things “ought” to be. We begin to see that God’s will most often is much better than the short sighted, short-lived, self-centered vision we are stuck on.
moment:
Just for tonight: As the text states in step 11; “God’s will for us becomes our own true will for ourselves. This happens in an intuitive manner that cannot be adequately explained in words”

Apr 8th, 2016 ko

quote: “Personality change was what we really needed. Change from a self-destructing to a self-affirming pattern of living was imperative.”
body:
When we were using, a change of any kind of personal nature was the farthest thing from our minds. We were getting and using every day, the only thing we wanted to change was the pain of living, that in many cases, we were unable to clearly see.

It wasn’t until we were able to get clean, when it was said to us, that in order to stay clean, all we had to do was pray, go to meetings and not pick up, and change everything in our lives. This was almost too much to absorb at once. After we saw other addicts working the 12 Steps and living the program, we began to see things differently. As our patterns of thought and action began to reflect the values and behaviour of people in the meetings, we could see we too were changing. And you know, it wasn’t all that bad. The more accustomed we became, the Program began to look better and better.

The reason we can adapt so quickly to the clean life is we have spent years in confusion and desperation. We experience something called the ‘pink cloud’ where the new freedom from our chaotic life style and the familiar routines support a good feeling inside. Learning to ‘live the Program’ gives us an edge that can’t be beat. Sometimes we will find ourselves falling away from the new way of life and it may take some honest sharing to ‘self-correct.’

(bs)

quote: “We place our recovery first and our own petty desires and egos last.”
body:
Watching people achieve recovery while those who fought the principles and ideals of N A, we realized the addicts we could identify with were in serious trouble. Inevitably, some of our best friends in the N A program relapsed and died. That’s when it all came into focus for us. What we thought was just having fun and cutting up, was seen in it real colors! “Hey, this addiction stuff can kill me!”

At this point we are beginning to see the seriousness of N A! While people come into the program at different levels, care is taken to word our recovery material so that members can understand and benefit regardless of clean time and where they are in working their 12 Steps.

This way, a person who is working the program can benefit from the writing. Personal preference is set to the side and writing groups read and discuss the material to insure it is going to help any addict seeking recovery. In recovery, we filter out ego based thinking and expression for the good of all. We include what each one of us has to say rather than exclude.

We begin to go to meetings daily as we start seeing that it puts our recovery into Turbo Mode. We start seeing even the smallest change in the meetings. We see people lightening us on their program and then we see tham go out the door. We see other get more involved and blossom.

We see changes in the N A fellowship start and then spread like wildfire. We hear something for the very first time. Like a person ends their share with “And I am Frank and I am an addict.”

Then we go across town the next day and hear the same thing, for the second time. Then we go to a different county and hear it there for the third time. We realize that there is some synergism in NA that cannot be explained. And this can work for the good or bad of N A. (JF/bs)

quote: “If we keep what we have to share, we lose the meaning.”
body:
When members share in N A recovery meetings a strange thing happens. We recall what we have heard, read or shared in other meetings. This means the meeting, taken as a whole, is like a soup with many flavors! Sometimes our minds will seem to drift off but our ears take in everything. When we stop sharing our hearts and beliefs, the meetings stop working. When we are open to one another, we grow from what we find we have in common.

I was taught that I could say anything I wanted to in a meetings. The idea was that we talk it out instead of using over it. We had a crosstalk rule to insure our moment in the sun of getting it out. That way, we did not have to worry about saying it right, it could be garbled and see still had the safety to say it and thereby not have to use over it. We could complain about the neighbor being a jerk. We could talk about our frustration with the area. We could curse God for not hearing us. There was something very profound about this vocal freedom. It made us somebody in the fellowship. We had rights. We had respect. We had a place at the table.

When crosstalk creeps in, it makes the meeting unsafe to share in. When someone cuts in while we are sharing, we feel belittled and unappreciated. Ours is a strange disease. When people can’t share at meetings, they may fall into relapse instead. That’s why the rule is there in the first place. No crosstalk. Our predecessors hard won experience revealed this. Cross talk gives some members a chance to play doctor. If what they have to share is that good, perhaps they can say it to the member after the meeting. Some of these problems may carry over from treatment or therapy.

(jf/bs)

quote: “Words mean nothing until we put them into action.”.
body:
Well, maybe words mean something but the quote means that talk is cheap if not acted on. Many of us addicts dodge action because we are afraid it will look silly or subject us to some unkind attention. When we let our Higher Power guide us, we are free to put things that stand out to us into action. Sometimes they work, sometimes not. But the effort is instructive. Fear is a habit we don’t let run our lives now that we have new resources to help us.

There was a famous book “I’ll quit tomorrow”. That says it all. Words mean nothing till we put them into action. In this case, action could be just going to a meeting.

One can never go wrong by going to a meeting. Part of this is because when we need a meeting more than anything else in the world, our brain tells us is that we have no time for meeting attendance, we need to get the laundry done or go look for a job. By knowing that going to a meeting is never a mistake, it gives us the green light to stop, go to a meeting and get centered. We gotta do this. Meeting are our lifeblood, our food and water.

When addicts call us, we must take the call and we must let them end the conversation when they are good and damn ready. They could be contemplating suicide and need us for that small window of human contact. God has given us cell phones, we need to keep them on and pickup. This is NA service work in this most direct form.

Meetings have to hold to a certain general criteria. Lots of times members who come in through treatment will find themselves slipping into what they do in the hospital. This is NA, we have our own ways of dealing with the things that come up in recovery.

(jf/bs)

quote: “Facing problems is a necessary ability to stay clean”
body:
Cash register honesty may be important to recovery but so many times we let fear keep us from asking for help. We call other members, that is why they gave us their number in the first place. We call our sponsor and tell them what’s bothering us. We take it to a meeting and share honestly so the members in the room can get with us after the meeting. Direct responses are sometimes permitted but we don’t want our meetings to turn into group therapy sessions. We also don’t trap newer members into trying the ‘help’ someone with their great ideas. We share experience. We share our strength. And we share our hope.

Once while racing down a mountain road in an RV with no brakes, I yelled to my friend who was driving, “We got a problem”. He responded to me, “I don’t see these things as problems, I see these as experiences.”

The next time I had a flat time, I told myself, “This is not a problem, it is an experience.” It was the experience of having a flat tire on the side of the road.”

Someone stopped and helped me and it all turned out good. I learned form this. God did not take me this far to dump me. Things were going to turn out alright. I was favored in God’s eyes as I was trying to deliver a clear message of recovery to his other suffering children. I was was very, very sincere about this. I would crawl over broken glass to tell a using addict “You never have to use again.”

(jf/bs)

quote: “One way to develop our conscious contact with God is to make up a “gratitude list,” count our blessings and thank our Higher Power for them.”
body:
This kind of applied spirituality may be new to us. Our discomfort and disease has blotted out our view of reality, selecting only for the negative and unpleasant. That’s why our newfound hope is so surprising. Active addiction removed our hope, bit by bit until there was nothing left.

Gratitude is being consciously thankful for what we have already achieved or secured. It feels good when we are grateful. Many addicts are so disconnected from their feelings, they have to sit up straight and pay attention to be grateful. After a while, it becomes a graceful state of mind for many of our members.

We never forget or minimize our goal of conscious contact with our Higher Power. Whatever spiritual path you follow, all our members are free to follow their own spirituality. Faith and active belief is the best way to keep our recovery moving in the right direction.

(bs)

quote: “We have only to believe what we see with our own eyes in the transformed lives of other N.A. members. That’s all it takes -- an open mind.”
body:
Seeing is believing! All our interaction with other clean addicts reinforces our knowledge and understanding of N A and our processes. Over time, surrender belief and doing away with the habits and peculiar living routines that had move to do with surviving than living make more and more sense to us. N A is a living program based on successful experience with recovery from addiction and extension into a new world of wonder and freedom.

The self-destructive nature of our illness makes it hard to accept good things into our lives. We have to force ourselves to be happy. What? Is this a surprise? Don’t you know addiction is a disease of the shadows. It hides in many places. Our fears, our negative projections, our inability to step up to life and demand our share of happiness and goodwill. Getting over the duck and dodge of active addiction is what makes recovery fun!

If addiction runs in families, that means people who are unhappy and see no way out look normal to us. Many times newer members wonder if our happy members were ever lonely, fear-based and ever stuck on stupid? It takes remembering our pain to push us into honesty, open-mindedness and willingness.

quote: “If we check to make sure that our actions are clearly within the bounds of our Traditions, if we don’t represent anyone but ourselves, if we don’t dictate to other groups or force anything upon them, and if we take the time to consider the consequences of our actions ahead of time, then all will be well.”
body:
These three questions make it easy to double check group decisions. Abiding by our 12 Traditions is not an optional thing! We follow 12 Traditions in NA to protect our members and our NA existence. We, as groups of NA members, can find a way to deal with the issues that come up, often when there are changes in who is attending like, whether they are from treatment or members coming in off the street, for instance. Until a member has been clean and active in a group for a while, it may be hard to see the connection between following our Traditions and a trip to the cemetery.

Not one of us is perfect and the ability to make amends frees us to step out on faith and try something new or different. Still, anything new or untried. may or may not work out. In our daily lives, we make changes all the time. Before they put the curves at the end of aircraft wings, an airplane was said to be ‘off course’ over 90% of the time due to wind and turbulence! Self-correction is a real bonus that comes with our new freedom from the insanity of active addiction. Our lives find benefits from the principles of our 12 Steps and Traditions because principles are spiritual and universal. It takes time for all this to become a working part of our lives. When problems come up in our daily lives, we can think about our Program and sometimes find things we can do to help correct some situation that has gone wrong. There is no way to get ‘recovery’ without making mistakes and learning to apply the new tools in our lives.

quote: “The 12 Step of our personal program also says that we should carry the message to the addict who still suffers. This is no coincidence.”
body:
As we progress to the benefits of recovery, we recall how our surrender make such a big difference In our early recovery. Through helping others, we become a “human plus sign.’ We offer a life giving solution to our situation of pain and endless suffering. Helping others gives us a mental, spiritual and even physical lift right away. We can see ourselves in others. We realize that those who help us do not feel grandiose or .better than us. It is actually the other way around. We are comforted because some of the words we use helping others will apply to us as well.

We call carry the N A message when we meeting someone knew. Being conscious of this, many of us pray on the way or walking into the meeting. The first Step of N A triggers a mini spiritual awakening! In a short time we experience a different level of consciousness whenever we are in a meeting. We learn to be relaxed, active listeners who bring to bear everything they have read, seen or heard concerning N A. So, you have fifteen members in a meeting. Except for a few members who are new, group members have attended hundreds and sometimes thousands of meetings. You get used to caring and by extension, what you want for them is also what we want for ourselves, so this puts in a good position to follow our own advice!

quote: “All Our groups can do is plant the seed for recovery and bring addicts together so that the magic of empathy, honesty, caring, sharing and service can do its thing.”
body:
This quote reminds us that our recovery is a God-given blessing far beyond what the mind of mankind can do. Recovery is a miraculous event. Those who work their Steps like their lives depend on it, know what is meant by these words. The disease of addiction affects three levels: the physical, mental and spiritual. The physical is easy, we can feel it. We can feel the mental also but the idea that we are in control can become a serious roadblock. Spiritual knowledge is what grants us real recovery. Many, many members who work their program, go to meetings, read the literature and follow the proven N A Program in all ways but one miss out on the biggest blessing of all: relinquishment, freedom from our human frailty through a supernatural agency called God or Higher Power.

While our sensitivity to the assumptions and pain inflicted by people who think they have a patent on spirituality prohibits our openness to what others think, through our own experience, we know the Higher Power is real and loving. We share our light with others the way we can share light by lighting the candle of an addict seeking recovery in N A.

While the situations that go with empathy, honest, caring, sharing and service can be described, it is the human feeling experiences by an individual that cannot be put into words. The changes in someone bound by fear and negative expectation is indeed magical. It produces changes inside by appealing to something unseen and without physical description.

quote: “In our past, we were usually irresponsible. After we face ourselves in the inventory Steps, and make amends to others in our amends steps, we can no longer allow other people the “pay our way” for us. We want to serve.”
body:
It is important to remind ourselves that we are making progress. We have been down so long we have to re-conditions ourselves to simple relief from pain or the joy of giving. We approach give and take relationships from a different place inside. Carrying and sharing makes us feel apart of and like we are doing our part to deserve the good things in life. Lack of stress and tension paints a new reality we couldn’t reach before.

Working the Steps of N A recovery removes the clouds that limited our vision and restricted our movements! Freedom from active addiction is more than a state of mind, it is a physical, mental and spiritual reality. Sometimes when you’ve been walking in mud, you find there is still a little more to scrape off. On the road to freedom, we remember where we came from and show our gratitude for a new life clean by continuing to work our program to find out what lies ahead!

quote: “We know that in doing God’s will, our lives will be fulfilled.”
body:
It is one of the quietest yet strongest truths of N A what the Program takes us from insanity and futility to a spiritual awakening wherein we live by spiritual principles and look out for other people. Literally, our life overflows into goodness - not just for ourselves but for all who come in contact with us. There is a real basis for our sadness and despair when we come to N A seeking help. We have had plenty of failure and confusion. Gradually, as we work our Steps, go to meetings and hear others share, we are re-built into what we were meant to be.

It is our dreams, yours and mine, that come true for us. The new understanding of a loving God, freedom from defects and amends with those we have harmed that come from the change inside each one of us. When you take a car to the garage to be repaired, the outside looks the same, but now the car runs of runs better! We are the same. The changes inside our bodies and our minds change the way our parts work and our feelings reflect a new element called ‘HOPE.’

With time, our hope may need to be shined up a bit by practicing feeling of gratitude for the many blessings we enjoy. Selfishness and ingratitude help us not at all!

quote: “For us in Narcotics Anonymous autonomy is also creative freedom. It gives our groups the freedom to act on their own to establish their atmosphere of recovery, to serve their members, and to fulfill their primary purpose. It is this aspect of autonomy that makes it one of our most precious principles.”
body:
This does not give a group license to violate the principles of the 12 Steps or Traditions. Among the important items of spiritual growth is the ability to sort out common sense. Almost all members visit other areas and travel to distant conventions or gatherings to see if members elsewhere work their Steps and practice the principles of N A the same way their members back home practice them. We call this visiting the ‘greater N A.

Like any freedom, we have boundaries that prevent breaking out in self-will by not considering the effects of our group changes may have on other groups and members. It can really get sticky when conflicting viewpoints resist thoughtful concern and discussion. Sometimes, it is best to just wait until folks settle down. One solution might be to just let the group supporting what may turn out to be ill considered do their thing. Deciding later if it works or now may teach something good to know of something to avoid. We all have time to correct our errors. We call this ‘clean time.’

Freedom always demands responsibility. Being short on freedom, we may have a lot to learn.

quote: “Not preaching or judging but sharing the experience strength, and hope that comes to anyone who accepts our way of life.”
body: In recovery, a new light shines in our eyes. We begin to see the phonies all around us and this helps us in our struggle to get real. It also gives some help with compassion since we can see ourselves reflected in their misery. We have heard for a long time that the secrets are inside. Through N A we find a way to reach for what we lack and plug into a new life. We can’t let mere comfort and peace of mind make us lazy and block our way forward. Experience is the past, strength is the present and hope is the future.

quote: “Today we have feelings of love, joy, hope, excitement, sadness and friendship. Before we were either elated or depressed with very little in between.”
body:
In recovery we find much of the color and beauty in life is found in the details we used to blow off as unimportant. Going more slowly and thinking things through gives is a chance to see what we have been missing. What breakthrough lies just ahead of us today if we take time to catch it on the fly?

Polynesians have over twenty words for love. They have time to think about it. The more clear our minds and the better our memory, we can find words to address fine variations of meanings to express our feelings. As we can hear and see others better, we can track our own internal dialogues. Recovery allows clarity and the absence of fear helps us understand what’s going on around us.

quote: “Rigorous honesty is the most important tool we have in learning to deal with the past and to live today.”
body:
Like sharpening a knife on a wet-stone, we ‘hone’ our daily habit patterns to re-route our lives into a form that we can live with without having to make amends later on. Honesty appears to have the same root word. The idea of honesty keeps us safe from buying into lies, our own or someone else’s. Even our memories lie because if we were loaded when it happened, how can we trust our shame or guilt based on things that happened when we were out of our minds?

We develop our internal resources as we grow forward in recovery. We learn from other clean addicts, from our literature, from our own experience. We can see trouble coming long before it arrives, in time to change things for the better.

The reactions of people around us when they realize we are being honest come as a surprise to many of us. Generally, even when we have made serious boo-boos, people are just glad to se the situation resolved, especially if they are among those who may be blamed for our wrong-doing!

quote: “In our groups, we all need to open up first, selfishly, for our own recovery, and later candidly, for the inspiration of the newer members.”
body:
The truth has great power to overcome the phantoms of our minds. Consciousness can give us a clear picture of what is happening when we are not deluded by mind changing drugs. We don’t want our new lives to be subject to our moods when we can see quite clearly since we are clean. Going to meetings helped us by being among people like us. We are also helped when we clear away the hypnosis of daily living and remember who we are, what we are and why we go to recovery meetings.

It is embarassing to make up reasons for our insanity among other clean addicts who know our game. When we tell the truth, it strengthens our resolve to build our new lives on solid ground. Genuine concern for others gets us out of ourselves and into helping others in some way. This selflessness is good for us and helps us get in touch with our true feelings. Don’t fake it till you make it. Make it and you don’t have to fake it!

(bs)

quote: “The pain of working the program did not seem as great as the pain of addiction, so we surrendered.”
body:
We have often heard one member or another lamenting the hardships of recovery or life on life’s terms in general, but how does this compare to the desperation of active addiction?

Perhaps each member might recall the fear, paranoia and degradation experienced while using. It took a monumental effort for most of us to maintain our addiction once our disease had progressed to the latter stages. The sheer number of lies we tried to remember to keep those we loved in the dark about our addiction could wear down even the strongest of wills.

The suggestions of the program seem simple, even easy by comparison. Going to meetings instead of to cop, calling our sponsor or talking with another recovering addict instead of a dealer are all very doable.

quote: “We don’t have to be religious. All that is required is a willingness to believe.”
body:
It is shocking how easy it is to get the spiritual life, once we are willing. The Narcotics Anonymous program is based upon one of the cornerstones found in Step Twelve that speaks of sharing experience, strength and hope rather than giving advice. We do not tell other members what to do, rather we share what it has been like for us and the hope we have found on our path of recovery. So when a new member first approaches Step Two and the process of coming to believe, rather than attempt to impress our own beliefs upon them, we share ESH and allow the person the respect, dignity, and compassion to come to their own understanding of a Power greater than ourselves, and in their own time.

This is a great leap of faith for the older member as well as the new. The newcomer stepping out, perhaps for the first time, with the initial willingness to be openminded, which leads to the hope that they too can recover, having the solid evidence of others on the recovery path before them. That hope eventually transcending into faith. Whatever our spiritual beliefs, if any, outside the program may be, we all learn to have faith in someone in N.A. that believes in us and wants to help us in our recovery.

Each member has the the right to believe in what works for them, to come to their own understanding of a Power greater than ourselves. Many of our member have various religious, philosophical, non religious views and beliefs. What we all have in common is the spiritual aspect of all beliefs, that we can do together what we could never do alone and that the therapeutic value of one addict helping another is truly without parallel when we focus on our similarities rather than our differences.

We are grateful that regardless of what we believe or do not believe, that we are accepted in Narcotics Anonymous and able to find recovery from the disease of addiction in the company of others like ourselves.

quote: “Step Ten is worked while the days ups and downs are fresh in our heads.”
body:
Daily inventory gives us freedom because we can clean up the problems before they expand and involve more people. Mostly, we forgive and forget in our minds - and pledge to not repeat the same mistake! If we have to make direct amends to someone, our fresh experience with surrender gives us internal resources we did not have before!

We sometimes feel relieved of the pressure error brings. Only very rarely does someone feel good about owing an amends. If we are not clear about what went wrong, it is best to talk to someone who knows your situations before jumping the gun and making things worse. .

Many times our inventory has to do with the future, envisioning things that haven’t happened yet. This is the wonderful twilight of NA recovery! We can see the future in time to change it! If we are also working Steps 11 and 12, inventory can really help us maintain a reasonable reality setting. Prayer and meditation are powerful Steps and inventory helps us maintain the balance we all need.

quote: “We become spiritually refreshed and are glad to be alive.”
body:
We don’t sugar coat reality or promise our members that there will be no problems along recovery road. What changes is the way we are inside and the way we react to the happenings in our lives. If you are not spiritually refreshed and glad to be alive, there is usually one or more of the basics that worked for you in the beginning that you have discontinued or forgotten to observe.

The spiritual life keeps us pointed in a direction that feeds us helpful information, a new attitude and people who are there for us when we need them. By not reading literature, going to meetings, calling your sponsor and, oh yeah, not using, we lay ourselves open to attack without warning. That’s what a relapse is - an attack on our our new lives.

Hopelessness and despair are always there, waiting around the corner, and come out when we run out of spiritual food and the opportunities that the Program brings to us.

quote: “Although our heads told us we would never make it, the people in the Fellowship gave us hope by insisting we could.”
body:
We will find that our hopelessness and despair is always waiting to take over any time we slack up on our prayer and meditation. Spiritual maintenance is what we use to get clean, stay clean and grow spiritually. When we turn our attention to helping others, we change our whole situation. Our concern for others makes a big difference in the way we think and feel.

We can move from despairing and needful to sitting up straight and thinking up ways to encourage someone else. This change is crucial to our new lives. The important thing for us to remember today, is we have to make this change of situation every day and do what it takes to help it come naturally. Close contact with other members gives us a constant source of different ways to handle our approach to life. The change in us makes a change in the way we experience life.

The using life kept us spinning in a soup of circumstances beyond our control. Even if we were basically good people, we became capable of terrible things. We literally couldn’t help it when our addiction was running our lives.

quote: “It isn’t easy, but, if we want to live happily, we have to learn to live ethically, and within our means, facing what we have and what we have not, with honest acceptance and gratitude.”
body:
Acceptance comes with seeing clearly where we are and what is expected of us. However much or little we have, we can always make ourselves miserable by wanting more. Gratitude is an emotional muscle that responds to exercise. Practicing gratitude gives us strength inside. Getting honest may be a problem.

One member was a working CPA. He was going to file his taxes and was thinking about cheating a bit. It would save him $50. He ran it past someone in the program and his friend asked him if his integrity was worth $50. It really put it all in a nutshell, the matter of ethics. Being ethical and moral is worth the price. He says he knows from experience that it doesn’t take much to trick himself into compromising his values!

Another member shared, “I was hanging with a guy with forty years clean, going to meetings and such. I asked him what his definition of honesty was. He replied, ‘Well, I will not perjure myself. I have not signed a court slip since that day, as the people would come in late and leave early and I was swearing under penalty of perjury that they were in attendance at the meeting, indicating, the entire meetings.’”

Nixon had a few minor league guys snoop around the Democratic Headquarters. A night watchman caught them. This destroyed Nixon’s presidency and caused him to resign in disgrace. The interesting part is that nothing would have been gained from their snooping. Do I leave myself hanging out to be arrested for minor activities? Something that would taint my record forever.

quote: “There are those of us who tried to stay clean without the benefit of “having had a spiritual awakening as a result of those steps,” and they are no longer with us.”
body:
The Program of Narcotics Anonymous is no joke. It is a set of principles we can live by with good results. Through the years we have seen many members put their program on hold while they took care of ‘more important things.’ Their relationship, their job, their passtimes and hobbies. They failed to realize for us addicts working the N A Program is a life and death matter. Recovery is what makes the rest of our lives possible!

When a close friend relapses, it may provide the kick in the ass we need to get real about recovery again! A spiritual awakening is an impossible goal for most of us. Our general culture does not credit everyday people with the ability to become spiritual adept.

Now, there are those who wind up the N A who are not addicts. A little controlled using convinced and made believers of them and they got back with the program. There are others who stuck with the Program and lived on to better things. The Program is designed to help those who suffer from the disease of addiction and want a new way of life. It is worth noting that the Program does work for non-addicts; it just works faster. The 12 Steps also work for some individuals who have other mental problems. How could be not be fine with that?

Surrendering, coming to believe and turning our life and will over to a Loving God is pretty strong for a good beginning. Going through the inventory process that deals with the things that have been bothering us is also very spiritual. When we have inventory, prayer and meditation in our experience, we really have walked a spiritual mile. Almost all of us have had the problem of assuming a spiritual awakening is some kind of impossible dream. But the real truth is that if you are reading this, it already qualifies you to pursue 12 Step recovery to the new continent of life on the clean side!

quote: “None of the Steps work by magic. We do not just say the words of the Steps; we live them.”
body:
Belief in magic was part of our illusions and delusions in our using days. We could convince ourselves of the most unbelievable things. In recovery we learn that we have to work for our achievements. First, we learn the words to the Steps. We hear them repeated in every meeting and their meaning starts to sink in. When we start to work them with a sponsor, they become real to us. They start to make sense and slowly become a part of our lives.

As we progress through the Steps, we learn spiritual principles and incorporate them into our lives because life becomes easier and makes more sense. The more we work the Steps and practice living by spiritual principles, we find ourselves living in the solution and finding self- value.

As we practice the spiritual principles taught by the Steps over and over, they become as natural as breathing to us. We master living in accordance with the will of our Higher Power and our lives become richer and more beautiful. Eventually, going against what we know to be the will of our Higher Power becomes difficult and the pain we get proves we have made a poor choice.

We start to have a light shining through us that is readily apparent to others. We become grateful for this new way of living and want to share it with others. The Steps are not come imaginary sequence that has no basis in reality or nature. The are realer than real and their depth and clarity can only be appreciated by those who live them.

quote: “Those of us who are consciously working and using the Twelve Steps to recovery in our lives are seldom bothered by gossip. Our lives are lived like “open books,” and really don’t feel like hiding anything, or judging anyone. We no longer feel a need to put up a front, because we are doing the best we can.”
body:
One member got into the habit of pumping other members for information. He would see a friend and just begin asking questions; what did he make, how much did he pay for his car, did he have a lot of debt. Then he would take this information and share it with other people.

He eventually found that when he approached people, they would pull out their cell phones and pretend to be talking to someone, thus cutting him out. Finally he had no friends. He never figured this out. Eventually he simply faded away from the fellowship.

I had told him I had the flu and it was bad. Later I went to a meeting across town and everyone was surprised to see me. They thought I was terminally ill. When I ask who they heard this from, they replied “Doc”. Then we all smiled as we realized it was just more of Doc’s gossip. He eventually lost all credibility.

As the people moved away from Doc, he simply turned up the volume and began gossiping even more. Finally he relapsed. He would come back around to the meetings loaded but never could get clean again. No one would help him. He had burned his bridges.Even now, there are those who would help him if they could but the first requirement of membership in NA is surrender. Membership doesn’t always come easy, but it is always worth it.

quote: “The first way in which we carry the message is by staying clean with the help of God and the Fellowship. Our new way of living speaks for itself better than our words ever could.”
body:
We don’t have to sell NA. The pain and desperation of active addiction is enough to make people want a change. Maybe it is a question of values and motivation. If we had to coddle people, it would change our profile from stand up integrity over to slick commercialism. You can’t get what we have to offer for any amount of money.

Self-evident recovery is a beautiful thing. The persons in NA have a glow about them. They attract addicts to recovery because the identification is there. Possibly some of our defects help work the miracle the way dirt produces flowers.

Our hope opens the doorway to recovery. Seeing others who remind us of ourselves, we imagine there may be a chance NA will work for us. We have to stop using and surender to the fact of our addiction, at least on a daily basis. After a short time, we will notice a difference.

quote: “A warning about rationalization is that it has killed more of us than anything else.”
body:
Many, many members are yet to realize that rationality alone is insufficient for the purposes of a spiritual life. Spiritual is on a higher level than mentality alone.

One member led meetings and when people brought a salad to the noon meeting, he would give them a hard time for not bringing enough for everyone. He thought it was kind of cool to give people a little shit and make then squirm. But there was nothing in the meeting reading about bringing food into the meeting and eventually people got tired of his “joking.” See, it was joking to him and being attacked by the brown baggers who left work on their noon hour to come to a meeting. Eventually all the working people quit coming to his noon meeting and then it consisted of just brand new people without jobs.

He had rationalized his attacks as being very subtle humor designed to bring him closer to those he was attacking. The theory was, I suppose, that if he could make you squirm, it was ok cause you were such good buddies and that’s what good buddies do. In Narcotics Anonymous, some members just love to excoriate others due to poor social skills. The tv show song goes “Welcome back Cotter. We love him a lot so we put him on the spot, welcome back, welcome back, welcome back”. Theory being, if you are really really close to someone, then you have the right to insult them as no one else could do and it would be ok, you get a pass.

This happens in NA but the attacked sees it as just that, an attack. While the attacker sees it as some sort of strange bonding process enabling the hapless sap being attacked as some sort of long lost friend when they really never knew him that good in the first place. Maybe it just manifestation of poor social skills on an NA level. We must understand that we can never say please and thank you too often. We can never be too kind and considerate. We are all trying to bond and we need to send a message of friendship loud and clear.

A group of people used to congregate around the meeting hall entry way and heckle those arriving. But we do not know what state of mind the new arrivals are in. Think about it, when you get into the car and go to the meeting hall, it could be that you are in trouble and are looking for some support ASAP. The dog could have died, you got fired, Mom died or you got a letter from the IRS. We have no idea of what that person is going thru.

quote: “ We read this book and attend N.A. meetings. We see for ourselves if the Fellowship has something to offer us. It is not where we are that counts, but where we are going!”
body:
We start to realize we are on a journey of recovery that will be a life-long trek of discovery. Changing, growing and learning becomes pleasurable to us and we look forward to the next day’s developments. The process of ongoing self-examination may begin to occur before we realize what is happening. It is a new thing, this being excited rather than worried as we start each new day.

Often others will notice changes in us before we do and surprise us with their insights. Upon reflection, we realize we are no longer who we were and we like who we are becoming. We are afforded glimpses of what we might become in our deepening friendships with long-timers who have infinite patience, humility and joy in their lives. This becomes our dreams and goals, to live happy, joyous and free. Serene in the love of our Higher Power and the new people in our lives. .

From the beginning we are encouraged to get in touch with our inner being, this is the being we lost touch with when addiction took over our lives. It put the answer out there, somewhere, not here where we are. Slow down, pay attention, practice being good to people until your learn to be good to yourself!

quote: “Those of us who do service work are the luckiest people on God’s earth!”
body:
Service is an expression of gratitude. We are truly grateful for the wonderful gift of recovery. We consider it a privilege to give nameless, selfless service to Narcotics Anonymous. Whether we help a newcomer, make coffee, sponsor someone, bring a meeting to a shut-in, serve as chairperson for a meeting, become a trusted servant for area, region, H&I, phone lines or other positions, we consider ourselves lucky.
