By working the Steps have learned the joys of living in the present in accordance with spiritual principles.

Doing for others, we learn all we need to know to treat ourselves with affection and genuine respect. The world begins to lose its hold on us and we learn to pray as we go through life. We can admit fault and ask for help. We don’t have to have all the answers. We fit in with others, we don’t have to dominate. We can be a part of humanity.

Luck comes from being aware and awake. The answers are often printed large on billboards if we go slow enough to read them. While many believe totally in karma, what comes around, many find that God is ready when we are. If God is in all of us, surely God would want to be comfortable in our house!

quote: “We often miss what we are looking for because it isn’t hidden.”
body:
It may take us a while to surrender enough to get this, but lots of things in life are not hard. We make them hard by trying too hard. Relaxing and calming ourselves changes dramatically the way others see us. If we exhibit tension and fear of things going wrong, others pick up on that. They would much rather interact with someone who is straight up and carries no air of negative anticipation.

One of the discoveries we may stumble upon is we just pray for something with positive expectation and let it come to us. We still stay active and meet our obligations but if there are unforeseen obstacles, getting what we want may be out of order and create problems we cannot foresee. A truly loving God would not want this to happen. Our minds and mental habits are set in a pattern established before we got clean. If we continue to surrender, it sometimes makes it easier to accept and be grateful for what comes our way. It is ok to hold out for good things in the future and what we find ourselves doing on a daily basis may well lead to that pleasant outcome.

The oldtimers used to always tell us “Don’t leave before the miracle happens.” The self- destructiveness of our addiction perpetuates the idea that life is hard and the odds are rigged against us, like a casino. Real casinos are rigged by human beings for self-gain. Our lives are under our control but first we have to learn how to drive! The telephone polls and sidewalks are not out to get us. We learn to keep it between the curbs. Our thinking and expectation may play a larger role than we have been told by people who didn’t know better!

quote: “Our lack of fear and our new found faith serves as a firm foundation for courage in the future .”
body:
One member owed the IRS $800,000 and he found another member that owed them $1,000,000. The two became friends and both eventually resolved their problems.

No matter what the problem, we can solve it with HP’s help. How do we eat an elephant, “One bite at a time”.

And we need to take a break once in awhile. We go to the movies, camp out in meetings or go to our favorite healing places. This rejuvenation process can boost us into the project at hand with turbo charged energy and laser focus.

When we focus on one item, we are unstoppable. When we disperse our focus, we waste time and get little done.

quote: “Those of us who have relapsed found the true progression the disease that plagues us. To know and come to understand that there is a Power greater than ourselves desiring to have a relationship with us, in spite of our faults, comforts us all.”
body:
The disease of addiction sometimes paints pretty pictures of our using days. Relapse brings all that to a screeching halt. The insecurity, the distrust we have for our mental processes, confusion, uncertainty, physical symptoms, negative projections - we don’t go through a grace period. Our active addiction cut deep grooves in us that further using quickly fills to the brim.

If we have had any relief from our addiction in recovery, we remember the calmness which began to fill each of our days and the feeling we got around the members who loved and understood us. The beauty of a day at a time. The feeling we had greeting a newcomer at their first meeting. Even it we were very new, we began to associate these feelings with a higher power.

In addiction, progression insures whatever is bothering us will get worse if we use. Usually, whatever is bothering us just dries up and goes away on its own! Conscious contact is better than having a genie in a bottle! Ask your Higher Power for help and it is amazing how things work out.

quote: “The program doesn’t work when we adapt it to our life, we have to adapt our life to the program.”
body:
The feeling that something isn’t right, that nothing we did turned out right was a very accurate feeling. Addicts often confess that they lie out of habit. That they expect things to go wrong for them. Well, honesty has to be learned and is one of the areas that turns up quick results in our new lives but goes on forever. The reason we fit into life so quickly when we get clean, is because we lose all the bumps that stick out from us and get us into trouble! They are like thorns that poke out and when others feel pain, they retaliate.

The world we see is not necessarily all there is. There are many things that don’t exist for us because we are conditioned not to see them. Sometimes, as our minds and personalities grow, we are surprised by opportunities and agreeable people all around us. Some of us think that we can’t see what we can’t accept inside. When we learn through a process of positive experiences that we can trust ourselves and rely on our ability to stand tall in situations where we used to fail all the time.

If the Program is honesty, openmindedness and the willingness to try, and that is how we learn to cope with things that come up in our lives, it becomes easier to see how adapting can benefit us. We are taught not to be too hard on ourselves because it takes time and application to adapt to the new life we have chosen.

quote: 32, 18 None of us stumbled into this Fellowship brimming with love, honesty or open-minded willingness. ”
source:
Memphis Review Form - page

Most of us actually showed up at our doors brimming with degradation, isolation and loneliness. We probably covered up our feelings and considerate members didn’t need to ask. We know what it takes to come to that first meeting!
body:
With that fear, we needed to see how simple it could be using N A as a means of fulfillment. Instead of the shooting galleries we used to frequent, NA gives us choices. We go to meetings, call members, hang out together and realize we are never alone.. Not near as stressful in ‘our’ rooms, we begin seeing a little more clearly. We see members laugh, have fun and genuinely care for us and want to help us. This hope gives us a clarity not seen in many years. We find that someone loves us even when we feel out of control. And with this new found hope we begin pushing the isolation out and away from us.

By hearing the Steps and Traditions read and studied at every meeting we attend, we start to feel some willingness. The other readings fill in the blanks for us. It just begins to sink in. Our hearts recognize the honesty we hear and we start to want to be honest ourselves.

quote: “The willingness to try new ideas and possible solutions will help open the door to our recovery.”
body:
Our recovery is our on doing. While NA can share information on how it works, we are the ones who surrender, come to believe and allow a loving God to run our lives. Staying clean from drugs, old friends and old ways, make a big difference but it is we who do the footwork. This becomes important when our disease tries to take over and discount all the wonderful things recovery brings us because it can force us to focus on some perceived imperfection or imperfect person. Since we work the Steps, we are entitled to the goodies. We tell ourselves, “Go back to sleep disease!”

Ideas contain various approaches to life that may work for us. All our willingness does is let us try them on for size. If they don’t work so well, we set them aside for the time being. But just being open doesn’t force us to accept them if they don’t work! Some have to wait their turn, they can’t work until we have made progress in some other area. That’s ok, at least we know they are available and that’s about all we can ask from a brief look-see.

There are a great many things in reality and it takes time to try them out. We wouldn’t want a job at the bank if we weren’t good with numbers or had problems with cash-register honesty. Recovery is no joke! It is the real thing! And while it may make it easier to be around us now that we are in recovery, we are the first to benefit - inside!

quote: “We have defects, but a willingness to live as our Higher Power would have us is freedom.”
body:
Freedom is slavery unless it rises from within. As the fog of active addiction falls away through disuses, we find ourselves stepping out on new ground. Some of our virtues are going to turn out to be defective as we work our 12 Steps and view them in the light of our recovery. Some of our defects are going to mutate into assets!

Being humble and somewhat ashamed of the way we used to do is to be expected, but the real defects keep us from seeing the world and our lives as they really are. Living according the faith we have chosen to guide us will allow us to benefit from those who have gone before us, in recovery or in our new beliefs.

Sometimes recovery is like walking down a corridor with other people all around us. We are correct to think we are protected by their clean time and recovery experience. In time and through practice, we will come to know what they know through our own successful experience.

Freedom is actually free because it comes from doing the right thing, the right way, at the right time. We pay the price for things as we generally don’t miss the price we pay. The clean life gives us resources we never had. We see and hear more clearly. Out thinking is better. We can admit fault or ask for help if we don’t understand. The Program gives us people like ourselves who have walked out mile before us in a thousand ways.

quote: “In working the Steps we need to come to depend on a Power greater than ourselves. We continue this relationship and utilize it for our Group purposes. If we each turned our will and our lives over to His care and seek to do his will, he will express Himself on a group level.”
body:
Sometimes things just go wrong. Maybe somebody thought they were praying for strength and guidance but had a belief system that made reliance on God a risky business. How could we know? We can only do the best we can on an individual basis with the understanding that we will honor our commitment to serve and cover the shortfall if anything goes wrong. This is what it means to say “Our Program” and mean it!

Believe it or not, there are people in NA who do not understand group conscience. They may think it is group opinion or hearsay. They may not know enough about our history as a Fellowship to know that addicts writing the Basic Text worked hard to look up words in the dictionary, discuss all things until a clear path appeared to them and actually asked group members specific questions on motions in the CAR before voting at the group, area and regional level. Without this system of informed members, NA would be flying blind!

They also found and wrote into the literature and structural guidelines the importance of having a group prayer before sessions in which there might be voting on issues before their service body. Without this spiritual process, we would be at the mercy of bureaucrats who think they know what’s best for us. N A by the members, for the members, is the only real spiritual Fellowship of Narcotics Anonymous. All else is not N A.

quote: “Meetings provide a safe place to share with others during this time. Many members whose recoveries have blessed them tell us that they continue to attend meetings on a daily basis even after several years of clean time.”
body:
We never get so much clean time that we lose our need for recovery meetings. Some of us are blessed with lives where we continuously talk, visit, host and travel within N A. Some of us can recall thinking, “This disease can kill me and all my dreams with it. I better get to more meetings, only to look up from the highway and realize we are driving in the middle of the night to a three day N A convention in a favorite recovery community. Back home, we still get to meetings if we want to stay clean. Recovery is the shore and the meetings are our life boats.

Getting used to being with people who love and understand us is essential to recovery. They can help us, whatever we are going through. Their trials and tribulations inspire us to go the extra mile in recovery. It is amazing to see hard core dope fiends behaving like civilized human beings. It is clear that we learned how to live from people who did not know how to live. So, whatever we missed, we have to get through the meetings. Home groups take it a step further because participating in group business meetings we learn how to conduct ourselves in an orderly manner. In many cases, a home group is the only honorable association they have had in their lives!

Long term associations take us deeper and deeper into our new lives. We have friends who have been doing better for a long time. They know about good jobs, owning houses and dealing with life beyond desperation.

quote: “The experience gained with time insures our ongoing recovery. We must use what we learn or we will lose it and probably relapse, no matter how long we have been clean.”
body:
Addicts are prone to egotism and we need to be constantly aware of our built in self-will if we are going to survive. Such humility helps with our surrender. It is our surrender that helps us be open-minded and prayerful. Most of us seem to take the ‘easy’ days for granted. It may stop short of really thinking, “We can take it from here!” but the yearning to fall back into the old ruts still beckons. Experience tells to take it one day at a time and fill in the blanks helping others.

“If you stop pedaling, the bicycle falls over.” This humble phrase covers so many situations is spells out what happens when addicts get proud and take recovery for granted. You are better and have earned the esteem of others because you are not longer staggering around trying to get through a day! Be grateful you’re in the Program. We laugh about the stupid stuff that goes through our minds at times. The same stuff that could kill us if we kept it inside.

It has taken the last forty or fifty years to get N A members with forty or fifty years. People still relapse after all that time and they still die using if they push away the principles and ideals that brought them so far. There are pressures today that did not exist thirty-five years ago when NA really began to grow! Our disease doesn’t offer a grace period when we relapse, it lulls us into erroneous thinking and takes away our internal resources so it can bust us in the chops. An addict alone is still in bad company.

quote: “The group we choose to be our home group will be like a spiritual savings account. The more we can invest in it through our care and sharing, the greater the dividends it will pay.”
body:
We must invest in our recovery. We need to put in time, energy and money to our recovery. Some people give thousands and thousands to their church every year. N A is our sanctuary.

Part of giving is providing for our meeting places but another important part is giving till it hurts. Giving until our giving inconveniences us a little bit. Until we reach this point, it may not be real to us. We skip visits to Starbucks. We have a garage sale. We work a side job. Our lives and recoveries are important too.

One member bought a $37 dollar Igloo cooler for iced tea when it was needed at a Unity Day. At each event he saw his $37 gift serving the fellowship. It was hard to glorify using and look at his sacrifice to the Fellowship at the same time. He stayed clean and now has many, many years and still sees that worn old Igloo cooler at events. It still bears his magic marker “Ice Tea” inscription from years earlier.

We must give till it hurts once in awhile for it to register with us that we are willing to go to any lengths to stay clean.

quote: “Remember we’ve don’t recover overnight. When we realize that we have made a bad decision or bad judgment, we make an attempt to rationalize it. We become extreme in our self-righteous attempts to cover our tracks.”
body:
Learning how to ‘catch ourselves’ is a big part of recovery. Personality change is necessary, not optional. As we become accustomed to the recovery process, we sort of relax in the saddle and sometimes this opens the doorway to our past behaviors. Each time we do this, we embed our chosen way of living and pave the way to a new life clean!

Helping others helps us realize our flaws are not that big a deal unless we act on them. To us recovering people, they are a gentle yet convincing argument for living a new life. It is not like the old ways got us what we wanted! They got us in trouble, into courtrooms, into jail cells. Hey, we don’t have to live that way anymore. Seeing what our newer members go through really helps us surrender and renew our commitment. We can identify with them and recall some of the foolishness we pulled when we were new.

Oh, and how embarrassed we were when we messed up. Clean, we can see the unwanted side effects of road rage, shoplifting, rationalizing why we do certain things. Now that we are clean, they really don’t make any sense. We are clean and see, hear and feel better than we used to. .

quote: “There is something in our self-destructive personality’s big cries for failure. It permeates our total being – “You do not deserve to succeed.” We have all experienced this and without the knowledge that we finally have earned the right to be responsible, respectable members of society. The manner in which we returned to our addiction is not important. What is essential to each of us is that we have the choice not to continue.”
body:
When we immerse ourselves in meetings, this cry for failure is eliminated. We see new people with three days clean who are elated. We see people who have landed a good job. We see people who finally got their own place after couch surfing for months. This is the reality of NA. Many of us slept on couches and floor, or in our cars, when we first got clean!

The failure syndrome grows in the dark. It grows when we are absent from the meetings. Some experienced members believe that most addicts simply do not attend enough meetings and therein lies the problem.

Our literature says that we attend ‘90 in 90’ and watch our lives change. But this promise is renewed every quarter. Four times a year our lives can change. The ‘90 in 90’ miracle does not apply just to the first 90 days clean. This concept is not to be found anywhere in our written literature.. In fact, some would maintain that the growth become greater as time goes on. The second ‘90 in 90’ may very well yield more growth than the first and so on. Meetings are a very, very powerful motivator and success builder. They get us out of the house and marking stuff off that to do list. They get us applying for jobs. They are energy pills. Maybe we should ask ourselves just how far we want to be from the polar bear that wants to eat us?

Never underestimate the power of going to just one more meeting that day. The oldtimers can be found burning up meetings with a vengeance and a regularity that would shame train schedules. “Meeting makers make it,” is not a new idea. It is said for a reason. Give yourself a break and try increasing your meeting attendance and see if things get better.

quote: “We’ve made a lot of bad decisions and pawned them off as Group conscience. This worries many of us. How can we really tell if our decisions are really Goup conscience or not, and how do we prevent painful mistakes?”
body:
Easy answer: we can’t! All we can do is work our program to the best of our ability. There are many choices involved with N A membership. We chose our friends and sponsors. We choose our home group. These choices sort out where we go and who we spend time with.

This takes care of many problems in a quiet and natural manner. Egos, personal preferences, and honest mistakes insure we will have some problems. Hey, when we are clean, we can handle most things that come up in a group so long as we follow our Traditions.

One trouble is many of us don’t know when we are violating the letter or spirit of a Tradition. We stay away from the law of the land because outsiders, however well intended, cannot be expected to know our Program or understand our ways. We avoid affiliation for the same reason. When we are really focused on recovery and helping others, it takes up enough time and energy to keep us out of trouble.

(bs)

quote: “It is very important to keep sharing with other people, so that when we come up with a rationalization for negative behavior, we can be told about it.”
body:
One of the things we can’t forget is that we have trouble being objective with ourselves. Sharing in meetings, we let others know about our strengths and weaknesses. It is like a personality filter that cleans out patterns and echos from our active addictions. Newcomers need meetings for learning our terminology and mindset - persons with longer clean time get the help they need in meetings on the level that is appropriate for them.

Most members are very aware how painful and frightening it can be to discover certain aspect of our personal beliefs and behavior are flawed. Sometimes they are just surprised. Remember, habit patterns are just a part of our mental landscape until we can see how they may upset others. Often our fellow members are aware of our flaws long before we find out about them. They exercise patience and tolerance thinking of those who helped them come to terms with their inventory items. The 12 Steps guide us through a series of processes prior to getting down to brass tacks with inventory. We surrender, come to believe, turn our life and will over to achieve the fearless state required to write a list and share it.

The relief we feel often prods us to go deeper because we realize we don’t have to be the way we were when we were out of our minds! It would just be pitiful if we had to be right all the time. What a joke! Nobody’s perfect and our program is very good at helping people of all sorts.

quote: “We have seen that we try to rest authority in the spiritual conscience of the group. In keeping with this, we make a special point of trying to prevent authoritarian leadership. We have learned that for our Fellowship, leadership by example and by self-less service works, and that direction and manipulation fails.”
body:
This is where clean time and experience comes into play. Without clean time or experience, all we can offer is opinion and personal projections. Perception is based on projection because that is what we know best. There is a need to have those who have gone before us to share what they have seen work in practice and what leads to trouble..

The old NA teaching model was based upon English craftsman guilds. Here a child was sent to a workhouse and learned under a master. The child was mentored by the older man. NA calls these roles ‘sponsor’ and ‘sponsee.’

The sponsors used to state that they owned us. One was expected to do whatever the sponsor requested without question. It was quite Zen like in nature. Lessons were taught thru simple tasks and assignments. There was always a bigger picture in the background.

As the sponsee grew, he was then allowed to sponsor. But he would go to his sponsor when he got stuck. Having a strong grand sponsor was a feather in one’s cap. Large sponsorship families evolved and large sponsorship picnics were held with a picture being taken of everyone and then being silk screened on a T shirt for each person.

Each sponsorship family had its own flavor. We became relatives with each other in a sense. If you were a great, great, great grand sponsor of someone, they had the right to come to you if their sponsor was not giving them what they needed. There was a lot of up and down communication, many parties and gatherings of parts of these large families.

quote: ““The main thing this Step [Step Nine] does for us is to build awareness that, little by little, we are gaining new attitudes about ourselves, and how we deal with other people.””
body:
Many of us find a bonus in the Ninth Step when we avoid situations where amends might be needed at a later date for what is happening in the present. It is easier to get real and be honest in the present tense than to carry bad feelings in the past tense. And it seems to be a principle that when we move one way, we free ourselves in the other. So a little relief leads to more relief and more relief frees us from the shame and guilt that has been chasing us all along.

Clean, we do better and have less to regret or feel shame and guilt over. Probably, this increasing distance makes it easier to single out events where we feel we owe amends to the other people involved. Amending things that bother us frees us to go forward with a clear conscience. It may be important to realize the very fact of feeling badly shows a degree of morality that emerges as we move up the recovery scale from active addiction to real freedom, the fullness of being. .

One important aspect of our making amends where they are due, is that we will make a mark in our behavior to prevent repetition of the mistakes we have made in the past. Also important is the power of recent good memories against the power of bad memories moving further into the past. Helping others, being good to yourself, following a spiritual path, gives us growing internal resources that help us face each day anew.

quote: “Am I being drawn in by some pattern of fear and resentment?”, Am I too tired “ “”Am I too hungry?” “ Is my thinking getting cloudy?” It’s a vaccination against insanity on a continuing basis.”
body:
In other words, personal, daily inventory is a vaccination against insanity. When in active addictions, we controlled our mental state with drugs. Today we are clean and take responsibility for our actions and make choices as to how we will be. Being clear headed makes a big difference! As we get better at monitoring ourselves including our thought processes, the less we need to act on our impulses. In NA, we learn from other members, meetings and recovery writings. Prayer and meditation enlarges the world we live in on a daily basis.

We allow our sponsors and friends to mentor us and help us find the way that best suits us. We get to become the persons we wanted to be all along, in many cases. A vaccination is much more acceptable than a week or two of flu! We learn to see things with new eyes. Patience, tolerance and willingness gives us internal resources to make is easier to cope with life on life’s terms.

It seems a vaccination gives us the illness we want to avoid in a weakened state and our bodies produce antibodies that specifically go after the target bacteria. So, we get a little taste of healthy selfishness in time to say, “Hey, wait a minute. That’s not how I want to be!” When we don’t say something or act out of fear or jealousy, we don’t have to take it back. Regaining our power of choice, we are able to change the future by changing our minds. This is the reality of spiritual growth. We change things in the world by changing things inside.

quote: “Those of us who have relapsed found the true progression of the disease that plagues us. To know and come to understand that there is a Power greater than ourselves desiring to have a relationship with us, in spite of our faults, comforts us all.”
body:
Most of our notions of a loving God change as we grow in recovery. Right from the beginning, feel the relief we have been seeking. Wherever we start from, we grow from there. We may not be sure what is difference but we can feel it. Hearing and studying recovery and learning from what other addicts share in meetings, we come to see parts of a new life of the spirit.

The prospect of having a relationship with a positive Power greater than ourselves can be scary. Pain brought on by beliefs that let us down makes us gun-shy and careful. Many turn back and only come to believe later in recovery. The sooner you can honestly make the decision in your 3rd Step, the better for you. But the part we eventually notice is where it says, “We made a decision to turn our life and will over to the CARE of ...” It is the ‘caring’ that allows us to give it a try.

Knowing can come is a flash. Coming to understand involves getting over past beliefs and learning new possibilities including the care and relief we have already received, even if we were not so nice.

quote: “Some of us reach a point of complacency in recovery. If we stay at this level for long, the recovery process ceases and we begin to backslide. Clean time in the program acts as insurance. If complacency is not acted upon the disease begins to manifest apparent symptoms in us. Denial returns along with obsession and compulsion.”
body:
If recovery is like eating cake at a table among friends who like us, a relapse is like a slap in the back of the head when we are not expecting it! Complacency can sneak up on us when we forget gratitude and the practices we learn from other members. It is so easy to forget where we came from and what it felt like to be without hope. Relapse happens long before we pick up.

Obsession is losing control of our thinking and obsession is losing control of our actions. Denial is like a dark veil that shields us from reality and makes it possible to remember only the good times. Many members compare complacency with a ‘sliding slope’ where by the time realize we are falling, we cannot stop. Warning: it begins with little acts of selfishness and egotism.

It may be that our disease is like a fluid that begins to fill our cup when we stop working our NA 12 Steps. When the cup is filled, it overflows into a bowl and when the disease reaches the top, we relapse. We have to give it away to keep the bowl filling. Asking for help, going to meetings, helping someone struggling with their program bleeds away the disease in the bowl of our life and soon our cup once more fills with joy and gratitude! We look at our cup and it is now filling again - this time with coffee! We look around us and we are no longer alone. We are among a group of recovering addicts.

quote: “Meetings are a healing source. Writing about what we want, what we are asking for and what we get and sharing this with our sponsor or another trusted person helps us to work through those feelings, and letting others share with us about their experience gives us hope that it does get better.”
body:
We addicts know more than anyone the pain and confusion to straps us down when we are ready to seek recovery. We do everything possible to make our meetings available and congenial. We set out coffee and condiments, sometimes cookies. Our rooms are comfortable, some even provide candlelight to make easier to share the pain in our hearts.

This is why we have no outside affiliation, no surveillance and a clear message in our group format. Being a part of a group steering committee is sometimes the most honorable position we have held. Anything we can do to make a newer member comfortable and supported is the second most important way we carry our message of hope and recovery! The first is being clean ourselves and paying attention that we adhere to the truth.

Along with social ineptitude, the drug-induced cloud prevents us from thinking effectively. Asking the three questions in the quotation consciously may help us get the answers we need to guide us. Frequently, the questions involve straightforward stuff like: “I want a job that pay enough money for me to live on.” We may be surprised to learn you cannot sleep on the job or steal tools if you want to keep the job! The connections between our actions and the results of our actions allows us to monitor our progress. We can’t change until we see the need for change.

quote: “We want to get free of our fear, but we don’t wish to do so at the price of anyone.”
body:
We do not want to create more difficulty and pain for others. We cannot push away our own embarrassment and disgust at the way we were living, while making new problems for others. That would only lead us back to feeling self-destructive about ourselves.

Instead, we move forward on a daily basis making amends as needed. Others see us as a different person. Without the fear, we find new motivation from the good feelings that come with raising our heads and examining the roles we are playing. We want to make sure we are not leaving damage behind us while we think we are going forward.

We build our new lives on solid ground by working our 12 Steps in order. Surrender, Faith, relinquishment - all three put us on a different path, the path of recovery. Acknowledging our defects, we are losing the hold they had on our lives. Through willingness and prayer, we ask that these defects be removed. With the barriers out of the way, we list and address our amends so we can walk down any road in life. .Inventory keeps us from rationalizing our faults when we get into prayer and meditation. We want our spiritual awakening to be real. We base our lives on following God’s plan for us and practicing spiritual principles and carry our message of hope and recovery.

quote: “We have discussed the preventative side of Tenth Step.. The love was there all the time, waiting for us to accept it.”
body:
For most all of us, we grew up without a lot of love. Or maybe there was love and we just could not feel it. It wasn’t the love we were looking for or that we thought we needed to survive. Or maybe the love was in the form of teasing and bullying. Our friend’s past on the sick type of love they got at home and in their own warped way, they thought they were helping us to feel like we were one of the gang. We were so loved starved that many of us accept a great deal of belittling and bullying just to feel like we belonged somewhere. The bullying is supposed to show it is ok to kid one another. We may even be in these types of relationships today with an abusive spouse or partner or we may be staying in a sick home life feeling we have nowhere else to go. We may felt trapped by life’s circumstances with no way out.

In recovery we find one true friend, a sponsor and we put our faith and trust in them. For some, that is the best we can do in the beginning. We know in our heart of hearts that we have no business running the show so we must get direction from outside of ourselves. This small act of faith will grow and grow until we find ourselves surrounded by an entire world wide fellowship of folks that love us unconditionally.
moment: Just for tonight: I will put my trust into this fellowship without reservation. I know that if I allow one NA member to love me today it is enough.

quote: “Some of us reach a point of complacency in recovery. If we stay at this level for long, the recovery process ceases and we begin to backslide. Clean time in the program access insurance. If complacency is not acted upon the disease begins to manifest apparent symptoms in us. Denial returns along with obsession and compulsion.”
body:
Our founder wrote “we know well what makes true addiction, obsession and compulsion. Obsession, that fixed idea that takes us back time and time again to our drug of choice or some substitute to recapture the ease and comfort we once knew.” Ain’t that the truth sister and brother! We have found that most NA members switch their obsession to drugs to one of our favorite substitutes: sex, sugar, over eating, gambling, road rage or compulsive spending.

The best line ever written about working Steps is at the beginning portion of Step one in the Step Writing Guide which states; “if we have been compulsive in some other area of our lives, we need to stop the acting out so our minds are not filled with using before we start working this Step.” Wow, wow, wow! No wiser words have ever been written about addiction. Knock it off! Whatever it is, pornography, credit card debting, lusting after newcomers, over eating. We need to surrender first as only then can we move forward into the solution.
moment: Just for tonight; I am sick and tired of being sick and tired of being sick and tired. I will surrender my behavior and share about it with my sponsor or home group. I will choose freedom today.

quote: “Meetings are a healing source. Writing about what we want, what we are asking for and what we get and sharing this with our sponsor or another trusted person helps us to work through those feelings, and letting other share with us about their experience gives us hope that it does get better.”
body:
The magic of writing in recovery cannot be understated. For some reason when we think about pain in our lives or in our past we tend to have a quick rationalization method in place for not truly feeling the feelings. This denial is often a healthy part of our minds, keeping us from too much pain at one time but it can be a huge hindrance as well, keeping us in denial most of our lives. By asking God’s help and letting our fingers do the talking, we tend to get to the truth of the matter. It’s very hard to lie to ourselves in black and white, it just looks like what it is, another story that really doesn’t add up?

By writing about our lives, we come to know the truth, the whole truth and nothing but the truth. When we write our inventories, the truth keeps coming out over and over and over again. This is how we begin to see patterns in our lives. This is where our character defects and the exact nature of our wrongs is revealed so we can work on them in Steps 6, 7, 8 and 9.

If the feelings are a bit too heavy, we can go to a meeting and share them so we can push on to complete our inventories and find true freedom from our defects. In meetings we will get healthy feedback from others when they share how they dealt with similar difficulties while writing their steps or dealing with similar wreckage from their past. One thing we have learned is that addiction and recovery are universally the same around the globe. We can find the answer anywhere if we are but willing to look for it.
moment: Just for tonight: I will push on with my Step work. If I am stuck I will go to a meeting and share about it. I now in my heart that the Steps will set me free!

quote: “Spirituality and sharing is the essence of their recovery. Keeping our illusion of reality rather than using the tools of the program and the people in the Fellowship will return us to isolation.”
body:
When we first come to meetings the last thing we want to do is open up in a room full of people! We have been living a life of deep dark secrets which we thought nobody knew about. Of course they knew! One of things we have learned about addiction is that by the time we see using as a problem it has been very evident in everyone else’s life for a long, long time. They may not have the label right but the condition is plain to see.

If we start by opening up to just one individual, our sponsor, or we just work on saying our name and that we are an addict, this will go a long way to breaking down the walls. One sponsor told his sponsee, share at every meeting, even if it’s just, “My name is so and so, I am an addict and I am ok, or I feel sad, or I feel mad.” Just check in with the group. There is some kind of magic that happens when we admit in open that we are addicts. The lie is dead.

You know, the lies we have told ourselves for years and years; Like, “If it gets really bad I will do something about it.” Or, “When I get bad as Suzy or Bobby or whoever we picked as our ‘less-than,’ I will get my shit together.” Or, “I am not an addict, an addict sells their body for drugs, or an addict sticks needles in their neck!” or, or, or… whatever is the next bottom we haven’t gotten to yet. Remember all those “I will never’s,” we let go of years ago? “I will never use drugs that aren’t natural, no chemicals.” Ok, that one went by the wayside, or “I will never stick a needle in my arm… whoops, we past that one years ago.” These may not be your whenever’s but you have a bunch of them or you would not be reading these words.
moment: Just for tonight; I will surrender to the program and get honest about something I am holding onto for dear life. We surrender to win!

quote: “To use is to die, often in more ways than one. We have found this program at the right time or we wouldn’t have found it at all. N.A. and its principles are here to help us achieve quality in our lives. If we were worthless we wouldn’t be alive.”
body:
What’s the old saying? “If he lives, we’ll quit the drugs.” It usually takes getting near death for something to break through our denial. The denial that we are not addicts, that we do not have a disease, that our using is not “that” bad. Often it is when we have no one else in our lives to blame stuff on that we finally begin to think, just maybe, it might be the drugs.

Do we really want to go back to that misery? We say to newcomers, “Keep your last high fresh in your mind.” That is to remember the worst that using brought us which is usually at the bitter end. Of course, we hope that the bitter end is not in jail for life or in a hospital having lost the use of our limbs and so on. If we haven’t gotten there in our active addiction, we can always say to ourselves “yet”. Which stands for,”You’re eligible too.”

For many of us, NA was the last house on the block. As one addict said; “If this NA stuff doesn’t work, I am surrendered to be a using drug addict for the rest of my life.” She got clean and just celebrated 31 years in NA. Others came to NA as the last resort before killing themselves. As we say, “When at the end of the road we find that we can no longer live with or without the use of drugs we all face the same dilemma, what is there left to do?”
moment: Just for tonight: If my surrender doesn’t feel very strong today, I will take a good hard look at my life at the end of the road and remind myself, “If I pick up it could be a whole lot worse!”

quote: “Left with a shadow of doubt, the mind of a newcomer and even the old-timer can prove to be this spark needed to set off the return of insanity and that first pill, fix, drink or toke.”
body:
A member shared that when someone said in their very first meeting, “If you don’t pick it up, you can’t get loaded.” It was a revelation! It was so simple, I had no clue how to stay clean, it couldn’t be that easy could it? A sponsor told his sponsee, “You can teach a monkey not to use drugs! Just control the movement of your hand from the pill to your mouth.” It’s surviving our emotions from this wretched disease.

Our life depends on surrendering to the truth we have learned in NA, “One is too many a thousand is never enough.” If you are an addict and have found recovery or not, you know in your heart of hearts this is a true statement. We never did just one. If we did, it was to show some non-addict that we could. It wasn’t very long that we needed two then three. For most of us there was no such thing as a wake up shot because we did all we had the night before. When we woke we were off to the races just to get the sick off or to mask all our negative feelings that we couldn’t live with for another minute, we had to use.

Recovery can be very fragile in the beginning. This is why we stick with the winners and follow suggestions as we never know when using might sound good again. When we get such ideas it is vital that we share them with our sponsor or in a meeting. We have learned it is much simpler to rat on our disease then live with the destruction that awaits us if we relapse.
moment: Just for tonight; I will come clean about my addictive thinking with someone today. I will get honest or die, it’s that simple.

quote: “Life’s flow takes place in this manner: we come here powerless and the power we seek comes to us through other people in the Fellowship if we can only reach out for it.”
body:
Get clean, get close and get clear of the wreckage and garbage that our life has dominated our lives. Even if we are idiots, we have learned something in our lives. With the loving support of others who understand and seem to care about us, we build on whatever we have learned that is still of use to us. Many things will be discarded only to be replaced by things that work or work better.

As we become evermore aware of our incompleteness, we hunger to be more than we are. Addiction has chewed a huge hole in our lives, our understanding. As we learn, we also help others. Doing this makes it more comfortable to ask for and receive help from others. We slowly realize that whatever goodness comes to us will be passed on to others. That makes it easier to progress. General improvement is not selfishness! NA recovery is a chain reaction of people helping people!

Reaching out is not entirely new to us. We used help from others to stay alive and out of jail. Many of us found jail time a relief from the scramble of active addiction whether in the streets or in the penthouses. We are careful - the world really is full of trickery! But since we are all sort of in the same boat, we often find ourselves exiting real dilemmas. Now, we are finding solutions rather than entrapment.

quote: “Living just for today relieves the burden of the past and alleviates fear of the future.”
body:
We find guilt is a large part of the burden of our past. Working Steps 8 and 9 teaches us forgiveness and relieves us of this load. Just writing out our amends list teaches us our wrongs are not limitless. Doing our inventory is a giant step to freedom because we have done something instead of just sitting on it!. It’s a step in the right direction. Because of our pasts, we have fears about the future and worry about things that have not yet happened - may happen if no action is taken. Step Nine deals with these things. Issues from our pasts tend to ruin our present. Dealing with the past also teaches us things are flexible, there is hope. We don’t know that making amends will make us feel better, but we suspect it will. The heat is off us.

By living here now in the present, we can make the absolute most of this moment. We learn to live in the solution. When we live by spiritual principles, continually doing the right thing right now, our past becomes pleasurable memories and the future begins to look golden. If we can clean up the past, there is a good chance our futures will change also.

Being clean is a paradigm shift all by itself. Even as we hover between disciplined aware of our past wrongdoings and our future in the unknowable giant question mark, we have a good feeling that grows with each passing day clean. If we can stay clean a day at a time, and work the preceding Steps, how many other good things are possible. Keep it simple and just do what you can on a daily basis. What we do first brings us improved feelings. These simple lessons don’t go out of style - they make us stronger and work even better as we build on our experience.

quote: “Slow down and live life. A cynic is someone who has been very disappointed. He does not hear until the heart decides to listen, then he can accept help. It is not shameful to relapse. The real shame is in not coming back.”
body:
“Man who lives in house that is burning down is always in a hurry.” - Gautama Buddha

Always behind, always trying to catch up up, addicts tend to rush forward even when they are not being chased. Survival skills are different when we are clean. Hopelessness and despair are replaced by positive expectation and newfound energy. Amazingly, from the first few days clean, we find a place to stay, food to eat. Soon there is a job and people look interesting in new ways. No longer victims of prey, we begin to explore our human potential. We slowly begin to find out what people are really thinking instead of endlessly projecting our thinking and attitudes onto others.

Dirty living has driven us into a deep well of cynicism. We may have to crawl up a ways before we tumble out on the ground around our pit of despair. The light and sound of being out of the pit woke us up and our cynicism faded. His - or her - heart warmed up and the sounds changed into words and he woke up to a new reality. Recovery started to mean much more than just not using. The excitement shook his world and he may have lost his footing but there was no fear of showing up at a meeting to testify that what he had been told was true. It is a disease and recovery is possible through the 12 Steps!

True members of NA are supportive of someone who comes back after a relapse whether it is for a few days or a few years. They instinctively know the courage it takes to come back. Surrender is a scary thing. Their need for recognition is great. It helps to re-enforce their decision to see recovery again through the 12 Steps of NA.

quote: “When someone returns the Fellowship after relapse, we stress the importance of living just for today. The past is the past. We cannot change what has happened. What is important is to stay clean today.”
body:
Recovery takes place with a daily decision not to use. Living just for today is stressed because it is a tool every one of us can use. It is a freedom statement that helps us step free of the life we left in active addiction.

To get recovery, we have to refocus our attention off the pain caused by addiction and onto the joy of having a new life clean. Our daily living pattern of waking up clear and clean and close to people who understand us reshapes who and what we are becoming - a clean addict. A few short decades ago, these two words could not go together. A clean addict, what a miracle!

Personality change may start with surfaces but a day at a time, it goes far deeper. A personality consists of the choices an individual makes. Personal habits don’t often change once they have become established. Personal choices in food, music, friends, lifestyles become seemingly embedded into a man or woman in their bones! To change is earth shaking. Long live the changes we experience in recovery!

quote: “We all find the feeling we get from helping others motivates us to do better in our own lives. If we are hurting, and most of us do from time to time, we learn to ask for help.”
body:
Giving help is somehow related to receiving help. Maybe it is the feelings that are similar. Maybe it is the openness. Exchange means the same door is open from the giver to the receiver.

When I am at the point where the need to give help in order to heal, sometimes we don’t have time to give help. You need to fix the problem. “I don’t have time to stop and help someone on the freeway because my utilities are going to get cut off,” and that’s the problem., So, I just stay sick because I can’t get out of my immediate concerns. This conundrum keeps us turning in lazy circles.

The very thing I need to get well, is to help somebody. My brain tells me,”You fool, you didn’t have time to help somebody.” I have to override my ordinary thinking processes to achieve the focus needed to give me relief from addiction.

The guy is late for the party, and he can’t stop and take out the map because it takes too long. I have to tell myself to stop and get help before going on. .

quote: “Recovery found [in] Narcotics Anonymous must come from within and no one gets clean for anyone but themselves.”
body:
In N A we have to cut to the chase to bridge the gaps between people and communicate. While on the surface, an onlooker may see and hear our interactions, they don’t know all that is happening below the surface. Addicts seem to catch on that they feel more potent and energetic when they are spending time with someone who is hurting. Their focus is on others. Their own problems just melt away in passing. Not all the time or all the way, but enough for us to sense the difference. Addicts track their inner feelings in a way that is similar to what they did when they were using.

In recovery, it is similar to persons in war or some kind of emergency setting where they are moving around, helping people with injuries or in need of food or shelter. Some addicts are natural caretakers. We probably take advantage of this in N A. Some addicts are very intuitive and good with feelings.

Longtimers heal when other members talk to them. Newer members draw hope and useful information from longtimers that helps a person in day to day life. Some kind of osmosis is in evident in our recovery. Interpersonal contact stabilizes addicts when they communicate. It is important that we find release from our feelings and joy in helping others.

quote: “When it gets to the point that we cannot hold the hurt any longer, amends will be made.”
body:
When time comes to make amends, often an occasion occurs that sets the stage. Events conspire and all we have to do is be willing and honest.

The Twelve Steps follow a natural sequence and move from one necessary focus to the next. The need for amends comes from inner pain, guilt and shame. Some part of us knows there is something that we, and only we, can straighten out. Whether it involves feelings, money or things, our amends brings order from the chaos. We feel better.

Those who receive our amends feel better also. Unanswered questions may produce disorder again and again for years without resolution. Putting closure on things allows everyone involved to go on with their lives.

For example, at some point we took something that wasn’t ours. The situation requires someone who ‘owned’ the stolen item, like a manager, who will wonder who took the items (s). Other innocent parties may be fired, accused, sanctioned even though they did nothing wrong. This could happen at work, in a family household and the same paradigm may fit countless scenarios.

The truth we share removes tensions, allows for restoration of whatever was taken, and everybody involved is off the hook.

Making amends will keep you clean. Stolen items may be returned anonymously. The Steps are in a certain order for a reason, however anytime we can do something positive is a good time.

quote: “Sharing our lives with others seems to keep us from feeling lonely. The Steps lead to the kind of honesty that can help us let others know and accept us.”
body:
The NA 12 Steps break us out our need for isolation. The Steps open up a new tool box for us. At first honesty may look like a bad idea. When we get to the Program, and hear others sharing their life experience, we find ourselves identifying. When a problem with the past comes up, we may feel like taking a different approach. Canceling out old personal debts frees the part of us that tracks such things.

Human beings are geared to communicate feelings, ideas and their need for help. Without this interaction, we become stifled and bored. Loneliness is a word to specify this lack of interaction. As we begin recovery by staying clean and going to meetings, we begin to take in the attitudes, feelings, problems and solutions of others who, like us, have had major problems with addiction. Suddenly we have an identity group. We are still addicts but with a difference. We are clean addicts, one among others. .

The myriad changes a person goes through are shared by our participation in NA recovery meetings. Our literature opens the door to many subjects that involve addicts who live clean and are growing spiritually. Our ideas about God and spirituality begin to change. Many doors open to us with seemingly little effort on our part. Many of us are surprised when we find ourselves helping others and repeating what we have learned in the meetings and literature.

quote: “Trusting others doesn’t come naturally to us, but we must learn to trust because an addict alone is in bad company.”
body:
At first we feel trapped by our addiction and we can’t see any way out. Our loneliness and despair have us boxed in. When we find NA, all this changes. As we share some of our feelings, we are surprised that it helps. We relate to what others are sharing and it gets even better.

Trust begins when we get to know someone, sharing problems, fears and hopes. We identify. As we get to know more group members, we begin to develop friendships and other relationships in an easy, natural way. Others trust us with their feelings and suddenly we find ourselves trusting them in return. Trust comes from honest sharing.

Active addiction showed us what ‘bad company’ will do for us. Then, in recovery, we find honesty leads us to trust one another. Of course, not everyone can be so honest but the members who stay clean usually have honesty in common.

Working with a sponsor is bound to increase our trust. The first three Steps bring us closer and the Fifth Step lets us bare our soul. The Fourth Step is almost like a rite of passage or initiation into the Fellowship of NA. For the first time we are dealing with how we have affected other people.

quote: “As we grow, we learn to overcome our tendency to run and hide our feelings. Learning to be totally honest about our feelings helps other people take us seriously.”
body:
Total honesty is pretty scary to begin with but we learn we are not alone. Hearing others share, encourages us to get it out and see what happens. Generally other members relate to us and our feelings. Having others around who are going through the same process helps us because we are not alone.

Facts and personal self-disclosure ring true to the listener and when they see us let our guard down, they are better able to share honestly with us. All this honesty is good for each one of us.

It is hard for us to share our feelings with people we are just meeting. Having addiction and a desire for recovery helps us relate to what others are going through. We realize the people we are meeting are just as nervous as we are!

This is what N A is all about! This is the process that makes N A attractive and effective. Honesty helps us be open minded and willing to try. Many of us have been told we have to ‘feel our feelings. Blocking our feelings is what led to our isolation.

quote: “What happens instead is, the more we improve our conscious contact with God through prayer and meditation, the more often we pause when doubtful and say,”God I don’t know what to do. Please teach me.””
body:
NA is a spiritual movement based on the facts of our experience. Our prayers are answered and conscious contact becomes a part of what we do daily. The Second Step deals with our insanity by encouraging us to find a belief we can trust with our lives. One of the most advanced spiritual statements on the planet is, “All we want to know is what you want to do about your problem and how we can help.”

Conscious contact is the basis for our spiritual growth in N A. This contact it is not elaborated on in terms of one religious belief or method of contact. Every person’s spiritual belief is left to the individual member. Because of this, many addicts have been able to find a spiritual life that works for them and find a unity of good feeling about a higher power. .

This freedom of encouraging our members to find a belief that works for them has opened the doorway to recovery. An open-ended prayer like the one quoted from the Grey Book seems to work for everybody, regardless of their personal belief system.

What happens when we say the prayer for help can be amazing. For many of us a solution forms almost right away. Something simple, so simple we could have thought of it ourselves but the questions is would we have found the solution on our own? For most of us, we know how often we have been baffled and confused only to find that with God’s help, things work out for the good of all.

quote: “We seek creativity through the spirit. This spirit dwells within all of us. We need to understand that the spirit within each individual is the same. The action being that we tap the available resources of the spirit within ourselves. Some have tapped more than others. We need to create more soul searching within one another. This process is the creative action of the spirit. We listen to ourselves and others in order to recover and to stay on that path.”
body:
NA is a spiritual, not religious program of recovery from addiction. You could say recovery from being hung up on things of the world. Many people live out their lives in fear. Faith put first things first and allows an average person the ability to create good fortune through positive action and good attitude. Recovery can mean much more than simple abstinence though it begins there. We have a tremendous need for spiritual learning.

Revelations come to us in God’s time, not ours. We cannot demand revelations, we must patiently wait for them to be revealed. One member asked his higher power what would make my life better and their higher power said, “Start going to a fluff and fold laundry, quit doing it yourself. You hate it!”

It is this sameness of spirit that makes N A a universal program. Our Steps and Principles have been applied and utilized in every part of the globe. Our original literature was written to include all people who might seek recovery in N A, regardless of their national language, history, religion or other factors that might hinder or limit their approach to N A recovery. Time has proven this hope to be true.

quote: “Pride, fear, and procrastination often seem an impossible barrier and stand in our way of progress and growth through the Ninth Step.”
body:
A thorn in our foot will keep hurting until it is removed. We may even fear the pain of pulling out the thorn and put off removal until it begins to fester and redden to the point of blood poisoning. One thing is sure: once the source of the pain is removed, the healing process begins. The same thing happens with various kinds of disorders stemming for our past behavior. Any form of meanness and wishing ill to others comes back on us to blindside us with new problems.

Pride keeps us from taking a close look at something that bothers us, even though it took place sometime in our past. It should be mentioned that we are paying a price for something that could be resolved by a simple, honest change of heart. In recovery when we realize, that change of heart comes pretty easy compared to the hell we went through getting to our point of willingness.

Fear is the same problem, we pay a price, getting nothing in return. Once we decide to inventory a feeling that’s been bothering us, we may even put it off some more!

Another valid factor is the possibility of retaliation. Talking with our sponsor is often a good idea before putting ourselves, and those who depend on us, at risk. Step Nine spells out “except when to do so would injure them or others.” You are one of the others - you count too!

quote: “Lack of spiritual quality within our lives leads us down the path to relapse. This part of our recovery is the essence of the program. Spiritual maintenance is indispensable if recovery is what we’re after.”
body:
Many addicts are so wounded by past experience with any sort of spirituality, they try to work their 12 Steps on the mental plane. This may in fact work for some if they are totally sincere. Others get caught on a semantic treadmill and without a belief in a power greater than one’s self, it is hard to imagine how someone could complete the course of the 12 Steps under their own power.

The purpose of a sponsor is to guide us through the Steps. When we share what we are doing in our Step work - or not doing, a sponsor can make a few well worded, gentle comments that help us move forward. Listening to others share is the primary service we offer in N A recovery. We really listen and care!

Seeking knowledge of God’s Will for us and the strength to carry out that Will is the basis for a spiritual life. Our disease still crops up at the oddest times. It is an old saying in the program of NA that we grow more from a bad day than from a day when everything goes our way. Dealing with life on life’s terms steadily increases our ability to trust and seek help from the God of our understanding.

As addicts, most of us are masters at getting material things, like a new car on crushing monthly payments. This comes from our well honed skill at going out and coping a bag of dope somehow. But when it comes to maintenance, most are miserable failures. We don't change the oil on time, don't rotate the tires and run late on our payments. This spiritual maintenance can be very challenging for us. Praying on our knees every night may be one of the hardest things we have ever done, but such prayer may be critical to our survival.

quote: “Living just for today relieves the burden of the past and alleviates fear of the future.”
body:
We find guilt is a large part of the burden of our past yet contains the wisdom of our experience. Working Steps Eight and Nine teaches us forgiveness and relieves us of this load. We have fears about the future and worry about things that have not yet happened. This tends to ruin our present.

By living here now in the present, we can make the absolute most of this moment. We learn to live in the solution. When we live by spiritual principles, continually doing the right thing now, our past becomes pleasurable memories and helpful experience. The future becomes an opportunity to find new good things that work for us. If we have problems, we use our spiritual principles to get the help we need. Writing is a form of prayer!.

The present is what our HP gives us to work with. The past can serve as a data bank for what works and what does not. Also, we have had some successes to build on. Humility, patience, tolerance and perseverance give us advantages we could not see before we experienced them. Helping others has a funny way of encouraging us to take our own recommendations.

Curiously, as we grow, our remembrances of the past seem to change. They don’t really change, but our perception is altered by our spiritual growth. In the same way, our perception of possible or likely futures shift towards good outcomes and exciting breakthroughs.

quote: “We, of Narcotics Anonymity, do not promise to have all the answers, but we’ve found that our lives steadily improved if we didn’t use and learned to maintain our spiritual condition. ”
body:
Recovery is what happens to us when we are clean, go to meetings and take time to help others. Whether new or well established in recovery, just by caring we are able to see into the lives of others. This helps reshape our own living attitudes and capacity to surrender. Life comes in a isolation walks out the door!

By trusting the program of N A, we discover recovery. By living differently, we change what we attract and what we involve ourselves with on a daily basis. We can just walk past side alleys and dead-end streets where we used to spend a great deal of our precious time.

Sometimes recovery in Narcotics Anonymous consists of long plateaus where it seems we are not really getting ahead. But these are followed by periods of intense growth with doors opening, opportunities presenting themselves and gifts being showered upon us. We have to change our hook-ups before we can plug into good new situations.

It can be difficult to just keep going to meetings in the plateau stretches and accepting that these times are the foundation for the coming growth periods. Thing about it! By definition, we need to be in a good, safe place to allow these changes to happen. While the changes occur, it may helps to reflect on our life is really pretty good! We’re just not ready for all this good treatment.

quote: “By nature we seem to be strong-willed, self-centered people, seeking self-gratification in the realms of money, power, and sex. As important part of our recovery is learning how to live with these drives; how to realign our misguided instincts, how to stop acting out our insanities, how to disarm our self-destruct mechanisms, and how to re-channel our energies toward constructive ends.”
body:
As human beings we want to be productive, effective and well liked by the people we come in contact with. The problems come in when we are willing to use lies, deceit and thievery to achieve these ends. Honesty, openmindedness and the willingness to try seem impractical and sometimes, unworkable principles to apply to real life on life’s terms. But since we heard about it in meetings, we sometimes go ahead not really expecting them to work for us. When they do, we get this little grim on our faces like we knew it all along.

The money, power, sex, food, gambling and codependency can actually cause some to go back out. If we can ask for help, our sponsor can help us with these things. But we have to open up and talk to him or her about them. There are solutions to all of these problems. We are not the first to have to face difficulties and we will not be the last.

It may help to recall just how hopeless and beaten down we were when we got here. Addictive disease has a way of sugarcoating the bad times and making the using days colorful and exciting. Actually, they were dull and depressing, with no way out in sight! Reading recovery literature and sharing honestly about our past insanity helps keep us honest and real about our need for help.

If we are blessed, we may find ourselves continuing to pursue spiritual growth and learning year after year as our clean time grows. One of the greatest gurus of all time once said, “I want to eat sugar. I don’t want to be sugar.” It is ok to enjoy things of the world. We just learn to avoid the rattlesnakes and black widow spiders.

quote: “One of our sayings is “Our best ideas got us here.” This seems apt as we look back and see how many times our schemes and plans got us into trouble despite their original intent.”
body:
Some of the worst times in our past using days began with good intentions. But the disease of lies presented a false picture of reality to us and we found ourselves in over our heads before we knew what was happening to us. In recovery we learn that this is the nature of our self- destructiveness. Nobody can make us blindly plunge ahead like the disease of addiction under the influence and control of some mind and mood altering chemistry.

The mental plane has to do with the words and logic centered in the left lobe of our brains. It is very pushy and seeks always to have its way. Rationalizations, selfishness and illogical justifications pave the way to wasting time and energy on bad ideas instead of taking a few moments to pray and meditate before jumping into a fire. Act in haste and repent at your leisure.

The spiritual way of life gives us time to consider other people and outcomes that may not have been visible in the rush to jump on a good idea. For instance, some of us wait a day or a week if we want to buy something, knowing even though we have the money, we may not really need the thing or have a place to put it! Clean time gives us people to talk things over with someone who may be affected by our actions. Sometimes they will advise us against something where we eventually go ahead with our original impulse. This is because we are free. The time of asking and waiting doesn’t cost us much and if we decide to go ahead, we have some vision beyond our immediate needs and wants.

quote: “The Eleventh Step helps us, in the face of a problem, to be aware of God. The underlying principle of this Step is God consciousness. We try not asking for specific things.”
body:
Spirituality leads us on a path that goes up a mountain of energy that underlies visible God. There are many paths up the mountain but only one top. A very wise teacher taught all he could reach that nations that believed in God should never go to war with one another. Addictions is a war within where nobody ever wins.

One member could not meditate. He prayed daily simply stating that he could not meditate. A couple of years later, he went to work for a very advanced meditator who paid most of the fee to go to a meditation organization and the training class. Now he has been meditating for almost 30 years. The key was that he never asked to go to a meditation class, he simply stated that he was stuck. HP heard and worked out a perfect solution.

Most members share that meditation gives them a time out to let their attachments to things of the world drop away long enough to keep their conscious contact shiny. Internal resources accumulate over time and these principles and methods lead to sound courses of action. Internal resources are evidence that the Program of NA works.

quote: “In our daily lives, we are subject to emotional, environmental, intellectual, mental and spiritual relapses, causing us to become defenseless against the physical relapse of addiction.”
body:
Most members of NA who have been clean for a while know that a spiritual relapse precedes physical relapse. Justifying anger, character assassination and harming others is a reflection of what is going on inside. Recovering addicts learn to track their feelings and mental states to ward off relapse. It is not that hard but it is that important.

Regular meeting attendance pulls us back from the abyss. Often we do not even know how close to the edge we actually are, but meetings heal us, center us and bring us back. Meetings are no substitute for the other tools, but they are quick, handy and very effective when we are grumpy and don’t want to work Steps or work with others. Never underestimate the power of going to a meeting.

If we have worked our First Step, we know the connection between our using and our pain. Using will hurt like hell if we fall into one of the many traps it lays for us, all pretty and tasty looking on top and steel jaws below. Want to feel lower than the sidewalk? You know the way. Want to feel joy and hopeful? These is a way called NA!

quote: “The unconditional love we experience will rejuvenate our will to live and each positive move on our part will be matched by an unexpected opportunity.”
body:
Many members get clean, stay clean and begin to work the 12 Steps of N A only to get bogged down in details and forget the larger picture recovery brings to light. Recovery is about freedom and the help comes from many sources which have common denominators like those who help us share our feelings and courage. They respect what we are doing because they have gone or are going through the same things themselves.

No one is going to come to our apartment and give us a job. We have to get up off the couch and go look for work. The magical thing we have found in recovery is that once we put our bodies in motion, all manner of assistance starts to show up. The first man to conquer Mount Everest speaks of this.

Sometimes it is very hard for us to get motivated. We lack inertia, that thing that puts us into action. Either we have too many fears or doubts, or we are just plain lazy from our past life of doing nothing except getting and using and finding ways and means to get more. For that, we had a ton of energy, right?

The 7th Step tells us “rebellion is a character defect that robs us here. We need not lose faith, rebellion can be overcome by persistent effort.” This is the key. When we have no willingness to move forward, we try to take baby steps in the direction we need to go and we will get there. A baby step for doing step work is buying a pencil. Setting out a place in our home that is quiet. Buying a Step guide etc. Even a very, very, very small willingness to move forward maybe all we need to get the job done.
moment: Just for tonight: I know in my heart that God is right on the other side of willingness. I will take baby steps and more to move into the solution today.

quote: “Finally, throughout our lives, we surround ourselves with fellow members who continue to let us know we can count on them. One of the advantages of the N.A. program is that is (it) places us in an intimate, regular contact with recovering addicts who can most understand and help us in our recovery.”
body:
As newcomers we, sometimes fearfully, share our problems and feel a deep kinship with our predecessors as they share their actual experience and solutions to our problems.

Today, secure in the love of the fellowship… as the literature so aptly states.. we can look another person in the eye and be grateful for who we are. Once we surrender to the NA way of life, we create a life connected to other members. We often find spouses in recovery. We spend a great deal of time with our sponsors than latter with our sponsees. We tend to go out with members to social events. We begin to see other members as part of our family. For many of us, we had very dysfunctional families or our families had given up on us a long time ago. We are at peace with other members as they truly understand who and what we are and they speak our language every day, the language of the heart.

As newcomers we, sometimes fearfully, share our problems and feel a deep kinship with our predecessors as they share their actual experience and solutions to our problems. We come to rely on their wisdom as we learn that following the direction of those who came before us make so much sense as they have already gone through the trials and tribulations of getting clean and are more than happy to pass this hard-won experience onto us at no cost whatsoever.
moment: Just for tonight; I will trust the saying that in NA there are no strangers, only old friends we’ve never met. I will make a family here in recovery that loves me and will be there for me no matter what. As long as I am doing the right things for the right reasons and even when I am not.

quote: “A person who has realized their powerlessness and seen the vision the Higher Power has for them will see why we pray only for knowledge of his will for us and the power to carry it out.”
body:
Learning spirituality involves study, discussion and listening to what others have to say. Spiritual principles may be based in a Higher Consciousness but the nuts and bolts are learned just like we learn everything else. We hear others share or read something that strikes us in a meaningful way and we try it out to see if it will work for us. Mostly we are amazed to find simple sounding solutions have great power to heal and instruct.

The Basic Text says, in Step 11, God’s will for us is but a fleeting glimpse of our own true will for ourselves. We come to realize over time that our Higher Power only wants the very best for us, nothing more and nothing less.

Over time we have come to finally understand, after learning the preceding steps, that our job is to get out of the way so God can do his work in our lives. The biggest problem we have is putting our hands in to it and screwing it up. We see that true deep spirituality comes to us when we learn to trust God, clean house and help others. Once we do our preceding step work, we come to a place of calm resignation that there is a loving God and that he/she is taking care of us way better than we ever could by ourselves.
moment: Just for tonight: There is a Japanese Haiku or short meaningful poem that states: “In the beginner's mind there are many questions, in the masters but few”

quote: “The structure of Narcotics Anonymous is quite unlike any health or welfare agency known to us.
