Although N.A. practices may vary from place to place, all N.A. services are performed voluntarily by the addicts themselves and without cost. N.A. does not accept money for its services, is not funded by any public or private sources of agencies and accepts no outside contributions.”
body:
Scientists can explain how to land a rocket on a planet millions of miles away. They can tell you how the body works, how diamonds are formed and so on. But they still have no idea why and how addicts get clean when they never wanted to before they came to NA? Treatment centers don’t have the answer to recovery from the disease of addiction, that is why they send addicts to us.

You cannot buy a miracle! We have found through a long period of trial and error that our messages comes through loud and clear in meetings or private conversation. When persons outside N.A. try to produce our writings using academic techniques, it comes through crippled or distorted. We don’t want insincerity or lofty thinkers taking pity on us. It is the identification and respect we share among our fellow members that brings our recovery process to life. Our message of hope and recovery is priceless. Whether in H&I, P.I., Literature or other services, it is our common bond that amazes the newcomer and holds their rapt attention.

The therapeutic value of one addict helping another truly is without parallel. For some unknown reason, a completely close-minded using addict will stop and listen to what an experienced member has to say to them. They may not heed our warnings right away but they will never, ever, ever deny that we are right when we tell them our truths; “One is too many, a thousands never enough” “if you don’t pick it up, you can’t get loaded,” “An addict alone is in bad company.”
moment: Just for tonight: I will remember that I may be the only copy of the Basic Text that a newcomer sees, I will act accordingly and responsibly remembering that newcomer once was me.

quote: “Many books have been written about the nature of addiction. This book primarily concerns itself with the nature of recovery. (There are no line numbers at top of page.)”
body:
How many times have we heard members share that the Basic Text told their whole life story, it was amazing! The members who wrote our book prayed every time they sat down to write or edit it. This spiritually based work allowed them to write what we have come to call a “faith- based” book rather than a “fear-based” one. They wrote what was true in their hearts regardless of what others said could or could not be done. They spoke their experiences of recovering from the disease of addiction and the book rings true to this day. The Basic Text has been translated into countless languages and everywhere it goes, addicts get and stay clean! That is a powerful piece of literature!

Many of us who got clean in the 1970’s knew from experience that outside interests have little information that we could use. There were and are many modes to treat the addictive personality. Many theories on what addiction is and methods of treatment abounded but we got nothing from them.

NA began to grow when addicts began to take up for ourselves, our message and our program. Our Traditions allow us to do things together that might otherwise result in conflicts and stalemates. One of the great benefits of our program is that we are not dependent on outside organizations for funding or direction. We are fully self-supporting through our own contributions and have only to seek a loving God in our group conscience for direction.

If not for the members in the early 80’s who decided to make a stand in NA by only going to NA meetings, using NA language and standing up for the concept of “one disease, one program”, NA too may have gone by the wayside as another failed experiment in the long line of useless modalities to treat the addictive personality, sigh.
moment: Just for tonight; “I know that NA works and I am proud to be an ambassador for recovery in Narcotics Anonymous!”

quote: “We had a lot of energy, but we channeled it into self-destruction.”
body:
We now have a choice and can choose to channel our energy into things that bring us ongoing happiness and joy. The more we do this, the more we feel good about our recovery. If we are not busy making these choices, we will probably get ungrateful and begin the old whining and ingratitude that characterized our active addiction. Eventually we will say, “I am clean but I haven’t changed any.” Start over today! Freedom means we don’t have to languish in our confusion and misery. We do the best we can on a daily basis and that leads us into our happy destiny!

The Basic Text states: “It is the nature of the addict to tear down” For many of us, when we got here, we weren’t sick and tired, we were just plain tired. Our ego’s once so large had been crushed by active addiction. We had no fight left. It is in this state of willingness that we call the gift of desperation, we finally come to feel in our heart-of-hearts what surrender truly means.
moment: Just for tonight: I will remember that “if I always think what I always thought, I will always feel what I always felt and if I always feel what I always felt, I will always do what I always did and if I always do what I always did, I will always get what I always got!” I will surrender and fight now more.

quote: “As a disease, addiction is subject to relapse. Reverting to using is a physical relapse. Physical relapse is on a symptom. We are never forced into relapse. We are given a choice.”
body:
Many - if not every single one of us - have to get into the habit of praying and meditating, whatever level we are on. Contact with other members inside and outside our meetings keeps us on our toes recovery-wise. Honest sharing keeps us aware of the dangers of thinking we have it made.

Relapse begins when we start getting comfortable and cutting corners in our recovery program. True, clean time builds up a sort of protection against relapse, but why abuse ourselves with a slide down hill? Surrender isn’t so bad. It just takes a little hope to keep us going in the right direction. Target sometimes you love and care about. Real growth comes when we’re having fun! Take a minute to think of something good in your life due to recovery. If we aren’t grateful for what we have, how do we think we’ll be grateful for what we want?

Poor mental habits contribute to relapse. Allowing ourselves to become needlessly angry at someone is bad for us because we identify with other members. If we are mad at them, we may also be mad at the part of ourselves that identifies with them. Resentments are sent and resent until we get the message we are not dealing with. As long as we put it off, it will be resent. When we deal with it, it stops being a problem. We add to our collection of things we have gained in recovery. We could not always do this before N A. Now we can. If we feel like it is too much for us, we can share our problem in general terms with our home group - or in specific terms with our close friends and sponsor.

Our choice is to pray for strength and guidance. That opens the door to our getting the help and support we need. Trying to deal with things we are powerless over is impossible by definition. Asking for help attracts the very people who want to help and it only takes one of them to have our answer!

quote: “Stagnation or just plain old character defects which we re-cultivate rather than let go of sends us back to where these defects were conceived.”
body:
A sponsor told his sponsee, “we repeat all the time…. This too shall pass, this too shall pass.”. he said, no “This too gets buried!” We put a Band-Aid on it and push it back down only for it to come up over and over again. He said; the answer is “This too shall dissolve”. It’s gone, it’s healed. When I write an inventory it’s no longer there as it is no longer an issue in my life.”

For most of us, our defects of character were formed in our childhood. What we thought were assets that kept us somewhat sane in an insane environment, no longer work for us. They were probably born as we were either growing up or on the streets. They were merely our defense mechanisms. We would be overly people-pleasing or put on a ‘don’t fuck with me’ face to keep people away. We would blame others for all that was wrong in our lives so we never had to take responsibility for anything, ever. As the Basic Text states: “Defects are those things that caused us pain and misery all our lives.” Hello! Why would we want to hang onto something that caused us pain and misery? Why, because we used our defense mechanisms for 20 to 30 to 40 years and now we are asked to let them go and walk in faith, trusting the process that has worked for countless others.

One addict told his sponsor, “I can’t stop being negative, I’ve been that way all my life.” His sponsor responded, “Yes, and I shoot heroin.” The sponsee looked quizzically at his sponsor and said, “No you don’t!” “Exactly,” said the sponsor, “If God can relieve me from sticking a needle in my arm every day, he can certainly relieve you of a character defect eh?”
moment: Just for tonight; “as my sponsor shared “the Steps are like Exlax, if you put them in one at a time, in order, the results come out the other side every single time.” I will trust the process and surrender my defects of character.

quote: “Addicts respond instinctively to honest sharing.”
body:
We have heard so many lies we pay little attention to what goes on around us. We stumbled into an N A meeting skeptical of anything we heard although we did feel something that piqued our attention at least for a moment. So we returned to another meeting not knowing why. It may have been the second or fourth meeting, we heard some truth. Instinctively, the truth attracted us and we began listening closer. The closer we listened, the more truth we heard. The more truths we heard, the more hope we felt.

These truths were starting to stick together into something we could wrap our minds around. It was when our minds found enough evidence to believe that our hearts started feeling soothed. Now we can surrender at least a little of our lives into our process. We find ourselves practicing honesty too and feeling good about these changes. What reaches the heart, touches the heart and nothing reaches the heart like honest sharing of common pain and the solutions that free us.
moment: Just for tonight: “The truth will set me free, but first it will probably piss me off.” If other addicts can accept the hard-cold-truth, so can I.

quote: “In our daily lives, we are subject to emotional, environmental, intellectual, mental and spiritual relapses, causing us to become defenseless against the physical relapse of addiction.”
body:
“Resentment is the number one offender” the chapter on Recovery and Relapse states. It is in times of despair that using seems to be a tempting idea. When we feel that no one understands us or how we in our current plight. We hammer away thinking “they” did this and “they” need to be held accountable! Resentment is like drinking poison and waiting for the other person to die!

If we have worked our First Step, we know the connection between our using and our pain. Using will hurt like hell if we fall into one of the many traps it lays for us, all pretty and tasty looking on top and steel jaws below. Want to feel lower than the sidewalk? You know the way. Want to feel joy and hopeful? There is a way called NA!

Someone once said, “Anger is too luxurious a feeling to waste on someone we don’t even like.” When I am in my head too long I know that I need to get with my sponsor or get to a meeting to share what’s going on between my ears. As the book says, “Honest sharing is the antidote to my diseased thinking.”.
moment: Just for tonight; I will rat on my disease and let another member know when I am in pain or thinking about using. This simple act can save my life and keep me from the bitter ends; “jails institutions or death!”

quote: “The Second Step is the most important thing that must happen for us to achieve any sort of ongoing recovery.”
body:
One sponsor said to his sponsee, “If there isn’t a power greater than you, we are all in a world of shit!” Surrender is just the beginning of the process of recovery. We have come to understand that if we are not getting direction from some other source, we are managing an unmanageable life. right? Even if it is just believing in a sponsor, or the literature it is probably for the first time in our lives that we are coming to believe something outside of ourselves could restore us to sanity. It is not brave to walk down the street with a few large friends and it is not cowardice to crouch down in an alleyway with large, hulking figures moving towards us in a hostile manner. Having a Higher Power to help out, only a breath of prayer away, changes our situation in a wonderful way. It most certainly does not have to be a deity. It is simply the idea that we, on our own, cannot manage an unmanageable life. Belief in our Higher Power gives us the ability to meet the needs of life head on.

Belief tends to define our boundaries and what we are willing to be open to in our personal reality. If we can only find something to believe in, we can firm up our hope for a new life. Hope is the cure for depression. For most of us, it is pretty obvious that the Fellowship of Narcotics Anonymous as a whole is most certainly a power greater than ourselves because they are staying clean a day at a time and we have been abject failures at it on our own.
moment: Just for tonight: If I am truly powerless and my life is truly unmanageable, I am willing to seek help from others in NA. I will turn my will and life over to the care of my sponsor and learn the NA way of life. It’s the best decision I could ever make.

quote: “We as addicts, suffer from a problem deeper than the drugs we took. Because of this, we must live by spiritual principles. Step Ten and all the Steps are to be applied to every area of our lives.”
body:
Starting with some basic touchstones of recovery. we learn about how to view the stages of recovery. While one stage blends into the next and all stages are linked up, we clearly start out as newcomers who must learn how we get along in the Fellowship. This stage is not a permanent label confining us to a ‘less-than’ status. When we experience some of the pink cloud and early lessening of pressure that comes from surrender, the joy of mutual support can make us almost manic. We all know addiction is a limiting and life threatening disease and yet the good feelings are important to our learning and growing also. As we go through these processes we often enjoy helping someone else who is just beginning recovery road.

The Basic Text says; “We thought we needed something and we thought we had found it in drugs” This tells us we had a lacking long before we even picked up. One member was listening to an NA know-it-all who was sharing from the podium “recovery is to take you back to where you were before you started using drugs!” and my friend thought to himself “if I was taken back to where I was before I started using drugs, I’D START USING DRUGS!

As one member shared; “Drugs weren’t my problem, they were my solution. It was when my solution became my problem that I was really in trouble! We have come to understand that drugs were merely a symptom of our disease and that we had an obsessive, compulsive self- centered personality long before we ever picked up drugs.

This is why NA is not a one or two Step program, don’t use and help others. We need to heal that which made us want to use in the first place. It is only by working the 12 Steps that we can get past the pain of active addiction to the real pain of our younger years where the real healing can be done. Only then can we truly be able to help others find their way back to their lonely lost child so they too can heal completely. If we stopped growing when we started using, we being with the emotions of a teenager.
moment: Just for tonight: “I refuse to give NA and the 12 steps a half-hearted attempt. I will give it all I’ve got and get the wonderful results recovery has to offer. I will trust the Basic Text when it says; “Not only is this life better than the hell we lived it is better than any life we could ever imagine!”

quote:
REDUNDANT - BUT ADDED NEW ESSAYS

“As a disease, addiction is subject to relapse. Reverting to using is a physical relapse. Physical relapse is only a symptom. We are never forced into relapse. We are given a choice.”
body:
Maintaining our spiritual condition gives us a set of opportunities to catch ourselves before we fall over the edge. Low and high impact events may knock us temporarily off balance. Our disease is a tendency, often subtle and conniving, to set us up for a fall rather than pushing us.

When we find ourselves making excuses for not working our program is usually a good clue to remind us of what is happening. Justification our resentments is one. Allowing ourselves to wallow in self-pity over things we cannot change is another. Not praying for help is yet another warning sign. Blaming others when things go wrong rather than taking personal responsibility only complicates difficult situations. Dropping home group attendance or failing to stay in touch with our NA friends can turn into isolation.

Physical relapse is a final step in a series of life threatening and life limiting events. Learning to watch out for these and reminding ourselves that we are not out to ‘get good’ or live up to the expectations of others. We simply want to find a form of happiness we can live with on a daily basis.

quote: “To us, at first, it feels like insanity to give another person knowledge of our pain. As we become more closely involved with others we will learn to share our pain and it will lessen. Part of the horror of addiction is being cut off from this human experience. Our fears and guilt kept us from receiving the benefits afforded to every day people.”
body:
Isolation keeps us from the love and attention that would give us the support we need to face life. It makes a big difference in how we process the events and concerns that come with daily life. Someone who is in this sort of isolation is unable to see, hear, touch or feel our love and caring. They can even repeat the words we say without taking in the meaning. What isolates us is our shame and guilt We feel unworthy and our sense of right and wrong works against us. Trouble is, we see ourselves as guilty of wrongdoing and begin to carry out a personalized punishment program, P-P-P.

With this attitude and viewpoint, we do not want to take on the anger and condemnation of someone else who is being nice to us. We are sure if they knew the truth about us, they would push us away. Our isolation and lack of internal resources prevents us from opening up to their care and concern. The situation is worsened because in many cases, their concern may be justified. It takes someone who has had similar experience to really help us find the path they took to self acceptance and forgiveness.

You can’t find good support hiding in a closet. You have to gather yourself up and shuffle out the door to get to where there are people who might identify and make few suggestions.

quote: “Before coming to the fellowship of N.A., we were irresponsible with our lives. We used drugs to cover up our humiliation, guilt, inferiority and feelings of inadequacy.”
body:
Blinking into the new life of living clean, we are able to make simple choices. We stabilize physically and emotionally. Our contact with insane individuals is eliminated and in their place we have friends who see life in positive colors and solution based living patterns. A lot of the upsets just literally disappear! Without these upsets, we are left in a clear-headed state where we take care of our business and take the rest of the day to enjoy our lives. The drugs we used to escape our problems complicated our problems.

Clean, we settle down to just live at first, then find our real likes and preferences. We are no longer dragged down with fear and guilt. Some of this comes from the meetings and free association with clean addicts but many things just come naturally. Our bodies have been designed over millions of years to live on this planet. We have survived incredible hardships, weather and diseases.

Most of us practice some sort of wake-up ritual, reading literature, listening to recovery tapes or CD’s to get our day off to a good start. It is vitally important that we keep our focus on recovery. We look for things that work in recovery if we are going to experience the benefits of N A! The simplest things can make so much difference in the way our days go. Just missing a few meetings can cause us to begin to experience the feelings we associate with our disease. Resentments crop up over very small issues. We snap at co-workers and spouses. Just a member calling us can put us back in a better place.”

quote: “There is no area of our lives that the disease does not affect. The truth is that fear is present in every area of our lives. So, in our experience, the program can and must be worked continuously in every area of our lives.”
body:
Just like our blood feeds and cleans our entire body as a natural part of circulation, what we learn in one area is applied in many others. And where we find fault with ourselves, we find the same need for correction in many other areas. Each instance of personal growth benefits the whole of our lives on a continuous basis.

Honesty may keep us out of jail but it also frees us from habits of caution and negative anticipation based on fear. Honesty lowers the stress. Fear of punishment for guilt that comes with whatever wrong thing we have done.

Humility gets our ego out of the way and lets us enjoy the clean life God gives us. We don’t have to worry about getting credit for things. We can be happy and glad when friends do well. Humility is awareness that our HP works through principles that can be learned and practiced in everyday life. Being openminded allows us access to information that helps tune us into reality.

Owning our willingness to try usually means we don’t have to do things in many cases. If we are not willing, we may be forced against our will.

quote: “We choose not to have presidents, masters, chairmen or directors. Instead we have secretaries, treasurers, and representatives .These titles in themselves imply service rather than control.”
body:
Jailhouse lawyers, inexperienced persons and those who speak without thinking or praying, may miss the point here. Start with personal powerlessness. A surrendered person would want help and listen to what others have to say. Someone with a tender ego may see everything in terms of what will make them seem important or look good. This is a distorted misperception. Egotistical perceptions make problems from the get-go.

We care about all our members and practice in decision making and group activities builds a person’s self-confidence and the skills we need for interpersonal exchanges.

In recovery, we move from attending a meeting to being a part of a group. As we attend several meetings, our circle of friends and acquaintances grows. None of this is by accident. Many of our group conscience processes do double duty by informing members at large while establishing and maintaining principled processes as well as spiritual principles. Good ideas that don’t work in real life and dismissed in favor of practical solutions. That is why so much of our literature is based on success in living instead of great ideas.

Proud egos and credit taking mentality sets peoples at odds with one another. When a group sees someone in a position of service and trust acting out of petty rivalry or self-interest, it makes it hard for them to stick with principles. That is the value of ‘trusted servants.’ Many of you may recall from the earlier history of NA. Proud or arrogant people in service were called “thrusted servants.’ Humor, gratitude and growing insight into the nature of things sets us free, slowly or eventually. It’s not so bad to go through tough time as long as we learn more about living clean.

quote: “For the newcomer this is how they find out about N A and how they stay clean; and for the members this reaffirms and clarifies what they have learned.”
body:
Sharing and caring are both active and positive expressions of our love for others. When we see the Steps and principles working for others, we get curious. Seeing things that work in real life confirms and strengthens our growth. Some principles may seem a little prissy in the beginning. After racking up some personal experience, our respect rises. Simple words keep people with addiction alive! We learn to live all over again or for the first time!

As we continue going to meetings and experiencing NA culture, new experiences and learnings will supply more food for thought and we are propelled forward into new discoveries. Real recovery is a stream of ‘aha’ moments. We discover new ways of seeing our world that just were not possible a short time before. Long held beliefs grow deeper and wider as we grow in our clean time. Honesty, openmindedness and the willingness to try become ever more applicable as time and experience accumulates.

This is important: what was seen as true in the beginning of our recovery is found to have ever wider application. While they are just as true a few years later, they have more uses and amazing new combinations. Honesty is important in the present tense. Openmindedness lets us see further ahead as we are growing. The willingness to try opens the world for us to try on a daily basis!

quote: “What would happen if our groups had other primary purposes? We feel our message would be diluted and then lost.”
body:
Part of the magic of NA is when we actively carry our message, we get the message. It’s like what comes out of our mouths goes into our ears! Life on planet earth has plenty of distractions. It seems like our society is constantly making bigger and better distractions! Sometimes we devolve rather than evolve!

The big three are money, property and prestige. Add sex, security and personal recognition and you’ll realize they are all related. Recovery involves staying alive and developing the quality of our lives!

In the 1980’s NA in San Francisco was asked by the Haight Ashbury Free Clinic to pass out needle exchange flyers in our meetings. We politely declined. To do so would have implied that we were advocates of needle exchange, possibly involved with the Haight Ashbury Free Clinic and were advocating IV drug use. The vote was unanimous. No, we would not! Some addicts are so addicted to the needle they have been known to shoot up orange juice.

quote: “An atmosphere of recovery in our groups is one of our most precious assets; and we must guard it carefully lest we lose it to politics and personalities.”
body:
If each one of us prays to relinquish their ego and self-will, the atmosphere of recovery will result. Honest sharing and personal disclosure set the tone for other members to follow suit. If there are people in the rooms who do not share or pickup on our message of hope and personal growth through the 12 Steps of NA, if throws our focus off recovery and our atmosphere of recovery will go ‘poof,’ up in smoke.

Addicts in recovery know how important the atmosphere of recovery is. Our lives and well-being depend on it. This atmosphere makes it socially acceptable to get real and honest about our personal experience, past and present. The reality of recovery beats any movie or tv show! Against the backdrop of our past lives in active addiction, the miracles of recovery really stand out.

Addiction will always come with some way to glorify using. Addiction will misrepresent the happiness, joy and freedom we experience in living the clean life. Early recovery is an adjustment to doing things while in a clean state. Grocery shopping, dancing, planning an outing or vacation clean brings home the reality of our altered state of existence.

Neuroscientists are finally pointing out that our consciousness is really all we personally possess. In recovery material, we have mentioned the drug-induced fog that clouds our thinking as we begin to experience clean living. A clear mind and good attitude makes all the difference in our daily lives! Be grateful!

If people do not want to be in the rooms, it puts a chill on recovery and the healing process ends. This may cause people to clam up and later use over bottled up emotions. We must safeguard our meetings to carry our message.

quote: “After coming to the Narcotics Anonymous Fellowship, we find ourselves among a very special group of people. Many were a great deal worse off than we were. Here we found hope.”
body:
Hope is the only spiritual principle that can be given. All other principles we must develop from within. Hope lets us know the program is working for us as it has worked for others. At each step in the process of recovery, we feel relief. Alternatives appear out of thin air and we find solutions that give us awareness that we are no longer trapped in a dead end.

When we attend meetings and see other addicts smiling and enjoying life in recovery, our spirit is renewed. This is the hope we share, the therapeutic value of one addict helping another. We must be careful not to quench the hope of others. Newfound hope can be a very fragile and must be encouraged and reinforced with positive suggestions and observations. Stepping on someone’s experience or suggestion is almost always wrong. Addicts are quick to see relationships between words and can relate subjects to recovery if they are paying attention..

Nobody hurt like we did. You know, that sharp burning pain. In the rooms we hear personal experiences shared and are sometimes shocked to realize there are people who had it worse than we did! If we engaged in embarrassing sexual activities, there is always a new wrinkle on that subject. If we killed or participated in someone’s death, we could always stayed at it and did even more horrible things. If we stole money or merchandise, there are always bigger and badder thieves out there and many find a home in our meetings. Recovery is for people who want a change and are willing to follow certain Steps to get it.

In the early days of NA, if you wanted to take out an NA girl, you had to first have a sit down. Your sponsor, her sponsor and the rest of the oldtimers would all meet and question your intentions, financial resources, and your program. If you got the OK, then a dinner party was arranged with you and the girl sitting next to each other. But you better not dump that girl or boyfriend without another sit down.

quote: “Step 10 is a continuation of a fearless and searching look within ourselves in order to repair disorders that fear, pride, jealousy, and other ruinous defects can cause.”
body:
It is worth mentioning that the disorders mentioned above involved keeping people at a distance. Distancing behavior is a regular pattern among addicts who have to be careful to cover up their using to avoid getting busted. The ongoing problems feed our addictive disease! Constantly justifying our actions and reactions that caused us problems with people will get more problems. If we are continually committing the same actions, present problems beget more problems.

When we tell a newcomer to stop making avoidable decisions, they generally do pretty good. It is when they start ‘deciding’ again that new upsets occur. Doing nothing is still doing something. We find food, shelter and clothing. Our feelings are tender in recovery. Some of our seemingly large problems are really small. They seem large because we are not used to dealing with them. New problems are diminished as we grow comfortable when dealing with them. Lots of recovery is making new habit patterns appropriate to our new lives.

NA’s pamphlet Living The Program may possibly be the best piece of recovery literature in existence. It lists questions to be answered nightly about your program on a separate piece of paper.Some of us photocopy this pamphlet and fill out the copies every night. It is easy to add other questions like, did I gamble today, have road rage, overspend or anything else that might become obsessive. Fantastic literature like this only comes from group conscious and fellowship wide approval. Left lobe thinking is designed to restrict and narrow down concepts and ideas. Spirituality comes from the right lobe and deals with wholistic, field perceptions. Some members use this distinction to reshape their inner life and areas of sensitivity.

quote: “The message is meaningless unless we live it. If we do live it, we give it more meaning with our lives than any words can express.”
body:
One must walk the walk, not talk the talk. We have seen the later go out the door after sounding so very good. Silver tongue devils can be deadly. This is why NA recovery is so unique and effective. We share our real experience and our success makes that very attractive. Living the Program involves a person's whole life.

Newcomers sometimes smile when they hear, “Recovery is easy! Your just have to change your whole life!” In time, they realize this is an understatement. The first three Steps help us get in touch and establish our relationship with a higher power. The next three pairs of Steps take us from intention to action to clean up our lives on the inside, the surface and among people we have known and places we have been. The last three Steps guide us into an ongoing spiritual life not possible without the first nine Steps.

Seeing addicts getting real life results from slogans and oft repeated phrases catches our attention because we are used to covering up things rather than getting lasting results. When we find ourselves saying these words to those seeking help we realize our membership is real, not just candy coated.

quote: “The Higher Power we use in N.A. is a lot like this: We begin by simply admitting to the possibility of a power greater than ourselves.”
body:
This simple admission allows us access prayers that work for us. We can pray for a higher power to help us find a belief we can live with. The help we need to stay clean multiplies the more we use it. We can pray for help to stay clean, just for today! We can pray for help dealing with things at work or with the people in our lives.

One of NA’s most cutting edge ideas is that we have Higher Powers of each person’s interpretations. Be it Buddha, The Force, Mother Nature, God, Jesus or Allah. When we refer to our higher power as HP in the meetings, it allows people to hear what we are saying without filtering the message. It truly puts NA light years ahead of most spiritual programs. We don’t get caught up in emotionally-charged labels.

There is a social custom not to talk about religion or politics among people you don’t know because it always leads to anger and conflicts. In NA we often talk about God in general terms and leave off the politics to be socially acceptable. God is good all the time!

quote: “We apply what we learn in the meetings to all our affairs, using these teachings as a basis for living, but returning, again and again to our groups, both to help and to be helped in our recovery from addictions.”
body:
The difference between superficial ideas and concepts and those that take us deeper lies in their applicability. It is like a magic screw driver that can turn all sorts of fastenings. We all, or most of us, fall prey to thinking we know all that is. It is seductive! Keeping an honest, open and willing state of mind allows us to take things a step further.

In NA we learn everything we need to stay clean in the first two weeks. Then we just keep forgetting it unless we continue going to meetings. When we forget, it can be deadly. This is what is meant by addiction being a recurrent condition. Surrender and continual learning through going to meetings and helping others keeps our program alive.

Sometimes a member with sixty days sitting in a meeting can transmit a message of hope to a newcomer like no one else. The newcomer can grasp someone having sixty days, but not twenty years, it’s just too huge. So when we just go to a meeting, it can have a profound effect we never realize at home or on our own. And at the meeting we need to hear and speak the truth!.

quote: “One of the simplest and most important parts of our whole recovery process is the concept of “live a day at a time.””
body:
By living in the present, fully focused on what is happening now, we can be mindful of our actions and not simply react to outside forces. We can take time to figure out an appropriate response in accordance with spiritual principles. We are also going to notice how beautiful our world has become and take pleasure in it. Joy is an essential element of recovery. Gratitude is a muscle that grows with exercise.

“A day at a time” is one of the great liberators for addicts in recovery because it removes the brick wall that confines us to things which do not work out. We can change and change back if we need to. We learn as much or more from our failures as our successes.

How do you eat an elephant? One bite at a time. We can stay clean until the meeting time. Then we can stay clean till bedtime. Our bites may need to be very, very small. Getting from one meeting to the next during the same day is sometimes required. Never underestimate the power of multiple meetings in one day.

quote: “Some of us sought spiritual guidance through churches, different religions, meditation and cultism. Some of us sought cure by geographical changes, blaming our surroundings and living situations for our problem. This attempt just gave us new people to take advantage of.”
body:
What we failed to realize is that wherever we are, we bring the problem with us. Solutions to our problems cannot come from outside sources, they must come from within. We have to be open to receive from within! The Steps are the answer to our problems if we can find the courage, willingness and honesty to use the Steps to look inside, see who we are, accept ourselves and seek change.

Finding and developing a relationship with a Higher Power gives us the spiritual power to accomplish great changes we would otherwise be incapable of accomplishing. We start to gain self-acceptance and self-love by realizing we are no longer the despicable depraved people we used to be but are becoming capable of giving hope and love to those coming in after us.

NA is bone head simple. You sit your ass in a lot of meetings, dump the old friends, get involved in service, work the Steps and such. NA is delivered on the third grade level. But doing religions and recovery at the same time and confusing religion with recovery from addiction can be so confusing that it can kill us. What some good members fail to understand is church people are not recovering addicts in most cases and some will offer beer or wine not knowing what a relapse can do to us.

NA is like the short bus. Once we are clean for some time, we are much better equipped to go on to more complicated things. In fact, NA can be boiled down to “go to a meeting”, “go to another meeting”. Ok, now “go to another meeting”. You can sit in the meeting and look at the ceiling fan and still be doing a great job. You do not have to think. Just put your ass in a chair...a lot.

quote: ”The only way we keep from continuing a habit is not to take that first fix, pill, drink or toke.”
body:
This line was taken out of the material reviewed for the Basic Text, Narcotics Anonymous. The idea was to focus on the disease and not the venue. While this is still a good and valid idea, there is power in breaking habits what makes it important to avoid the context as well as the content. The context involves habit patterns, mood swings, availability of other drugs like road rage, suppressed anger or resentment. When we avoid the specific drugs we took, it may help us make it. But any drug can become our drug of choice. Body drugs are drugs too! The whole program revolves around changing our personalities and getting away from escaping reality. Newcomers are still told to avoid people, places and things associated with their personal using patterns and that will come to include drugs in various forms.

“It’s not the caboose that kills you” read a sign in one meeting room. The first one opens the door and then we are no longer in control. The disease has taken control. We may not get back to NA for 15 years...if at all. We may be dead in 24 hours or be facing life in prison. If you are in the rooms of NA, it is almost never by accident.

There was this good looking guy who had a few months and went out. When he came back, he was missing a foot. I will always remember that. A young lady thought she cold smoke a joint. The guy she was with killed her with a brick. Addiction is a tragic and progressive disease. What we learn and put into practice changes our whole life for the better!

quote: “We looked at our differences rather than our sameness. Yet the things we had in common put us all in the same dimension, the dimension of addiction.”
body:
We are often told as newcomers that we need to look for the similarities, not the differences. As newcomers we often looked for anything that would disqualify us, free us from the reality of our addiction. As we look around the rooms, we start to realize that we are not unique. Although our stories might be different, the feelings we have are in common. The self-centeredness, the insecurity, the unworthiness, the fear, guilt, remorse, regret, uselessness we feel we hear shared by others. We also hear how they overcame their negative self-images to have lives of joy and purpose, of peace, serenity and usefulness. We realize we have two things in common, addiction and recovery.

The spiritual life makes us aware of some of the fears and concerns that occupy space in our heads without paying rent. There are words we learn to deal with these things. There are ways to deal with these things that are learnable. The dimension we share grows and this growth gives us room unavailable before the program. “Diction” is the root word in addiction can so imbeds the idea of expression or statement in our disorder. When we are a mess inside our minds and bodies, that gets expressed in our behavior. The characteristics of a using addict are all too well known! The characteristics of a clean addict are only now becoming known to the general public.

Time, movies and personal experience will eventually replace the old picture of an addict stuck in addiction and unable to get out into a new picture of someone happy, balanced and especially grateful to be alive.

quote: “Now that we are clean, we have recognized the advantage of getting our own house in order. We can recognize ourselves as the heart of the problem.”
body:
It was no surprise to discover that our lives had become quite unmanageable as a result of our drug use. This was very clear to see. The real eye opener was after we had been clean for a while, our attempts at controlling our lives, caused us to experience feelings of unmanageability similar to what we experienced when we were using. This is when we discover surrender is the key, and we are the heart of the problem. This discovery is a blessing because if we’re the problem we can use the 12 Steps to change.

We might recall the Serenity Prayer at this time, knowing we cannot change another person, we can only change ourselves and pray for the wisdom to know the difference. Part of the miracle of recovery is knowing we can actively participate in our own recovery and cease to be the problem. When we do this, we find ourselves becoming part of the solution.

Computer companies worked hard to make more perfect devices. Much time and energy was devoted to agonizing over which white was the whitest. They also looked at the experience of the customer opening the box. Care and concern help make things better and we want to get better ourselves!

quote: “Managing our own lives got us to the program of Narcotics Anonymous. What we knew about living when we got here had almost killed us.”
body:
We may find a way to do what we used to stumble into with something we took time to think through. This is a program of surrender. Each Step will gently ask us to surrender a little deeper. This is why the first step is about surrender. If we fail to grasp this first spiritual principle of surrender, none of the other steps will make much sense. All 12 Steps are based on surrender. As we say in the meetings “we surrender to win.” On a practical level this means we have come to a place in our lives where forcing life had not worked very well for us. We never seemed to grasp that using force to get our way usually puts us at odds with someone else. This can result in the wreckage of our present.

This conflict usually concerns our family and then the law. When our lives become unmanageable, someone tends to step in to manage it. This may be spouses, sometimes our parents but most often toward the end of our addiction, it is the law. We have lawyers who tell us where to go and what to say or we have judges that tell us what to do. Larger and larger consequences will result if we don’t do what we are told. We lose our say in our own lives!

In NA we surrender to recovery. We turn our will, our lives, our decision making process over to NA and a loving sponsor who will help guide us into this new way of living. Many sponsors have been living a surrendered life for a many years. They are very knowledgeable about how surrender is done on a daily basis. Most often they will be more than willing to help us learn this new way of life. How to let go of our lives and not try to run the show. How to let go and let God or just how to take our fingers off the situation and let Good Orderly Direction takes its naturally correcting way in the situation. We have learned in recovery that oftentimes our attempts to control causes more harm than good. If we just let life happen it often has a naturally self- correcting way about it.
moment: Just For Tonight: As the comic book character Pogo Possum said; “We have met the enemy and they is us!”

quote: “Learning the art of helping others when it is appropriate, without creating resentments, is a marvelous benefit of the N.A. program.”
body:
As the 3rd Tradition states: “The only requirement for membership is a desire to stop using.” The first thing we do when helping the still suffering addict is to make sure they have this desire. We have learned through much difficulty there are many people who come to NA that are not through using. They are not done manipulating others. They are not done with their addiction and are just looking for someone else to get over on. They are so lost in their addiction that they don’t even have a clue, nor think they need one. They are not even sure they need help. Except someone or some institution says they need this help. They are not even sure they have a problem and actually require help to change. Often times, we have learned to give them minimal assistance by giving them some literature or aiding them in getting to meetings. This is where the magic happens. We don’t bring them to our homes as they are sure to come to size up what they can take from us.

For every one of these ‘not-so surrendered folks,’ there are dozens of addicts who are basically through using. They want no more of their old way of living. They are ready and willing to follow directions, take the suggestions and do the assignments without question. We learn to focus more of our efforts on these folks and spend less time on those who just want to take and take.

We have come to understand that the therapeutic value of one addict helping another is the magic of simply sharing our experience, strength and hope as to how we have met an overcome the challenges of getting and staying clean. The amazing thing is that by carrying the message to others, it helps solidify these spiritual principles in our own lives.
moment: Just For Tonight: I can only keep what I have by giving it away!

quote: “Any thought that we are going to be perfect has to be smashed.”
body:
The idea that perfection for humans ignores our experience. If we are willing to surrender and do the best we can, that is enough more often than not. In ourselves, or in our friends, we pray for strength and guidance when overwhelmed by a demand or situation over which we are powerless. Personal surrender frees us of much of the poor thinking we practiced in active addiction. Feeling badly about ourselves makes us set unreachable goals to feel better. As we listen to others sharing in meetings, we begin to realize other people have the same problems. We see how turned around we have been. We find ourselves telling someone to not be so hard on themselves - then we apply the same to our lives!

Constantly striving for perfection and falling short can give us feelings of not being good enough, that can result again in those feelings of low self worth. We may not be perfect , but we are definitely doing better that we ever had in the past.`

Setting reasonable goals and standards for ourselves give us immediate relief. Without the tension and stress that goes with trying to reach impossible goals, we can see, hear and feel more accurately. Part of the magic of helping others is having information on how other people feel as hopeless and confused as we do at times. This information based on our own observation and experience mixes in a little reality. We can deal with life on life’s terms when we have a better idea of what those terms are!

quote: “The only way we recover is to apply what we learn from each other, on a daily basis. Our growth is a lifetime process.”
body:
The more we learn, the more we are capable of learning. Generally, our next best move is right in front of us. We don’t recognize it because we dislike and distrust openness and honesty. When we try something new and it works for us, we become curious about checking out some of our other scary opportunities!

One member was at a Unity Day and a woman approached him and asked how he was doing. After explaining his complicated life and game plan, she stopped him and said “You need to get out of your head”. He was stunned at how she had just hit the nail on the head.

Later in the day he was walking down a hall and she was talking to another man. As he walked past he heard her say “You need to get out of your head”. He realized that this piece of universal advice was so powerful it applied to many situations.

Clean is a whole new experience for us. Many of us associate being clean with cleaning up wreckage as we prepare for our next spree! Living the clean life on a daily basis is a whole new ball game. Generally, we over-analyse everything. Recovery is getting in touch with our feelings and balancing them against what we are learning.

quote: “Many fortunate things occur mysteriously, but there are no accidents. We come to understand that our recoveries are a gift from a Power greater than ourselves.”
body:
Getting used to having a higher power may seem a bit strange at first. Spiritual growth can feel frightening or unreal. Elusive understandings and surprising good luck can lull us into taking it all for granted. After all, if we are not using, lots of things do get better, apparently on their own! Relapse often follows taking recovery for granted. If this happens to us, we get back to a meeting and start taking the N.A. program more seriously. As addicts. our very lives depend on it. This is the harsh reality we have to revisit time and time again. Addicts die of addiction al the time!

Developing habits of thoughtfulness and living one day at a time, we hear others talking about higher power and conscious contact. We get curious about what they might mean. One day, we are sitting quietly and letting our mind go out of gear and realize we are doing it. We are doing conscious contact. And things are working out for us! One thing about clean time, we have more of it to consider and ask for help. We also find the NA Program gives us people who understand and many who are willing to help us! And the things that are happening are evidence of our higher power! Why didn’t someone tell us about this! Then we realize many of these things are repeated in the meetings and written in the literature we have been reading.

Having a Higher Power we can love and trust with our lives, makes it easier to practice our conscious contact. At some point we discover that this is not confined to recovering addicts only, it is a regular part of the healthy human condition. Surrender, prayer and meditation are part of our daily lives in NA.

quote: “We admitted that we used drugs, but many of us did not think we had a problem.”
body:
What we knew about recovery came from uninformed people like fellow students in school and co-workers. TV and movies were another untrustworthy source of information. Budweiser will get you dates, Chanel No. 5 will make you beautiful and smoking Marlboros will make you a real man! By the time we get to N A, we are hungry to get some real answers - answers that will work in real life! Using drugs was confused with partying, having a good time, an attitude adjustment or dealing with tension. Taking more drugs was not an answer we could live with.

Another great realization came when we realized we were getting our information from fellow addicts who were also in denial and still using drugs!

This is the most confusing disease of all. The oldtimers are the most expert people at understanding this disease because they have years of experience working with other addicts, listening in meetings and working the steps. These are the few people on the planet who can help newcomers recover.

We hit what we call a bottom. This consists of two parts: hitting the low point and perceiving we are in trouble. We must have both to have that moment of clarity to want to quit. Going to meetings, working with a sponsor and reading the literature increases our perception. Sitting in prison has given many addicts the time needed to realize they have hit their bottoms!.

quote: “The smart ones who argue a lot usually die. The open-minded ones get to live.”
body:
It is harder for more intelligent people to surrender because in our world many things give way to intelligence. It is easier for them to rationalize. There are things that require sensitivity and savvy to understand. These are sometimes wordless, or defined by special words that go beyond objects and labels. A loving, caring person is not especially respected in certain parts of our society, yet their value is beyond price. Those who go along, get along. This may help us at work and in some areas of our lives.

Many of us used drugs that disrupted our minds and left us blasted. Fortunately, we regain many of our former skills and abilities. Others may have to learn to be grateful they have any good sense at all. Many suffer permanent damage to some extent. Addiction is a seriously limiting disease. Mental and physical disability may be something we have to learn to live with. Compassion and giving comfort to those in distress is a positive ability and helps to see someone disabled like we are get better. We can be grateful when we someone asks us for help..

Many addicts in recovery are clean for years only to find themselves maneuvering into positions of control. Learning to laugh at oneself is a handy tool on many occasions. The man who cannot laugh at himself is missing one of life’s biggest jokes! Humor and talking about the crazy thoughts that wander through our minds from time to time gives us a healthy outlet.

quote: “Being able to serve as an instrument of a loving God and participation in the recovery of others keeps a sense of wonder and gratitude in our lives. Giving comfort and encouragement to others encourages and comforts us.”
body:
In general, when we keep our focus on gratitude and doing our part, everything gets better. Being open to what others are screaming at us, allows us to take pressure off a difficult situation and shift over to doing something to help things along. Apologizing for making someone uncomfortable can’t hurt in most cases. Treating someone the way we want to be treated is also a good idea. Saying a quiet, silent prayer keeps us in touch with our higher power and this is our main source of solutions.

Often the smallest kindness on our part can make a huge difference to newcomers. Simply shaking their hand, getting to know their name, these can be huge to a frightened addict just coming in. They know no one and everything familiar is gone. It’s terrifying to just go into a meeting room for the new person. They need a friend and they need one fast if they are to not cut and run. We are warm people who know how to do this. We forget just how it was when we first walked in the door. How quickly we forget.

Many members may not know about the secret of saying a silent prayer like, “God, use me as an instrument if it be your will. I feel good about this person and would be glad to help.” When we do that, we find the words come to us with surprising ease. The response from the newcomer tells us when we get through to them. It is a definite thrill to identify with an addict who does not yet know they can live clean!

quote: “We are experts at making ourselves so frustrated that we lose perspective completely.”
body:
Losing perspective means our thinking is off and we need to take a break. Since surrender is a keyword in our recovery, we find we get relief by not fighting. We stop exerting pressure and take a break. Usually we can see right off where we were going wrong.

We lose perspective all the time and don’t even realize it, we just feel lost and confused. Fear of loss of control is common among us. Learning to stop and pray for help is a multipurpose tool in recovery. We use it to deal with things we are powerless over.

Actually, a lot of the tasks we do come off without a hitch. We show up at the Department of Motor vehicles as soon as they open and breeze right thru. We go to the grocery store with an exact list of what we need. We call the wireless carrier and make arrangement to pay the bill a bit late and keep it turned on. We need to give ourselves credit for these small wins.

It's easy to beat ourselves up completely if we read these quotes too much. Information is like food, eat a little and let it go down before eating more. Acting on good information keeps it from going stale.

quote: “Being open-minded allows us to hear something that might save our lives.”
body:
If this sounds pretty dramatic, it is. Most of us have developed defense mechanisms that keep us locked up in a narrow, defensive and lonely state of mind. Our new found hope opens many doors for us, starting with believing change is possible. Hope is like a flame that warms us. Even if it is small in the beginning, the warmth makes a difference and feels good. We learn to trust our feelings and go with them as our life steadily improves. Sometimes it is hard for us to see the old ways creeping in and don’t accurately notice we can begin to work our recovery program in reverse - using our tools to justify and condone behaviour of self-righteousness and isolation.

One addict noticed that if he went to three meetings in a row, that he became more receptive to hearing new ideas and looking at himself. He concluded that the first meeting found him shut down, the second began to open him up and the third found him almost completely open minded, anxious to learn and willing to change. Missing meetings works against this effect. We begin to fall into our old habit patterns. We begin to justify picking people apart. We start thinking they comb their hair funny. We look for differences, right past the similarities.

Being open lets in the fresh air. We identify. We develop an ability to acquire memories without having to go through all the pain! We can try on new ideas without having to necessarily adopt them permanently. Our interaction becomes a treasure house of new ideas, attitudes and opportunities.

I knew an addict who sold mobile homes. Often he would be baffled about why a person failed to qualify for a loan. He would wait a few weeks and call up the loan officer and chat them up. Eventually, he would ask them why the person failed to qualify. And get this: the loan officer would tell him exactly where the deal went wrong! Gradually, he began to see the world from their point of view. He changed the way he filled out the forms and his loans began to go through. Through honesty, open-mindedness and willingness to try, he stepped right past former limitations.

quote: “Abstinence is the basis of our program. Any mood or mind-altering chemical, prescription or not, is poison to our bodies.”
body:
There is literature available outside our approved material that goes right to the heart of the thinking behind taking drugs to ‘feel better’ or adjust our moods without changing the things that produce stress or bad feelings in our day to day lives. This is hardly an outside issue. There are plenty of people who may benefit from various treatments that include meds.They don’t have to call themselves N A members or follow our 12 Steps. There are also many members in good standing who both work our program and get the relief they seek from good doctors. Folks have to find what works for them. Our role is supportive, not directive. The important thing for me to remember is that I can’t use drugs in any form. That’s total abstinence. If someone else can, that’s ok by me. But do not call it clean of try to ‘adjust’ our literature to open the door to use of chemicals that might make us feel a little better but also keep us from dropping the habits and attitudes that create the stress and tension.

One addict noted that in his 30 plus years, he had only seen 7 people take antidepressants and stay clean long term.The ones who didn’t want to hear it, said it was just his opinion. He responded that it was his observation. Once we open the door to mind and mood altering chemicals, we are no longer driving the bus, our disease is behind the wheel. We know from sad personal experience that any one of us is susceptible to the disease of lies, the disease of addiction.

To the man with a hammer, everything looks like a nail. To a doctor with a prescription pad, everybody needs a pill. Antidepressants are so over prescribed, especially when the doctor hears the word “Addict”, that they are a drug epidemic in themselves. These doctors are not versed on addiction. They are taught to treat the symptoms and to prescribe an anecdote to those symptoms,meaning that for each symptom goes along with a drug. A computer may make the decision for them! Of course they do not understand the disease, it is baffling. That’s what baffling means! We addicts cannot afford to be confused about this, our very lives are at stake. There are many ways our being nice takes the edge off our stricture that NA is a program of total abstinence.

quote: “If we clean our bodies by daily abstinence we should clean our minds of preconceptions based on past experience. It is those who stay clean when it seems like it isn’t worth it who make it.”
body:
Not picking up isn’t everything, it's the only thing. If we are going to get loaded over a car or a relationship, we must have not wanted recovery very bad. In the old days the rules were much stricter. Naturally we want to find an easier, softer way. We like to be enabled. If recovery were easy, we wouldn’t appreciate it. We learn self-respect as we continue to stay clean and begin the process of working the Steps. We learn that by taking the focus off of self and go to a meeting to find someone asking for help. It may be that they may not want help, but just taking the focus off off self, is a success in and of itself. When we are not thinking about using, we begin to focus more on recovery.

Preconceptions based on past experience often do not work because if we are clean, our situation has changed for the better. Past experience belongs in our crazy past, not our clean present. We get up, clean up and go to work every day. We don’t need to know who’s got what and what’s coming in next week. Our life gets simpler, then our life gets better. We get to spend more time with our new friends and travel to check out new meetings. When we dump old habits and mannerisms, we make room for new stuff. We can actually sit down and plan a charming evening for a special person. We can remember someone’s birthday!

While it may seem like these are little things, they are the very things we could not do before. Clean time means we have more time to take with living. And as we all know, the more you learn, the more you can learn.

quote: “During our active using, we often lived way beyond our means, out of necessity. Unfortunately, not all of us lose our extravagance and greed easily, ever after we stop using.”
body:
Living within our means and practicing a little restraint just means we’ll pay the rent on time and our lights won’t be cut off next month. Later, it means more, much more. It means we will be free of financial worries that we had become accustomed to enduring. Many of us were addicted to a painful way of living. By creating conflicts, we could sometimes hide our games. We hid behind clouds of confusion.

We love the grandiose lifestyle. Big cars, big jobs and big breasted women. Some ladies live to flaunt! We can flaunt it, but we also need to have some degree of humility. No one says we have to live like monks once we got clean. If this were so, not many people would stick. Working the 12 Step provides a steady supply of good feelings of accomplishment. Happy npeople make things happen!

One member wore a lot of turquoise jewelry to establish that he was not broke. But he also went to lots of meetings and his service commitment was cleaning the mens bathroom weekly.

In China, everyone works in the rice fields one day per month to keep them humble. The best service commitments in NA can be at the group level.

quote: “Clean living is only the beginning of a new life. Life without fear is a gift we receive for the price of acceptance.”
body:
Incredible as this may be, it is really that simple. Considering the pain and despair we have suffered, it almost seems unfair to find such a simple way out of our insanity. There are things ahead of you down recovery road that you won’t be able to see until you get there. That’s why we practice the NA Program on a daily basis. Do what’s in front of you and don’t let yesterday and tomorrow distract you from taking full advantage of what’s in front of you right now, today!

Freedom from active addiction sounds almost impossible to us when we first hear it, until we see others smiling and laughing . As we stay clean for a while, we begin to feel better about life as we begin to experience surrender and acceptance. We feel some of that freedom from active addiction that we read in our literature,and hear about in our meetings! We can get clean, stay clean and find a new way to live.This is how we become free from active addiction. Stopping using has to come first. We learn to stay clean, work with our sponsor, and see evidence that active addiction is not just actively using drugs. It is also our reaction to people and situations as well.

Many of us lose track of how much ‘fear’ controls much of our lives, thinking and actions. Fear can be treated like ‘just another habit!’ You break habits by doing things differently until the new way replaces the old. If you are drug free today, don’t let fear drag you around by the nose. Life without fear is a gift we receive for the price of acceptance.

quote: ”Humility is an honest self-acceptance, which leads to further acceptance of the conditions around us.”
body:
While humility is seen by most persons in our culture as a matter of bowing and scraping, real humility is more likely plain honesty. Humble persons have strength of character and will be staunch if mistreated or dealt with unfairly. It is not ignorance or a lack of inner strength. Probably it seems that way to those for whom humility is just a word. Internal resources build up within our minds and hearts that allow us to acquire what is known as good character.

Adaptation to the reality around us puts us in a condition where we are able to meet the demands and situation we are likely to encounter. This results in personal self- confidence and the ability to help those in need.

One of the nice things about recovery is it gets ‘in your face’ real about what’s been bothering us and what we can do about it. Addiction is a life-limiting and life- extinguishing disease. Recovery is a life expanding and life affirming process where we gather strength with others and make common-cause of rebuilding and reconstructing our shattered lives.

Our internal state determines what we can perceive and how we meet reality on it’s own ground.

quote: “Addiction is physical, mental, and spiritual . Therefore,we believe that we must develop faith in a Higher Power before we can hope to recover from its destruction.”
body:
The entire basis of the 12 step programs rests on self-evident truth. Helping one another access the people and information that will help pave the way to new lives. Helping others like ourselves gives us a window on reality we can get no other way. . The Twelve Steps of NA lead us from surrender to discovery of a higher power we can trust with our lives. Our will is our thinking and our lives are our bodies. Care is loving attention and concern.

Then we reduce our personal histories to a list of things we did right and things we wish had gone differently. By sharing our inventory with God and another human being, we take power away from the gnawing pain of negative expectation that we will repeat our errors. Our willingness to let go of personal habits and preferences allow our old ways to drop away and make room for new pathways to open. We move through these pathways in our new freedom. Closure removes the barriers to our personal relationships, even if it is only on the level of willingness. It is not merely symbolic, it changes things is reality.

The big changes come from personal prayer and regular meditation with inventory cleaning up messes as we go. Meditation stops the endless rush forward and we learn to reprogram ourselves. This is why NA is not a cult! These lead to our spiritual awakening and the galaxy of principles which help guide us in situations as they arise. All this comes from our origins in simple faith and trust in a proven pathway to recovery from addiction. We have to stop doing the things that make us feel badly if we want to feel goodness. We leave the world of things behind as we move into the realm of spiritual reality.

quote: “If you want to change your life -- risk sharing! It is by taking risks we have almost lost our lives, by asking for help in the same way, we can change.”
body:
Sharing allows us to see into the lives of others who like us have lost their way. We can choose and pick up on things that we can try to practice on a daily basis. In this way we find what works for us. If we try out enough shoes, we are bound to find a pair that fits. If your ego keeps you from asking for help, you are not likely to get help. Even if help comes to us, it is hard or impossible to accept help until we believe it possible. Addiction keeps us from recognizing help even if it is staring us in the face! We often feel insulted that someone presumes to offer us help! It makes us feel ‘less than.’ Big mistake!

Many of us have found that abstinence, meetings and interacting with clean addicts helps us see through the fog and take notice that help is at hand! Even if it is hard for many of us to understand in the beginning, it may help in the beginning to realize that addiction is a self- destructive disease. Addiction attacks us in our ability to perceive and communicate. This is why meetings are so important. We do a group reality check. Most often we find we are in there, creating the very problems that seem to have us tied in knots.

Helping others helps us see ourselves differently and after a while we treat ourselves differently. Everyone has heard the old saying, “Slow to anger, quick to forgive.” How about a new one? “Slow to get in trouble and quick to get out!” “Take it easy” is an NA slogan because if something is hard, we have learned to slow down and look more closely. Sometimes our HP is just trying to avoid difficulty that we cannot see coming with our physical eyes. We have to use our spiritual eyesight!

quote: “We no longer have to make fools of ourselves by standing up for some non-existent values.”
body:
Pretending to be more than we are is a fear-based behavior that seemed to protect us from negative judgements by others as to our conduct or behavior. Hiding from the truth is not necessary if our house is in order.

False pride and ego may be all someone has to hang on to if they lack real skills and abilities. As they calm down and get with the Program of NA, they acquire new skills their need to put up a false front disappears. The more we grow, the more we know. The more we know, the more we can do things that were once out of reach.

A lot of the changes we do not see, but from time to time we happen to recognize that we are able to do things that we could never do before, put up with irritants that we could not endure before and visualize what move to make next in almost any situation!.

From time to time we have huge leaps ahead that simply cannot be ignored. All of a sudden we seem to be completely different people, able to do seemingly impossible tasks a few weeks ago. But most of the time the changes are so slow and subtle that they don’t even register till someone says “You have grown.” Sometimes we realize life is like a rapidly processed movie that runs by itself, and then,we realize we are all writers, actors and directors. Our ideas, our actions, and our focus makes up our experience of life. God grant me a good role, please! I will, I can and I’m going to.

quote: ”One of our greatest enemies is resentment. It has the power to kill. The only way to be rid of resentment is to develop humility, in the form of forgiveness.”
body:
Actually, praying for those people whom we resent is a very effective way of clearing resentments. We can pray for them to have all the stuff that we wish to have. Strangely, curiously, when we do this, we experience relief. It is almost like the resentment is like a bone stuck in our throat that keeps is from swallowing. Free of the re-sent-ment, we get to go on with our lives.

One addict, very angry at NAWS, prayed for the World Board to have unlimited money, property and prestige. His resentment at NAWS was so completely removed that he could no longer talk with others who were angry with NAWS. The message is that as the resentment decreased, he could study, discuss, and envision ways to accomplish things that once seemed blocked by a small West Coast corporation. It’s all our money. When we want low cost, high quality conventions, we will have them. When we want a meeting in that jail, we will go see the warden and talk it up until we find some members who agree with us. You rarely get good results when you are loud and negative.

There appear to be some spiritual laws that regulate reality. Like you have to give it away to keep it. You have to teach what you have learned to make room for more learning. While discussion may not always prove you to be right, it always tends to make you more correct! Recovery allows us to pursue these spiritual laws with others doing the same. The result is knowledge of spirituality that amounts to a great series of breakthroughs that continue all through our cleantime.

Make no mistake about it, just as a lion trainer is most likely to die from a big cat attack, we as addicts are most likely to be killed by a justified resentment. This is our nemesis. The problem is that in the midst of a justified resentment, we forget our training. We are over taken by rage and are likely to go out and revert to our old ways.

Black belts train incessantly to automatically go into their stance. For recovering addicts, we go to meetings all the time to remember the basic tools of recovery including being aware of justified resentments. This is how we survive. And unless we regularly go to meetings, when we are in the midst of resentment, we will not go to a meeting. Going to meetings has to be an automatic routine. We just go every few days regardless. Then when we are in trouble, it occurs to us, “I have not been to a meeting for 3 days. I guess I better go.” This then saves us from out resentment.

quote: “The bottom line of Narcotics Anonymous is staying clean. When we realize that we can’t use drugs in any form and live, we are ready to admit our powerlessness.”
body:
When we are exhausted, out of money, friends and excuses, it may be easier to admit we are powerless and our lives have become unmanageable. We may find the resulting teachability can be applied to many other areas. Surrender is a mighty tool. We can ask for and accept help and help comes from many sources. Puffed up on pride and ego, we may find our ability to ask for and receive gratefully help decreases. Then when the help slows down or stops coming entirely, you’ll hear some members complain the Program doesn’t work! After a time, we begin to see the ‘trouble’ is actually helping us by keeping us in touch with our need for help!

Relapse is not a subtle state to be in! The effects are immediate and devastating. If we are a little quieter and go to work on time, that may be a misleading benefit. Like the prohibition against taking the first drug in any form, the visible relapse comes later when we imagine we are using successfully. This vicious cycle must be avoided at all costs.It’s not the last one that gets us loaded, it is all the times before that!

Relapsing into self-will is quite instructive. Many businessmen are supportive, positive and alert to their employees need for instruction and guidance. Their businesses flourish and sometimes their competitors become bitter and jealous of their workforce! They become critical and get drawn into a negative mindset that forces their best employees to run for cover. They dare not excel else the boss will get angry! A company that retains bad employees will find that their best will leave lest they become undercut by misplaced blame. Like many other things, it may take a while to see through to the core of these problems.

quote: “Those of us who have been involved in service for a long time or in getting a group started and keeping the doors open through the hard early days sometimes have a hard time letting go of the reins. Sometimes our egos get in the way, sometimes ungrounded fears get in the way, and sometimes the group gets in the way.”
body:
No one claims to completely understand the miracle of our our message and our recovery. Therefore there is no safe and sure way to make rules for members to obey blindly. We all need to look up now and then to see how we are doing.
