Paying attention to the facial expressions of other members gives us the best way to determine when to pass the baton of leadership to someone else. If the moment passes, it can break the spirit of interpersonal sharing. The optimum is usually brief, genuine shares about what is happening in one’s life or a current concern, then sit back for others to share. Cross talk and a few members swapping comments leaves out the rest of the group and that too can interrupt the sharing. If you and I share our personal truth and experience, we leave it at that and pay attention to what others are sharing.

Someone observed that people generally share with other people who are at or near the same level of growth and development. This may be true. If it is, it might partly explain why people hold back or leave the room if someone is talking down to them or gives an unscheduled lecture. It disadvantages others and stops the open sharing of personal truth. They may not be onsciously mad or angry, they just feel unwanted or unneeded. This is one reason we wait from someone who is hurting to ask for help. Questions signal readiness to receive help.

For some reason, many of our best members forget to pray or meditate to get their answers. What if we found out someday that many of our biggest questions could only be answered if we slowed down and opened our minds and our hearts.

quote: “ When we have a strong faith in a Power greater than ourselves, and use that faith in our daily lives, that Power will be able to reach us and supply us with the strength and guidance that we need to recover.”
body:
Faith is visible in the changes we can see in our lives. Staying open to what other members share is a good practice because it gives us dozens of windows into the recovery experience of others. There is no end to what we can learn and practice. Sometimes it takes a few tries to get it right. Nobody recovers overnight. We practice what we learn and may have to wait a while to see the results. Hey, we waited for the dope man! Don’t give up before the next miracle.

Going to meetings, reading good recovery literature and visiting with our HP gets us in the ‘mood to receive.’ The mood to receive keeps the door open in our minds and we find some strange, helpful, stuff tumbling through to our conscious minds. We use these spiritual hints and clues to assemble our own mental picture. We usually find it useful the same day. .

Conscious contact seems to be a spiritual doorway to our personal belief system that best suits us. We all have a right to our beliefs and what we build on that platform becomes a tower of strength and principles that fill in the canyons of our pain and swamps of our confusion. When we are able to pass it on, it becomes part of our internal resources.

quote: “It is a way of life for the addicts who want to learn to live clean and have discovered one another. We share our mental and spiritual resources for the good of everyone.”
body:
NA must be woven into the fabric of our lives. It must be part of the daily routine, the weekly routine. We go to the 5:30 meeting simply because we go there all the time. It’s like school children getting hungry at the scheduled time for lunch.

We get to know the people in the meeting and what they are into. It takes time to make friends. This is why we go out for coffee after the meetings. We learn about people's hobbies, passions, families, dogs. It seems trivial but this is what being friends is all about. Knowing that other person’s world opens the door of friendship and creates the bonds between us. .

And they learn of our world. The irritating neighbor, our beloved dog, the Mustang in the garage that we hope to get running again. We become a band of brothers. Many times we pick up where we left off at some point before addiction began to crumple our lives. Human beings are built to live in communities that are mutually supportive. Each person has perceptions and talents that help feed and protect the members of their communities from the youngest babe to the oldest lady or gentleman.

quote: “Along with this they also accept that they do have special and valuable experiences which can benefit the group. They become a resource for the group; seeking neither to control the group not to set themselves apart. It is at this time that their long service truly contributes the most. They encourage us, inspire us, and teach us by example.”
body:
The material in the Grey Book leads up to this sentence enumerating the things that a person can go through as they progress in service. Direct personal service is the most important service NA has to offer. Calling up members we barely know and writing down what they say, leads us into the amazing recovery world that connects all our members. The ‘resource’ idea is crucially important. A group without someone to turn to in the advent of unexpected difficulty is in trouble. This happens if everyone is getting confused and ready to set aside spiritual principles to get their way. Having a resource to get them back on the same page is a non-toxic exit to conflicts resulting from simple disagreements.

Realizing that the differences among our identity group is an asset not a liability is an important jump forward. If we are having trouble with something and call a few members, we can sort out what they say in general along with helpful particulars. Shared recovery experience is the basis for all NA recovery. Since we focus on what we really do in our lives, it gives power to the general ideas and principles that guide us.

Each one of us become ‘resources to NA’ as we accumulate information from our our travels, readings and personal experience. In general, persons serving as resources are careful to qualify the information they give out as to hearsay, personal observation, or reliable source. They respect anonymity unless they person asking for help knows personally the person they are speaking of. We get numbers from the questioner and pass them along to another member who can then contact the person wanting information. We don’t give out numbers to third parties. This practice is not cut in stone but is the practice applied in most cases.

quote: “The newcomer is our lifeblood of N.A. and when one comes to us seeking help we welcome them with open arms.”
body:
The principle here is obvious. Evaluating the situation requires some common sense. Sometimes newcomers exhibit symptoms of mental disturbance that make it hard to determine if they are serious about recovery or dancing on air mentally. Most often we are qualified to determine which is the case. We have always seen people transit into N A members if the disease and desire are there. Some people with mental illness benefit from the 12 Step process where all else fails!.

Surrender, belief in the process and just calling a halt opens them to help.Sometimes it just takes time. Perhaps the important thing here is to remind us everyone in N A is a miracle and we were all in bad shape when we got here! Patience, tolerance and humility are tools we use to carry our message. No sense in telling people something they are not ready to hear. Fear and memories of past pain erect walls to protect us. We allow others to have their own individual preferences out of respect. We never know for sure what a person has been through. Anyone smart enough to seek help has earned our respect. We don’t use their asking for help as an opportunity to play God or lay down the law to them. When we walls come down through trust and faith, it is time to gently bring up sensitive subjects.

Seen in this light, recovery and carrying our message become like an art form. We move by feel, trusting the Spirit to guide us. Since we pray to be used as instruments, we are constantly growing in our ability. Newcomers will establish a few members they turn to for help over time. It keeps our message free and open to the many variations that occur among addicts seeking recovery.

quote: “Many of us could not visualize a life without drugs, let alone want it. Many of us had reached the point in our addiction where we felt there was no hope for us, we only wanted a little relief.”
body:
Carrying our message requires us to pay attention and raise our consciousness to scope out what the newcomer is seeking and what they need to know next. Staying clean is the main thing. Our open door policy insures that there will always be issues about medication and total abstinence. Some people have to take meds to survive. But taking meds which do not get them high. They are not drugs sold on the street. There is now and probably will be plenty of confusion on this subject. So we have to pray and be open minded. Our combined experience is a great resource.

Still there will always be addicts seeking recovery who need and require total abstinence. Generally those who practice total abstinence need to be sensitive to those who have problems. Those who have problems, should not criticize those who practice total abstinence. We practice ‘consciousness based recovery.’ The most important thing is to surrender and practice what works for us. Unfortunately, members on meds may not really need them and will want to make their usage part of the NA program. This cannot be. In a world populated by profit oriented prescribers there is plenty of room for care and concern on this subject. Many members stay on their meds and later get off under their doctor's supervision. Many are mis-prescribed. We cannot control this. We are not all powerful and never claim to be!

The information we gain about addiction helps us stay clean as long as we are focused on adding to that information with our own personal experience. If we let ourselves drift into distraction and forget the basics of recovery, we can pave the way to relapse by dropping the new attitudes and living habits that helped us get clean in the first place.There are countless time when we feel all is lost and the next day, we just wake up and go about our business.

quote: “Many of us have come to believe that the forces of life know what our real needs are and will take care of us when undisturbed by self-will.”
body:
One of the things we get to do in recovery is inventory our lives. What we have done in the past, how we are doing at present and what we foresee ourselves doing in the future. In fear and desperation, we were often driven to do things that led to shame and guilt. In recovery we are gently led away from obsession and compulsion. It may be that the strong drive to deal with things helped us survive. Today, we have a choice. We can stop, look and listen. We can pray for help from our HP. We can ask our new friends and sponsors for help. All these take out some of the bumps and many of the grinds that used to comprise our lives.

It is obvious that the forces of life are in our favor, else we would not be here. Surrender and faith are the keys to a new life. The next is to straighten out our muddy thinking and problematic living habits. Then we begin our awesome journey into the life of the spirit. Spiritual principles based on honesty, open mindedness and the willingness to try take us far beyond our former boundaries. Honesty about what happened in the past, open-mindedness about finding what role we played in events and willingness to try for the future. A good attitude always helps.

We find our futures are controlled by our beliefs. When we change our beliefs, our futures change to match our beliefs. Believing ourselves to be in the loving care of our higher power allows us a chance to sit back and let God paddle the canoe. Our past inventory is taken out of the dark and into the light. We put our defects into the light and are willing to let them go. When we ask God to remove them they go away. All that is left are the occasions and events we want to put closure on by making amends. Now we are free to go on with our lives and follow the road to our spiritual awakening.

quote: ”Do we really want to be rid of our old resentments, our anger, our fears? Do we really understand that they are a deadly poison in the heart of an addict?”
body:
When we argue for our limitations, sure enough, they are ours. Most all of us have a few complaints about things that bother us when we get clean. We will never be happy until we get rid of our resentments, anger and fear. Much of our concern stems from our inability to surrender and our lack of a belief in a loving, caring higher power. Each time we eliminate something that blocks our happiness, we make room for something good to take its place.

We hear during readings at the beginning of each meeting that “most of all we harmed ourselves.” Once we have done a fearless and searching moral inventory we come to understand the truth in this statement and how our actions and behaviors have damaged more than just our relationships with others. The negative behaviors during our active addiction damaged our spirit, which led to low self esteem as we diminished ourselves in our own eyes. That in turn caused us to seek relief and escape through using, which led to even lower self esteem. This cycle of diminishing returns is part and parcel of active addiction.

If we want to break this cycle, when faced with the choice of holding onto resentment, anger and fear or letting go, we can begin by asking ourselves, “How is this going to harm my spirit? Do I really want to do that to myself again?” The power to choose, to make healthier choices is a gift of recovery. We are no longer trapped in a cycle of negativity. Letting go and turning over our fear and resentments to the God of our understanding is a process we can undertake by making a simple decision to show ourselves the love and kindness we all deserve.

quote: “Recovering addicts take great pleasure in helping other suffering addicts recover. Recovery as found in Narcotics Anonymous must come from within and no one can get clean for anyone else.”
body:
Nothing is so exhilarating as taking a homeless addict to lunch. We see the disbelief in their eyes that someone actually cared about them. Often, we see them waiting for us to talk down to us. Make a point of telling them someone helped us. Don’t give advice. We do not have all the answers! Then as we see them putting days together, we get a warm fuzzy feeling inside. A life has been saved and we carried the messenger. Others have befriended us and now we befriend others in our turn.

Through this process, we are praying for strength and guidance. We are careful to keep our egos in check. Sticking to spiritual principles, we share our own true experience. We do not want our recovery to be based on mumbo-jumbo. Facts of real addict experience guiding us in our recovery. Listening to their problems reminds us of what drugs do to us. Listen for questings. They indicate what the person is ready to receive.

Our spirit bubbles up when we pray to be used as an instrument. It does the work for us. We smile as we realize once again, the principles of NA work when nothing else will do.

quote: “Many of us cling to our fears, doubts, and self-loathing or hatred of others, because there is a certain distorted security in familiar pain.”
body:
It is natural to seek comfort and pleasure yet there is a danger we addicts must face. Getting clean and staying clean means we do things a little differently each day. This is the basis for our strength. We dare to change - for the better. Some of the things we try don’t work out. There is a great advantage in living clean one day at a time. We don’t have to keep doing things that don’t work out. We share at meetings and carefully listen others. It is this cleansing learning process that produces recovery growth!

These ways of seeing the world have a way of hanging on for addicts. That is why N A is a day at a time program. Our personal list of N A basics are so important to the formation of “new habits.” How regularly we find ourselves thinking we have come quite a ways in recovery only to realize we were thinking unkind thoughts about other members who were not going the way we wanted them to go! We are lucky if we catch ourselves before we begin to work a vengeance on our fellows. When we forgive others, we may find we are really forgiving ourselves!

It’s a deep, deep hole that we have dug for ourselves. It takes a lot of gumption and faith to humble ourselves and pull ourselves up by our bootstraps. This is not an easy thing to do, but it’s simple. Anyone can get clean, you just have to want it more than anything else in the world.

[bs]

quote: “This is a Step of willingness. That is the spiritual principle of Step Six.”
body:
This principle is also what we find in Step Three. Without willingness what do we have? We must become willing and have a desire for us to get clean and to continue to stay clean. When we become ready to try a new way of life. Life has become easier rather than the pain of fighting.

Willingness is a state of readiness to get into the solution, replacing depression, anxiety and self-pity. There is a viewpoint that we do something to help others, something different, and something we hear or read about in recovery material each day. Each tiny step forward eventually add up to milestones in recovery.

Our NA recovery is all about willingness. The willingness to get outside of our comfort zone. The willingness to do assignments that make our skin crawl. The willingness to do what we are told regardless of whether or not we think it’s a waste of time. Sometimes it is just a matter of trying out something different - only to find that it really does work!

[MSG]

quote: “Spiritual and religious freedom is one of our most basic principles. Each of us are free to work out our own concept of God, or reject the concept of a God..”
body:
Like other freedoms, the power is in the right of choice. If our old ideas of a higher power did not produce the results we wanted, that is encouragement to go looking for a new understanding! Many of us come in to recovery, with the God of our parents or families. Take your time reading, studying and asking others what they believe. Whatever we choose, we stick with it in order to grow. If we add to our believe, that’s a good thing. If something in our belief is faulty, we change it. Not casually or capricially, but seriously for our lives and our futures depend on it.

Through the practice of regular simple prayers, we find that the powers found in the principles in the Second Step: Surrender, Faith, Trust, Hope, Sanity, begin to give us a more clear view and understanding of powers greater than ourselves. We begin to see these powers helping us almost immediately. We begin to see that other addicts have been helping us along. Eventually we begin to see signs of a more clear vision and develop a concept of God for ourselves. No one else can determine that for us. We begin to trust this Power as we go forward in our recovery.

One of the common problems we have with the Second Step is “how do you put something wordless and beyond this world into words?” Many members shy away from the ‘concept’ of God because of this semantic problem. Yet they can tell a difference when they experience conscious contact. They can experience the ‘presence’ of their Higher Power. The great fact of belief is THAT you believe, not what you believe. The writers of the Basic Text, Narcotics Anonymous, openly discussed this matter when considering the pronoun to be used in reference to the Higher Power. Should it be he, she, it, they or something else? English grammar clearly states that ‘Him’ refers to belief with no gender reference. So, ‘Him’ can be anything you want it to be. As long as It is loving and kind. Grammar books state that ‘Him’ refers to all human beings.

quote: “Sometimes, the best revenge we can have over a manipulative or unpleasant person, is in not allowing them to “pull our strings” by making us react unkindly to them.”
body:
No one, except ourselves, and our own character defects can get in the way of anyone else's behavior. The only thing that we can do is to practice the principles that we have learned in our own lives. This begins with the 1st Step. We have no power over another addict's behavior. They don’t have to listen to us unless they want to. We can, however be a positive example, by not reacting to negativity ourselves by feeding into theirs. This is certainly not always an easy thing to do. It takes practice surrendering to our own emotions so as not to react to our own feelings that we still carry with us. Sometimes the other person catches on that we are practicing principles. There must be a cosmic law that people react in kind.

Whether they stop their fooling around or not, we don’t have to dance to their tune! Power is the ability to define reality and we exercise that power now that we are clean. With the help of our Higher Power, we rise above old reactions and attempts to control us.

In Shaolin Temple training, the goal is mind like water. When a pebble is thrown into a pool how does it respond? Exactly correctly. Ripples go out, calm down and the water soon becomes calm again. The Shoalin Warrior must have his mind go back to calm after every attack upon himself. This way he is ready for the next one.

quote: “Remember, we can’t change other people. We can, through the program of Narcotics Anonymous, change ourselves.”
body:
We are powerless over people, places and things. When we were out there, we felt we had to control everything to avert disaster and get our pleasures. It was exhausting. When we get clean, surrendering is a very, very different mode of operation. It's 180 degrees from our old way. And not surprisingly it’s damn hard to do most of the time. But the results are great! If getting clean was easy there would be no drug epidemic. Sometimes it hurts really bad, but we gotta do it. No pain, no gain. Fortunately we don’t have to do it alone. And the proof is all around us. When we surrender to the disease concept, we stop fighting and that energy is put to better purposes.

We check in with our and practice taking things one day at a time. Jogging our memory and starting off in the recovery mindset each day brings us extra blessings and helps us see opportunities that might otherwise miss.

As most NA members know, the little things we do build up our recovery and lead to bigger things! The old ways of going for ‘newer, bigger, better, quicker, faster’ may bring us into close contact with brick walls we didn’t see coming. Slowing down a little and staying focused give us advantages. Like we have a better chance to see what’s coming in time to change it!

quote: “Being human we will of course fall short. We still get mad and still feel hurt, especially if we are too hungry, angry ,lonely, tired, or too serious (H.A.L.T.S).”
body:
While our human condition is subject to limitations, we learn and practice spiritual principles. We stop running into obstacles that trip us up and prevent our human joy and freedom. As addicts who have experienced hell on earth, happiness and comfort seem like impossible goals for us. HALTS gives us a simple way to identify where some of our negative feelings come from. We will catch ourselves thinking some negative stuff and realize it is only fatigue or hunger that tries to assign our internal feelings to some outside force. Taking better care of ourselves, we quickly recovery our senses and shake out heads.

One new addict was on a road trip with some seasoned addicts. The car broke down in a strange town. The older addicts decided to get something to eat and then deal with the car. This was a revelation for the new addict. A new way of doing things. A new tool. Doing things differently gets different results. Putting more pressure can break things and make it worse. We will never stop learning as long as we are putting the NA 12 Steps into action. After the meal, they all felt better and found a way to get the car fixed and got on the road again.

We are accustomed to adversity and having to fight for what we want or need. Taking a few breaths and making a quick prayer for strength and guidance lets us see past obstacles. Taking our foot off the accelerator and slowing down gives us time to think things through. You are at a time in your life when you can breathe easier and work with new approaches beyond what desperation allows!

quote: “Alienation and isolation are symptoms of the mental part of our disease. To the practicing addict, life is just a movement between connections and oblivion. Normal concerns are pushed aside, as the disease progresses.”
body:
The great French writer Jean Paul Sarte said,“Hell is other people.” One wonders if he was an addict. We often seek refuge in isolation. Sometimes life is overwhelming and we must go into our cave. We just can’t live there all the time. For some of us going to a meeting and mingling is our social time. This can be good because it gets us out of the narrow confines of our minds!. The meetings are peopled by persons who have learned to live without using and practice principles that open doors long closed to them

For our very survival it was important getting to NA. Now, we practice another form of survival, one that introduces ideas and practices that work in daily living. Before recovery, many of our sayings and terminology sounded weak and ineffectual. In recovery, we find principles that are stronger than steel and harder than a diamond! Once we realize what is happening to us, we want more, more and still more. Amazingly, our growth is endless. Helping others we discover that we feel compassion without feeling better or wiser than those we help. This is anonymity in a pure form. We no longer feel alone and needful. We feel fortunate and often amazed. These feelings make it easier for us to ask for help and try out new ways of living.

We know from our own experience that others feel the same way when they help us. A whole new world opens to us. Imagine if we were a wolf and had to make it alone! Friends make our world bigger. When we play like puppies, we learn rough and tumble without pain or hurt. So, with our new NA friends we jump and bounce feeling alive and wonderful. It takes time but now that we are clean, we have the time.

quote: “Three “musts” are honesty, open-mindedness, and willingness to try. Agnostics and atheists generally start out by just talking to “Whatever’s there.””
body:
This daily meditation stresses the importance of being open and experimental. We learn positive ways to investigate and check out things before committing ourselves. Because of our former limitations, we may not know we are free to really take a look around. We can read books, ask others to share their experience, and think over our experiences before adopting or committing ourselves. We each have our own personal understanding of a higher power. When we have our own belief, we begin to capitalize higher power. A Higher Power of your own understanding is the key to ongoing recovery and spiritual growth. Asking for help is a daily tool for living a spiritual life. It is the difference between a defeated person and a person filled with spirit.

Sometimes people call their spirit “The Force” or a “Presence.” It doesn’t seem as churchy. It takes the formality away. It seems more friendly and available. Religion has a lot of different connotations for people. Sometimes it can get confusing. We stress the right of our members to find their own Higher Power. We regularly remind ourselves and our members to avoid preaching or telling each other what to think. We share our experience from the past, our strength from today and our hope for the future.

Belief is what controls what we are open to. Sometimes it is like being able to tune in to a different tv or radio station. If our device is not in working order or we are having trouble with our channel selector, we have to snap out of our loss of service and restore our contact!

quote: “Personality change is a natural progression set in motion by our surrender to the program.”
body:
When we came into the Fellowship, we were full of fear and doubt. For many of our members surrender was a gradual process, not an event. We began to see our lives less chaotic when we slow down and learn to take it easy. If it’s not easy, something is wrong. We can save ourselves time and trouble by stopping and reduce the friction or fix what’s broken. It may be a simple misunderstanding. We begin to see that our efforts to control sometimes continue to make our lives unmanageable. As it says in our Prologue, “That at the end of the road, we can no longer function as a human being ,either with or without drugs.” We become more human.

Our lives can become unmanageable again when we are not practicing surrender. That is why the surrender is so important. When we put conditions on anything ahead of our recovery, we put limitations on our recovery.

One member was sharing in a group that he needed to know God’s will on taking a great job but which required working on the Sabbath. How could he know God’s will. An oldtimer said “Ask your sponsor. God’s words will come from his mouth as if he is an Oracle.” At this point the meeting secretary said “Kiss the ring”. This is being hip, slick and cool. We have not seen the person who made the suggestion since that time. Persons with quality recovery will go elsewhere if made fun of.

For example a guy came into a meeting and said that he had just gotten into it with his mother. The oldtimer simply said that he was sorry to hear that. It validated the original speaker. So how hard was that? The response spread unity instead of disunity.

quote: “Most addicts have strong feelings about their Higher Power and vigorously defend their right to their own understanding of a Higher Power. The strength to move into action comes from our Higher Power.”
body:
There is the illusion by non-believers that “higher power” is just an excuse to do as we please. The reality of life in the care of a loving, caring, all powerful being is not self-will. It lifts us to better things and gives us the strength and guidance to deal with the worst things that happen to us.

In NA our higher power is our friend, our buddy. This can be confusing to church goers. But many think this is what our HP craves. To be our very best friend. And it makes perfect sense. Why would God want barriers between Him and us. The phrase “God is my copilot” speaks volumes. We take road trips with our higher power. Talk to Him while we walk down the street. We don’t ask for stuff and become comfortable just hanging out with HP. Spirituality lets us see beyond selfish boundaries.

Our belief system is comforting and based on real life experience. It has to be located and practiced to become useful. Everything that comes up doesn’t have to be a crisis. Praying and meditating at the end of a day is a good time to focus and renew our belief in the ways of the God of our understanding.

quote: “We have also met members who are uncertain about where they really belong. We suggest that they ask themselves three questions: 1. What message do you carry? (What is the nature of your recovery and what have you recovered from?) 2. Who are you trying to carry this message to? 3. Where are you trying to carry this message?”
body:
Reviewing basic ideas gives us a chance to make sure we are on common ground with reality. We are great ones for charging off in directions composed of our own projections and negative expectations. Just because we run a definite risk of running off the road does not mean we have to run through a catalog of fear every time we get behind a wheel! Obsession can really mess up your perception of reality. The clean life requires a few adjustments! Compulsion means acting without thinking. When we do this, things can go from bad to worse in the blink of an eye.

Carrying a message of love and hope to an addict who really wants the program recharges our recovery batteries! We set up an energy exchange that works for both of us. There is a “Spirit” in the rooms of NA that guides us and is always ready to lend a hand when we ask for help. Asking for help is very important because we don’t boss people or treat them like we’re the royalty and they are our subjects. Membership is a great honor!

To carry our message, we have to have three things: 1. Experience with NA recovery, 2. An addict who wants our help and asks for it, and 3. An atmosphere of recovery that may be in a meetings place, a private phone call or two chairs and a little privacy.

quote: “In our groups we share our experience, strength, and hope, and this is our message - that an addict, any addict, can stop using drugs; lose the desire to use again; and find a new way to live. Their message is hope and the promise of freedom.”
body:
When an addict gives their opinions, rather than their personal experience ,strength, and hope to a newcomer, they may be forgetting the courage it takes to ask for help. When we allow our Higher Power to use us as instruments, respect, care and genuine concern are appropriate. We do not judge the addict asking for help. Addicts are very perceptive people and can see through the untruths. When we are honest and speak from the heart rather than our head. It is not our ego which provides the message of hope. Shared experience is usually clear, and carries the hope that we can give to another suffering addict.

One addict recently got into another twelve step group on some issue and wondered if it really worked. Then he realized that many newcomers were sitting in NA meetings wondering the same thing. Does this thing actually work or not. Is it possible to actually get clean. So from time to time we need to emphatically state that this program is 100% effective in every case if the person just follows direction, surrenders and works the tools with a sponsor. It’s a guaranteed program of recovery. The only way to fail is to not get involved up past your eyeballs. You have to want it more than anything else in the world. Period.

quote: “Our Fifth Tradition defines our primary purpose and our Sixth Tradition tells us some of the things we must do to preserve and protect this spiritual aim. This Tradition tells us that we ought never endorse, finance or lend the N.A. name to any outside enterprise. And then we are warned exactly what can happen if we ignore this advice.”
body:
If we do not adhere to these Traditions, if we violate them, then the recovery process stops. People go to meetings and such but don’t stay clean. That’s why it's said that our Traditions are non-negotiable.

Our Traditions are just as important as the Steps. A glass of water is water on the ground without the glass! One addict was in a town where Area Service Committee demanded that every meeting sign court slips, regardless of whether they believed it was affiliation or not. . Soon meetings began to go dark and people were getting loaded right and left. Each meeting was no longer autonomous so the miracle of recovery stopped.

Another meeting had people coming in with SCRAM units to blow into and had tv cameras on them to identify the person blowing. One guy began swinging the camera around the room taking everyone’s picture. It was not long till people quit going to that meeting because their anonymity was being violated. Some had warrants. The meeting eventually went dark.

We have to follow these Traditions. They are there for a reason. They guide our Fellowship by giving us boundaries that keep our vehicle out of the ditches!

quote: “The choice of membership rests with the individual.. We feel the ideal state for our fellowship exists when an addict can openly and freely come to an N.A. meeting; wherever and whenever they choose and leave just as freely if they want to. We realize that there is nothing we can do to make an addict stop using.”
body:
Without understanding and acceptance of this basic idea, the rest of our recovery process fails. Taking first Step makes us a member. The old saying “You can bring a horse to water , but you can’t make him drink.” Holds true. It can also be said that ,” we sure can make him thirsty.” Once again it comes to the Addicts willingness to get clean, stay clean, and find a new and better way to live.

We have some meetings filled with horses who are not thirsty. They make it hard for the thirsty one to survive. Forced attendance is the government’s idea of solving the problem. We are not affiliated, so these meetings fail the definition given in our Twelve Traditions. This is not an opinion, it is a reality. The impact on NA has been brutal and devastating. Many meetings have gone dark, oldtimers have fled and many have died.

The disease is baffling and so people and institutions can be expected to make mistakes. Once recovery becomes a business, it gets really confusing. The simple fact is we cannot play God and make addicts stop using. NA recovery is based on simple truths like this.

quote: “We may still, however, feel that we cannot have a happy life without drugs. We may suffer from the fear of insanity and feel we have no escape from using other than an insane and depressed existence.”
body:
By staying clean and going to meetings, we stop many of the things that set us up for failure and confusion. It doesn’t take too long before we begin to take care of our basic needs, have a roof over our heads, food in our bellies, and begin to see and to identify with other addicts. We listen to others share their experiences and we can identify. We can see that they too have very similar feelings and that we are not alone. Sometimes we have to stand in a room for a while before we can see, hear and feel others in the room with us.

Identification is what we need to feel a part of the group. Then we can see that we are not as bad as we thought we were. This keeps us coming back. As we put together clean time, many unsolvable problems resolve themselves. The stability we establish in our lives is infectious in a good way. It bleeds over to problem areas of our lives, bills, taxes, teeth, cars. Everything stabilizes at the rate at which we work the tools: surrender, faith and positive prayer with action.

The disease of addiction is powerful and seeps into our lives from time to time. We have tools that help us deal with the disease on a daily basis! It all begins with surrender but develops into ten thousand pathways of recovery. One of the worse mistakes we can make is to think recovery is optional and take it for granted. Recovery is the result or product of our desire. We cause our recovery by seeking and absorbing new forms of goodness and positive action into our lives.

quote: “What a change from how we used to be! That’s how we know that the N.A. program works.”
body:
Time, and our disease of addiction, will constantly work to make us forget and ignore all our many blessings. Some of us stop working the program that worked for them in the beginning. Addiction never sleeps, like rust. Even if the sayings and catchy phrases get boring and repetitions, we keep working the program. Many of us write our gratitude lists to maintain our awareness of the new things that could never have happen to us when we were dirty (using). When we work on getting better, often others can see a change in us before we can see it in ourselves. Slowly we realize we are not losing our temper as often, gossiping as much or lying quite as easily. Prayer makes good effects in our daily lives. Praying for strength and guidance is one of the best prayers we can practice on a daily basis.

It is said that we get an extra second for every year clean before we go off on someone. This gives us time to walk away if nothing else. It does get easier, much easier. But the rate is full of fits and starts. It’s not an even sort of healing. It can be two steps forward and one step back. Much of this is our Higher Power teaching us lessons. Lessons on how to live life and help each other. Growth is not optional, it is a result of the spiritual life.

We must embrace every flat tire and broke shoelace. God cannot teach us all lessons by showering us with endless gifts. Some of them have to be learned thru the school of hard knocks.

As a boy, one member received wonderful Christmas gifts from his dad who had a good job. Electric train set, Three speed English Bike, etc. Every year the boy would make up his list and every year it would be under the tree. But one year he asked for a Michigan Crane. A big metal Tonka like Crane with steerable front wheels and outriggers for stability. It even had a hook and a clamshell. Upon inspection, Christmas Day he saw it was not under the tree. In its place was a wood burning set. The father said to the son, “Lets take a walk.” On the walk, the father explained that by showering his son with gifts, he was spoiling him. The boy nodded but still felt upset. Later, in college, the boy had a beautiful girlfriend. She dumped him for the star football player. It was then that the boy understood what the old man was telling him. You can’t always get what you want. Being grateful for what you have precedes being grateful for what you get.

quote: “We had nothing left to lose. We gave up… quit struggling...surrendered, completely and unconditionally. Then and only then did we begin to recover from the disease of addiction.”
body:
Paradoxically, with this complete unconditional surrender, we started to find some power. The resources we had depleted fighting our addiction started to rebuild - giving us whatever we needed to start recovering. Literally, from the very beginning, we are guided to what is most necessary for our eventual happiness and release from our disease. All those reservations are like little hooks that hold us back. We might as well look at recovery as a magic kingdom where all the rules are remade in our favor. Because we let go, it is understood we are free to rebuild our fractured lives.

Look at the words in the Memphis Review Form exerpt: “quit struggling, surrendered, completely and unconditionally.” If we hold back in any way, it creates a hole in the blanket of our recovery. We remain weak and vulnerable in that area. If it is old habits, there you go. If it is financial, that’s easy to see. If we still fear and distrust others, we will find it hard to make progress in that area.

All the things we recommend in our recovery program are designed to help us cast off from the dock of active addiction and enter the sea of personality change. Often. We are so nuts the release from stress and anxiety of pain and confusion leads us to imagine nothing is happening. Hey, didn’t anyone tells you to get ready for few differences when you start working the NA 12 Steps? There will always be some addicts who try to wiggle out of full and complete surrender. You can watch them and decide if they have what you want! If you target higher consciousness and raised vibrations, you can start by finding ways to accept them and finding ways to help them.

quote: “The Steps guide us into our place in society. We begin by straightening our internal disorder and obtaining release from the defects that prevent us from being all we should be.”
body:
While ‘should’ is often times regarded as a bad word, the idea is still pretty useful. Perhaps it could be rephrased to read, “If we want powerful results and noticeable changes for the better, it is recommended that we go at recovery with a whole heart and an open mind.”

It is useful and humorous to say, “I will not ‘should’ on myself today.” however if you think you are to first addict to try to get the goodies of the clean life without giving up the old games and trick your way into a new life, you might want to think that through a little bit. What is the cause and effect? If I do so-and-so, what will happen? If I follow the pathway taken by so many others addicts just like me, I might just get what these good people are recommending to me.

After all our games and attempts to live the wild and crazy life failed, perhaps it is time for a change! Isolation did not work for us either. Is it really so hard to be loving and kind? Time spent with others like ourselves changes our self-image as we become part of humanity again. Recent good experiences has great power over our painful past. Surrender stops the accumulation of bad outcomes. Finding a loving, caring Higher Power can redirect all our energies into correcting the errors of our past and check out what has worked for countless others.

quote: “Recovery is a beautiful chance that many addicts thought had passed them by until they found the Fellowship of N.A. It’s a chance to live again.”
body:
Right now, alive in the present and living fully! It is a new day and we commit to the courage we need to step up to the challenges and opportunities we find rising up to meet us. The longer we are clean and the more we let ourselves experience the miracles of personality changes and spiritual growth, the more we can accept and believe a whole new life opens up to us one day at a time!

We see people that love themselves, love their lives, have dreams, are pursuing dreams and most importantly, achieving dreams. They assure us that we, too, can have this. We start by having attainable goals like getting one more clean time tag. Then, maybe, we get a job or a car or a bank account.

Then the day of ultimate success, we have completed the Steps for the first time and have achieved a spiritual awakening. We slowly realize we have become people that love ourselves and our lives. We are pursuing and achieving our dreams and most importantly, we are capable of helping others reach for their new lives. A miracle has occurred. Don’t get off the bus just because in your opinion it’s not going fast enough! Compared with the hopelessness and despair we come from, the 12 Steps move us forward at the speed of light.

quote: “The essence of addiction is that it is easier to change our perception of reality than the reality we perceive.”
body:
Living clean switches all that around. Changing reality means the changes we acquire in recovery stay put and need less correction as we go along in our new lives. Imagining we are in any position to judge the results of our investment in our personal program belies the facts that led us to our First Step! Remember being broken with no help in sight? Remember trying to cover our tracks with tearful admissions of fault to those who loved and cared about us only to turn around and do the same things again? Remember wondering what is wrong with us without seeing the connection between our actions and the consequences of those actions?

We may have some amends to make but that is infinitely better than endless feelings of guilt and shame and all the avoidance patterns that go with unmade amends. When we humbly ask our personal Higher Power to remove our defects of character, the bad stuff just stops happening. Don’t let your disease blindside you into fallen back into your old ways. We have seen good members fall into this predictable trap many times. Relapse begins when we stop praying for help.

It may help to consider how we lacked internal resources to do better in life. Without patience, humility and tolerance, we are jerking people around, puffing up with arrogance and kicking people when they aren’t doing too well. Our ego can blind us to the reality that we are inflicting the same unworthy treatment on ourselves without realizing it. Keeping our cool and doing push-ups of gratitude builds our spiritual strenght to deal with the good things appearing in our lives.

quote: “The autonomy of our groups is one of our most precious possessions. This sounds great but what does it mean? What is it to be autonomous? Webster’s defines autonomous as “having the right or power of self government,” “undertaken or carried on without outside control,” “existing or capable of existing independently,“ “ responding, reacting or developing independently of the whole.”
body:
There is a spirit that develops among individuals dedicated to a common cause or purpose. Common understanding and agreement results in unison of action and purpose that works to get the desired result. It takes freedom of belief and action to achieve this. Addicts who want to stay clean and enjoy a life in recovery should by all means be free to do so.

A classical example of our God given ability to find ways to disagree is over where we say, “a half dozen or six.” Of course, it makes no real difference and with a little effort most people can see this but there of those who insist on their terminology and reject any variation out of hand. Considering word choice helps us understand more deeply the meaning of words in recovery. This openness is good. Insisting others accept and adopt our terms of expression is dogmatic and insulting. We don’t want cookie-cutter recovery. The very essence of real and lasting recovery is being able to hear others and share our own feelings as best we can,

If getting clean, staying clean, and helping others is why we go to meetings, we stand on common group and are likely to be effective. Needless conflicts should not ruin our efforts to achieve our primary purpose. Spiritual maintenance brings us back to the wellspring of kindness again and again.

quote: “Recovery begins with the first admission of powerlessness. From that point forward, we can see that every clean day is a successful day, and that any seeming failure is only a temporary setback.”
body:
Our disease of addiction enlarges our painful past through the miracle of tunnel vision. Time with clean addicts, reading recovery writing from the NA point of view and the search for a loving God of our own understanding renew our lives. Personality change doesn’t seem strong enough to cover the way we step into a new life, one day at a time. Many of us cling to our new existence as if our lives depend on waking up in a new world because it does!

Sometimes all we get done on certain days is to just sit on our hands and stay clean. These can the some of the most successful day of our lives. One addict wanted to use and sat on the couch at detox for three days till it passed. There may have been the most successful days of his life. He now had over thirty years clean. Looking back it seems incredible yet it happened. Spiritual growth goes on with a steady series of awakenings. We gain new understandings both from our freak new understandings as well as the wonderful way discovery re-defines our worst memories.

In some strange way, our bodies and our minds rebuild what was once broken. It is internal, Lost dreams appear anew right before our very eyes. The life that comes to us through vibrations of sight, sound and body rearrange themselves into clean, healthy patterns.

quote: “Our deepest longings and recurring images of the kind of people we’d like to be are only glimpses of God’s will for us.”
body:
Later in this paragraph, we read, “It is our own real dreams that come true.” To continue the idea that many people seem to know intuitively. God is in all of us. The idea that a supreme all knowing and all powerful creator lives within each of us seems overwhelming to begin with. But as a solution to all our living problems and the resolution to all our thinkings, it certainly fulfills the criteria.

Our 12th Step makes clear that to carry our message, the disease concept keeps us from wandering off on a tangent mis-using notions of spirituality. We are more apt to rationalize what God told us than to relinquish control of our life and will! Prayer and meditation open doorways to a new reality that most of us only saw occasionally, if at all! Drugs messed up our minds and we would lose track of our spiritual direction as it flew past us.

After a great deal of dust has settled, we find ourselves on track with God’s Will for us. As progress is made from that point forwards, we enjoy a deep sense of accomplishment. It’s like the feelings we get at the end of solving an interlocking puzzle. Many of the pieces are already in place and then the next pieces fairly jump into place! Or perhaps we go to a special place to pray and meditate. After a while, just going there gives us a sense of completion and satisfaction by association. .

quote: “The answer, of course, is that these things are not N.A. They are services that we can utilize to help us in our recovery and further the primary purpose of our groups.”
body:
Clarity of mind and heart-felt feelings help us distinguish between reality and fantasy. Those who get caught up in symbols can miss out on the real thing. A recovery group or member has some claim to conscious contact and therefore some claim to divine guidance as opposed to personal preference in matters affecting others.

The notion that we pray before voting in a service body seems to be more of an afterthought rather that a guiding principle. “Directly responsible” is lost in the shuffle. Real service makes a member feel good inside. Emotions of love, gratitude and a touch of wonder let us know the Spirit of NA is using us as instruments.

NA politics have run off more members than the disease of addiction - or maybe they are the same thing. When we put personal preference ahead of group purpose, it is safe to say God is out of the picture. Service is doing positive things to help addicts just like us get to a meeting and have the tools we need for a lasting recovery. Jockeying for a service position can deceive us Into violating principles and doing unseemly things to get elected. Actions unfounded in God’s Will for us push us away from our Higher Power. Our disease is based on fear and so we expect to be disappointed in some way at some future time. Seeking to put God in a jar, we change the nature of what we love and care about when we try to trap and control what once was free.

quote: “When we came to N.A., we were physically, mentally and spiritually bankrupt. We hurt long enough and badly enough that we were willing to go to any lengths to stay clean.”
body:
Nothing motivates us like pain and, boy, did we hurt. We became willing to follow suggestions we didn’t completely understand because of hope. Hope offered by N A members we recognized as fellow addicts who were obviously no longer in pain but enjoying life. It was their continued assurances that we could get better, too, that helped us find the courage to go on for one more day, one more hour or maybe just one more heartbeat.

We learned that gratitude defeats self-pity. Mountainous problems become small molehills when we accept the circumstances and look for solutions. Life doesn’t need chaos to have pleasure.

The worse we hit our bottoms, even in early recovery, the more we think “I don’t want to ever go thru this again”. And they do what it takes to stay clean: work the Steps. Positive actions take the impulse of goodness and make it into a reality in our daily lives.

quote: “Remarkably, the Twelve Steps guide us from a state of humiliation and despair to a state wherein we are able to act as instruments of God’s will.”
body:
“We have seen many miracles thru people working the Twelve Steps. I have seen people learn to read, get advanced degrees and regain their health and families. I have seen severely mentally ill people become whole again in recovery. I have seen hopeless addicts stay clean for many decades. People who could not stay clean a day on their own, never use again on a daily basis.

“We have seen one addict start a meeting that some thirty years later is still helping people hear the message and learning to live clean. “I have seen recovery sweep thru families like wildfire. I saw all four brothers get clean one by one and stay clean.”

The power of NA, if handled properly is one of the most powerful forces on earth. It can move mountains and heal the masses.

The expanding drug epidemic will be a wakeup call as to the effectiveness of NA in the past. At least within our Fellowship, we are aware that there are hundreds of thousands of clean addicts in the world today. Right now NA is in a difficult point in time. Traditions have not been followed, group conscious has not been honored and many have gotten loaded on money, property and prestige. Some believe that the drug epidemic would not have happened if NA had been left alone to do its work.

When the dust has settled some ten years from now, the errors of our leaders will be blaringly obvious and their corruption as hangable offenses. The drug epidemic could have all been prevented quite easily had the NA way been followed. Perhaps the millions who will be dying will be a lesson that we have to tether our trusted servants before they get addicted to power.

quote: “There is a danger that we will minimize or rationalize away our part in situations.”
body:
To do our inventory, we may use a practice that works for many experienced members. Just divide the process into two or more parts so you can write out whatever comes to mind and when you stop writing, take a short break and then review what you have written. You have plenty of time to add and remove so that your input makes good sense, at least to you! The trouble with minimizing or rationalizing our inventory is it gives the illusion that you have done an inventory! At best you have begun the inventory process. Your life, the quality of your life, is at stake here. When we addicts begin to feel badly, we are apt to go into a downhill plunge that ends in relapse. Just monitoring and tracking things that bother us as we go along our way allows us the opportunity to change.

If we get out of the habit of daily inventory, we suggest just resuming the process. The idea that we are ‘behind’ just makes us confused and may act to keep us from even trying!

We are masters at finding scapegoats to blame. It’s always someone else’s fault. In rationalization, it’s never wholly our fault. We are Monday morning quarterbacks. We could have run the Viet Nam War better, prevented 9/11 and had JFK put on the bubble top. We would have stopped Hitler early on and radioed the Titanic to head south into warmer waters. We are legends in our own minds.

This seems to be one of the defining attributes of being an addict, this finger pointing and attributing blame to others. We have our work cut out just addressing this one defect, the character defect or ‘blame assessment.”

quote: “We give freely and gratefully of our time, our services and our experiences to our fellow addicts.”
body:
Giving freely is one of the most effective tools of NA. We give without expectation of reimbursement. If we give till it hurts, then we must be getting some real results. One addict bought three gallons of baby blue paint to paint a meeting room. He is still clean today.

One addict went to speak in another city and refused a motel room. He slept in his car and ate at Jack in The Box. This made his speech very powerful as people saw him asleep in his car the night before and then he walked up to the podium. Quiet sincerity can sometimes be heard over the roar of a crowd.

It is safe to say, our program rests on our ability to help and be helped. Helping others not only feels good, it gives us feelings of joy and self-worth. These feeling encourage us to work the 12 Steps of NA and so guide each of us to our own spiritual awakening.

quote: “Narcotics Anonymous is a Fellowship of men and women, addicts, meeting together in groups, and using a given set of spiritual principles to find freedom from addiction and a new way to live. All else is not N.A.”
body:
This simple statement has been made to appear disunifying and obscure enough to be removed by three members before the first publication of our Basic Text, Narcotics Anonymous. We hope through time and personal growth we will come to a high level of basic agreement.

The importance of limiting perceived power in our service bodies is great. The huge majority need not be restricted to the level of a very few members. Even if they are gifted or full of noble sentiments, they do violence by setting themselves above the individual members and groups who wrote and reviewed the Basic Text.

If they have trouble seeing this simple point, it illustrates the importance of having a general and agreeable basic understanding in our written recovery material. Else readers will sooner or later take issue with offending items they fail to understand and some will leave N A.

It is simple enough to write in an inclusive manner. Giving undue excessive to trusted servants who surely may have problems understanding and maintaining proper boundaries for the sake of our common welfare. After all, most trusted servants have leeway to do their job and ask for help as needed.

Whereas who takes up for the members if excesses occur or accumulate? Irreparable harm may continue for years without correction. All because a few ‘powerful’ members take it upon themselves to disregard the deep consideration that went into the original wording of the 12 Traditions. They really don’t understand. They take off in a direction based on their lack of understanding. They choose instead personal profit and gain.

quote: “This Tradition is the basis for our policy of nonaffiliation and is extremely important to the continuation and growth of N.A. Unfortunately, this Tradition has also been a point of controversy within our Fellowship.”
body:
Tradition Six warns us in plain wording that money, property and prestige threaten our recovery. Simple logic makes it clear that affiliation with outside groups or organizations opens a door to encroachment and unending problems will ensue. Our primary group purpose is essential, not optional, to our recovery process.

If we make a mistake and affiliate and do not believe we have done so, the recovery process still stops. We simply cannot violate the Traditions and get results. Its poison to the recovery process. When we are new to recovery, it’s hard to grasp this.

In San Francisco in 1983 they wanted us to exchange needles in our meetings to prevent spread of disease among those who were more likely to relapse. We had to refuse as it was an outside issue. We could not have an opinion on it and could not appear to be endorsing it. We had to walk away. Otherwise, many, many more would die than from dirty needles. The message would not be effectively carried and the destruction would have been massive.

quote: “A direct endorsement is often used to try and persuade someone to do something.”
body:
Isn’t persuading someone to do something against their will the same as manipulation? And isn’t manipulation along with control a primary symptom of our disease of addiction? This is part of what Tradition Six is warning us about? We have to stake our own area of focus or concern to have a Program of recovery.

When something is being endorsed, people can smell it. It blares out at them. Addicts are particularly sensitive to this.

When one member came in, none of the meetings were in churches as it was feared that an implied endorsement might be sent to newcomers. No, you do not have to be a Methodist to belong to NA or even a Christian or other religion. All are welcome with respect and appreciation. We have among our recovering membership people who are uncomfortable with calling their Higher Power God! Heck, being an atheist is fine for some. Now that’s open mindedness. They may or may not change their position on belief in time. That’s freedom to find a God of their understanding.

We stay back from direct alignment in any form because it would generate the same lack of freedom and belief. Since we want all addicts to be free to come to NA for help, our policy is ‘open to all.’ While some later modify their belief, other do not and no one says a thing.

quote: “The third thing warned against is lending the N.A. name. This means letting someone use the name, Narcotics Anonymous, for something that is not Narcotics Anonymous. It also means letting an outsider mention or utilize our name for their own purposes.”
body:
One Federal program points out that they send their charges to Narcotics Anonymous. Is this a violation? There is actually controversy over this issue which seems clear cut to many.

NA can be so effective that it can be touted as elements of other programs.

The problem with the Federal government program is that they never talk to us, they just send people. We have no idea what goes on with them and vice versa. It’s a total lack of communication. This is simply what we have decided is acceptable.

While we do this we see our meetings going dark and our recovery rate crashing. But because the disease is baffling, people can’t see what’s really happening. In Kansas City, Missouri, we have gone from 94 meetings to 16 meetings per week. The number of meetings keeps falling. NA works as a stand-alone program of recovery. Affiliation modifies our Program of recovery based on the truth of our experience. .

quote: “We who were unable to function as human beings now find anything is possible for us. Those dreams we gave up long ago can now become realities with God’s help.”
body:
“One member had an American Flyer electric train as a child. Every year he would receive a new catalog from AC Gilbert about the trains and accessories. He would go thru the catalogues so much that it would finally fall apart. When he got clean he went into a hobby store on his lunch hour and picked up a model railroad magazine. Listed there was an American Flyer Club in his city. He began going and bought most of the dream equipment from his childhood.” This is just one example of demolishing our past pain points!.

We usually require a reminder to jolt us into remembering past dreams, dreams gobbled up by our addiction. Finding and fulfilling these dreams can have unforeseen positive effects. Remember how amazing our nervous system is and how our memories work over decades of time! It may be that disappointment is buried deep within our memories and actualizing them may awaken other remembrances and continually enlarge parts of our personalities.

Free your mind to remember your own life lessons! Our society knows little or nothing of recovery. No need to allow uninformed persons to set boundaries for us. Contact with those who have experienced miracles of recovery, will help you experience the same miracles. “Lost dreams awaken.” is a fact of life in recovery!

Remember also, each day clean is a miracle and an important part of recovery is daily renewal. What we were ready and open to yesterday may have changed. We owe it to ourselves to ask God to give us nice wake up calls on what the day could hold lor us!

quote: “We know that the more eagerly we wade in and work to stay clean, the higher we’re going to get and the richer our spiritual awakening will be!”
body:
“The first step to recovery is to stop using” The first sentence found in the Chapter Five in our Basic Text.That is where we begin our recovery. Going to a meeting a day for the first ninety days is suggested. The more meetings we go to, we see a change happening . We begin to feel hope, and begin to see, and feel change. We feel more comfortable in our skin.

As we continue going to meetings each day,hours become days, days become weeks, weeks become months and, before we know it a year has past. Our involvement increases, and we see others participating in service.We feel a part of and no longer feeling alone. As we begin to get to know other members,we find new friends who truly care about us. A feeling of peace comes over us , and we are actually having a spiritual awakening right before our eyes. Growth and freedom comes with surrender. No longer fearful or afraid, other people sense our calm. We are no longer hemmed in by a bad attitude. Just as negative begets negative, peace begets peace.

We work with our sponsor, learning and writing our steps, talking with others, “How was it for you?” We find it easier to look within ourselves with the help of a loving God of our understanding, and our sponsor. The companionship of fellow members is encouraging. Helping others overcome the invisible habits of mind and body language, we find our own habits are attractive, not repelling.

It helps to know that the 12 Steps of NA lead us to our very own spiritual awakening. Imagine that, we’re going to be free to live a life of the spirit. Is that a miracle or what?

quote: “Spiritual principles are basic truths that do not change with time or place; they simply work in all cases.”
body:
Universality is characteristic of spiritual reality. This may be a new idea for many of us. Just think how many situations we can utilize the principle of surrender! Humility cancels out all or most of the conflicts we have with others. Patience allows reality to rearrange itself to include our own personal choices! What we focus on tends to materialize before us because we select for whatever it takes to actualize. It seems clear when we are clean. The things that used to block our way forward are simply gone.

Unfortunately, the same spiritual function can bring into existence things we fear, if that’s what we focus on! This is part of what we mean when we say, “Watch what you pray for!” Praying for specific things narrows the range of our focus. Although there are no guidelines for what we pray for, we do have cautions. Some blessings are more expensive than others! In the course of things, we find what works for us!

A principle is a fundamental truth or proposition that serves as the foundation for a system of belief or behavior or for a chain of reasoning. Spiritual principles come from the spirit world have to be experienced to become part of our working, everyday life. Spiritual reality is eternal, worldly reality is transitory.

quote: “When we were told in meetings we were flirting with relapse, we had to make a decision. We had reservations about anything which did not describe us to a tee and built these up until we thought we could use again.”
body:
What a disease! The stupid stuff we use to build long standing resentments against other members is incredible. We don’t have to worry about outside issues disrupting N A as long as the ties that bind us together remain strong. Lies and gossip can weaken those ties! Most all of us have sponsors with much more clean time and recovery than we do. They tell us of the painful times they have seen otherwise good members fight and carefully build resentments based on rumor and gossip that last for decades. Maybe someday we will be able to address all these issues but until then we have to pray for strength and guidance to not fall into the same traps ourselves!

How clear can you get? Unless we connect our using with our pain, we cannot work the First Step. The admission of our powerlessness is the basis for all recovery. The person who cannot ask for help is helpless. Those who ask for help get help. When we are learning about N A, we also learn to monitor our progress. In recovery, we find we are our own worst enemies because we have not been able to see or hear clearly. This keeps us from sorting out our feelings properly.

Recovery is like combing your hair. Uncombed, our hair gets matted and twisted. Combing only works for a little while, then you have to comb some more! Inventory only works for a little while too! The closer we look, the more we find.

quote: “When we realized this had related to our new group oriented way of life we often experienced a sort of gut-level panicky feeling.”
body:
The panicky feeling occurs when a change challenges a long held belief or attitude. It may take time to understand where these feelings come from. Change requires faith and courage. Many times what we lose is replaced by something better and more workable. Interlocking with others can be scary to us loners. But there is strength in the warmth of others near us.

Depending on people we are just getting to know can feel like risky business. In ‘real’ N A, we come to know down deep that those who are really working their 12 Steps have a vested interest in recovery and live the program like their very lives depended it. Their words and actions reflect the changes from within our mind and hearts.

Not everyone is able to jump into recovery this way. They hold back from habitual fear that is based on their past experiences. N A recovery is only available for those who work our 12 Steps. Recovery is not an everyday miracle. It is the result of faith, experience and positive intention.

This is where a good sponsor or home group can help. Sponsoring others helps us as well. We need to look at recovery from every angle. The NA Program seems to grow as our knowledge and personal experience reveals just how wonderful recovery is.

quote: “Relapse is a sign we have had reservations in our program. We slighted our program and left loopholes in our daily lives. Aware of the pitfalls ahead, we trod blindly on in the belief we could make it on our own.”
body:
Most underestimate their disease and then relapse as not enough effort was put into staying clean. But to the addict with a week clean, they feel that they have mastered recovery. In the old days the ratio of oldtimers to newcomers favored long clean time. It was so overwhelming that newer members could not argue with the oldtimers. Now the ratio is reversed and newbies explain that “they have got this”. All we can do is sit back and watch the passing parade as we pray and do service to NA on a daily basis..

Our addictive disease expresses itself outwardly in so many ways, it is surprising how much has to do with honesty, open-mindedness and the willingness to try. Perhaps it is the built-in memory of a painful past that continues to build up protective walls long after the need for them is gone. Respect for our own past pain helps make us patient with the pain of another member who has come to us for help.

In certain cases there has been a purge of oldtimers when the money, property and prestige caused uninformed leadership to fear being corrected. This war on oldtimers was very effective and today we are feeling the pain some 30 years later. Even to this day, many, many long time members will not go to meetings remembering what happened back then.

Our most valuable, dedicated, enlightened and generous members were shunned and effectively thrown out of NA. But the joke is on the ones that chased them out for now people are waking up that they have been bamboozled. What is coming is not a pretty sight.

quote: “The breakdown of old ideas and old ways seems to be beyond our conscious control. The only control of the situation we seem to have is a choice of acceptance.”
body:
Acceptance allows us to take all sides of a concern into account and take whatever action seems to be indicated. Critical thinking is one of the abilities that comes with recovery. Critical thinking is not necessarily negative. It just holds up something like a thought, feeling or perception to the light of a new understanding. We can then decide to include or exclude items once we have had the opportunity to think it over. What we learn increases our ability to learn more. One of the “pay offs” of learning is we get to a point where we can talk about almost anything without fear or anger. By doing these things we are no longer chained or limited to our past low levels of understanding. We choose to accept only things that we have gone over in our hearts and minds.

We learn to be patient and wait for the next sign from our HP. We want to move forward, but often we must wait. That is how it works. The messages sometimes come from out of the blue like bolts of lightning. Then we must be ready, willing and able to go to any lengths. Sometimes these ideas come from people’s mouths.

One addict bought a beautiful hot food server from a steamship at an antique show. It was surprisingly cheap and the seller explained the low price by saying that it was missing the insert or tray. He went on to say that Pyrex used to make these but had stopped and so the trick would be to find an old one of the right size. The addict thought “Piece of cake. I will find one”. But months later had had no luck.

Discouraged, he asked a woman in an antique store how he might solve his delima. She said to go to a certain kitchen store. The addict went there, although they sold no antiques. The kitchen store owner told the addict to go to a restaurant supply store at 783 Mission Street. The addict went then and at that address was a beauty supply store. He went in and inquired and was told the Restaurant supply store had moved to China Basin.

The addict went to China Basin and found the huge Restaurant supply store. He went in and told them his problem. He was told to follow one of the salesmen who explained that the trays were actually made of metal. He pointed the addict to two 7’ high stacks of these trays..one regular and one that was heavy duty. The addict did the footwork and was persistent.

quote: “We believe the solutions for the problem of having drug-fogged minds, sick bodies and tormented emotions is in a spiritual way of life.”
body:
Spiritual means learning about the things that affect how we think, feel or act. Anger, resentment, judgemental attitudes, allowing ourselves to focus on what is bothering us rather that looking for new ways of looking at things will generally put us in a bad mood. Focusing on what we have to be grateful for, seeking help from our sponsor or other members we trust, making a gratitude list and a whole lot of other different ways to process our feelings, improve our spiritual condition and open the many doors that will lead us out of our misery and ingratitude.

Many members are addicted to their powerlessness. Reading material on prayer and meditation can put us in touch with others seeking to improve their lives by following a spiritual path. Old experiences with religion and hokey non-sense may bar the door to new ideas and ways to improve our condition. Spiritual involves our quality of life.

A spiritual way of life is acquired by prayer, meditation, smiling and putting ourselves in a good mood by recalling the many things we have to be grateful for in our new lives. We used to be powerless but when we admitted it, we gained the power to change our lives. We could seek and find a higher power we could trust and get the answers we needed. Conscious contact changes everything!

quote: “We emphasize setting our own house in order and trying to do more than our part. We trust in our Higher Power to give us strength and to meet our needs.”
body:
Making the bed in the morning, listing monthly bills and sitting down to pay them once a month, remembering and honoring our commitments is keeping our house in order. Inventory, prayer and meditation daily keeps our spiritual house in order. Study and learning keeps our mental house in order. These good habits wipe out most of our day to day concerns.

When unexpected demands come up, we learn to pray immediately for strength and guidance. This too changes everything. Enjoying conscious contact makes solutions and energy available to meet almost every need. We get extra help from our friends and loved ones.

Trust comes from ongoing positive expectation based on our own good experience. Rust is decomposition. T-rust is the opposite. T-rust is accumulated positive experience in recent clean time! Solutions in one area tend to apply in other nearby locations or situations. Trust becomes one of our most prized and useful internal resources.
