quote: “It takes some of us a while to realize how unmanageable we were and are still. For others this is the only thing that (of) which we can be sure.”
body:
The First Step lets us know that we are powerless and our lives were unmanageable. It was easily evident that the lives that we had led brought us to places we hadn’t planned on going. They led us to jails, institutions, near death. We also witnessed the death of our friends. After getting clean and learning to live clean, we experience the care, concern, and love from other addicts. They had lived unmanageable lives just as we had. Our stories and experiences may have been different, however, the pain, shame, and woundedness that we lived all feel the same . Living clean, going to meetings and getting personal information on other addicts seeking recovery rounds out our picture of ‘unmanageability’ to include things we could not see before. Many things we realize we are doing some of these things even in our new lives. Seeing and acknowledging these things enlarges our awareness and gives us an opportunity to self-correct before they get any worse.

The recovery process leads us to expand our recovery until we can hardly recognize our new selves. Learning creates it own energy, like a ball rolling downhill. We literally do not know what it feels like to pay our bills on time until we do it for a while! When we are poor and disorganized, we get pulled over for tails light that have burnt out and a thousand other things that happen when our resources are low. After a few years clean and working our NA Program, we pull to the side of the road with the words, “What seems to be the problem officer.” People who haven’t been to jail don’t usually know that policemen can tell just by looking at us!

quote: “Our motivation is simple; this program was given freely to us by addicts who cared. We only do the same.”
body:
The newly recovering addict deserves to get the same chance we got, a clear message of recovery. We need to focus on that one person who needs to hear the message of recovery. The other ones who are talking and playing grab ass should not prevent the eager newcomer from hearing a clear message of recovery.

It can be hard for us to not walk away from the chaos of some NA meetings, but the interested newcomer should not be punished for it. Ours is a cunning, baffling disease and defies recovery. Newer members can tell if we really care and hear their questions about recovery. Just stay tuned into what they are telling you they are ready to hear about. Their questions are a good clue.

Attention and appreciation are things all people crave. We respect the courage it takes to walk through that meeting room door. When they share their pain and condustion, they can tell if we are paying attention. We know their pain. It seems painful to even ask for help when we have been disappointed so often.

quote: “In the Twelfth Step, we practice the spiritual principle of giving away the N.A. message of recovery in order to keep it.”
body:
We learn thousands of tiny lessons on the road to recovery. They all add up to create a loving, caring human being. Instead of being obsessed with our own problems and issues, we have lots of time to take with those who need help. Helping others burns in the lessons and we always find ways to apply or re-apply the lessons to ourselves.

Surgeons have a saying about performing surgical operations: “See one, do one, teach one.” Point being, you must jump in with both feet. You must teach others to really learn the subject matter. The first step is study and learning. The second is putting into action that which you got from books. The third is developing deeper insight by helping others learn from you.

In a similar fashion, we must carry the message of recovery to keep ourselves clean. It’s why we are clean, the only reason we are clean according to some people. Learning facts without application is dead. Acting on learning makes it real to us and our bodies remember learning put into practice.

quote: “We have learned the value of teaching by example rather than direction. This has worked for us and we utilize this principle when we work with other addicts. Our message is most obvious in our lives.”
body:
If we are paying attention, they pay attention. If we seek to understand and ask good questions, they do too. If we are somewhat selfless, they can do that too. In NA we take advantage of these basic human traits to get good results in letting people we know and we care. They can feel it.

So, just by working a good program, we are encouraging them to be honest, open and willing to try. They emulate us and our actions. Later, they will apply what they see and hear from us to encourage another new person. Just do it! It feels good and it is good.

This honest, genuine human experience is what becomes rare as our disease of addiction progresses. We are hungry for caring and sharing and that’s the basis of carrying our message.We don’t lie, cheat and steal our way into their hearts, we just give them the truth of our experience instead of telling them what to do!

quote: “Promotion is representing ourselves as something we are not. In order to accomplish something we want, we don’t use promotion to encourage addicts to come to us and we don’t use promotions to make ourselves more acceptable. Our successes speak for themselves.”
body:
Attraction rather than promotion is a main difference between us and religious organizations. Our antennas are being sold that particular religion and it may make the religion seem desperate for new members as what they are offering is not good enough in itself to draw in new members. It’s sad really, to be sold a religion when you are seeking spiritual healing out of a genuine desire to get help.

Many feel that our attraction rather than promotion puts NA miles ahead of any other spiritual group. That sincerity is our main difference. NA is not like a store having loss leaders. It is like the emergency room. The people working in the emergency room wait around for people to walk in. They help the walkins as best as they can. There are never sales at the emergency room or loss leaders.

Many long time NA members feel that our long affiliation with Drug Court is a form of promotion. We sign the slips and they come because they are forced to. And then, perhaps, just perhaps, they will want what we have. It’s a source of fresh blood. A way of promotion. But it doesn’t produce recovery to hang around waiting for people who don’t want to be there and only come under threat of incarceration.

Many of our best members have left NA over this issue. Message givers that we sorely need, especially when we get 5,10,15 years clean. It can be hard to find sponsors when your sponsor dies and you have 20 years clean.

EDIT LINE

quote: “Each of us has our story, and our own recovery. Individually, we’re powerless but as a Fellowship we can achieve great things.”
body:
This is one of the main advantages of NA recovery. We relate, share and our load is spread out equally among our peers. A strong fellowship can move mountains, but an addict alone is in bad company. This is one reason that we begin and maintain recovery in meetings. Interaction with out fellows allows us to help and be helped in a natural setting. Whatever we need, we pick up on as other share their experience. Whatever we have experienced is available to others as we share. It is human nature at its best.

We carry what we have gained or shared with us in our hearts and in our minds. After hundreds of times, honesty comes a little easier. Opening our minds is less scary and threatening. Repeated acts of trusting interactions makes trying out new approaches gains an air of positive expectation.

While many times we can look back with awe on how well some of our problems have been resolved, it doesn’t feel like that big of a deal. Baby steps lead to large accomplishments. And in combination with others we go well beyond our former boundaries and brick walls!

quote: “Personal anonymity is really much more. It is a corner of freedom, and personal recovery.”
body:
We have no anonymity with people sent by the courts. They are not bound by our rules, They don’t want to be here and further are mad at us for making them stay through the whole meeting. Our literature was designed for people who seek recovery, not persons under pressure from a courtroom. We are not affiliated with outside organizations and certainly not with the legal system.

SCRAM Systems monitors with tv cameras in them that video record the people in the meeting and use facial recognition to see exactly who is there should never be tolerated in a meeting of Narcotics Anonymous. Really inexperienced people can make all sorts of errors but that changes nothing anymore than leaving a scalpel inside a surgical patient defines hospital rules. .

When we must sign our name and supply our phone number on court slips, we have no anonymity. When we are signing attendence slips under penalty of perjury, we have no anonymity. When rehabs bus people in, we have no anonymity. When we are meeting in government building with video and audio surveillance of the entire meeting, we have no anonymity.

Attending meetings in the name of NA, we have to speak up to Tradition Violations and leave the meeting. Where Traditions are routinely violated, we do not have any anonymity any more.

quote: “These are the things we are and how our program works. They are a reality for us. We have our Steps; we have mobility; we really understand and care; and we are motivated by survival. All these things are a contradiction to traditional recovery approaches and to professionalism. The professional has no place in our fellowship; our very nature prohibits this.”
body:
For those who have not yet experienced the life and joy of NA recovery, these lines can appear to be mistakes of language. They are taken from the real experience of addicts who live clean in Narcotics Anonymous. They are crucial elements of what works in NA. The lack of these things is why we need our recovery program tailored to meet our very special and important needs.

Steps are arranged so that while each one builds on the Step before, they lead us out of the confusion and despair of active addiction. First surrender, admit our addiction and go looking for belief we can trust with our lives. That belief is the key to our prison cell.

Mobility lets us meet freely wherever we find a location that is close enough to attend on an everyday basis or good enough to make an extra effort to get to from time to time. We can reach other addicts and they can reach us.

Identification brings us together in a natural and easy manner. We recognize our common ground as we share in meetings or fellowship. We can speak openly of our pain and errors among others who have made the same or similar mistakes.

Survival gives us experience that means hope both to ourselves and those who may have the same problems. No one can argue with our experience. They can pick at our good ideas or take issue with what we have heard or read from books but experience is different. Even surprising or unlikely events or outcomes hold up under close examination.

Traditional recovery and professionalism bundle what can be known of the recovery of others without the identification of personal experience.

quote: “We don’t set ourselves up as God. That is why it is spiritual. We get to be an instrument of God’s grace in action.”
body:
We exist with all our strengths and shortcomings. Recovery allows us to acquire knowledge and information that goes beyond what we know to be true for ourselves. Some of it helps and other things seems pointless. Occasionally, we find things that seem to answer many questions at once. These things are called principles. They work often and well to help us out in a variety of situations and challenges that may have had us backed up against a wall in our pasts.

Spiritual is a word we associate with how we feel inside and what encourages or empowers us. Spiritual is beyond what is physical or mental. Sometimes it is workless and is only recognized by how we feel.

Some believe that God speaks thru one’s sponsor. It just pops out of their mouth. One NA member became a famous actor became his sponsor told him to go on a casting call for a movie. He got a bit piece and is now a household name. Did the sponsor know this would happen. Probably not.

Some sponsors have admitted that when they gave direction they were later on surprised at what had come out of their mouth. It just popped into their heads and came out of their mouth. And they are even more amazed when their sponsee came back thanking them for their shrewd, insightful and effective counsel. There is something spiritual in the sponsorship relationship. It is often called a two-way street.

quote: Tradition Nine “This Tradition defines the way we run our Fellowship. A lot of confusion has occurred because of misinterpretations of our Ninth Tradition. Our members are addicts who have the desire to stop using, who want what we have to offer, and who have chosen to join us. Our meetings are a gathering of members for the purpose of staying clean.”
body:
Not putting a member qualified for NA by the disease of addiction in a position of perceived personal power protects us and them from many errors of perspective. If the group is the source of our combined wisdom and power, it is properly positioned to cushion personal errors and bring out many viewpoints that go beyond what individual group members have to offer. We don’t get ahead by leaving people out.

NA began to grow as a Fellowship when we learned to share in service settings where information was routinely distributed to keep the general Fellowship informed. Information is brain food. Without food, we starve. You cannot have group conscience from an unconscious group. . You can cut up a phone book into little strips and put them in a barrel but you cannot find the one you are looking for without putting them into alphabetical order. In other words, in ‘formation.’ And the formation needs every single listening if it is to contain the one you’re looking for. So, you can’t leave one out. Each one is important, just like our group members. You never know which member is going to have what we are looking for!

The desire, the need, and chosen to be among us. These make each member special. Our servants can carry out group conscience but they can’t tell us what to do. If they do, they fall into error whether they know it or not. And in time, they will be replaced by those who can follow group direction.

quote: “The 12 Traditions of Narcotics Anonymous are even more interrelated than our Steps. They complement each other and are bound together by the principle of anonymity.”
body:
“Anonymity is the whole basis of the program; it is truly the foundation. In order to survive, we must set aside the differences we live by and become a part of a greater whole.” Recovery leads us back into the fabric of our relationships among members. To enjoy this new way of life we have to drop a lot of the personal preferences that get in the way of our relationships. The preferences are not as important as the relationships. In the end they usually don’t mean that much to us and we later find they blocked the way to our happy destiny.

“Anonymity is the spiritual foundation of all our traditions”. One member saw this as the Leaning Tower of Pizza. If the anonymity is lost, the foundation fails and all of the Traditions begin to lean, eventually crashing to the ground in a big pile. Without anonymity the Traditions will eventually crash to the ground and the clear message cannot be carried.

When we violate one tradition, we violate them all. We may not realize it but later we find we violate even more.

One member went to the Area Service Committee and asked to start a non-court slips signing meeting. They were told,:”We have had a very long affiliation with the Drug Court. We promote Drug Court and let them use the NA name in their brochure. In our opinion, the Drug Court is very effective.” Without travel and study, it is easy to make mistakes. The reason we don’t affiliate is because every organization has its own strengths and weaknesses. We take care to protect what we as a Fellowship have found to be important and essential for NA to exist as a spiritual Fellowship.

quote: “If we’re not grateful for the things we’ve got, we won’t be grateful for the things we are yet to receive.”
body:
We make gratitude lists often. These can be of small things like shoes with no holes in them, a sandwich for lunch, a noon meeting to go to. They can be of profound things like being clean, being healthy and having a roof over our heads.

These lists are profound and should be used often. They put things in perspective. We get lost in habitual negativity. It takes some work on our part to get used to being grateful. Listing brings the reality close enough for us to see it.

One member, at his nightly prayers, counted off on his fingers all the nice things that happened to him every day. These could include a free cup of coffee at the bank, a woman who let him go ahead at the store since he only had one item, a good lunch, a nice compliment from the boss and even someone letting him go in front of them in traffic. Isn’t it wonderful how something so simple can help save our lives in recovery!

quote: “Those of us who do service work are the luckiest people on God’s earth! Those who have been in the pits of despair, now strive to help other people to find a new and better way to live.”
body:
This statement only works when the service is genuine giving. Blunt routines may support but do not constitute the evidence of our loving and caring. Loving is the thought or intention. Caring is doing the actions that stem from the intention. When we help others we find it easier to accept help ourselves. We slowly realize we don’t feel better than or proud and arrogant when we do service. It feels good to do good. You can’t be useless if you are being useful!

When we are helping others, it puts us in a creative state of mind and we think of things we could easily afford to do better ourselves. Addiction boxes us in and our encounters with other people become defensive and evasive. For years people with polio were locked in full body casts to prevent them from shriveling up in contorted positions. How’s that for a world of hurt? Movement, polio vaccine, diet, and general exercise turned the whole treatment attitude around. For years, addiction was thought to be an incurable disease of heart, mind and body. Now we get help from admission of truth, belief, faith and inventory which leads to spiritual practices. It makes all the difference.

Listening, reading, discussing and learning what works for others give us immediate access to the things we need most to re-discover happy, healthy living.

quote: “Honesty, open-mindedness, and willingness to try, work hand in hand. The lack of one of these principles in our programs can kill us.”
body:
Many people who call themselves members may be under a false impression that addiction is not a disease that can kill us. Rocking chair recovery can appear to work for a while, but it may leave us without the tools and principles that comprise the N A Program. Inaction replaces healthy strength and movement with weakness and disability. Many members rest on the widespread meetings and body of literature as if there is nothing left to do for the growth and continuance of N A. Nothing could be further from the truth. When we stop working the Program, the Program stops working! Without knowledge of who we are and where we come from we remain children.

When the Basic Text was written, there were discussions as to whether honesty, open- mindedness and the willingness to try (HOW) were spiritual principles. Honesty requires surrender, belief and faith.. Open-mindedness sets our minds ‘open-to-receive.’ This takes away the filter we use to exclude un-approved information! We don’t have to buy the new information, just pay attention and consider what someone is saying. The willingness to try is a strange one because if we are willing, we usually don’t have to do whatever is under consideration. If we are unwilling, the reverse is true. The subject will reappear until we do it!

Honesty, open-mindedness and the willingness to try remain our big three spiritual principles because they are so useful in recovery. We addicts in recovery can attest to the need for repetition and re-focusing our attention as we grow spiritually.

quote: “Our Steps and Traditions are uniform and set in a specific order. They are numbered; they are not random and unstructured. Certainly they are organized, but this is not the organization or our Ninth Tradition. For the purpose of this Tradition, organized means having an administrative structure, and this implies management and control.”
body:
Businesses are arranged in order this way. Do it our way or the highway! But businesses are not spiritual Fellowships. Addicts in recovery often make recovery ‘mistakes’ because they have not learned better! Persons with clean time wait for them to ask for help because we have learned not to crash through the doors of their old ways. What they learn builds up and new connections become to visible to them. We are always learning in this way.

The growth of NA recovery literature leads us to realize no one will ever know it all. Discovery is a big part of the thrill of recovery. Many addicts are afraid of spiritual growth. Things of the world seem more pertinent and real. While this is certainly true, you don’t hear of many people moved to awe and wonder by constructing erector sets! Recovery requires us to learn how to get the results we need to live clean.

It may be what we learn in recovery is we are given the keys to unlock the doorways that led to what we sought through our drug use. When we try out some of the recovery principles and find they work for us, many of us get downright curious. We want to know more. Others get a little better and try to leave the game with their winnings and resume something like their old life. We have learned that this can lead to other changes, like the things that used to make us feel good.

quote: “Many of us have nowhere else to go if we could not trust our N.A. groups and members. We were no longer afraid to ask for help. How then did we lose our fear?”
body:
At some point we realize that we are the baby in the meeting. We don’t know how to stay clean or live life. Those who do not realize they are the baby, often become hip, slick and cool and find they cannot stay clean at the same time.

Asking for help can be very hard for most addicts. We were self sufficient as cowboys in the old west. All they needed was their cowboy hat, cowboy boots, six shooter and horse and they were good to go. They could live off the land and shift for themselves. They shot a wild boar cooked it, unrolled their sleeping bag and put their rope in a circle around it to keep the rattlesnakes from crossing over.

Addicts are like this, we got up every morning and went out to boost something to be able to score. We licked our wounds when hurt, always had a backup plan and knew how to survive until the end when the disease simply took over our lives completely and started trying to kill us in earnest.

We adapted to the clean life like that cowboy coming into Dodge City. We got a hot soapy bath, ate a home cooked meal and slept in a bed. We realized that this was much better than living out there in the brush on our own.

quote: “The awakening of anonymity in each of us occurs when we’ve finally given up trying to manage our lives and begin to depend on a power greater than ourselves.”
body:
Thinking we are different from others, insisting on having our way, needless conflicts with others: all these are the prison bars of our active addiction. They keep us more or less in conflict with all the people we come in contact with in our day to day lives. Newer members by definition are accustomed to the company of people who are using! Clean addicts walk to the beat of a different drummer.

Anonymity, meaning here that we are all the same in kind. Seeing the similarities, even in our faults and shortcomings, we discover our new identity. It occurs to us, it is ok to be imperfect. In fact, all people are imperfect. Getting better is not an unattainable goal! It is merely a process that feels better as we get used to living a new way of life. We all have the disease of addiction in common, which is what promotes unity, and our common welfare has to come first. Our personal recovery depends on this identification.

Our differences no longer set us apart or result in loneliness. Our choices may be changed if we find they don’t really suit us. Choice allows us to improve our situation rather than locks us into permanent fixation. As freedom becomes our state of existence we realize the importance of regaining our human right to make changes!

quote: “As the benefits of basic honesty in the world begin to roll in, we are ready to consider honesty at a deeper level. Self-honesty is being in touch with the way we really feel and the way we spend our time.”
body:
Admitting faults is one of the gifts of honesty. We don’t have to shadow box with life as if we are punished for each and every mistake that happens in the course of daily existence. “Opps, I’m sorry.” costs little and eases situations where an unexpected error pops up. “That’s ok.” says someone and life goes on.

One member went to a past business partner and apologized. The stunned business partner blurted out “It takes a big man to apologize”. Many cannot bring themselves to do this and wind up relapsing. We must get honest and do the work if we are to survive.

As we scrape away the layers of fact, fiction and supposition, we find the most useful tool is honesty. Many items on our list of personal concerns didn’t even happen. Others happened but not the way we thought or recalled and with a different residue. Some of the worst things happened but were blocked out of our consciousness by our built in defence mechanisms. Constant contact with the higher power of our understanding will help us no matter what the case at hand may be. “It’s all about love.” applies to humanity in general. Mind and mood altering drugs distort and confuse our perceptions and our ability to process what we hear, see and feel correctly. Recovery and the the 12 Steps of NA help us sort out the sad mess of our lives.

At the end of the process, we are on different terms with reality. We make amends where possible and are freed to move on with our lives.

quote: “Remember too that H.O.W. are spiritual principles which mean that they can be relied on to get us out of trouble that dishonesty, closed-mindedness and unwillingness got us into.”
body:
Interesting how the words line up. In active addiction, we practiced honesty when it was convenient but lies and distortion of facts kept us alive and out of jail. Lots of times we still have a tendency to lie even when it is just out of habit. Many of us are still amazed when truth telling eases a tense situation and then there is no more trouble. How about that? Honesty results in ‘no more trouble.’

Many drugs induce hallucinations where we see and hear things that are not there. This can be a real kick for some of us, especially if our living situation is not too hot. After a while, we have to avoid non-users who would freak out if we started rambling all the stuff rattling through our brains. Ordinary learning and practicing social skills was an encumbrance. Our world shrunk to include only addicts like us where we felt somewhat safe and comfortable. In recovery, open- mindedness is a tool we practice to work our very own 12 Steps and rediscover life with the drug-induced side-effects out of the picture..

After some memorable difficulties, jail, beatings and car crashes, we began to play it safe whenever possible. Anything new and difficult was best avoided. Failure and shortfall became the rule, not the exception. So we just stopped trying. In recovery, we have to learn failure is not so bad in most cases. It may only a step in learning valuable to us. Other times willingness is a necessary item in a series of events designed, perhaps by an all loving higher power, to help us move on with life.

quote: “It wasn’t until we made a real mess of our lives that we realized that we couldn’t do it alone.”
body:
Being clean clears our vision and increases our ability to hear what others are saying. It may be that the isolation that characterizes our disease comes from the many, many times we wake up when the drugs wear off and look around for all the things we have to answer for. Then, we go around saying, “Hey man. Sorry I did that, it was the dope not me.” Like the using gives us a smoke screen to avoid personal responsibility.

Clean, we can string together events as they occur in our lives and see more vividly how one thing leads to all the rest. It may take awhile, and that’s ok. Healing should not be a rush job. It takes time clean for recovery to penetrate past our surface issues into the depths of our being but the result is the workable, more complete and satisfied being we become.

God, life and nature conspire to make us happy. If we just have to touch the hot stove to believe it will burn, that may be our choice. But, boy do we learn from some experience. Recovery moves us from a world where we many not be able to trust our senses to a world where sense perceptions are more accurate and useful.

Finding ourselves amazingly sitting in an NA meeting, we recognize what we are hearing from the people in the room exactly the same things we have been going through. The more we hear, the more recognize our identification at some point we try out something that worked for someone else. We were surprised when it got us good results and began to try other things. This is when we realized we could do better trying out things that worked for other addicts than when tried to control things.

quote: “The ego of the addict must be busted for him to have a chance at recovery. “Terminal hipness” and “fatal cool” are symptoms of the addictive personality.”
body:
When we stopped beating the square peg, trying to get it to fit in the round hole, we considered giving up. It just wasn’t working. We noticed no one was being fooled by our efforts and set the hammer down. We probably grinned a little and tried to act like it was no big deal. That’s right, we didn’t want to seem embarrassed in front of people and wanted to keep our cool. Right.

Ego is not a bad thing, in and of itself. It is just us being ourselves. But, you know, drugs will mess with your mind, and in that sense it’s not our fault. Yeah, no ego trip here! The longer we stay clean and our vision improves along with our hearing, we leave off the ego bit. We’re just human. And clean, well things steadily improve. Good food, sleep and exercise begin to bring us back into focus.Fear, loneliness and general confusion loosen their grip. It is important to recall what the last one did for us, maybe it will stay the last one.

What used to be one of our biggest problems, our ego, shifts into a healthy sense of self. We have a God given right to our preferences whether they fit in with everyone else or not. There are some good reasons why we don’t drink milk or milk products if we discover we are milk intolerant. If we had some happy years in high school, we may prefer certain forms of music over others. We have all sorts of preferences in clothing, food, excursions, whatever. But they don’t isolate us or keep us from enjoying the company of others. The pretensions fall away and the real self, in our case, can come out!

quote: “At least one meeting a day for ninety days seems to be a good guide for those who are going to any lengths.”
body:
Going to meetings, reading recovery literature, calling your sponsor, asking for God’s help, don’t pick up - these are five recommendations that seem to work for all members regardless of age, sex, etc. It is astonishing how helpful these simple things give us a daily reprieve from the hell of active addiction!

Only a sincere member of N A gets to participate in this wonderful thing we call simply “recovery.” The price of membership is high - it costs our self-will and ego. The idea we can handle it on our own can kill us. You are the one who decides if you are sincere or not. Identification with other N A members brings a special calm and new self appraisal since we begin to compare our feelings with others in the same boat we are.

You may not be new to NA but we know our addiction is waiting to make its move. Some of us fight back by doing ninety in ninety again. We pray for a newcomer and check out a new meeting now and then. Traveling in the Fellowship reminds us, it is principles, not personalities that keep us clean and growing!

quote: “N.A. should never be run by bureaucracy or management nor controlled by individuals within administrative structure. If we were to allow this, N.A. this would surely lose the best it has to offer and choke to death on our insanities.”
body:
The man that carved the Crazy Horse Memorial in the black hills of south Dakota had worked on the four presidential faces on mount Rushmore. His mentor told him that it had been a mistake to take funds from the Federal Government as there were too many strings attached.

So when the Crazy Horse Memorial was begun, Korcjack Kilowiski paid for every stick of dynamite, every gallon of diesel fuel for his Catterpillar and every dynamite fuse out of his own pocket or with donations from people visiting the site.

Twice the federal government offered him ten million in grants and both times he refused. When asked if he doubted the validity of the contract offered, he replied, “You broke every treaty with the Indians. Crazy Horse spent his life trying to enforce the treaties given by the Federal Government and fought wars over it.

The bureaucrat had no response. We must beware of these folks. Caution is the watchword here. We have learned from many painful lessons that keeping it simple and avoiding contact with political bureaucracies puts our whole program at risk Big fish eat little fish. Bureaucracies are the enemy of self-help programs.

Too often our great mission to live and live clean gets lost in platitudes. We are on our own and survival depends on our courage and gratitude. It ok if we are slow and imperfect. We don’t aspire to perfection. Life is dirty sometimes and unclean but then NA is a self-correcting program. We inventory and pray for our Higher Power to free us of our defects and give us the strength to make amends when they are due. Prayer and meditation reveal the path before us and we take it in gratitude. God, help us be humble, patient and tolerant.

quote: “We were in the depths of despair, degradation and lost in a destructive chaos. We came in puking, sweating and shaking. Some of us stayed. Was it through our own merit? We think not! Our way got us here. Recovery was done through the help of others, the tools they shared with us, and a Higher Power.”
body:
It is oh so easy to forget just how bad it was and what it felt like to have no hope, no way out and no one to turn to. Almost all humans select for a softer, easier, way. The difference for us is that that way offers only the illusion of security because we put only a coat of paint over our old behavior and call it clean! A softer, easier, way can kill us because it offers no change in how we live our lives. And without change, our recovery, our freedom and our life may be at risk.

“Enough is enough!” doesn’t set any boundaries. One size doesn’t fit all. “It takes what it takes.” Our using resulted in making a mess of our lives. Recovery calls for us to bring the progress of our addiction to a halt by avoiding that next drug. Once we can do that, the pain and tension will eventually subside.While we get better, we have to bear in mind that it was through no effort our own. It depended on what worked for others working for us. The help, the tools and above all, our Higher Power gets all the credit. Just because it gets better doesn’t mean we can return to our egotism and take control again.

It may be safe to say, that any addict who has gone back to using has found a way to wiggle out of the disease concept - or never been properly 12 Stepped to begin with! Some people are not addicts. That’s not us! We begin - and continue - our recovery when we can admit to our powerlessness over addiction! Our unmanageability is a sure sign we need help!

quote: “Guilt and worry keep us from living in the here and now. The denial of our disease, or reservations, keep us sick. We lack humility, clinging to old ways.”
body:
Gaining humility takes time and a thorough working of Steps 6 and 7. Humility is a total acceptance of ourselves, both our good points and our character defects. We need to achieve such a total acceptance of ourselves that we don’t feel the need to justify our actions or viewpoints. We don’t have to explain ourselves, defend ourselves or promote ourselves. We can just calmly state our truth and move on without further discussion or argument. Our sponsors and home groups are there to help us in difficulty.

Guilt reviews our past and contains events and instances that make us ashamed and embarrassed. Worry is the mental state we experience when we see bad things coming our way or just ‘feel funny.’. Both lock us out of the moment we are standing in. The present is the only time we have an opportunity to surrender to what we are powerless over or admit fault and go on with our lives, making amends as we go. Basic recovery repeats the truth that it is only through being conscious in the present moment that we can take effective action to change things for the better.

Old ways did not work when we had nothing better and there is a very good chance they won’t work now! Even if we s tumble, we stumble upward.

quote: “We came to this program for many different reasons. Those of us who stay, do so for the same reason--to stop using and stay clean.”
body:
This is our one chance, today! Some of us recognized that. Others missed it entirely and went back out never to revisit NA ever again. Today, I am grateful that I had the clarity and desperation to realize that it was my one chance. Had I not been so beaten down, I would have missed it and died from my disease. Staying clean leads me into a new state of existence wherein I can see, hear and feel accurately and think through what’s happening to make better choices. Conscious contact and help from my higher power give me resources I didn’t have before.

We are all amazed how easy it is to forget our basic dependence on our HP as soon as things get a little bit better! Praying seems like so much trouble when what we really need is a miracle! Here’s how it goes, “God, I don’t think I can handle this. Please help and guide me in my recovery. I need your strength and guidance!” See? Was that so hard?

We have been taught to snatch and grab, sure that is the only way we can get a fair shake. Actually, when we give we open our hands and hearts to receive. Spiritual non-attachment frees us to move and make new choices. Attachment weighs us down with fear and acts of desperation. Letting go reduces our burden and when we relax, we can see, hear and feel better. Attention is a price we all have to pay. Important things make it worthwhile to take our time. We are important. Thinking gives us a chance to check our facts before jumping into trouble. If you want something, wait a few days. You may not want it later on. You get paid for your waiting!

quote: “Usually one of the first things we try to do when we’re working with a newcomer is to get them to a meeting. After all, that’s what worked for us.”
body:
An N A group has one primary purpose, and that is to carry this message to the addict who still suffers. This can only be done through our members sharing their honest experience, strength and hope. By watching newcomers who have raised their hands, or when we notice the scared addict sitting by him or herself in the back of the room. In a loving way , remembering their name or just simply introducing ourselves, “Hi, I am an addict named ____.”

Meeting attendance helps the new member to identify with other recovering addicts and provides a pathway to the experience, strength and hope of those who came before us. It also provides an opportunity to be of service for our more experienced members when we “12th Step” newcomers by taking them to meeting.

This approach also helps our long timers to remain humble. Our surrender helps us put ego aside and remember that it is the group that is our most powerful vehicle for carrying the message. Our recovery fosters the spirit of the therapeutic value of one addict helping another. Even long time members of NA find it impossible to stay clean if they do not work their 12th Step. You can’t carry our message if you don’t have gratitude and hope.

quote: “We react angrily as recovering addicts if anyone tells us what to do. In N.A. meetings, we share what it was like in our practicing addiction, our suffering that brought us to the turning point and how we stay clean today.”
body:
One of the unspoken facts our our clean life and recovery is there are people in the rooms who do not share our enthusiasm, energy and willingness to commit to our Program. They may go further with their program and realize the stakes are their lives, their happiness and their dreams - lose all three. One general statement stands out clearly and that is the Program as we know it was built by people who lived it and wrote it down in the Basic Text. Some caretakers have attempted to ‘own’ and take credit for our literature yet are almost completely in the dark as to how it was written and the spirituality involved! So, in our daily recovery we are free to embrace, learn and apply the deepest and most loving N A light we can find and apply to our lives.

The old saying was “You can tell an addict, but you can’t tell him much.” The ratios between long timers and newcomers were very different today from in the past. Back then the long timers outnumbered the newcomers and could convey information thru sheer numbers. Now there are places where in many meetings the number of newcomers outweighs the long timers by 10 to 1 and then the long timers become voices in the wilderness.

If someone in the meeting has no clean time and can’t surrender, they will find it difficult to make the program. Only the sincere will be able to work our 12 Steps. Interrupting members or discounting on their shares is a sign of ego and self-centeredness. Experienced members may try to talk with them after a meeting if the spirit moves them. But each members has a God given right to say no to NA and go their on way even if it kills them.

quote: “Before coming to the fellowship of N.A., we were irresponsible with our lives. We used drugs to cover up our humiliation, guilt, inferiority and feelings of inadequacy.”
body:
Most of us practice some sort of wake-up ritual, reading literature, listening to recovery tapes or CD’s to get our day off to a good start. It is vitally important that we keep our focus on recovery and things that work in recovery if we are going to experience the benefits of N A! The simplest things can make so much difference in the way our days go.

Just missing a few meetings can cause us to begin to experience the feelings we associate with our disease. Resentments crop up over very small issues. We may snap at co-workers and spouses. Just a member calling us can put us back in a better place.

We must realize when we are uncentered that the longer we stay there, the greater the likelihood that disastrous things will happen. They may simply be stupid decisions or may result in the calamity of picking up. The person who left the bar or shooting gallery did not know they were going to kill someone or die trying to drive home. Learning how to live clean is an ongoing, daily process. There is a saying, “If we aren’t growing, we are going.” Scary but true.

quote: “Many of us were convinced that we were right and the world was wrong, and used this belief to justify our self-destructive behavior.”
body:
When we judge ourselves and find ourselves unworthy, self-destructive behavior results. We are in a terrific position to punish ourselves. There are quirks in our nervous system that make punishing ourselves a kind of answer. The more we punish ourselves for doing things we didn’t feel good about, the less pressure was on, at least for the moment. Beyond simple mistakes and lost jobs, some go so far that they cut themselves and engage in other dangerous forms of abuse.

In N A we find a new mindset and a new vocabulary: the NA Steps and Traditions. Words like responsibility, forgiveness, gratitude, caring and sharing lead us into a strange new world where all the rules are changed. Honesty is a way to stay out of jail, not a way to get arrested! How many other words have changed meaning or usefullness? Looking up words in a dictionary can surprise us!

When first we found drugs, they seemed to make the world look crazy and weird. It was funny! As our using progressed, crazy and weird began to look normal. Coming out of active addiction, we have to make a few changes. Generally, the more we change, the better it gets!

quote: “In the past we have relied on desperation to give us the strength to periodically re-order our lives. There is another way.”
body:
When we stay in the middle of the herd, work the tools of NA and attend meetings regularly, we operate like a well oiled Swiss watch. We take bad news in stride and keep on trucking. We become efficient, pleasant and happy more often than not.

When we neglect our program, and things begin to fall apart, sometimes we must hit a severe crisis to wake up and run back to the program and redouble our efforts at recovery. Sometimes even this crisis does not wake us up and stop the slide.

It seems that given to our own devices, we tend to slack off on our program. We blame it on relationships, being too busy and the program changing for the worse. Some believe it is the disease causing this in order to get us away from the program so that it can start moving us towards using again. For us there is no safe using! It is like starting a forest fire forgetting our house will be burnt to the ground by the blaze!

quote: “When we pray a remarkable thing happens; we find the means, the ways, and energies, to perform tasks far beyond our capabilities. By the surrender of our own power, we gain a far greater power that will see us through.”
body:
The truth of this statement bears out our spiritual power being ‘higher.’ The easy way to say this is that surrender and prayer gives us the ‘luck.’

“Expect a miracle, you’ll get one” was the old saying. But we must open the vortex for HP to step in and make the miracle. Sometimes, intensive prayer will break things loose for us. But however it is packaged, it is the quiet, simple surrender that leads us back to the path that works for us in NA.

Seeking and practicing conscious contact with the higher power of our understand give us a multipurpose tool that opens all doors. Begin with small things. “God, handle this or handle that.” We we find that things are working out, go for more! Apparently, there is a secret compartment hidden in the back of our minds - or hearts - and we can contact this force by stopping and taking time to as for God’s help.

quote: “Life then becomes for us what we always wished it to be -- a constant state of awakening.”
body:
Many of us used drugs to intensify our minds and this helped drive us crazy! Sick, tired, on our knees, we sought and found recovery. Looking inward for answers encourages us to go further inward. The quiet little voice within becomes our new best friend.

Some people believe addicts turn to drugs to make an unbearable situation bearable. That may be. We were sure looking for something. Active addiction tore our minds up and almost all addicts agree that they reached a point where they could no longer think clearly and lost control of themselves. They would do whatever the drugs told them to do. So, recovery allows us to snap out of the old ways of thinking and new thoughts arise to take their place.

Waking up, we find opportunities to change. We look at life differently. Just having friends with many of the same living problems helps. When we get involved with helping others, many of us find ourselves taking our own good advice. Seeing others asking for help taking our advice, helps us lower our guards and ask for help ourselves!

quote: “The tools we use to maintain our willingness are practice and prayer. How sincerely we work Step Six will be proportionate to our desire for change.”
body:
Every member of N A has experienced the desire for recovery before they got clean. Practice gives us a way to get better by repeating our efforts until we get it right. Prayer sets us in the mood to receive without putting up a fight to receive what we are going after. Our efforts are based on our desire for recovery. This is what gives us the power we need to work all 12 Steps.

It is our desire for recovery that powers and guides us through the process of the NA`12 Steps. We want to get clean, so we surrender. We want a higher power we can trust and so came to believe. We wanted the care of our higher power and found it only awaited our willingness. Desire is a little like a candle flame, a little bit destroys the darkness. Then a little more lets us look around. We can keep going as long as we are willing to take care of the flame. It was there, inside, all along.

As we become more sensical, we realize we can explore and try on ideas before adopting them. We are really allowed to pick and choose what we like and move away from what turns us off. Recovery is all about positive change to suit ourselves.

quote: “This force in the meetings isn’t just the people there, but also something within the people. We return to these meetings and use them like medicine in the form of unconditional love.”
body:
There is a strong spirituality in the love we share at our N A meetings. We express as best we can what we are doing in our day to day to lives. We share the way we use the principles of N A to deal with things that used to have us on the run. Hearing some of the hair-raising episodes that occur in the lives of our friends will automatically encourage us and change the way we do things. Sometimes it helps us in our daily living just to stop and get in that “N A state of mind” in the midst of whatever is going on. We focus better, we recall things we need to deal with life. We are more honest and considerate of others. As we succeed using N A principles, honesty, openmindedness and the willingness to try become woven into the fabric of our lives.

One member was hopelessly stuck on a big project. He went to a meeting and it was terrible. The room was full of smoke and they had opened the windows which made it cold inside. The speaker talked about getting loaded while kids ran screaming thru the entire meeting.

The member went home shaking his head and woke the next morning totally centered. He focused on the project and completed it in good order. He realized that there was a presence in the room that had healed him. He never looked at meetings the same ever since that time. Every meeting was a perfect meeting from then on. See, it isn’t just what’s in the meeting. When we are in the meeting, we are there too. Our surrender helps change us inside.

quote: “What is it about our meetings that’s so special? Usually, about all we can say is that there is a feeling there, a feeling of strength and hope and love; an atmosphere of recovery. Our meetings are very special to most of us. They’re a place where we feel safe; a place where we fit in.”
body:
For many of us this is the first time we have felt that we fit in. Many addicts have felt different from everyone else all their lives and to finally feel we belong is an incredible feeling. We love it. We are greeted, told we will be loved until we learn to love ourselves and accepted just as we are. Later, we want to help the newcomer feel this way too.

Because I came in to meetings where no one was forced to attend against their will, the feeling was that I was safe there.

Drug court has proved to be a growing problem. Our program is designed to carry our message to sick and suffering addicts. Judges are sending people who are diruptive. This is not the NA Way. Angry people there against their will can kill that force. In fact, it’s hard to have both in the same room. Many feel that this is a small price to pay for the unwilling addict facing prison to avoid incarceration. They believe that if we can just enable them, then we will feel wonderful. They believe that chaotic meetings can be very effective and pleasant for us. Was that attractive to you when you came into recovery?

DRT is another modern problem for us in a program of total abstinence. Many members are having to sort this out for themselves. NA will always be a program of total abstinence so we hve to find ways to make this clear. Naturally some recovering addicts are silting ducks waiting in the pond! The unfairness of this situation seems not to bother some people. Sometimes all we can do is stay clean ourselves and hope these unfortunates make it through to recovery.

Chaotic meetings are upsetting and eventually run off addicts seeking recovery by offering none. Then the meeting shrinks until the sincere and surrendered begin to relight the desire for recovery. This is a tough process.

quote: “Even without this Tradition (9), organization such as this would be in opposition to our spiritual principles. A loving God as He may express himself in our group conscience would find no place within an administrative structure.”
body:
Once we abandon the spiritual principles, we are dead ducks. The Fellowship begins to unravel and a clear message is no longer carried. Newcomers do not get clean or stay clean. They cannot stay clean unless they get clean first. Many are years from hitting their bottom. Since recovery is based on desire, they have to hurt bad enough to ask for help.

Some people simply do not believe that the Traditions are important or even relevant. It’s hard to stay clean when ignoring the Traditions. We have grown tremendously in recent decades. New problems have developed that were hard to foresee. Outside forces in government and medicine have pushed their way into our rooms. We must deal with these matters of we will die. There is no outside force to turn to except the great spiritual reality of our Higher Power.

Administration means applying the conclusions of a small group of people on a larger group of people. This is one of the big blind spots for members seeking recovery. They may not yet fully realize that Narcotics Anonymous is built on the accumulated successes of thousands of clean addicts. It is not a matter of personal opinion. Newer members have a hard time seeing this. They depend on us for the truth.

Our service structure was built on direct responsibility to the many and entrusted to a few. These few members must be capable and willing to stay true to NA members in recovery. An administrator would flop around and try to substitute corporate tactics in place of spiritual principles. Eventually this leads to failure and removal of procedures and policies that work for us. The Twelve Traditions are not negotiable because nothing else works for us.

quote: “Once a group registers they are sent a starter kit. This contains many suggestions and is one of the way we share our experience to help the group. Whether or not they take our suggestion is their decision. In this starter kit is says that we must abide by the Twelve Traditions in order to call ourselves Narcotics Anonymous. This is also true, but these Traditions are part of the set of spiritual principles that are N.A. Without the Traditions, N.A. does not exist.”
body:
Just as a surveyor can get careless and mark his charts quarter of an inch off, it may make him a mile off down the line. Where great numbers of persons are concerned, great care must be exerted. For a few people to come in and re-write the work of very intense effort and review is a tremendous liability.

Recovery teaches us to take care and proper concern in all areas of our lives. Members who have a vested interest in the growth and continuance of NA have only three ways of dealing with bureaucratic problems: correction, relapse or death. In time we hope for restitution of our original, attractive and edifying literature. We cannot permit statements and treatments of subjects in our approved literature that go against our Steps or Traditions. For instance, total abstinence and DRT or no surveillance and drug slip attendance. We have to be very careful and able to state the truth of our experience regardless of corporate or medical pressure to serve the views or issues of outside enterprizes.

The new SCRAM monitors have tv cameras in them with facial recognition. They are used in the meetings and it tend to drive the long timers away. But the newcomers will argue that the long timers are jerks for leaving. So they have to try and get clean with little experienced members to guide them. The results are mediocre.

For NA is continue, we have to shield our members and meetings from outside influences. This is not close mindedness. It is necessary to keep our focus on recovery and spiritual growth. We are all free to pursue whatever belief system we love and trust. Trouble is people who are not in the Program know little or nothing of what we go through to get clean and stay clean. So, be careful and stick to Traditional NA.

quote: “Having decided we want God, as we understood Him, to relieve us of the useless or destructive aspects of our personalities, we have arrived at the Seventh Step.”
body:
This Step is the ‘game changer’ because we finally leave our defects behind. Because everyone who works the 12 Steps is human, we have to take apart the illusion we are in control by making an inventory list, sharing it with someone and becoming willing before we ask our higher power to remove our defects. This allows for corrective personality change. Because HP is inside of us, we have to shut off the engine before we work on the car. Our willingness lets us step aside while God helps us with the necessary changes.

Defects are things that don’t work and the the ways we fall short of our goals are our shortcomings. Like changing a flat tire on a car, we first have to realize we have a flat, find the tools and a spare tire and then go through the process of correction. These things have to be done in a certain order. Addicts seem to try to do it all at once, like snapping their fingers.

All kinds of tiny miracles lead up to our birth and beginnings. Our parents and people around us prepare us for life in various ways. Addicts come from dysfunctional families and our jumbled values and habit patterns reflect unworkable approaches to life. Recovery is finding a safe place to refit our fractured personalities and rebuild.

quote: “Groups of self-centered, self-willed, isolated individuals just can’t meet together peacefully and safely’ but we do. The reason that we can is that we have Twelve Traditions that help to keep our groups “safe” and “free.”
body:
Often we forget or never learn just how essential our Traditions are when it comes to basic stuff like the atmosphere of recovery in our meetings. Study and open discussion give us insight and a deeper understanding of how our freedom has to mesh with the freedoms of others. There is no doubt that our members go on binges of obsession on a single point or two and for the time can’t see a broader picture, even if others are trying to help them.

Fortunately we have extensive training in dealing with nuts. Patience, humility and tolerance come easily to some of us. Loving and caring for others is not seen as an ongoing affair. Mostly, our members come out from obsession to gain information or the get to the bottom of something that bothers them.

It is said, “The NA 12 Steps keep us from killing ourselves, and the NA12 Traditions keep us from killing each other.” Most experienced members with long clean time will agree that all of recovery in NA is a giant, incomprehensible miracle. No one can explain it but just like a mountain - it is there and cannot be denied. Personal and collective conflicts are inevitable and each carefully drawn Tradition plays a role in a comprehensive system of ongoing recovery. As a car consists of parts which wear out in time, we have to maintain our Traditions to keep our Program working.

quote: “Living clean each day at a time will reveal to us the things that truly come from within and give us better understanding over things that would interrupt our flow.”
body:
Without the drugs and addictive distractions, what we go through reconnects us to reality and if we want to add or take away something, our new way of living gives us that power. Many members forget their power to ‘change gears.’ If something is bothering us, we can do something about it! Just because our lives are better doesn’t mean we are stuck at a certain level. Ongoing recovery is ongoing inventory and growth!

At this point, many of us have learned to get back to what worked for us in the beginning! We read literature again, check out some new meetings, write down our feelings and read short examples of what seems to be bothering us. While it is always the disease in one form or another, it helps to let others know and get things out in the open within our loving, caring Fellowship.

Our flow is what we are doing with our lives. Letting our checking account get out of hand, missing appointments and other small things going wrong let us know it is time to get back to our program - the things that we do to stay clean and grow spiritually. If we don’t tell others what’s happening with us, we have to stagger on helpless because we are not asking for help.

quote: “Most of us had never experienced the kind of attention and personal care that we found in the program. We are accepted and loved for ‘what we are’ instead of “in spite” of what we are.”
body:
Because we have adapted our Steps and Traditions from another program, we have to remind ourselves we have only taken a recipe from another program but also we have baked our own cake by writing our own literature and working our program. .

We have expanded our program of recovery to include the many forms addictive disease can take. Once you take away the drugs from a drug crazed maniac, you just have a more efficient maniac. Addiction comes out in many ways. Recovery reflects our surrender to the fact of our addiction and our need for ongoing inventory, prayer and meditation. By giving the same love and good treatment we received when we came to NA, we renew and breath life into minutes, hours and days of our life.

We get addicted to ideas, places, people, moods, activities: anything that qualifies for obsession or compulsion. Recovery is regaining our choice. Addiction is not having a say in what we do, when we do it or why we do it. We lose our rights to freedom when the addictive process takes over.

quote: “Specialization and professionalism are the basis of any management scheme. Any administrative structure, by its very nature, eliminated the possibility of autonomy. An organized N.A. is a contradiction in terms and any attempt to force organization on us would destroy us.”
body:
This program is based on our personal desire for recovery. We can have a dance together. We can have a service structure as long as it listens to what we say and does what it is told. The minute it can’t do that, the so called structure ceases to exist because it fails the definition of “...we may create....” Nobody can make us follow their orders. The service structure is a one way street coming from the membership and our service boards and committees can exist only if they are directly responsible to the membership. There can be no two ways about this. This truth is not clearly understood by members who get into service and forget where they came from. Spiritual principles are not negotiable, just like Traditions!

If any of this bothers you, best check your inventory. As the disease of addiction would have it, many times our ‘twisted serpents’ get confused and wander around a bit expecting others to follow orders. Let it be. They will come off their cloud eventually. Pray for strength and guidance and cover the service shortfall the best way you can.

Organizations and professionals have failed to make progress when it comes to addiction for thousands of years. The only thing that ever helped us is to get away from chemicals that make us crazy and begin to take responsibility for our living practices. We created NA by the grace of a loving God and the quality of our surrender.

quote: “When we first came into the Fellowship of N.A., we were often resentful at the suggestions some of the members made to us.”
body:
As we learn more about how N.A. works, we realize members try to wait until we ask for help. There is a chance that we may have asked for help in some area and the members responded to our request without our fully understanding. Sometimes body language speaks better than words. When it comes to recovery, almost every single thing we share has withstood the repetition of working in practice. It came from those who came before. Be clear about this: recovery is escaping our limitations, not warmed over egotism.

None of us claim to be perfect and, while we are very capable of slipping into ego trips and authoritative roles, it is just our disease acting up. Really, we know better and some of us are comfortable laughing at ourselves. There is a good saying for this. ”Man who cannot laugh at himself is missing one of the world’s greatest jokes!”

Our awareness grows and as we discover the linkage between our actions and the results, we frequently say “Oh, my God.” when we catch ourselves doing things on impulse.. The truth often seems threatening at first but later on it proves to be a great friend! Honesty is not only the best policy, it is a spiritual principle!

quote: “The Ninth Tradition goes on to define the nature of the things that we can do, outside N.A., to help N.A. It says that we may create service boards or committees directly responsible to those they serve.”
body:
This Tradition is making it clear that our service boards and committees cannot tell members of the NA Fellowship what to do as if they were an employer. May seem like a tiny difference but talking down to people and giving orders is killing to the spirit within. The fact is taking on the role of a caregiver or an employer can only result is the member eventually walking away - which they have every right to do. We don’t want our members leaving our Fellowship because of misguided leadership. Leave that ego at the door! Ego tries to take over when we forget to pray for help and forget who we are and where we came from.

Humility, patience and tolerance allow us to work miracles by leaving the authority in divine hands. We are beneficiaries, not benefactors. The help we give is based on our surrender and willingness to follow divine guidance. The Program of NA developed because we need help accepting goodness into our lives. Addiction is losing control of our thinking and our actions. When we cut ourselves off from our Higher Power, we slip back into powerlessness.

Working on our 12 Steps is necessary because if we can’t turn our lives and wills over to a loving, caring and competent power greater than ourselves, we will justify our actions rather than ask for help. Being a trusted servant worthy of trust is behind many of the successes in our history.

quote: “Letting go of character defects should be done with love. It is important we think, to be gentle with ourselves when putting our ego to rest.”
body:
When we are gentle with ourselves, we may be more likely to be gentle with others. We adjust our literature to soften the truth just enough that it doesn’t blast away at the reader. Humor, self disclosure, restatement create a learning atmosphere and when we are having trouble understanding something new to us, it can really help to lead by example or suggestion rather than a heavy handed approach!

It is easy to get all worked up and create new problems by lashing out in anger, even if our intent is to change our ways for the better. We can attack others and do damage to ourselves without intention. We need always to bear in mind our program works a day at a time and going slow and deep is better that quick and shallow.

The program is constantly guiding us to be responsible and alert to our side of the road while being considerate to others we interact with. We may be missing the point if we push others away for the sake of being right in matters of mutual misunderstanding.

quote: “Unity is the direct result of application of the principle of anonymity within the group and the fellowship.”
body:
It is not who made a proposal but what was proposed. A popular person may make bad proposals because they are trusted. An unpopular person may make a great proposal that fails to get support due to prejudice. Justice is to discuss proposals to filter out the “who” and get to the “what” prior to voting on what may be an important issue.

Generally, the best group decisions are made when they fit in with other decisions and have the benefit of experienced members familiar with the subject or issue at hand. This may be the ‘unity’ referred to in the sentence quoted from the Grey Book. All things considered makes the best agreement and the anonymity means it is usually self-enforcing.

We are all subject to limitations which we may not be aware of due to proximity. Open discussion and evaluation raises consciousness and that is the basis for “group conscience.’

quote: “If we continue to fall back into the illusion that drugs would make life easier, we must not be ready yet. We believed that drugs would change us and forgot the changes would be fatal. Unless the illusion is shattered that we, in any way, can stop using on our own, we will use again.”
body:
In general, people do not reach an understanding of why and how the N A program works. Try paying more attention to what others are saying with their words and their faces or body language. Some will be attending every word spoken by a newcomer to our rooms because they came to the meeting to help others. What they share can tell you where they are at. Crosstalk breaks the atmosphere of recovery, after the meeting is the time to get with someone and interact. Trying to do it within the context of a meeting creates an interruption of the “meeting” of minds and hearts of attending members.

A dialogue is between a few people and a monologue is even less. Because of the various gatherings of persons in treatment or therapy, it is easy for members to slip into the interruptive format without realizing they are out of order or inappropriate. They may fail to see what they are doing takes away from the purpose and function of a Narcotics Anonymous meeting, which is a different thing entirely. They don’t mean to take away from the group members because they are conditioned to share in different formats like group meetings in a recovery house or therapy..

Many times the group members or secretaries do not know how to handle situations like this and it takes a good sponsor or an effective NA learning day to inform people what to do to pleasantly bring the meeting back to order. We share our E, S and H. Interruption is egotism. .

quote: “We took the opportunity to avoid several of our responsibilities, missing meetings, skipping work, neglecting Twelve Step work, and most important, we stopped asking for help. Growth in the program stopped.”
body:
Other good stuff in this paragraph from the Grey Book. The idea is that certain things we do in recovery can be written out and listed work together to help us stay clean and grow spiritually. If we stop doing one or more of these things, we may put our whole recovery at risk.

Yes, we could coast forward for a little ways. But our spiritual deposits were soon matched by our spiritual withdrawals. The flow of miracles stops coming. We need these miracles and it is sometimes hard for us to see where the prayers make such a big difference.

Sal things are not of this world and so are difficult to quantify in terms like sight, sound, piritutaste, color, etc. They are spiritual and beyond things of this world. Does someone need to spell this out for you? Is this not your experience? We see the difference in the way things work out for us. The way others react to us when we are working the Program gives us the feedback we are on the right track! When we get twitchy and unreasonable, it is a signpost of discomfort because we are out of sorts spiritually. We find the power to stop, look and listen for what we have forgotten or done wrong in time to correct it and so restore the balance within. No dope, no trouble, no need for amends. Just self-correction.

.

quote: “No single person (no personality) has authority. This is vested in a loving God to whom we have turned over our will and lives.”
body:
Although self-willed, self-appointed addicts will seek control, it is a farce. NA is built up on addicts who can share honestly from their hearts what they are feeling and the concern they have for others., Authority is based on putting one person’s certainty above what their find true about service and recovery. Service without feelings is a misnomer. Recovery without helping others is misguided.

We do not help ourselves at the expense of others. When we help others, we get better because of the good feelings we have about ourselves. It is these feelings that wash away the shame and guilt of our past by replacing it will our new life experiences. Where we used to act without thinking, we pause to pray and ask for the help of our Higher Power.

Every time someone asks for help, we make a prayer to be used as instruments. God answer’s these prayers and our betterment is proof positive that prayer works. It is is not ‘us’ that gets better, it is the roles we play in the times of our life!

quote: “In order to achieve our spiritual aim, Narcotics Anonymous must be known and respected. Nowhere is this more obvious than our history. N.A. was founded 1953. For twenty years our Fellowship remain small and obscure. In the 1970s, society realized that addiction had become a worldwide condition and began to look for answers.”
body:
Among those looking for answers, there were many addicts. From the bare bones of what worked in another Fellowship, we found a solid foundation to build our NA Program. What works in NA moves quickly where it is not interrupted by ego and relapse. One addict helping another in an atmosphere of truthfully sharing our pain and desire for recovery allowed us to move, as a Fellowship, beyond our former boundaries and almost overnight, meetings sprang up here in America as well as many other countries worldwide. The newly written and approved service structure, the NA Tree, supplied a working group conscience base to bring addicts together. Addicts pulling together pooled our resources into a great movement of love and caring.

While problems of dealing with success and money flourished, we still surrendered and applied 12 Step recovery among our membership. This is where we continued to grow and prosper disconnected from those who profited and built on our successes. While many of their efforts proved helpful, many members felt alienated and left out. We have never gotten anything easy. Frustrating as it has been at times, answers were found and important principles were utilized to keep members moving forward together. Aided by modern communications, addicts continued to learn from their mistakes. Pulling together and working on personal recovery continued to feed a growing pool of members who never lost hope and were willing to do their part for the general good.

In the 80’s and 90’s many changes were made that left groups and individual members largely out of the loop yet communications still allowed enough members to maintain their attitude of gratitude and interpersonal bonds to keep the spirit of NA strong. As the drug epidemic spread, we continued to grow from within. It is from this internal repository of love and wisdom our heart and spine grew stronger. In making spiritual growth and active part of our lives, we too must be looking for answers on a daily basis. We need to repeat what works and discard what fails.

quote: “We do not define our members. We only insist they have a desire to stop using. Nothing else should matter. This desire is the one crucial must of our program.”
body:
There will be many times when a member comes to us so broken in mind and spirit that they cannot see or hear clearly. In our early days, there were many sincere members who could not understand that pot and liquor were not as powerful as the drugs they could relate to addiction. They had to learn from other members or the painful additional experience of relapse that any mood or mind altering drugs breaks the continuum of recovery that cleantime represents.

The notion that there are no musts in NA misleads members who disagree with portions of the Program they do not yet see as true or important. Only time will tell what they need to complete their understanding. This is the growing body of written recovery that gives us the benefit of acquiring information from others in an atmosphere of surrender and shared experience. Only someone with an honest desire for recovery can learn in this way. If’s and but’s have no place here. If you want a new life and believe your life depends on it, you become what can only be described as ‘totally willing.’ This is what we mean by desire!

We are careful to never put a ceiling on our desire to get better. Addiction is not a disease that goes away just because we are good boys and girls! It stays waiting for the day when we are ‘recovered enough.’ Then it is prepared to bite a real hunk out of our behinds!

quote: “We seek creativity through the spirit. The spirit dwells within all of us. We need to understand that the spirit within each individual is the same.“
body:
The spirit we are talking about is wordless yet finds endless meaning in each moment of our daily lives. Conscious contact is probably the most important single blessing we have to share in NA. Just stopping the show long enough to check in with our Higher Power gives us ongoing advantage over the vagaries of daily living. When we can’t do this because we are too busy or regard conscious contact as hocus pocus, we are in trouble.

It is this inner connection that guides us through the countless opportunities to go right or go wrong.

A member asked how we stay clean in the early days of his recovery. He was asked if he felt like using at the moment. He said, “No, I’m at a meeting.” He was told to stay in that meeting state of mind all day long!
