That’s what going to meetings, reading the literature and picking up the five thousand pound telephone does for us. You’re in NA. You become a member when you say you are. Our disease is capable of the most despicable maneuvers to trick us into using again!

quote: “It is easy to slip back into our old ways. We have to learn to maintain our new lives on a spiritually sound basis to insure our continued growth and recover. God will not force his goodness on us, but we will receive it if we ask.”
body:
Many important spiritual principles are presented in quiet and a carefully worded manner. There is a selfishness hidden when a mortal tries to tell G-O-D what to do. While those early in their understanding of our higher power’s love for us may not quite agree, the reality is if we could tell our higher power what to do, we might, in effect, be talking down to God.

NA member’s! Do not sell yourself short in this manner! Get a higher power, not a equal or lesser power! If you want a higher power to help your paddle your canoe in this life, get a big higher power, one with great bulging muscles that can really do the job! Many of us did not understand this in the beginning of our recovery. We thought any help would do some good. We later discovered we could do better if we chose a belief system that covered more ground than we could see the need for when we were still in the grip of active addiction. It may have even seemed egotistical to get a really larger higher power! We may want to review choices on this important point!

Asking for God’s goodness is a quiet, clear-headed way to live our new life. Building on sand will get old after a while. Ask for strength and guidance because the help available may go far beyond our personal power of vision. It might be like asking our higher power to clean part of the floor in our room or prepare part of our meal. They may be others at our table who need to eat with us. A partially swept floor is still half dirty!

quote: “Anonymity is everywhere in our Traditions and in our Fellowship. It is one of the basics of recovery. The principle of anonymity protects us from our defects of personality and character. Where anonymity exists, personalities and differences have no power. Anonymity in action makes it impossible for personalities to come before principals.”
body:
Anonymity is a principle that will continue to grow in our personal program so long as we are working our Steps. One way to get a glimpse of anonymity is to understand that reality is like a great river we live in all our lives. When we are going with the current, we understand it is the power of the river that carries us forward, not the strength of our arms or the kick of our legs. The current has all the power. Our higher power may be thought of as the current from which we come and with which we go forward.

The power of the group is greater than our personal power. We can access part of this power when we listen to everyone who has a stake in the health and well-being of the group. When we leave people - their viewpoints and perceptions - out of our reckoning, we weaken the potential power of the group.

Think of voting that the sky is chartreuse. Vote how you want to, the sky remains blue. If we win the vote, we can delude ourselves and those who follow our lead, but the sky is still blue. Our personal power is limited. Our games of manipulation and control are empty. Our loving, caring members may declare, “Well, Billy Bob, the sky is really a nice shade or chartreuse today,” out of acceptance and understanding of your personal need to feel secure and powerful, but it is still just a courtesy. It is understood if your desire for recovery is sincere, without wax, you will grow to notice the nice color of the sky is not exactly red or yellow, more like a color you could not see before.

quote: “If the defects we have discovered are real and we have a chance to be rid of them, we would surely experience a sense of well being when we rid ourselves of them.”
body:
Recovery is not something imposed on us from the outside. That is probably one of the reasons N A works for us. It is an inside job. It is our desire for recovery that propels us and our desire to know ourselves better and discover what our real self is like.

Having habitually done things a certain way makes them familiar and somewhat ‘normal’ - at least to us. The more we get in touch with what ‘defective’ actually means, the more embarrassment sets in and more happy we are not to have to live that way anymore. This is what happens when we raise our consciousness.

‘Facts’ are little breaks in reality due to an inability to see the context clearly. ‘De’ is a latin prefix so the whole word, “defect.” It means incomplete and partial. Not really workable and not enough to bear the load demanded by reality. Perhaps we got it wrong when we were growing up. Or perhaps some painful experience taught us to avoid emotional vulnerability even if it cost us the ability to fall in love.

quote: “For us, to use is to die. We have seen addicts come to our Fellowship, try our program, say clean for a period of time, only to drift gradually away.”
body:
It is hard to see why a person would work their Steps unless it was a matter of life and death for them. I know people, and whole populations, are subject to changes we can’t really see unless we stop our busy minds and think about it. The Anti-War Movement certainly fired the flames of addiction. The residue in the 1970’s resulted in many men and women realizing they had some serious problems and needed help. NA was fertile ground for recovery.

Our population, and individuals, today have developed a strange aversion to reading more than a few pages at a time. It is probably and outgrowth from computers and the silver screens.There are still college students and job demands that push these limits. Many folks just breeze through life trying to give the right answer and move on with what they’re doing. Not entirely shallow but you don’t hear terms of praise like, “He’s a really deep thinker.” or “She is so thoughtful!”

Addicts seeking recovery have special motivations for reading and study. Once they learn the disease concept, they begin to realize they are seeking a state of existence not otherwise available to addicts in general and drug addicts in particular. Recovery is an abnormal state for us and it requires some study and learning if we are to have any hope at all. Reading, study and discussion offset the powerful force of addiction.

quote: “No longer comfortable with our using friends, we were not yet at home in meetings. Basically, we lost our fear through the experience of sharing. We share our fears with other addicts.”
body:
NA has a wide landing strip right at our front door. It is labeled, “Keep coming back!” We come to the program from a mission to self-destruct. We are equipped to do ourselves in by a variety of ways. We have to learn the meaning of big new words like Patience, Tolerance and Humility. Our vocabulary of spiritual words and states of existence is in need of repair. You can’t talk about things without words for what you mean or need to know.

A two word term for something important we all need to study and practice is “SLOW DOWN.” It means we have to take more time with our living and learning. Recovery is not a road race. It involves reflection, sincerity and going deeper into who we are and what we want. The new people we meet in the rooms reflect our feelings and life problems. Listening to them and sharing with other people seeking recovery, we are often pleasantly surprised to notice they really listen, care, identify and seem to understand what we are going through. Sometimes, when they share what works for them, we go home and try out what worked for them. A new approach to things often results in personal breakthroughs to a world of happiness and joy. It is not defective to get your hopes up anymore. In fact, it is a lot like stretching your muscles to dream and envision happier times.

Many of the delusions created in active addiction pop into focus when we realize they make no sense when we are clean! Happy people make things happen! Former boundaries were real for us in the past but in the now, they just don’t apply!

quote: “For us to use is to die. We have seen addicts come to our fellowship, try our program, stay clean for a period of time only to drift away.”
body:
It is important to remember that the disease will undercut and derail our efforts to get with the N A program. Saying, “I’m clean but I haven’t changed.” is a really sad statement because it is up to each individual member to surrender, admit unmanageability and begin to learn what has worked for others. We go looking for a Higher Power and find ways to go on from there. Strong egos can stop the recovery process before it has a chance. Surrender is the cure for egotism!

We attend funerals of fellow members and this brings home the importance of working a strong program and carrying a clear message of recovery. Anyone who has been in the program a while has seen a person nurse a futile resentment until it drives them out of the rooms. What has changed besides our not using? Are we collecting resentments or forgiveness where others seem to conflict with us? Or are we seeking resolution of past events instead of carrying them forward?

This does not have to happen when we do the things that work for us. We go beyond ust staying clean off drugs, we use our sponsor, go to meetings regularly, find a homegroup that we attend and support, where we can feel safe and comfortable. By simple prayer, and discovering that we are experiencing true miracles, in our lives, we are becoming more comfortable in our own skin. We see that our obsessions have left us. Now we can believe and see that maybe this thing, called Narcotics Anonymous is working for us.

quote: “Basically, we lost our fear through the experience of sharing. We share our fears with other addicts. The more we do this, the more our fear slips away.”
body:
The real scary things are the things we can’t talk about. As soon as the air moves over our vocal cords and the sound forms the words, the fear slips away. That’s when we know people care and we are being brave enough to stick our necks out and expose heretofore unspoken feelings. While we are sharing and focusing our thoughts, we may also realize the change in our body and heart as the fear is lessened. We can actually feel the pain and tension going away. We can do this without talking in meditation.

Through clean time and many meetings, we hear most of our secrets shared by others through their own personal experience. Fear just dries up. “Joy shared is joy increased. Pain shared is pain lessened.”

As our knowledge and experience taking our own inventory goes forward, we find ourselves applying some of our learnings to other areas of our life. The past becomes largely a ‘done deal.’ Our future is clearly not here yet. The only thing we have real power over is the present. Tomorrow never quite gets here. By the time it gets here, there is another tomorrow waiting.

quote: “However we want to handle it, we go all the way. Think of what we have to lose! As soon as we feel ourselves willing, we should go ahead and ask God to remove our shortcomings.”
body:
We gain momentum as relief replaces reluctance and we realize we are not going to miss those damning defects! Their removal makes for effective habits and routines that allow our real selves to come out and take over. Our ego may want to jump in and wallow in the glory but the grace of God helps us remember when all this recovery stuff felt weird. In the present, we experience the changes as improvements and not hindrances. We are free to go and see and do things that have long been beyond our reach. Surrender works because when we get up off our knees, our feet are finally on solid ground!

It is the insanity of addiction that holds us back. Shame and guilt have a positive effect when we find there is a way out! The combined experience of others strengthens us. Our prayers and faith further strengthens us. Our hope for the future makes us brave.

The beauty of the Seventh Step is not only the relief from character defects, it is the good feeling and ability to envision wants and desires we have not yet fulfilled. Fulfillment means means great joy and becoming the person we always hoped to be someday. Addiction and the things that happened blocked our way and held us back. Now we get to be free.

quote: “It all begins with our first admission and surrender. From that point, each addict is reminded that a day clean is a day won. At first we can little more than go to meetings.Probably we cannot even remember a single name, word or thought from our first meeting. What we do remember is the feeling we got.”
body:
Keep that feeling alive and it will come to be your best friend. Learning to treasure certain feelings helps us build our new life clean. They fill our minds and memories and replace the sadness and lack of hope..

The idea that by staying clean one more day we are winning a victory is a powerful tool for newcomers. There is also a time that comes later in recovery where we have to dig into our early experience to get a grip on early surrender and recall the necessity to surrender to our need for help.

The feelings from our early meetings can awaken our gratitude and hope. The whole world looks different after you’ve been clean a few years - or a few decades, one day at a time. Every step forward leads to another. Some of the situations we fall into become great learning experiences. Going through stuff isn’t so bad when we realize it’s getting better, not worse.

quote: “Addicts must patiently re-learn things forgotten and learn how to live. This is what Narcotics Anonymous is about.”
body:
“Everything we did while we were using, we will do again in recovery.” I saw this in my recovery. Working in the same town, going to the same car clubs, meeting a lot of the same people and doing a lot of the same activities.

We learn from our mistakes. We can only learn so much from books and teachers, after that it is the school of hard knocks. We really can only see our mistakes as learning experiences. Otherwise, we would beat ourselves to death.

I learned much from the oldtimers. They had been down the path before me. They could tell me to watch out for that limb, watch out for that snake and watch out for that hole so you don’t twist your ankle. It was much less painful to hang around them and bounce my plans off of them. They would give me helpful advice.

Some now would see this as controlling, but I was grateful for it. With my ego safely tucked away, the helps I receive from others sure makes me glad I didn’t have to go through pain and trouble to get the benefits!

quote: “If we are to continue abstinence we must be responsible and productive, not necessarily in terms of normal definitions, but in terms of spiritual principles. We must grow.”
body:
Each one of us is different and we have different requirements. The freedom N A offers allows us to take in and concentrate on the subject we need most. As we grow a divine hand guides us to exactly what we need to be happier and more effective. We cannot always understand what is happening at the time, but clean time gives us a chance to review what happens and the results of our growth.

Every so often I need to take out the meeting schedule and start going to different meetings and checking them out. I can only go to the same meeting and hear the same story so many times without screaming.

A guy was sent to prison for the first time in his life. He looked to his cell mate for direction. At dinner, someone shouted out 17 and everyone laughed. Then another person shouted out 23 and everyone laughed. The new guy ask his cellmate what was going on. The cellmate explained, “We have been in here a very long time and we only knew so many jokes. And after a while, instead of telling each joke in its entirety we decided to give each joke a number.”

The new guy asked the cellmate “Can I try it?” The cellmate told him to go ahead and the new guy shouted out 11. A lot of the other prisoners stared at the new guy with serious faces. The new guy turned to the experienced prisoner and asked “What did I do wrong?” The cellmate said “You didn’t tell it right”.

quote: “When we accept that we are responsible for our problems, we realize that we can be equally responsible for our solutions.”
body:
True freedom starts when we take full responsibility for our actions, the consequences of our actions, our feelings and our attitudes. We no longer are victims, either of others or of circumstances. We become free to “be here now” finding the solution to our problems without wasting time or effort on how we got here. Having the ability to live in the solution is a result of incorporating spiritual principles in every aspect of our lives.

One of the problems that comes up in ongoing recovery is we get so comfortable that we begin to leave off the daily practices that feed our recovery. Recovery is the result of positive thinking and gratitude painting our daily lives in cheerful or thoughtful colors. If we forget our prayers for health and recovery, our colors fade, become pale and go over to black and white - or grey! When we make a decision for the best, we change right then into a mindset that allows us to look for the positive and experience anew the joy of living.

Even when problems come up, if we surrender and open ourselves to help and help comes along. Sometimes we don’t recognize solutions when they appear. Pain hurts and clouds our thinking. We learn to relax and search for something to be grateful for - then even something small comes and the gratitude puts a little wind in our sails.

quote: “That no matter what we have done or what course our addiction has taken, we can relax and enjoy the love that fills the room at every meeting which follows the Twelve Traditions.”
body:
In each one of us is a shrivelled up little pissed off addict. To that shriveled up little addict he is a parasite and the person attending NA is the host. A clear NA message is poison to the shriveled up little addict parasite. Love and hope dilute the poison of negative thinking and then suddenly, the sun comes up. Life is learning to be grateful and plunge into the water of life again. Call up someone who is hurting. Pray for someone sick in the hospital. Pray for a newcomer who really wants to get clean and needs help learning how to use the 12 Steps!

We know addiction is a disease and dit doesn’t play fair. The clear message delivered in meetings is so painful that it hurts to try of optimize. The shrivelled up little addict uses crosstalk to stop the meeting and break up the message. The little addict steals energy from someone sharing honestly by interrupting. Their ego prevents them understanding their rudeness and discourtesy.

Because many are forced to attend NA and the shriveled up addict is so threatened that they must cross talk all the time. Hence, our meeting now have much chaos of crosstalk in it.

quote: “Spirituality as a way of life for an addict seems a total contradiction. Many feel that they have found what they were searching for in drugs through spirituality. By working the Steps we are relieved of our obsession to use and many gratefully serve the Fellowship for this reason. Growth means change and we feel we must live on a spiritual basis in order to change.”
body:
It should not go without saying our pettiness and ingratitude will resurface as soon as we stop focusing on gratitude and hope. Anyone can find something to complain about no matter how great their lives are going. It is possible that negativity is part of humanity but it doesn’t get us anywhere. It just makes us dissolute and ungrateful.

Addiction denies the power of spirituality. It pretends physical comfort and an agile mind can supply all our needs! This is a lie. Many times and in many ways people find that they need more than the physical and mental can offer. We always want more and the only way to satisfy our needs is through the power of the Spirit.

Change of mind and mood is available through a life of the spirit. This is most easily available when we sit quietly, close our eyes and meditate. Isn’t it surprising that when our eyes are closed, we still see things? The confused images can resolve into words or visions. In meditation we learn to be more fully awake than when we are going about our daily lives. This wakefulness allows us to experience life more fully and many small matters cease to give us trouble.

quote: “From self-centered, angry, frightened isolated people, we become loving, sharing, self- assured spiritual parts of a greater whole. We changed from hopelessly helpless to hopefully helping.”
body:
Our daily recovery offsets the gravity of our addictive disease to draw us into the same selfish and benighted condition we were before recovery with a suitable coat of paint to protect us from the notice of our fellow N A members. We want to look good. This is not a badness in us, it is simply our disease and all members admit they have it! Rationalization, justification and projection are tools we use to stay sick and avoid recovery.

Only when our sense of unworthiness is washed out by faith and inventory will we begin to outweigh our hopelessness. Recovery comes by degrees many small acts of seeking and surrender build up to increase our tolerance for good things in our lives.

Who was there every time we messed up in our active addiction? Mr. Ego. What got us into recovery in the first place? The idea we could overcome our former boundaries and live clean and free. We send Mr. Ego on his way and let down our defense mechanisms and calmly walk into the light of recovery. Things still change but they change for the better.

EDITING

quote: “We don’t want to settle for the limitations of the past. We want to examine and re- examine all our old ideas, and constantly improve on them or replace them with new ones.”
body:
Being able to change our lives by changing our beliefs and entertain new approaches to old problems is a great discovery.

The courage to change our old ways puts us on the path of recovery. The 12 Steps are the tools we use to navigate that path that leads us to freedom from active addiction. We can feel ourselves coming alive!

The 10th Step can keep on-going spiritual growth a reality. Every time we cancel out an old habit that no longer works or fits our life, our view expands and we can see more defects that can be changed. The more we grow, the more we can see. The more we see, the more we can change. As we grow, we realize we get closer to our Higher Power and eventually His Will becomes Our Will. We do not have to fear anymore.

quote: “Asking for help in specific terms usually precedes getting that help. By opening the gates of our hearts, we become ready to receive the help we need.”
body:
This applies to prayer as well as people. Loosening the grip of our egocentric nature makes it more reasonable to just ask for help instead of contriving a way to steal or trick someone into getting what we need. Everyone has different needs and faces different problems. Learning to seek help from a Higher Power, we are frequently surprised by how well prayer works! One common thread that keeps coming up in the stories of our members is that at some point they said the words “Please help me”. We have heard this over and over and over thousands of times. The funny thing is a prayer is not selfish - it includes a special component: surrender and faith in a HP!

Getting what we need, sometimes even when we need it, is a good sign we are working our N.A. program. Other times, our HP says, no, wait a bit or offers something better. Even when we don’t get our way, it usually works out for us. A prolonged ‘best’ is better than a brief ‘good.’

Obsession can be simply habitual desperation. When our bellies are full and our most pressing needs are met, it gets easier to take our time and attend to the details in our daily life. Recovery is learning to use spirituality even when things are going well for us.

quote: “We must puncture our egos and realize that we have much more work to do. When someone points out a shortcoming, our first reaction is one of defensiveness.”
body:
We learn to realize that someone cares enough about us to show us our character defects. Then we learn to find the willingness to take a look at them. It is only with awareness, acceptance and action that we have any hope of changing.

We learn to pause instead of instantly react. We think about our responses and what is the right thing to do in accordance with our growing understanding of spiritual principles. Only then do we take action. It is this momentary pause before action that helps redirect efforts in a direction that is not only good for ourselves but often for those we love and care about.

We gotta get in the habit of keeping our mouth shut. Its critical, really. We simply cannot go off on people all the time. We just create messes that we have to clean up. It is said that whoever is the first to raise their voice, loses the argument.

We always tend to error on the side of shooting off our mouths. So we have to train to error on the side of not speaking up. It seldom does any good to sound off anyway, so why even go there.

quote: “When a resentment or any other emotional relapse occurs, failure to practice the Steps and result in physical relapse. Many of our newcomers have difficulty coming into the Fellowship because they do not understand we have a disease called “addiction.” We are doomed (spelling corrected) to see our past behavior as part of ourselves and not part of our disease.”
body:
After we have experienced a loss or serious difficulty in recovery we then learn to take a hit. Some people believe that changes for success in the program depend on one's ability to take a hit. We are talking repossessed cars, IRS problems, angry creditors attaching wages and dire medical diagnosis. One member had his house burn to the ground and did not miss a beat. This is working the program.

I pray for them for a few weeks in the shower to have classic cars and fantastic sex. It works every time. It may be that when we are emotionally charged, the energy can strengthen a wordless prayer that manifests in our own lives.

Our recovery is an ongoing study of our disease concept. Addiction substitutes a mind or mood altering drug for real change or improvement. Changing our reality works better and through recovery, we have the internal resources to do the job.

quote: “We remain honest to free ourselves from guilt. As we begin to function in society, our creative freedom helps us sort out priorities and do the most basic things first. Daily practice of the Twelve Step program enables us to change from what we were to what our Higher Power would have us become.”
body:
We learn that addiction is a disease of confusion and when loaded we couldn’t really help ourselves. Clean, we practice personality changes with the help of many others and learn to make better choices and substitute inspiration for desperation.

Functioning in society, we become active parts of a greater whole. When others hurt, we feel their pain and discomfort. When they get help for their needs, we experience a matching sense of relief. Becoming a part of humanity, we regain our sense of purpose and experiencing community with other people. We realize this is what was missing in our former lives. No wonder we were so lost and confused!

Doing first things first, we have less to bother us or worry about. It may take some time to accustom ourselves to our new situation. The 12 Steps give us powerful tools to resolve old issues and delete unwanted defects of character. This gives us extra time and resources to find our own happy destiny.

quote: “Having begun attending meetings regularly, we were introduced to the 12 Steps. Working the Steps got us out of our old attitudes.”
body:
Attitude is another word for beliefs. Poorly formed beliefs, personal habits and unmade amends can really cloud a person’s view of reality. Point is that while it is all really there, most of it can be changed without great cost or expense. Personality change is mostly a change of heart or mind.

Just hearing “How It Works” in meetings seems overwhelming. This may be because we have not had a clear enough mind to ask questions. After a few meetings and our minds begin to clear, we may speak up in a meeting for the first time. This is a good time to ask questions in meetings and begin seeking a sponsor. Asking someone to sponsor us can also seem insurmountable yet once you ask, you realize it’s no big deal. Just another doorway we open to self-improvement.

Because of fear and a lack of understanding, we go to more meetings to find if we really desire this lifestyle. After all, addicts with a strong desire for recovery built the Program and wrote our literature. Their gratitude and love shine forth unmistakably.

.

quote: “We cry easily and find it safe to cry and express love and share all our emotions in the atmosphere of recovery found at N.A. meetings. The steps become our framework of daily change. Continuous abstinence requires spiritual growth which leads to emotional recovery in our lives.”
body:
Getting in touch with our feelings, we begin to come together as human beings. Our perceptions and responses change from defensive and fearful over to empathetic and caring. We tend to look for ways to help.

Our brain deals with information, our hearts deal with how we feel. Both are necessary and important for healthy human beings. In modern times, it has become more common knowledge that antidepressants dull our senses and result in deadening our sense of personal responsibility. Spiritual growth rebuilds our internal resources to the point of a spiritual awakening expressed in caring and sharing.

The commonality we share with all other people gives us a general awareness of others and suggest ways we can help. Acting on the positive, restorative impulses strengthens us and gives us confidence in applying spiritual principles and carrying our message.

Over time, we adjust our habits and beliefs to reflect the principles that have proven to work for us when nothing else could! The feeling that no matter what happens we are somehow at fault goes away to be replaced by automatic concern. We identity emotionally and don’t deny our emotions.

quote: “Though the freedom in our new lives , we are finally able to see the special qualities that we possess as individuals--qualities that we used to envy in others--never realizing the potential within ourselves.”
body:
Our defects kept us from seeing our own good qualities. Freed from our defects, we can finally find what we were looking for all the time. "Don't judge your insides by another person's outsides" is a cliché we have often heard. Our low self-image was no longer valid, when we began to see ourselves as we really are.

Accepting ourselves as we are, just for today, with the understanding that there will always be room for improvement. We gain new insight each time we revisit the Steps. Watching other members grow, encourages us to make our own improvements.

Where once we suffered from low self-esteem to the degree that many of us shunned even the image of ourselves in the mirror, we now face life on life's terms with a sense of dignity and purpose.

quote: “It has been our experience that personal problems will be resolved when we are willing to accept responsibility for them.”
body:
Personality change used to be rare among addicts. Once an addict, always an addict used to be the way it was. Now, through the 12 Steps of N A, we have a new way to deal with our disease of addiction. We can follow a certain order and remake our self- will and ego at its roots.

First we surrender to the truth of our addiction. Then we find a belief system that works for us and place ourselves the care of a Higher Power. The rest of the 12 Steps have to do with clearing the wreckage of our past and paving the road to a new life.

Accepting responsibility is what gives us the right to change. We may be powerless over other people, places and things, but we enjoy great power over taking our own inventory and changing ourselves!

quote: “Each day we stay clean by practicing the Steps in our lives, our chances of relapse decrease. Each of us is only one drug away from a painful active addiction, but living our program through regular honest sharing in the Fellowship of Narcotics Anonymous increases our chances for uninterrupted clean time.”
body:
Living our Program of NA, we restore our self-image into someone who can admit fault and admit their need for help. This is why we stay clean on days good and bad! At first it may feel funny to be honest, but after a few experiences we begin to adjust to a new attitude. We learn not to fear the truth. We grow even when we are hurting. Everyone of us can recall going through hard times only to look back and thank our HP we didn’t use when things weren’t going our way.

Spirituality is full of surprises. The more we grow, the more we can look inside and see things that used to be hidden. The power of our Loving God is enormous and we are often amazed at how many ways we are protected and guided past harm and hurting others.

Day by day we practice our new way of life until it becomes our natural way of doing things. The further we get from our last usage, the closer we get to our Higher Power and the relief recovery brings. The more we practice, the better we get. Honesty comes easier and asking for help becomes an impulse.

EDITING

quote: ‘Being clean is abnormal to us, and we must learn how to live in an ongoing manner. A great magic is found when we help others. As clean addicts in the Fellowship of Narcotics Anonymous, we help ourselves by helping others.”
body:
Clear headed awareness tells us what we experience and guides us into our responses. Narcotics are a self-imposed sleep disorder. We are not really awake when we are loaded and our perceptions and conclusions are distorted by the drugs we are taking. Clean it may take a while to see, hear and feel more accurately what the heck is going on!

Helping others we can identity with gives us a personal, ongoing education in the problems addicts face and different perspectives on what improvements can help. Since we are getting our information from others, our ego is not so threatened and our willingness is prodded by observing the embarrassing quality of what is clearly not working. Sharing the pain of others gives us insight into our own dilemmas. This insight gives us great power to see our own faults and strength to make changes as we grow.

The change is something we carry in our hearts as well as in our heads. We find our feelings are actually located in our hearts and our feelings become more accurate and trustworthy with time. When we reach out and put ourselves in the shoes of someone else, we are blessed with insight and ability to change rather than feelings of condemnation or fear.

quote: “Much of the loneliness and fear will have been replaced by the love of the Fellowship and the security of being a part of a new way of life.”
body:
The loneliness inside becomes peopled by those we come to know well. We have internal resources to see into problems and make connections we could not see, feel or hear before the changes worked by the 12 Steps. As we work our Steps in recovery, the Steps work on us! It is hard to be afraid of things once we gain insight and understanding.

Love coming from other members prevents us from feeling isolated and trapped. It we were in jail with a few hundreds of our new friends, it would be a whole different experience! People who take our side in things or help us clarify misunderstanding wipe away pain and confusion. It is a far cry from facing life alone.

Secure in the family of new friends and associates opens doors that used to look like brick walls. God and the NA way gives us more than one answer when we are in trouble. We can actually ask our HP to handle things that we are powerless over.

quote: “The enemy we have to fight is our own self-destructiveness. When we begin to work our program, we will like ourselves better.”
body:
The changes we make prepare the way for us to live happy and effective lives. We are not self-destructive by accident, we simply cannot see any way out of the messes we make of our lives. Than we surrender and the closed doors swing open and we can see alternatives where there used to be none.

Self-destructiveness can actually guide us forward in ways that used to have no options. When we change inside, our outside changes because our new attitudes and behaviors make it possible. Self approval gets us past the blockages and admitting fault or apologizing is no big deal.

For one thing, when those we are interacting with see we are more relaxed and able to take personal responsibility, they will often lighten up. They may even offer suggestions that may work out well for both of us. Our negative position may be one of the causes of our difficulty. They can’t dance with a partner who seems to be deliberately stepping on their toes!

When we like ourselves better, the world around us seems to like us better as well!

quote: “Complacency is the enemy of members with substantial clean time. We never fully recover. Guilt, remorse, fear, lust and pride may all become unbearable if we fail to invest ourselves totally in the program, no matter how much clean time we have.”
body:
Write it on the wall! The idea that our good time drug use was fun will be like the call of the wild and at some point we will confuse ourselves enough to the point using seems like an option. We may actually think just one or two won’t be a problem. This is amazing considering all we’ve been through. All we have to do is just remember what the last one did for us and after a few moments a miracle happens and we are restored to sanity!

Complacency is a sort of mind-trick that goes against our own most painful experience. It allows us to set aside painful memories of confusion and hopeless despair. Addiction is a tricky disease. It will sneak right up beside us and stick a knife in us before we know it! It will drain our gratitude and punch holes in our brand new tires!

Remember feeling guilty over taking advantage of a friend’s trust? Remember a car accident that resulted in someone’s injury or death? Remember tensing up when the phone rang or someone was at your door? Remember getting turned on by the attentions of another where you knew a price would be paid by you for your betrayal? How did it feel to take credit for someone else’s work. Hmm.

The time and energy we put into helping others fills us with joy and good feelings. We never have to use again against our will. We do have to practice focusing on our gratitude and do the things we do to work our program. Whether we know it or not, we have a lot to be grateful for in our new lives.

quote: “We had to reach out and to accept the love and understanding that the Fellowship had to offer.”
body:
Going to meetings, asking hard questions, making new friends - these are things that will give you a new life. Our disease will minimize the attention and friendliness because we may not realize members are being polite and considerate. We learn to avoid overwhelming newer members with attention that may be interpreted as ‘social pressure.’

Reaching out can be scary when we are accustomed to being alone and when we have trouble making ‘small talk.’ So, giving new folks their space and letting them show they are ready before asking questions. We learn not to bring up subjects that may make someone feel uncomfortable. Many new members are caught up in trying to decide if they should take NA seriously!. Who knows what they may have heard or experienced on their way to the rooms of recovery!

Coming to the Program of NA may be the biggest decision a person has made in their lives. Respect and forbearance may be received as kind and considerate, as is intended. When someone is ready, they will let you know. Remember how it was for you in the beginning.

quote: “We want desperately for the place where addicts recover to be a safe, free from outside influences. We feel safe at our closed meetings. Everyone's an addict. We feel totally free to express ourselves because no law enforcement agencies are involved. ”
body:
Many of our members desire personnel anonymity, especially when we are new to the program, though within a short time most come to cherish the therapeutic value of one addict helping another that is found in the process of identification, and that process is often fostered only in the safety of a circle of clean addicts living a program of recovery.

Anonymity is half our name. The ability to share peer-to-peer gives us a chance to share and express ideas we had been forced to keep to ourselves. Many times we ‘self-correct’ when we hear ourselves say something that doesn’t make sense. Keeping things inside perpetuates some really dumb ideas.Sharing our feelings and ideas with other addicts who are free to call us on our bullshit can give us great insight into where we have gone wrong.

As we have grown rapidly, we have also suffered intrusion where new members have adopted some really strange ideas. One idea is that the newcomer is the most important person at any meeting. They omit or never learned the second half of the sentence, “because we can only keep what we have by giving it away.” While this can be humorous, it can also be a serious problem. If enough ‘important members’ repeat it they apparently come to believe they are hot stuff. The reality is they know little or nothing of recovery when they are totally knew!

Rapid growth can punch a few holes in a well formed and effective program. There are place where the newer members practically run things and openly attack members with clean time. It is up to you to stand up to these poor souls and try to carry a genuine message of recovery. We pass on what works in NA!

quote: “This Step must cut into our character defects and expose our motives and our actions for what they really were. We have no right to expect these things to reveal themselves.”
body:
The saying, “How come everything I let go of has claw marks all over it?” lampoons the way we tend to cling to our established habit patterns. Since Step Five continues the inventory process begun in Step Four, it helps us sort out our assets and liabilities. To make this clear, liabilities are things that cost us plenty without delivering anything worth keeping. Inventory is what leads to our personal spiritual awakening and general betterment.

Active addiction clouded our minds and threw our thinking off track. We were unaware of just how cookoo we had become! It often appeared as if people were over-reacting to our regular habits and mannerisms. We were just kidding or cutting up! Recovery gives us new resources we carry with us inside.. We can hear and see more accurately. When people ask us what’s wrong, they are really being pretty nice. Some of our behavior is awful and we don’t even pick up on it!

Working with others makes us more sensitive to bad manners and prods us to check-out our own behavior. We catch ourselves and have great power to correct our errors thought it may feel strange to do so.. As we get better, we lose track of our improvement and don’t realize that in some cases people mistake our kindness for weakness. And recovery doesn’t allow us to react in kind to bad behavior in others. But part of getting better is gaining internal resources and social skills that can stand firm against the pressure put on us by addicts who may be seeking recovery but still operating in the same old ways.

quote: “N.A. reawakened old memories of what it felt like to be a member of the human family. Slowly, we opened up, reached our, warmed up, and let ourselves love and be loved. The original desire to be clean leads us to a desire to help others.”
body:
Each time we helped others was like a stitch weaving us back into teh human race. For those members with long clean time, it is important to remember how NA became their family when they first came into NA. Being one among many people submitting to a process that allows us to change our lives give us a good feeling. It was like a true loving family and very nurturing. One difference in the Program is we are not punished when we screw up. Other people notice but their reactions are different. In many cases they understood and were supportive. We need to reflect back on those days which were so tender to us. These memories of yesteryear can save our bacon and give us vitality since they were based on our own experiences. These were the golden days of NA. We were so fortunate to have been there and to have been a part of it. We are truly blessed.

As those with long clean time become more numerous, it affects the whole Fellowship. We can read, study and become ever more informed about out NA history and what works for addicts. It is also important to note that simple NA became more effective as we learned more and more. Some got lost in the crowd. The maxim that NA works doesn’t include those who stop working their 12 Steps! Stop pedaling and the bicycle falls over. Recovery can be arrested and forward progress reversed if we let ourselves slip onto our old ways, even if we are still otherwise clean!

The reason we stress helping others so much is that each member is a mirror image of ourselves to some degree. Helping others we see up close how things can go wrong and self- correct to avoid the problems we see others create for themselves unwittingly. .

quote: “Many times, our efforts have produced in us feelings of peace and serenity that we have never known before.”
body:
I was in a one year meeting tonight (these are meetings where only people in their first year are allowed to share). Someone read the sentence from our literature, “The newcomer is the most important person in the room because we can only keep what we have by giving it away.” Imagine what happens if you don’t read the whole sentence!

I felt very calm and at ease. I had no major problems in my life and no deadlines to meet. All was well. Had I been able to share, I would have told the newcomers that someday, they would be at ease and calm too with no impending problems. Peace and serenity come to those who surrender and work the 12 Steps. The Traditions work with the Steps to provide a healthy atmosphere to encourage spiritual growth.

Spiritual growth is affected when we surrender and find a higher power. Turning our life and will over to the care of this higher power grants immediate results: peace of mind, freedom from fear and curiosity about new good things coming our way. Being addicts, many of us begin to loaf and act like recovery is an effortless process. Many fall into a popular trap wherein the members will say, “See, NA isn’t working out so good for me!” We are quick to forget the hopelessness and despair that had us on the ground when we first sought recovery in NA!

The good part is all we have to do is renew our surrender and go back to working our program on a daily basis. Every day is a new beginning. Remember the basic principles like patience, tolerance and humility. Getting back in the habit of thinking and speaking with your higher power get the ball rolling again.

quote: “In our addiction we consistently placed our personal welfare before anything else. Here we found that in the long run what’s best for the group was usually good for us. We chose to conform to the common good because that’s what worked for us.”
body:
We learned to put group purpose ahead of personal preference. Where there were conflicts, it was understood that we are free to re-consider our stand on issues. No one can make us do much of anything. We’ll leave the group if we are not happy there. We can even reconsider and come back later if we like. But for purposes of recovery, we all have to pull together if we are going to enjoy the miracle of recovery.

One addict told me, “I may leave a group in a huff but I have learned never to tell them that I will not be back. I cannot afford to burn any bridges, meeting wise. I may have an urge to use and need to run into that meeting and I always want it to be there for me.” I considered this very wise advice.

Fortunately, NA Is a giving and forgiving program of recovery. We all make stupid mistakes and self-correction gives immediate relief. Consequences can be dealt with because surrender changes our attitude. Other members may understand and help. Group members are quick to forgive the same mistakes they make on a day to day basis. Life allows us to keep correcting our mistakes and staying on the right track.

quote: “Any addict clean, without the compulsion to use, is a miracle. We keep this miracle alive in ongoing recovery with positive attitudes and awareness involving personal growth.”
body:
When we first came in, most of us could only relate to the person with a week or two clean. The idea of having years clean was something we simply could not comprehend. This shows just what a miracle a day clean can be to an addict.

As the weeks, months, years and decades pile up and we acquire toys and life’s problems fall away, it is easy for the miracle to seen like a given. Not like the ultimate gift. Lack of gratitude seeps in a we seem to be doing so well, the maintenance Steps may seem less important. Perhaps it is a mistake to look at Steps Ten, Eleven and Twelve as maintenance Steps! Perhaps they are like the other nine Steps - paths to the future growth that is just as important as the other Steps forward.

Recovery is the gift of life, freedom and joy. It is the gift of unlimited dreams come true. It really cannot be compared to any other gift in the world. It is the cure for a fatal disease, the key to unlock any door and mental serenity on a level not experienced by most people in their entire life. This is why we sometimes hear someone say that they are grateful addicts. Just because we passed our early learning periods, might we not have Steps ahead that can only be taken once our waters have cooled and we are more able to see and hear more clearly. The early grades of learning make us able to take on new journeys we could not undertake before. Look for new doorways that pique our curinsities. Ways that fill in the many blanks still missing due to our addictive personalities.

quote: “As our lives become more comfortable , many of us lapse into spiritual complacency, and we find ourselves in the same horror and loss of purpose from which we came.”
body:
Some of us longtimers become fixated on the rules that got us clean, complete abstinence, non- affiliation, etc.We must remember that NA is a living breathing entity. Sure, group conscious was cool back in the day, but these days time is scarce and we need to figure out ways to do stuff faster and quicker. Sometimes procedures must change. Mostly, all we are required to do is study how our procedures worked when they were first adopted and make sure the changes don’t eliminate other working parts of our program.

We cannot hang onto the past and wallow in it. Those were very different times. Today we have pressures on us to adopt new and improved methods of getting clean, even if they are not a part of the NA Program. It is nuts to take drugs to correct addiction. Gasoline is not a cure for fire. Outside enterprises had invested incredible sums of money and effort to produce drug replacement therapy that promise relief from withdrawal symptoms and used to have packaging that contained warnings against taking the withdrawal drug beyoud a few months. Other meds which neatly point to ‘harm reduction’ as more pertinent than ‘total abstinence.’

Newcomers can easily be fed information that doesn’t accord with NA Steps and principles of recovery while in treatment or under threat of incarceration. .Clean means you are not taking mood or mind altering drugs. While there are many good members who have to take medication to deal with special problems, never should they try to change NA members away from total abstinence. NA is a program of recovery from addiction. Patience and tolerance in the case of members who have to take something to stabilize is not a weakness. We don’t use, no matter what. We have never promised to have all the answers for all the people!. Those who follow our path have accumulated decades of recovery, one day at a time.

quote: ”The Twelve Traditions of N.A. are not suggested, and they are not negotiable. There are the rules that keep our fellowship alive and free.”
body:
Not all share these views. But those that stay clean for the long haul tend to be very rigid on honoring the Traditions. We are here for the duration. We need to be rigid on certain important items if we are going to remain a program of recovery from drug addiction. Changing our Program to satisfy a minority of our membership miss-represents the larger body of our Fellowship. Accepting outside authorities who often get their information from those who have a conflict of interest and no personal experience with the subject puts our lives at risk.

There are some people with mental illness who get help from working the NA 12 Steps when nothing else works for them. Surely we who have so much to be grateful for would not deny these kind and worthy people. Life can be tough and choose to be grateful and honorable while maintaining our spiritual beliefs and the bedrock of abstinence. Those who would argue for basic change apparently do not realize they have no right to change what works for so many.

It may come as a shock to some looking for dominance among our membership that egotism may be the source or trying to change our Program. NA is a place where we can come in out of the storm of active addiction and begin to recovery process in the safety of people who have learned to live clean and deal with issues that used to trip them op.

quote: “When we came to our first meeting it was though a big weight was lifted from our shoulders, guilt feelings rolled off, and a feeling of peace came over us, as we realized that with our surrender to our higher power, would come the help we so desperately needed.”
body:
We kept trying to do it on our own. It never worked. After our most strenuous intentions, we would ourselves using again. So, we came to this new fangled thing called Narcotics Anonymous. For most of us, our first meeting is vividly remembered just as we remember where we were when we heard that Kennedy had been shot. There was a genuine thrill of recovery!

One member recalls, “I was in room with two couples and a pink bathroom door. It was in a church and they said that I never had to use again no matter what.” This was exactly what I wanted to hear. It was music to my ears and I lapped it up. It was a turning point in my life and I somehow knew it at the time. In the time when I might have picked up I recall that moment and it remains a turning point for me.

It takes what it takes. Giving ourselves the privilege of thinking, we can sit and quietly recall the sense of adventure and joy of relief of our moment of decision to stay clean and live the Steps of NA recovery. There will probably always be moments of confusion when our recovery hangs by a thread. That’s only natural for an addict. We stay clean because if we can remember what our last one did for us and to us, it will remain our last one. Call a member, go to a meeting, read come literature - Don’t Pick Up!

quote: “We also felt the great feeling of warmth and love that came from the group.”
body:
When I came into the Fellowship of NA, the group meeting was very tight. We always went out for coffee after the meeting. We talked about our lives, our sponsors those we were interested in helping and those we depended on to help us. For many of us it was the intimate family that I never had growing up. Mom’s and Pop’s, aunts and uncles, even your people we felt protective of replaced the days, months and years of pain and confusion of active addiction.

We were all incredibly grateful, each one of us. The daily drag was replaced by the daily delight! Real recovery is fun and interesting. Those who hold onto grudges of get lost in clouds of confusion just need to settle down to the claen life and work their Steps. Surrender sets us free, new believe in a HP feed a growing hope that recover is real and will last, one day at a time. Turning our life and will over to our HP is very different from letting our connection provide a temporary high.

I cannot express how wonderful it was. It is hard to put into words how good it feels to not have to bother with all the confusion and discomfort that used to rule my days and nights.

quote: “It is only through understanding and application that they have power.”
body:
We have all heard this line read at each N A meeting we attend. You can become a member when you say you are. You become a member in good standing when you stop using, go to meetings and work the N A 12 Steps. Members in good standing also become familiar with our N A 12 Traditions. Pulling together, learning about our group purpose and dealing with powerful personalities are extremely important as a floor plan for recovery to take place. All the Traditions interlock. You can consider a Tradition violation and with a little thought and study you can see where it affects the other eleven Traditions as well. The spirit of the Traditions brings us together with plenty of individual freedom along with the loving support of the group to pull on for what we need.

The traditions provide a platform for carrying a clear message. Without this clear platform, it is impossible to carry the clear message. Carrying a clear message depends on mundane things like having the room unlocked early and not shooing people out after the meeting when they need to talk. It means having the A/C or heat turned on in time to make it comfortable inside. We need to have literature, order and no crosstalk.

The time in the meeting is a window for addicts to learn they can do it and how. Every moment is precious. For some addicts, this one meeting will be all the NA they ever hear. For others, what they hear will determine if they ever come back to NA again or go back to using.

The importance of leading a meeting right may be the most important service position in NA. We are talking life and death here. Death chased us into our first meeting. Many of our friends had died in the past. We knew we could be next .Many of us only came to NA when someone near and dear passed away.

quote: “We cannot make these changes alone. We need our Higher Power’s help, and the help of the Narcotics Anonymous Fellowship. ”
body:
One of the greatest blessings in the N A 12 Steps is realizing our HP is always with us and always on our side! This is where “Never Alone” comes from. Some of us constantly pray, “God I need your help. I don’t have to power. Please help me.” This not a empty idea. It really works when you work it! Pray to have a great day - today.

N A also means “Need Answers” which our Higher Power is willing to give us if we learn to listen to the still small quiet voice inside. We can also get answers by asking more experienced members to share their experience, strength and hope with us. The importance of a wide support network comes into bearing here. By building relationships with many members, we build a repository of strength and experience to draw upon in troubling times.

NA can become like a huge library of what to do when the going gets rough. Simple surrender eliminates some of life’s thorniest problems for addicts. Gaining a workable belief we take to heart and allow to help us every single day delivers a whole new outlook that make possible all the changes we need to live the clean life, free of flaws and defects what used to be our standard daily diet. This may sound impossibly good but it is the honest truth. Most humans have some sort of a belief, they just don’t take it seriously enough to get real results. Maybe we should say the results we would like to enjoy!

quote: “Our attention focuses on the solutions, and our old ideas break up and dissolve like icebergs in the tropics. When the compulsion to use is lifted from us, and we begin to think of others before ourselves, a true miracle begins. Working the steps, practicing the principles and using the tools, we begin to see ourselves in a new light.”
body:
One reason I love going to NA is to see people being reborn. They learn how to read, become humble, get jobs, go back to school and learn to cope. The growth can be at light speed. I have never seen else anything like it. Honesty becomes a way to avoid trouble instead of a hazard to avoid being put in jaill. Open-mindedness is a way to access information that used to hide behind a doorway marked ‘Closed.’ The amazing behind our willingness to try is that many times when we are simply willing, we don’t have to do whatever it is.

Many addicts have commented that as soon as they surrendered and came to believe miracles began to happen. Our defects fell away as we progressed through Steps Four, Five, Six and Seven. We still had to deal our pasts by making amends. Once we worked those Steps, we continued with inventory, prayer and meditation. A spiritual awakening brought everything together and became spiritual leaders on the many levels that make each member special and important.

Helping others as no one else can, we constantly find ways to improve our lives through being there for others. To be effective, our grasp of spiritual principles have to stand the test of watching others progress in recovery. What we have been through become active working principles that provide a sense of safety and positive expectation to those who come to us. We know all the time it is the power of a loving God working through us and not some cheesy egotism drawing us back into self-will. We truly turn our lives and will over to the power of a Loving God.

quote: “One of the most important parts of our new way of life is being a part of a group of addicts also seeking recovery.”
body:
When I came in NA was truly a tribe. It was the high school clique, the college frat, the army band of brothers except closer. We lived together, traveled together, ate together and recovered together. It was the most wonderful experience I have ever known.

One member shares, “It is so wonderful that I miss it even now, thirty-four years later. I love to sit and reflect on it. It makes me happy to this day. It was true bliss. We were broke but happy. In time even our worldly needs are met.” This feeling of togetherness generally comes as a result of following standard program recommendations like, “Keep coming back!” “Never Alone” and “Caring and Sharing the NA Way.”

Isolation and despair characterized our lives because our personal resources were exhausted in active addiction as a necessary step towards our surrender to our need for help. The parts our personalities that had either been chewed up by our disease or omitted by coming from a dysfunctional family are supplied by the literal change of estate by our membership in NA.

quote: “A great many addicts have a hard time with acceptance in recovery. For so long we wanted and demanded that things go our way .When we came into the program of Narcotics Anonymous we were asked to be more patient and accepting. This is a critical part of our recovery.”
body:
Some would argue that acceptance is the key to recovery. You can’t have things not go your way and let that cause you to use. Our disease is characterized by resistance to change and reliance on beliefs that never worked for us before! Surrender lets the light shine in through our personal doorway.

Life happens and some of the time it’s not what we want. It can be unfair and painful. It can be very frustrating and annoying. It comes in various forms but they all grate on us. Learning the skill of letting them go and just taking a hit or injury is an acquired skill. Recovery doesn’t come all at once although some instances of improvement come as a wonderful surprise. Remember, our surrender and openness to help changes everything. Building an attitude of gratitude cements these good changes into functional parts of our lives.

Someday, it’s going to happen to the best of us. We use the tools of NA to cope and get thru these vexations. Once we have been thru one or more changes for the better, it gets easier to go thru the next and so on to the next.

We can do this. People file bankruptcy, get diagnosed as terminal and get fired. It’s all part of the game. We find that often the circumstances and situations we hold on to are actually impediments to our personal progress. We have to let go and let God help us move forward. Many times if we had gotten what we thought we wanted, it could have led to big time trouble!

quote: “Asking for help in specific terms usually precedes getting that help. By opening the gates of our hearts, we become ready to receive the help we need.”
body:
This applies to prayer as well as people. Loosening the grip of our egocentric nature makes it more reasonable to just ask for help instead of contriving a way to steal or trick someone into getting what we need. Recovery is about conditioning yourself into really getting ahead. You deserve to get what you want. You disease has fooled you into feeling unworthy. All you have to do is do some unworthy things and that’s how you’ll feel. To feel worthy, you have to do some worthy things! Then you will be worthy, not just feel it.

Everyone has different needs and faces different problems. Getting what we need, sometimes even when we realize we need it, is a good sign we are working our N.A. program. Other times, our HP says, no, wait a bit and creates something better and brings that into reality. Remember, you are dealing with the supreme creator of the universe!. Even when we don’t get our way, it usually works what’s best for us.

Obsession can be simply habitual desperation. When our bellies are full and our most pressing needs are met, it gets easier to take our time and attend to the details in our daily life. After all, this is what real life is all about! Simple things, done on time, pave the way for our general happiness. Compulsion is sometimes the need to do something to move forward when nothing seems to be working. Most, if not all, of us can remember a time in early recovery when we slowed our lives down in recovery, and we were surprised things went so well.

It is always interesting to watch someone begin recovery by not using and just going to meetings. They find a place to stay, get meals, find a job, or a better job and all goes well. Many get ungrateful and imagine they were just going through changes and tried to use again. Then things can really blow up on them! Don’t use, go to meetings, find a higher power are more than just words. And they bring miracles into your life. Miracles you can live with!

quote: “We put spiritual living first and exercise patience, tolerance and humility in our daily lives. The further we get from the last pill, fix, drink or take, the more we see of our past and more we realize the miracle of the release from our disease of addiction.”
body:
Practicing a life of spiritual practices, whatever our belief is, seems to work incredible changes for the better for addicts seeking recovery. All we know is it works for anyone who surrenders, stays clean and builds a strong desire. Things that did not work for us in the past may suprise you by working just fine, now that you are clean.

We are like the holy men in India who only own a begging bowl. They are called Masts and we would call them beggars. They do not even own clothing. Meher Babba was an Avatar who did not speak for around 30 years. He talks about these Masts and says they sometimes need intensive caring and loving. Being spiritual can take a toll on us.

Learning about spiritual practices that have had good results provide a growing repository of information that illuminates the fascinating ways God has worked for people all over the world regardless of their belief system. The many forms taken by the love of God enrich and ennoble our own personal experiences. The more we learn, the more we know and the more we desire. Desire is the vital element that propels our recovery into the open waters of clean time!.

quote: “The key to this Step is an understanding of the humility. Humility is a part of staying clean, as food and water are to staying alive.”
body:
Ego is so pervasive and tricky, we can be really selfish and not even realize. Cultivating humility by praying to have our egos reduced to a level that gets the garbage taken out without demanding the whole world do it our way. Like most addicts when I first started with N A, some of the lingo threw me. Surrender to win? Let go and let God? Routinely expanding our positions and ideas until they become unworkable paints us into many corners. Different information about surrender and driving home the point that it is the losing battles we stop fighting helped me with surrender. Getting some new ideas and changing my belief allowed me to see where a higher power could cover bases I couldn’t reach because my human arm wasn’t long enough. Well, that actually began to make sense. And it goes further on a daily basis..

I realize now that humility is giving God credit for the things God does and still be set on ready when something comes up I can do. When I meet or have contact with someone I feel is humble, they seems steady and solid. Competent. Yet they lack a certain presumption and arrogance that would indicate they are self-bound and apt to overdo things. Those things would limit my co-existence with them. You have trouble paddling the boat if others are smacking you with their paddles instead of pushing water!

Humility is usually brought up along with patience and tolerance. All defects start out as virtues and go overboard into trying to trying to do things their way without bothering to hear what others are trying to say. Good listeners are the best conversationalists. Toning myself down, listening and thinking through makes a big difference, more in focus. I give myself time to fit our mutual points of agreement before launching into another tirade.

quote: “We have never before experienced gut-level honesty because we covered up our feelings by using.”
body:
Most often we were unaware of avoiding our true feelings because using distorted our feelings. Adapting to this bizarre mish-mash of feeling became our daily view of the world. Recovery is re-adapting to the world as it is, so there is no need to run and hide they way we used to do. Honesty has always seemed like a losing set-up for us before recovery.

When we look at our past lives, it usually not a pretty picture. One woman in NA told me “You have a right to be very angry having to live life as an addict”. That answered a lot of my questions. It was rough out there, especially at the end, when I had had enough.

However we found the NA Program, it always worked well and quickly once we were ready to surrender and began working the Program in earnest. Some members take five or ten years to become ready! Even so, we find we have been strangely guided by a power greater than ourselves and the changes we had to go through were precisely what we needed to make a complete and lasting surrender. Recovery is a real event in our lives and the changes are for the better in most cases. Even if you are going through a difficult period right now, surrender and do what you can. Doing the right thing often feels like we are sticking out necks out!

quote: “This program can do for us what we could not do for ourselves. We came and saw this program work in the lives of other addicts.”
body:
Some of us were trying to quit on our own with little success. Then we came to NA and met and talked to people who had gotten clean and were staying clean. Then we knew that it could be done. But could we do it. They told us “You never have to use again, ever.” To a using addict that is huge. The impossible dream. The solution to our biggest problem.

Could it work? Would it work? Would it work for me? Many of us came back out of curiosity if nothing else! Identification did the rest. Every time we listened, really listened, to a fellow member share, we could hear ourselves talking. And we occasionally noticed the number of days beyond our last usage grew longer. Trouble was, we were so unused to having drugs wash out of our system, we had forgotten what it felt like to be clean!

But they were adamant, “You never have to use again.” Again and again we heard their words until we began to guess they applied to us. We we doing so much better, we had trouble focusing on something to worry about. We began worrying about things months in the future. We have a court date three months in the future. We begin to spend our income tax return half a year before next April! One day we heard a recovering addict doing the same thing and realized the importance of living the program one day at a time. Helping others, helps us!

quote: “Truth seems to be something that is impossible to understand until it becomes obvious. Truth is something we never suspected until we knew it. There are no exceptions--only incomplete truths. Everything we know is subject to revision, especially what we know about truth.”
body:
When we close our eyes to the truth, we deny ourselves the new possibilities that come with our new way of life. Freedom comes from not just staying clean , but in our ability to surrender unconditionally. When we learn to just let go, we experience a freedom many of us have never experienced. The only way to do this is not just for the day, but right now, in the moment. It takes practice, but is attainable.

Seeking the truth always pays off because we find new things, unexpected things that open new doors or close old ones. Even when things appear to be going wrong we are surprised how they turn out. We lose some things to make way for other important things that lead to betterment. We live in time, the endless procession of surprises. The best things are yet to come.

Helping others is one way to come to an appreciation of just how wonderful life can be. Painful moments clear the not possible to reveal reality. Sometimes death is the end of pain and many believe life goes on beyond the body. The more we learn about the life of the spirit, the less we fear and the more we can accept. Truth is what’s left after illusions are blown away.

quote: “This surrender was the starting point of our recovery, and is a primary point of unity for the Fellowship.”
body:
One member shared “I had a sponsor in the eighties when Japan was booming. He thought they were head and shoulders ahead of us. He had Japanse furniture, Japanese plates, Japanese car and a Japanese girlfriend. He would say, ‘Japan surrendered and look at them now. They took over my life!’””

He was trying to teach me to surrender and let go and not fight it. We are mortals and subject to things of the world. We learn about spiritual reality and suddenly one day, everything looks bigger, more peaceful, with opportunities we could not see the day before. Learning to sit in peace and gratitude break open the strongest locks and freedom awaits just beyond the doorway.

Fighting would be great if it worked. Mostly, we get bloody knuckles! Surrender to things we are powerless over leaves things ahead of us that we can do. When we do what we can, new possibilities come into view. If you are powerless, you can always ask for the help of your Higher Power. Those who ask for help, get help.

quote: “This is our road to spiritual growth, to change our character, day by day, to gradually, carefully and simply pull ourselves out of the isolation and loneliness of addiction into this mainstream of useful Fellowship.”
body:
Instead of following a professional approach to recovery subject to the ebbs and flows of trends within the treatment industry, we follow a sensible set of principled steps that lead us reliably and predictably into exactly the things we need to work on to complete our fractured personalities and gain the habits and associations needed to live our new lives.

Some of our growth can be expressed in words and many are in the realm of feelings that do not fit into the little boxes of language. Our hugs and body language convey our feelings along with what words come to us. Finding out and participating with others going through similar changes keeps us rolling forward. We finally realize what it means to be be never alone.

The program of NA is a tried and proven method of recovering from addiction. It has been proven to work for many decades. Its bulletproof. But you have to follow the rules, use the tools and be willing to take suggestions. It must be worked correctly to guarantee that it will be 100% effective.

Anyone can recover in NA, anyone. There are no exceptions. If you want it more than anything else in the world, then you can have it. Period. But you must do the footwork. Other recovering addicts can appreciate your courage and will respect what you are doing if you work the principles. Make sure you are continuing to do the things that worked for you in the beginning. Build on your own experience.

quote: “Many of our dreams, forgotten and obscured by our addiction, return and help us regain the sense of wonder and excitement at the miracle of living clean. The old compulsions fade and the habits of mind associated with addiction weaken and are broken. A great many things become possible for us. Since we live clean and grow, we are able to take our place in the world.”
body:
The healing can take place in fits and starts. There are long plateaus, deep valleys and high mountains in our journey. Sometimes we are on a roll. Other times it seems that nothing happens for weeks on end and then things break loose. Many break throughs come to us out of the blue.

We can have pretty much whatever we want, it’s just a matter of making a plan and working it, keeping God in the loop and staying connected to the fellowship. Usually we will find others seeking similar goals. We may help each other but our best source of guidance is from within.

When things get rough we do not go shopping, we get really close to the program, going to a ton of meetings and talking to our sponsor often. We connect with newcomers. We read literature and do step work. We help others. This is how we get thru the bad times. Gratitude and deepening our commitment to recovery will keep us on the track. Like drugs stored in our bone matter, we sometimes discover ourselves doing what we did in active addiction. Just make a prayer for strength and guidance and feel it wash away. We have to go through some painful changes now and then but it gets better after the bend in the road straightens out and we follow the path before us.

quote: “Each of us has learned to depend upon a Power greater than ourselves, which is our source of strength.”
body:
