Our Higher Power is the engine that drives all of this. After a few years we begin to realize that he has our back. It’s such a new phenomena that it’s difficult to accept but possible. It’s a big, big step for us. But we must do it again and again at many points along the way.

We all have many things to learn about the life of the spirit. Most addicts resist being told what to do, so we have to experience the pain of powerlessness before we reach the point of being able to ask for spiritual help. Later on we cringe at the struggles we had to go through! As we work with a new comer, we notice they have trouble asking for help, even in the most desperate times. We ask ourselves, “Did I do that? Am I doing that right now?” Later on, it makes more sense and feels kind of natural.

Depending on our Higher Power is not a weakness any more than depending on our heart to pump our blood or our lungs to suck air. To live, all our parts have to pull together and God is very much part of our lives.

quote: “The individual is precious to the group, and the group is precious to the individual. No one can revoke our membership or punish us, or make us do anything that we don’t choose to do.”
body:
Balance and focus are two very important points to keep in mind. We are all important and contribute something to the people we are with at any given point. Likewise, we are not in the group by accident. We give and receive and need both for health.

While the combination of personal views strengthens the whole, the whole is dependent on accurate information from each group member. On just the feeling level, we need the presence of other people in our lives. Isolation deprives us of this good company and we find ourselves substituting fantasy for reality with no one to help catch our errors. Just thinking of the members in our lives, we feel their presence. ‘Never Alone’ means we always have members to turn to for love and caring. Even if we are brand new to recovery, there is something comforting and restful about realizing we are home among people to love and understand.

If we are growing, we will always have answers to seek, time to find them and those who need them. Prayer and meditation become like breathing in and breathing out. Focusing on our breathing opens a doorway to the spirit that cannot be denied. Filling our lungs with clean, pure air brings us into a state of concentration on the life force within and the God of our understanding is ready at hand. There is energy in the air you breathe, breathe deeply. Take your time, you have it coming. Strength and guidance help us all the make the most of our lives and feel the satisfaction we have long sought.

quote: “In dealing with the other people in our lives, we develop a loving attitude. We forgive more easily, anger more slowly, expect less, and give more....”
body:
They say that for every year that you have clean, you get one more second before you go off on someone. This allows us to at least leave the room before we explode and then have to go back and clean it up. At some point we realize life has given us many changes, we just didn’t hang in there long enough to appreciate the opportunities at hand. Good things take time. Being unafraid we don’t act defensively and others don’t have protect themselves. Makes it easier to give us what we want when they don’’t fear us.

As we are entering new territory, it should not be surprising to need a little time to survey our surroundings before we begin to recognize the opportunities! One of the amazing things about clean time is realizing new things may be challenging at first but yield many new opportunities. Knowing about conscious contact gives us the opportunity to ask our higher power directly for help making the most of our surroundings. This seems the stuff of science fiction - and it really is!

Expecting less is the key to happiness. This is not to say minimize everything. Just don’t habitually demand more than your share aggressively. It makes you unpleasant and hard to deal with. It runs off potential clients or friends. When we apply for a position, hope we get a free sample, go to pick-up something being repaired, sometimes the answer is not what we desire. The key to to mentally say “I am going to be Ok with this however it goes.” It makes life tremendously easier.

“God helps me see the world anew today, and help me enjoy the new things before me!” This may be a useful prayer. Strength and guidance is a real blessing.

quote: “In our addiction we consistently placed our personal welfare before anything else. Here we found that in the long run what’s best for the group was usually good for us.”
body:
This is a revolution in perspective, a complete turnaround from our negative viewpoint of fear and loss. It will occur to you at some point just how habitually we have seen the world as a hostile and negative place. You can go overboard on good stuff also! Recovery seeks to balance our personality so that we can enjoy the good stuff, tolerate the not so good and avoid the negative almost completely!

Once we get our hearts and heads right, our defects removed and our amends made, we can go deeper with inventory, prayer and meditation. It is amazing how much improvement is there once we get the blockage out of the way.

One member gave an orange Igloo cooler to the group for holding iced tea at NA gatherings. Everything he attended one of these, he saw “his cooler” sitting there. It's hard to use when you become invested in NA in such a manner. Using would be inconsistent with his actions. The subconscious just would have a hard time reconciling the two. Giving stuff and time and services to NA is almost a sure way to insure staying clean.

One member gave two gallons of baby blue paint to paint a wall in a meeting room. He considered it an honor that he was the one allowed to buy that paint. Early literature reveals how we have grown. Quality comes from sincerity that money can’t buy.

quote: “If we want to reap the benefits of staying clean, we find it necessary to take continual inventories of ourselves. Hidden fears and needs are still potential driving forces. Just because we don’t recognize fear or anger doesn’t mean that it isn’t influencing our lives. We found it important to examine places where we grew angry or our beliefs were tested.”
body:
In the spirit of reciprocity we must continually look inside to receive the benefits we are seeking for our lives. For so long we were living in darkness. In order to place love in our lives we need to objectively see the darkness that has covered our spirit. This takes continual surveying and plotting new courses to steer our way to a more healthy lifestyle. Once seeing the darkness does not serve us, we replace it with a loving attribute. If we desire different results, we learn to vary our approach to get different results.

We can do this as often as needed to find what works best for us in our creative freedom. When something we are experimenting with does not serve us well, we inventory again seeking another idea that may serve us better. Each avenue finds something else we may use for finding our freedom. We also may take each perspective we have found, mix and match to find what works best for us in the moment.

We find that it is important to remain flexible, remembering that the only constant is change and that nothing - good or bad - should ever be perceived as being set in stone

It takes continual searching and fearless inventory to replace the unhealthy attributes with the light we seek. To find light we needed to seek the light we had covered in darkness.

An inventory is a tool to take stock of our assets and liabilities. Seeing and hearing clearly gives us a chance to take a reading on where we are and what we are doing.

quote: “The last five Steps of Narcotics Anonymous, the Eighth through the Twelfth, are the “get out and live” Steps.”
body:
There are many references and correlations within the Twelve Steps of N A. One to Three and Ten to Twelve match-up to open the doorway to recovery and to maintain your new life. The six Steps from Four to Nine directly address mechanisms that help us change. The quotation from the Memphis Review Form addresses the rock over from the first six to the last six. The changes within make the changes outside stick and help us work forward with our Program.

It just seems to have worked out that the first Three lead us from surrender to a state of empowerment that is clearly miraculous. And the last Three represent an almost totally spiritual daily life that gives expression and increase of what is best in each of us and continues to help us change for the better and better.

In between, there are three pairs of Steps that go together. Four and Five bring what’s bothering us out into the open. Six and Seven open the doorway to a new life by appealing to our Higher Power to remove defects that no longer have a place in our lives. Steps Eight and Nine eliminate the fear and concern we may have over the consequences of our old defects.

It takes our willingness to allow do this for us without the injury that would come from having our defects forcibly taken away. As we grow what has been wrong with us becomes ever more visible and clear. This embeds the changes recovery has given us in our new lives.

quote: “Though the freedom in our new lives , we are finally able to see the special qualities that we possess as individuals--qualities that we used to envy in others--never realizing the potential within ourselves.”
body:
Our defects kept us from seeing our own good qualities. Freed from our defects, we can finally find what we were looking for all the time. "Don't judge your insides by another person's outsides" is a cliche we have often heard. Our low self image was no longer valid, when we began to see ourselves as we really are.

Accepting ourselves as we are, just for today with the understanding that there will always be room for improvement and new insight to be gained each time we revisit the Steps. Watching other members grow, encourages us to make our own improvements.

Where once we suffered from low self esteem to the degree that many of us shunned even the image of ourselves in the mirror, we now face life on life's terms with a sense of dignity and newfound purpose. Strength and guidance give us the direction we need to enjoy the clean life while continuing to work our NA 12 Steps!

When we get clean, we become sought after by employers. We always show up with a smile on our face and work hard. We do not steal stuff, argue with customers and can be counted on. This is exactly what the doctor ordered in the workplace. Many doors open for us as we work the NA program. Just look around at the other members.

Every day is a new day for us!

NOTE: THIS SECTION IS IN ORDER BY PAGE AND LINE NUMBER

i, Forward “The full fruit of a labor of love lives in the harvest, and that always comes in its right season…” (There are no line numbers at top of page.)”

Memphis Review Form - page

A labor of love, just think, a labor of love. Our Grey Book and our Basic Text were written as labors of love. This meditation book is written as a labor of love. Love by members of this Fellowship for this lifesaving program that has given us new lives, lives we are proud of. The full fruit describes how we grow and change as we work the Steps and start living the Program. Doing this work is labor, hard labor, but it becomes labor we love. We were barely little buds of hope when we came into the rooms, we were nurtured and fed by the love of our predecessors. We were pruned and encouraged by the love of our sponsors and grew abundantly by the light of our Higher Power as we came to understand Him. We bloom and become ripe fruit in our proper seasons as our lives are shaped by spiritual principles and our relationship with our Higher Power grows. Then we start new little buds of hope in the hearts of newcomers just as our buds were encouraged when we arrived. The ongoing process of birth, growth and maturation continues and will continue as long as we hold true to our Program, our Steps and our Traditions. Our right season is ever now as we strive to live by spiritual principles in the here and now. We are proud to be members of Narcotics Anonymous.

1 Who is an Addict. “We use to live and live to use. Very simply an addict is a man or woman whose life is controlled by drugs.” (There are no line numbers at top of page.)

We are all the same, victims of the disease of addiction. We all want the same thing, an end to pain and recovery from our disease. Although our stories may be different, the feelings brought about by this disease are the same: worthlessness, abject misery, regret, hopelessness, remorse, resentment, fear, anger, grief, shame, self-pity. We have all done things we would take back if we could and things we will never want to admit to. But, by the same token, if we have a deep enough desire, if we are willing to make the effort, we can be given hope, we can learn honesty, willingness and open-mindedness, we develop acceptance, courage, strength and perseverance and we are gifted with power and wisdom by the God of our understanding. We become respectable members of society with integrity, freedom, joy and serenity if we are willing to do the work required to take the Steps. The Steps lead us to total creative freedom to become the very best people we can become. Respected, responsible, productive members of society. People we are proud to be, recovering addicts.

1, 5 We admitted that we used drugs, but many of us did not think we had a problem.

What we knew about recovery came from uninformed sources like fellow students in school and co-workers. TV was another untrustworthy source of information. Budweiser will get you dates, Chanel No. 5 will make you beautiful and smoking Marlboros will make you a real man. By the time we get to NA, we are hungry to get some real answers - answers that will work in real life! Using drugs was confused with partying, having a good time, an attitude adjustment or dealing with tension. Taking more drugs was not an answer we could live with.

Another great realization came when we realized we were asking fellow addicts who were also in denial and using drugs! We were, in fact, asking the disease of addiction if we had addiction? The answers we got where usually distorted by the other addicts perceptions of what “normal” use was. Which to them was massive daily consumption of drugs. None-users were generally looked down on as lame or not cool. Problems we were having related to our heavy drug use were generally explained away in one artful form or another as our denial merely reinforced each of us our right to use drugs at will.

It never occurred to us that our disease had us living in close association with other addicts and kept us sheltered from most “normal” people, or those that might question our heavy drug use. When life brought us in contact with non-addicts, more often than not, we felt either out-of-place or they confronted us in one form or another. The more reason to avoid them at all cost.

In recovery we come to own our problem. We don’t need a Physician to tell us we are in too deep, we know it in our innermost-selves. We once were blind but now we see. This is what it means to admit that we are powerless over our addiction, that our lives had become unmanageable. Today we own the truth, not hide from it.

just for today, have I admitted to myself, if to no one else, that I am an addict. If I have, I must be willing to do whatever it takes to stay clean a day at a time or face a life of jails, institutions or death.

1, 8 Many of us were convinced that we were right and the world was wrong, and used this belief to justify our self-destructive behavior.

We can let go of drugs a day at a time, our lives will get better. We will have a lot less daily problems as we are not stealing, or avoiding people. But if we do not work the 12 steps we will have the same inner low self esteem that we came to recovery with. If we don’t heal the inside we will have what one of our early members talked about… “when you take the drugs away from a drug-crazed maniac, you get a more efficient maniac!”

If we don’t heal the hurt, damaged child within, we will do everything in our power to create the chaos and rejection that little guy feels he deserves in life. We will self-sabotage without even knowing it. We will pick partners that put us down or belittle us as our parents or peers once did. We will do what is needed to co-sign our inner feelings of worthlessness.

When we judge ourselves and find ourselves unworthy, self-destructive behavior results. We are in a terrific position to punish ourselves. There are quirks in our nervous system that make punishing ourselves a kind of answer. The more we punish ourselves for doing things we didn’t feel good about, the less pressure was on, at least for the moment. Beyond simple mistakes and lost jobs, some go so far that they cut themselves and engage in other dangerous forms of abuse.

The only answer is to work the steps and free ourselves once and for all of the low self-esteem we have lived with for so long.

Just For Today

I will do whatever it takes to be free from the bondage of self, forever!

2, 4 We became capable of depravity--of betrayal to ourselves and to others. Temporarily clean, we ran around trying to get it all squared away before our next spree.

Waking up can be a painful process when we recall what we did last night or in the recent past. Befuddled and cloudy from mood and mind altering drugs, we suspended good sense and found misbehavior funny. Angry or resentful, we felt liberated when, in our own minds, we were released from unwanted pressure from authority figures we did not respect. Sometimes our friends egged us on. This only gave us excuses to act out in even more bizarre behavior.

Today in recovery, if we work the 12 steps, we tend to go to bed with all our tasks completed so we have peace when our head hits the pillow. When we awake it is generally in a peaceful well rested state. When using we had bouts of abstinence that gave us respite or rest from our last run which had usually ended in total collapse. We were broke, busted and disgusted with ourselves and the world around us. We stayed clean for a while out of sheer fear and exhaustion. Hell, we weren’t sick and tired we were just plain tired! Once the fear let up, the exhaustion was replenished, our disease told us it wasn’t that bad? IT WASN’T THAT BAD??? IT SURE AS HELL WAS THAT BAD!

Just for today

I will surrender to the truth, that it was that bad and that I cannot take just one of any mind or mood-altering drug as I no longer am willing to live in hell. I am willing to work the steps and live in heaven with my fellow survivors who have found true freedom from active addiction.

2, 15 We regretted the past; we feared the future.

This is the way we lived in our addiction. We were never aware in the present but were using to escape our feelings and, mistakenly thinking, making our present bearable. In reality, we were obliterating our present so we could regret it in the future. Very few of us were ever really in the present except perhaps when calamity or disaster overcame us. Then, of course, we had to use again. Now through working the Steps, we learn to live in the present.

Developing a deep sense of self-honesty is essential to our recovery. By giving ourselves completely to the program of Narcotics Anonymous and working the Steps, we begin learning to live by spiritual principles. As we progress through the Steps we clean up our pasts and acquire the skills necessary to live in the present. Eventually, we are able to live fully in the present, doing the right thing for the right reason. This brings us the great freedom to be able to focus our full attention on the present, right here right now. When we can fully focus on the present, we become aware of the great joy and beauty surrounding us all the time. We see how much we have missed in the past and get a sense of how much richer our lives are now. We are grateful to be in the present.

Just For Today

I know that recovery is not in the past or the future but in the present. Yesterday is history, tomorrow is a mystery but today is a gift, that’s why they call it the present.

2, 22 Drugs could no longer hide the pain. We just wanted an easy way out. Suicide was on many of our minds.

Unfortunately, many of us masked our pain from conscious memory. With or without drugs, we lost the ability to remember what was making us feel so badly. It was all we could do just to get through a day! People yelling at us or putting pressure on us kept us even more distracted.

Recovery gives us a brand new life without all the self-inflicted wounds. While recovery takes place on a day by day or moment by moment basis, the changes are often at a level so fundamental that they touch every part of our lives and attitudes. Personality change for the better is possible through the NA 12 Steps. Spiritual growth replaces the broken parts with things that work for us.

2, 25 Part of ourselves could see what was happening; another part would not accept it. We were caught in an illusion of “what if,” “if only” and “just one more try.”

During our using, the part of us that could see what was happening got ignored or discounted so often, we stopped believing it. Our illusion of “what if,” “if only” and “just one more try” got us building walls of denial so thick, the truth could not be seen. The Steps and the Program gives us the tools to break down our walls of denial (Don’t Even Notice I Am Lying-DENIAL) with honesty, open-mindedness and willingness. Our insane illusions are replaced with sanity. Sanity

has been defined as living in harmony with reality. We learn to be fully focused in the present, observing all the joy and beauty that we used to miss in our search for “just one more.”

Not thinking clearly, we sought easy answers and easy answers usually turned out to be time and energy consuming substitutes for real action. We were bumping into the walls without really getting anywhere. Even in recovery we can find ourselves coasting without thinking things through. It helps to review our goals and methods every once in a while. The drug induced fog can become so familiar that we lived there. Snapping out of habitual non-reaction gives us a break from the insanity.

2, 31 We had used all sorts of drugs over the years and experienced numerous living problems as the result of our using and yet did not consider ourselves addicted.

It usually takes a total catastrophe or disaster to penetrate our walls of denial. Denial is so all pervasive, we actually believe our own lies. The acronym “Don’t Even Notice I Am Lying” is such a wonderful description of denial. Spinning stories and then believing them is an ability we have all become so skilled at we often are not aware we are doing it. When we hit our bottoms, when our lives are so unmanageable and out of control we can’t believe our own lies anymore, our addiction becomes apparent. Even then, it often takes a long time before we are ready to do anything about it.

When we finally truly accept that we are addicts and can’t live this life of addiction anymore, we can make the admission and surrender required by Step One and begin to recover. We must start developing a relationship with honesty, not just cash register honesty, but the deeper level of self-honesty required to look at ourselves. It takes courage and faith to take a deep look inside and see all the mistakes we have made over the years. We need to realize that we are not evil, malicious horrible monsters, but we are simply human beings who made mistakes and poor choices. We are capable of learning to make good choices and fewer mistakes. This is where the Fellowship, the Program, a Sponsor and a strong relationship with a Higher Power can help us. There is indeed hope for all of us. We can learn to love ourselves, have lives filled with joy and happiness and become people we are proud to be.

3, 16 We looked at our differences rather than our sameness. Yet the things we had in common put us all in the same dimension, the dimension of addiction.

We are often told as newcomers that we need to look for the similarities, not the differences. As newcomers we often looked for anything that would disqualify us, free us from the reality of our addiction. As we look around the rooms, we start to realize that we are not unique. Although our stories might be different, the feelings we have are in common. The self-centeredness, the insecurity, the unworthiness, the fear, guilt, remorse, regret, uselessness we feel we hear shared by others. We also hear how they overcame their negative self-images to have lives of joy and purpose, of peace, serenity and usefulness. We realize we have two things in common, addiction and recovery.

The spiritual life makes us aware of some of the fears and concerns that occupy space in our heads without paying rent. There are words we learn to deal with these things. There are ways to deal with these things that are learnable. The dimension we share grows and this growth gives us room unavailable before the program. “Diction” is the root word in addiction can so imbeds the idea of expression or statement in our disorder. When we are a mess inside our minds and bodies, that gets expressed in our behavior.

14, 19 We can die from untreated addiction. But before we die, the disease takes from us our pride, our self-esteem, our families and loved ones. And finally, it takes our very will to live.

The pain and confusion of active addiction makes it hard to realize we are slipping into a hole until it is too late. Actually, there is no such thing as ‘too late.’ Most of us arrive in an NA meeting feeling hopeless and expecting things to continue getting worse. The lessening of despair and the early glimmers of hope come as a surprise!

In our recovery program, we do not dwell on the horror show of active addiction but rather share and emphasize the processes of recovery. We surrender and therefore end the struggle to deny our addiction and continue our efforts to control our lives. Just by not using, going to meetings and reading NA literature we become focused on a new way to live. Hope is given to us and grows in us.

15, 1 The structure of Narcotics Anonymous is quite unlike any health or welfare agency known to us. Although N.A. practices may vary from place to place, all N.A. services are performed voluntarily by the addicts themselves and without cost. N.A. does not accept money for its services, is not funded by any public or private sources of agencies and accepts no outside contributions.

You cannot buy a miracle! We have found through a long period of trial and error that our messages comes through loud and clear in meetings or private conversation. (When persons outside the N.A.’s point of view try to produce our writings using academic techniques, it comes through crippled. When academics and treatment professionals try to reproduce our writings, they come through crippled.) We don’t want insincerity or lofty thinkers taking pity on us. It is the identification and respect we share among our fellow members that brings our recovery process to life. Our message of hope and recovery is priceless. Whether in H&I, P.I., Literature or other services, it is our common bond that amazes us and holds our attention.

16, 4 Addicts respond instinctively to honest sharing.

We have heard so many lies we pay little attention to what goes on around us. We stumbled into an NA meeting skeptical of anything we heard although we did feel something that piqued our attention at least for a moment. So we returned to another meeting not knowing why. It may have been the second or fourth meeting, we heard some truth. Instinctively, the truth attracted us and we began listening closer. The closer we listened, the more truth we heard. The more truths we heard, the more hope we felt. These truths were starting to stick together into

something we could wrap our minds around. It was when our minds found enough evidence to believe that our hearts started feeling soothed. Now we can surrender at least a little of our lives into our process. We find ourselves practicing honesty too and feeling good about these changes. What reaches the heart, touches the heart and nothing reaches the heart like honest sharing of common pain and the solution out of it.

17, 9 After coming to the Narcotics Anonymous Fellowship, we find ourselves among a very special group of people. Many were a great deal worse off than we were. Here we found hope.

Hope is the only spiritual principle that can be given. All other principles we must develop from within. Hope lets us know the program is working for us as it has worked for others. At each step in the process of recovery, we feel relief. Alternatives appear out of thin air and give us awareness that we are no longer trapped in a dead end. When we attend meetings and see other addicts smiling and enjoying life in recovery, our spirit is renewed. This is one aspect of the hope we share, the therapeutic value of one addict helping another. We must be careful not to quench the hope of others. Newfound hope can be a very fragile and must be encouraged.

18, 6 We don’t want to settle for the limitations of the past. We want to examine and reexamine all our old ideas, and constantly improve on them or replace them with new ones.

Being able to change our lives by changing our beliefs and entertain new approaches to old problems is a great discovery.

The courage to change our old ways puts us on the path of recovery. The 12 Steps are the tools we use to navigate that path that leads us to freedom from active addiction. We can feel ourselves coming alive!

The 10th Step can keep on-going spiritual growth a reality. Every time we cancel out an old habit that no longer works or fits our life, our view expands and we can see more defects that can be changed. The more we grow, the more we can see. The more we see, the more we can change.

20, 19 We want desperately for the place where addicts recover to be a safe, free from outside influences. We feel safe at our closed meetings. Everyone is an addict. We feel totally free to express ourselves because no law enforcement agencies are involved.

Many of our members desire personnel anonymity, especially when we are new to the program, though within a short time most come to cherish the therapeutic value of one addict helping another that is found in the process of identification, and that process is often fostered only in the safety of a circle of clean addicts living a program of recovery.

Anonymity is half our name. The ability to share peer-to-peer gives us a chance to share and express ideas we had been forced to keep to ourselves. Many times we ‘self-correct’ when we hear ourselves say something that doesn’t make sense. Keeping things inside perpetuates

some really dumb ideas.Sharing our feelings and ideas with other addicts who are free to call us on our bullshit can give us great insight into where we have gone wrong.

21, 8 Our program is in fact a way of life. We learn the value of principles such as humility, surrender, and service. The idea that we have to do it alone is obsolete.

As a way of life, we enjoy the company of others who are in the same process we are engaged in ourselves. We become so closely associated with fellow members, we really enjoy the type of closeness otherwise found only in families. This is a very natural way to obtain feedback that is simple and honest. Many times their answers turn out to be our answers.

Humility is simply honestly setting aside our egotistical ideas and being open to others. Surrender gets us out of ourselves and lets us shake off many of the chains that used to bind us. Service involves extending ourselves for others. We can practice projecting our feelings into those in need and help them find solutions that will work for them.

21,12 We, of Narcotics Anonymity, do not promise to have all the answers, but we’ve found that our lives steadily improved if we didn’t use and learned to maintain our spiritual condition.

Actually, recovery is what happens to us when we are clean, going to meetings and taking time to help others. Whether new or well established in recovery, just by caring we are able to see into the lives of others. This helps reshape our own living attitudes and capacity to surrender and trust the program of NA. By living differently, we change what we attract and what we involve ourselves with on a daily basis.

22, 22 Many books have been written about the nature of addiction. This book primarily concerns itself with the nature of recovery. (There are no line numbers at top of page.)

Many of us who got clean in the 1970’s, know from experience that outside interests have little information that we could use. Many theories on what addiction is and methods of treatment abounded but we got nothing from them. NA began to grow when addicts began to take up for ourselves, our message and our program. Our Traditions allow us to do things together that might otherwise result in conflicts and stalemates. One of the great benefits of our program is that we are not dependent on outside organizations for funding or direction. We are fully self- supporting through our own contributions and have only to seek a loving God in our group conscience for direction.

23, 1 Before coming to the fellowship of N.A., we were irresponsible with our lives. We used drugs to cover up our humiliation, guilt, inferiority and feelings of inadequacy.

Most of us practice some sort of wake-up ritual, reading literature, listening to recovery tapes or CD’s to get our day off to a good start. It is vitally important that we keep our focus on recovery and things that work in recovery if we are going to experience the benefits of NA! The simplest things can make so much difference in the way our days go. Just missing a few meetings can

cause us to begin to experience the feelings we associate with our disease. Resentments crop up over very small issues. We snap at co-workers and spouses. Just a member calling us can put us back in a better place.

24, 25 Some of us sought spiritual guidance through churches, different religions, meditation and cultism. Some of us sought cure by geographical changes, blaming our surroundings and living situations for our problem. This attempt just gave us new people to take advantage of.

What we failed to realize is that wherever we are, we bring the problem with us. Solutions to our problems cannot come from outside sources, they must come from within. The Steps are the answer to our problems if we can find the courage, willingness and honesty to use the Steps to look inside, see who we are, accept ourselves and seek change. Finding and developing a relationship with a Higher Power gives us the spiritual power to accomplish great changes we would otherwise be incapable of accomplishing. We start to gain self-acceptance and self-love by realizing we are no longer the despicable depraved people we used to be but are becoming capable of giving hope and love to those coming in after us.

25, 4 When we came to the Fellowship of Narcotics Anonymous, we faced many disturbing realizations.

We realized we had run from our feelings, we had lost control of our lives and become victims of the disease of addiction. The compulsion and obsession that had ruled our lives had forced us to take many actions that were against our will. We have lied, stolen, cheated, sold ourselves and many other vile things all in the name of getting one more. Now faced with the total unmanageability of our lives, we were filled with guilt, remorse, shame and sorrow. We couldn’t see a way out. Going to meetings started to give us hope. We heard others share about how their lives got better. We started to learn there was a better way.

Learning is an ongoing process in recovery. Rethinking and sharing with others one on one or in meetings, we wake up to where we have been and what we have been doing! Living a day at a time frees us from repeating things we no longer approve of or care for. Each day is a blank slate. Hope and the freedom that comes from surrender and belief enables us to take a good look and review our lives as casually as we comb our hair. We can substitute actions and viewpoints until we are satisfied. Even then, when they stand for inspection, we are free to make other improvements. Our progress encourages us to continue the process. Instead of feeling lost when something comes to mind, we just think, “I’ll share about this at the meetings tonight. Surely I am not the only one who has this problem.” Not longer trapped and out of touch with supportive people we can move forward. No more concrete shoes!

25, 5 We were sick and tired of pain and trouble. We were frightened and ran from the fear, but no matter how far we ran, the fear followed us. We were hopeless, useless and lost. Feelings of worthlessness overcame us.

Our insidious disease will always try to re-assert itself regardless of the period of our clean time. One of the things we wake up to is the fears come inside and need to be dealt with before they will stop manifesting. Self-worth is based on the abilities we value. False pride and ego take the place of real skills and assets. The more skills and assets we gain, the less we need false pride and ego. Hope is positive expectation and it grows larger as we move forward in recovery clean time and experience. Helping others resets many of the things that seem to permanently block our path. All it takes is one moment of realization to being the change process.

25, 11 In N.A., we learned that members concentrated on recovery and how they felt, not what they had done in the past.

Everyday clean we have an opportunity to start afresh. Many of us find that things we have been putting off are the very things we have to do before we can go on with our lives. Writing down things on the edge of our consciousness is one way to help move us forward to correction of details that may prove to be of great importance later on.

Learning to identify our feelings and how to cope with them is an important early step in recovery. We begin to realize we don’t have to act out on our feelings but can simply accept them and move on. Recovery takes a giant step forward when we realize we can learn from our past and forgive ourselves. We are not doomed to repeat it.

25, 18 Personality change was what we really needed. Change from a self-destructing to a self- affirming pattern of living was imperative.

When we were using, a change of any kind of personal nature was the farthest thing from our minds. We were getting and using every day, the only thing we wanted to change was the pain of living, that in many cases, we were unable to clearly see.

It wasn’t until we were able to get clean, when it was said to us, that in order to stay clean, all we had to do was pray, go to meetings and not pick up, and change everything in our lives. This was almost too much to absorb at once. After It

25, 24 Today we have feelings of love, joy, hope, excitement, sadness and friendship. Before we were either elated or depressed with very little in between.

In recovery we find much of the color and beauty in life is found in the details we used to blow off as unimportant. Going more slowly and thinking things through gives is a chance to see what we have been missing. What breakthrough lies just ahead of us today if we take time to catch it?

25, 29 Although our heads told us we would never make it, the people in the Fellowship gave us hope by insisting we could.

We will find that our hopelessness and despair is always waiting to take over any time we slack up on our prayer and meditation. Spiritual maintenance is what we use to get clean, stay clean and grow spiritually. When we turn our attention to helping others, we change our whole situation. We can move from despairing and needful to sitting up straight and thinking up ways to encourage someone else. This change is crucial to our new lives. The important thing for us to remember today, is we have to make this change of situation every day.

27, 3 What a change from how we used to be! That’s how we know that the N.A. program works.

Time, and our disease of addiction, will constantly work to make us forget and ignore all our many blessings. Many of us write our gratitude lists to maintain our awareness of the new things that could never have happen to us when we were dirty (using). When we work on getting better, often others can see a change in us before we can see it in ourselves. Slowly we realize we are not losing our temper as often, gossiping as much or lying quite as easily.

27, 18 Recovery is a beautiful chance that many addicts thought had passed them by until they found the Fellowship of N.A. It’s a chance to live again.

Right now, alive in the present and living fully! It is a new day and we commit to the courage we need to step up to the challenges and opportunities we have rising up to meet us. The longer we are clean and the more we let ourselves experience the miracles of personality changes and spiritual growth, the more we can accept and believe a whole new life opens up to us one day at a time!

We see people that love themselves, love their lives, have dreams, are pursuing dreams and most importantly, achieving dreams. They assure us that we, too, can have this. We start by having attainable goals like getting one more key tag. Then, maybe, a job or a car or a bank account. Then the day of ultimate success, we have completed the Steps for the first time and have achieved a spiritual awakening. We slowly realize we have become people that love ourselves and our lives. We are pursuing and achieving our dreams and most importantly, we are capable of helping others reach for their new lives. A miracle has occurred.

29, 10 When we came to N.A., we were physically, mentally and spiritually bankrupt. We hurt long enough and badly enough that we were willing to go to any lengths to stay clean.

Nothing motivates like pain and, boy, did we hurt. We became willing to follow suggestions we didn’t completely understand because of hope. Hope offered by NA members we recognized as fellow addicts who were obviously no longer in pain but enjoying life. It was their continued assurances that we could get better, too, that helped us find the courage to go on for one more day, one more hour or maybe just one more heartbeat.

We learned that gratitude defeats self-pity. Mountainous problems become small molehills when we accept the circumstances and look for solutions. Life doesn’t need chaos to have pleasure.

30, 1 When we first came into the Fellowship of N.A., we were often resentful at the suggestions some of the members made to us.

As we learn more about how N.A. works, we realize members try to wait until we ask for help. There is a chance that we may have asked for help in some area and the members responded to our request. None of us claim to be perfect and, while we are very capable of slipping into ego trips and authoritative roles, it is just our disease acting up. Really, we know better and some of us are comfortable laughing at ourselves. There is a good saying for this. ”Man who cannot laugh at himself is missing one of the world’s greatest jokes!” Our awareness grows and as we discover the linkage between our actions and the results, we frequently say “Oh, my God.” when we catch ourselves doing this. The truth often seems threatening at first but later on it proves to be a great friend!

30, 11 After attending a few meetings, we began to feel like we were finally getting somewhere. It was in these meetings that we were first introduced to the Twelve Steps of N.A.

Surrender seems like the last thing that would help us dispel the negative feelings and emptiness we feel inside. But, really, if we are powerless, we get instant relief when we stop fighting. life is not our adversary. Addiction is our problem, substituting using and parallel behaviour for real life and solutions that deal with things as they come up. Usually, we are listening to someone share about what happened when they stopped fighting the inevitable fact of their addiction and began looking for new answers or ways of understanding. Identifying with the speaker, the idea caught hold and helped us change our loser’s point of view. We become winners when we can accept life as it is and learn ways closed to us before.

30, 20 When we admit our powerlessness and inability to manage our own lives, we open the door to a power greater than ourselves.

The belief that a higher power exists and can helps us get results where we used to have this naked, lost feeling is a powerful bringer of hope. Praying for guidance, reading, listening to others share their experience and feelings gives us courage to approach the same goals. Clinging to the notion that we can manage our lives is a control game. By the time we reach the NA program, control is something we left behind. If it isn’t practical, it isn’t spiritual reminds us to keep our feet on the ground and use spiritual principles to get what we want or need.

30, 23 It doesn’t matter whether we just took a few pills, fixed eight times a day, suck on a pipe, drink bottles of cough remedy behind drug stores, or have one tranquilizer with our first martini

each day….. we have certain things in common, no matter to what degree or what kind of addict we are.

The obsession and compulsion were two things we had in common. We were also all familiar with the feelings: guilt, remorse, fear, self-loathing, anger, desperation, self-pity, inadequacy. The twin attributes of obsession and compulsion just didn’t work. It is like an animal stuck in a laboratory that keeps mashing a button without getting the food. Desperation usually the driving force and as our needs are met, we slow down and make more sense.

31, 1 Upon working Step One, we affirmed our surrender to the principles of N.A, only then did we overcome the alienation of addiction.

For many of us this is our first experience with the life saving principle of vulnerability. Our unconditional surrender is the key that will unlock the door and allow others into our lives, providing the help we need and averting the penchant to continue in self will.

The pain of being clean and having the realization, that we cannot successfully, not only not use drugs, but in addition, not use others, control situations through manipulation and control. When we find that we are unable to control life and see how our lives are unmanageable we can free ourselves through surrender. Surrender becomes a positive move away from things that no longer work for us.

31, 7 We needed proof. In that N.A. meeting we found people like ourselves with the same, or worse patterns of of drug dependency and failure, yet they were clean. They smiled. Their eyes were clear. They cared for each other.

Patterning after happy people seems to be an innate quality in human beings. We can pick up good vibes and without much preparation, find ourselves looking, feeling and sounding better as our internal state changes from fear-base to something new to us.

31, 30 Step One means that we don’t have to make excuses for the way we are, and that is a great freedom.

Our freedom results in more energy and open-mindedness. This is the raft we ride into the Second Step where we find connections to a power that can help us. Surrender would not work out on the streets or places where we have all been. In the loving, caring circle of friends in an NA meeting we find a protected environment made just for us. As we grow in recovery, our need for and ability to surrender grows as well.

31, 31 Surrender means not having to fight anymore. It took a while, for some of us, to realize how unmanageable our lives had become; for others, this was about the only thing of which we could be sure.

Surrender becomes one of the most powerful weapons in our arsenal. As we truly accept our powerlessness over just about everything but ourselves, we find our power over ourselves is growing. We can change our attitudes, our beliefs, our feelings, our actions and our responses. We learn to take a moment to think about our actions in accordance with our growing belief system and then respond instead of just blindly react.

32, 5 The pain of working the program did not seem as great as the pain of addiction, so we surrendered.

We have often heard one member or another lamenting the hardships of recovery or life on life’s terms in general, but how does this compare to the desperation of active addiction?

Perhaps each member might recall the fear, paranoia and degradation experienced while using. It took a monumental effort for most of us to maintain our addiction once our disease had progressed to the latter stages. The sheer number of lies we tried to remember to keep those we loved in the dark about our addiction could wear down even the strongest of wills.

The suggestions of the program seem simple, even easy by comparison. Going to meetings instead of to cop, calling our sponsor or talking with another recovering addict instead of a dealer or reading literature are all very doable.

32, 10 None of the Steps work by magic. We do not just say the words of the Steps; we live them.

Belief in magic was part of our illusions and delusions in our using days. We could convince ourselves of the most unbelievable things. In recovery we learn that we have to work for our achievements. First, we learn the words to the Steps. We hear them repeated in every meeting and their meaning starts to sink in. When we start to work them with a sponsor, they become real to us. They start to make sense and slowly become a part of our lives. As we progress through the Steps, we learn spiritual principles and incorporate them into our lives because life becomes easier and makes more sense. The more we work the Steps and practice living by spiritual principles, we find ourselves living in the solution and finding self-value. As we practice the spiritual principles taught by the Steps over and over, they become as natural as breathing to us. We master living in accordance with the will of our Higher Power and our lives become richer and more beautiful. Eventually, going against what we know to be the will of our Higher Power becomes difficult and the pain we get proves we have made a poor choice. We start to have a light shining through us that is readily apparent to others. We become grateful for this new way of live and want to share it with others.

32, 14 We read this book and attend N.A. meetings. We see for ourselves if the Fellowship has something to offer us. It is not where we are that counts, but where we are going!

We start to realize we are on a journey of recovery that will be a life-long trek of discovery. Changing, growing and learning become pleasurable to us and we look forward to the next

day’s developments. Often others will notice changes in use before we do and surprise us with their insights. Upon reflection, we realize we are no longer who we were and we like who we are becoming. We are afforded glimpses of what we might become in our deepening friendships with long-timers who have infinite patience, humility and joy in their lives. This becomes our dreams and goals, to live happy, joyous and free. Serene in the love of our Higher Power.

32, 18 None of us stumbled into this Fellowship brimming with love, honesty or open-minded willingness.

Most of us actually showed up at our doors brimming with degradation, isolation and loneliness. With that fear, we needed to see how simple it could be using NA as a means of fulfillment instead of the shooting galleries we used to frequent. Not near as stressful in ‘our’ rooms, we begin seeing a little more clearly. We see members laugh, have fun and genuinely care for us and want to help us. This hope gives us a clarity not seen in many years. We find that someone loves us even when we feel out of control. And with this new found hope we begin pushing the isolation out and away from us.

By hearing the Steps and Traditions read and studied at every meeting we attend, we start to feel some willingness. Our hearts recognize the honesty we hear and we start to want to be honest ourselves.

33, 13 We had nothing left to lose. We gave up… quit struggling...surrendered, completely and unconditionally. Then and only then did we begin to recover from the disease of addiction.

Paradoxically, with this complete unconditional surrender, we started to find some power. The resources we had depleted fighting our addiction started to rebuild giving us whatever we needed to start recovering.

33, 16 Recovery begins with the first admission of powerlessness. From that point forward, we can see that every clean day is a successful day, and that any seeming failure is only a temporary setback.

Essay starts.

33, 20 We quit fighting. We accept our addiction and life the way it is.

How smoothly we find ourselves slipping back into our old habit patterns. When we quit fighting, we can start our days on a whole new platform. We can start our days being grateful and surrendered. It is funny how we find ways to get along with others when we are mindful of our release from our addictive prisons. Pain can make us real open-minded! Gratitude can make us more open to what others are saying or going through. When we pay attention, we get attention.

34, 7 All of us have this one thing in common -- we but the disease that kills us and, one way or another, we usually pay for it, a bag at a time, a few pills at a time, or a bottle at a time until we die.

Putting our energy into the things we really care about or blindly following the obsession of the moment without considering the cost is a choice we have today. Taking life a day at a time helps us reach escape velocity from our old habits and preferences we developed when we were out of our minds. Being in the present is an important tool for finding a new way of living. Stopping now and then to make a written list of all we have to be grateful for is a good way to improve our mood and raise our energy for the day to come. Saying ‘’thank you” and asking permission before forcing an issue or getting someone’s help smooths out our relations with everyone we encounter.

34, 21 The Second Step is the most important thing that must happen for us to achieve any sort of ongoing recovery.

Belief defines our boundaries and what we are open to in our personal reality. If we can only find something to believe in, we can firm up our hope for a new life. A power greater than ourselves changes what we bring to the table on a daily basis. It is not brave to walk down the street with a few large friends and it is not cowardice to crouch down in an alleyway with large, hulking figures moving towards us in a hostile manner. Having a Higher Power to help out only a breath of prayer away changes our situation in a wonderful way.

35, 30 The Higher Power we use in N.A. is a lot like this: We begin by simply admitting to the possibility of a power greater than ourselves.

This simple admission allows us access to prayers that work for us. We can pray for a higher power to help us find a belief we can live with. The help we need to stay clean multiplies the more we use it. We can pray for help to stay clean, just for today! We can pray for help dealing with things at work or with the people in our lives.

36, 3 Many fortunate things occur mysteriously, but there are no accidents. We come to understand that our recoveries are a gift from a Power greater than ourselves.

Getting used to having a higher power may seem a bit strange at first. Spiritual growth can feel frightening or unreal. Elusive understandings and surprising good luck can lull us into taking it all

for granted. After all, if we are not using, lots of things do get better, seemingly on their own! Relapse often follows taking recovery for granted instead of remembering the other things that go with not using and taking in meetings on a regular basis. If this happens to us, we start taking the N.A. program more seriously, our very lives depend on it.

36, 12 Clean living is only the beginning of a new life. Life without fear is a gift we receive for the price of acceptance.

Incredible as this may be, it is really that simple. Considering the pain and despair we have suffered, it almost seems unfair to find such a simple and wrap around way out of our insanity.

36, 19 Many of us have come to believe that the forces of life know what our real needs are and will take care of us when undisturbed by self-will.

One of the things we get to do in recovery is inventory our lives, how we are doing and what we are doing. In fear and desperation, we were often driven to do things that led to shame and guilt. In recovery we are gently led away from obsession and compulsion. It may be that the strong drive to deal with things helped us survive. Today, we have a choice. We can stop, look and listen. We can pray for help from our HP. We can ask our new friends and sponsors for help. All these take our some of the bumps and many of the grinds that used to comprise our lives.

37, 2 Most addicts have strong feelings about their Higher Power and vigorously defend their right to their own understanding of Higher Power. The strength to move into action comes from our Higher Power.

There is the illusion by non-believers that “higher power” is just an excuse to do as we please. The reality of life in the care of a loving, caring, all powerful being is not self-will. It lifts us to better things and gives us the strength and guidance to deal with the worst things that happen to us.

37, 6 Asking for help in specific terms usually precedes getting that help. By opening the gates of our hearts, we become ready to receive the help we need.

This applies to prayer as well as people. Loosening the grip of our egocentric nature makes it more reasonable to just ask for help instead of contriving a way to steal or trick someone into getting what we need. Everyone has different needs and faces different problems. Getting what we need, sometimes even when we need it, is a good sign we are working our N.A. program. Other times, our HP says, no, wait a bit or offers something better. Even when we don’t get our way, it usually works out for us. Obsession can be simply habitual desperation. When our bellies are full and our most pressing needs are met, it gets easier to take our time and attend to the details in our daily life.

37. 27 Obviously our way did not work. In utter desperation, we looked for another way. In Narcotics Anonymous,we are told that we can turn our will and our lives over to a care of a God of our own understanding

Essay starts

38, 1 We don’t have to be religious. All that is required is a willingness to believe.

It is shocking how easy it is to get the spiritual life once we are willing.

38. 5 We have only to believe what we see with our own eyes in the transformed lives of other N.A. members. That’s all it takes -- an open mind.

Essay starts

38, 27 Go off by yourself and say silently, “God, I’ve made a mess of my life. I can’t solve my problems and I ask you to take care of me and show me how to live.”

This simple prayer has changed countless lives for the better. These few words show self- awareness, surrender, acceptance, humility, willingness to change, honesty, hope, trust, faith,

simplicity, dedication and devotion. When said with sincerity from the heart, we are no longer spiritually bankrupt.

38, 35 In the Third Step, we simply recognize that there is a force for good in the world and we cooperate with that force. We let good things happen to us.

This incredibly beautiful and inclusive observation opens the doorway to a spiritual life for all kinds of people. In NA, we need to get on a good footing with a God of our understanding. Like many things in life, it doesn’t matter where we are at, it matters where we are going. One step at a time, we can move towards new goals in recovery. The road never ends and the learnings are amazing.

Putting what we learn into practice turns up new items to think about and usually lead us to even brighter horizons of discovery and application.

39, 6 If we have come this far in the N.A. program, we have already noticed some change in our lives. However, the change may not be as fast nor as dramatic as we wish.

Astonishingly, almost everyone of us is surprised to find out that gratitude is a muscle instead of an exterior force. We exercise gratitude and find we are able more often to become consciously grateful.

39, 13 We find that we will continue to have living problems.

In time we may see where these ‘problems’ are part of our learning curve and lead us to understandings we could get no other way. We may even reach the point where they are no longer problems but simply challenges we take in stride as we continue to live in the solution. Pain can be a motivator, in that when we don't clearly look at our wounds that still can be haunting us after years of clean time time ,we may need to search inside a little more with our sponsor, with other members, and to continue to remember that we cannot do this alone.

39, 22 We have surrendered our will and our lives to the care of a power greater than ourselves. We are now a part of the Ultimate Reality which has brought Order out of Chaos.

As long as your belief works for you, we are happy for you. We are serious about finding a loving Higher Power. There are many lower powers.

Our Program only asks that this Power being loving, caring and greater than we are. Ascribing the characteristics of loving and caring is not difficult for us because we certainly had surrendered our lives to a vengeful harmful, even killing, power greater than we are, addiction. To surrender to our growing understanding of a Higher Power which will not only do us no harm but actually help us to relieve our pain is easy.

39, 28 Day by day we discover the magnitude of the Third Step.

If we put pennies in a jar every day we are clean, the jar would fill to overflowing, We would have to get another jar! After some time, we would have a row of jars. We have countless opportunities to make bad choices and regrettable decisions each and every day. Not making these poor choices becomes a tidal wave of goodness touching every current and ripple of our lives. Recovery gives us back our losses while eliminating new mistakes as we go.

40, 15 We are finally beginning to become honest when we admit that our addiction had whipped us and that we need help.

When we first arrive to NA, it is suggested to pray,go to meetings ,don’t use, and change our entire lives. It was also suggested while in meetings to listen to the similarities and not the differences. By remaining open-minded we may hear someone that moves us in a way that we can consider that addict as our sponsor. It is only when we ask for help that we can receive it. None of us are mind readers. Others can help us in our recovery but we must be willing to meet them halfway.

As we become more closely involved with others we will learn to share our pain and it will lessen. As addicts, we are all wounded individuals, to one degree or another, some of us may be more wounded than others.This does not make us different. It really makes us one in a kind, that is the true meaning of anonymity. We are the same in kind. As we continue to get closer and develop relationships with other addicts the more we will see this to be true.This is the way we develop long lasting friendships

40, 21 Ask anyone who has some time on the program and who has the kind of life you want for yourself, they will tell you that the Fourth Step was a turning point in their lives.

In our early days, many members viewed the Fourth Step as upsetting and many felt pain and upset. The reality is Step Four takes us out of our old habits and ways of dealing with life. We are free to examine and evaluate what we want to keep and what no longer fits our life.

41, 21 You may approach the Fourth Step in a number of ways. It is advisable that before you start, go over the first Three Steps with your sponsor.

We need our surrender, belief and our decision to let our Higher Power care for us - lest we try to take control and work our Fourth Step on self-will! If this happens to you, just take a break and think about how it feels to push harder on something that is not working. Slow down and think about your HP until you get that ‘cared for feeling.’ Remember, we don’t want to lose our assets, just the attributes and habits of mind and action that make trouble for us.

42, 18 When we pray and take action it always goes better for us.

Turning one’s will and life over to our Higher Power grants us access to great power and tremendous vision. In recovery we can use this Power to build our new lives. Every day is a new chance to practice our new life and do things differently.

42, 26 We examine in depth our relationships with people, places and situations asking ourselves what we have demanded of these relationships. Often the answers will show that we are placing unreasonable demands on reality. WE find that we are demanding other people to stop being who they are.

What we demand of others, we need to be willing to give of ourselves. Setting ourselves above those claim to care for is not only bad manners, it angers people instead of helps.

43, 2 This Fourth Step can be a wonderful adventure, reviewing our past performance and our present behavior to see what we want to keep and what we want to be rid of.

The ‘rubber hits the road’ when we work our Fourth Step. Steps Four through Seven make real the personality changes we have been seeking. The journey of a thousand miles begins with that one simple step forward.

43, 11 We are not going to be perfect. If we were perfect, we would not be human.

In all organizations there are ranking systems. Sometimes these are simple roles that are employed without sanctions or coercion. The mental plane if full of surface appearances and labels. Anonymity allows principles to emerge where we all get to work together for our mutual satisfaction and benefit. Perfection - there can be no perfection in the final sense of accomplishment. As living beings, we always have a ways to go...

43, 31 Many of us, before we take Step Five, fear that God will turn away from us, when we reveal ourselves to Him.

There are plenty of curves our disease will throw at us to keep us worried and fearful. We have to look to our oldtimers to see what they have learned. They share that the process of spiritual growth is never ending. The higher we climb the mountain of spiritual growth and recovery the more mountain appears before us.

As soon as we think we are approaching the top of the mountain, we see a little more appear before our very eyes!

44, 8 For years, we avoided seeing ourselves as we are.

After years of masking our feelings, we begin the program scattered and hurting. Our beginnings are full of anxiety, fear and apprehension. We may search for a reason to continue staying clean at all. If we stay clean, we began hearing stories we can relate to. Seeing members looking more healthy than we’ve have felt in many years, we start believing in the hope we are starting to experience. A few weeks into abstinence, our thoughts are beginning to clear some and we can see, feel and hear a part of the true us returning. “There is hope for us also”, we exclaim! With this in our forefront we sometimes trudge, sometimes gleefully forge ahead in our new way.

44, 23 We must carefully choose the person who is to hear our Fifth Step.

Like all things spiritual, our Higher Power gives us lots of helpful guidance in this area. We pray for our HP to guide us to the right person! We want someone who cares about us and yet is able to be honest with us. We find someone we trust will keep our Fourth Step secrets confidential. Also, sharing with God, ourselves and another human being does not mean we give that person the right to judge us one way or the other.

45, 7 There is a danger that we will minimize or rationalize away our part in situations.

To do our inventory, we may use a practice that works for many experienced members. Just divide the process into two or more parts so you can write out whatever comes to mind and when you stop writing, take a short break and then review what you have written. You have plenty of time to add and remove so that your input makes good sense, at least to you! The trouble with minimizing or rationalizing our inventory is it gives the illusion that you have done an inventory! At best you have begun the inventory process. Your life, the quality of your life, is at stake here. When we addicts begin to feel badly, we are apt to go into a downhill plunge that ends in relapse. Just monitoring and tracking things that bother us as we go along our way allows us the opportunity to change.

If we get out of the habit of daily inventory, we suggest just resuming the process. The idea that we are ‘behind’ just makes us confused and may act to keep us from even trying!

45, 11 This Step must cut into our character defects and expose our motives and our actions for what they really were. We have no right to expect these things to reveal themselves.

The saying, “How come everything I let go of has claw marks all over it?” lampoons the way we tend to cling to our established habit patterns. Since Step Five continues the inventory process begun in Step Four, it helps us sort out our assets and liabilities. To make this clear, liabilities are things that cost us plenty without delivering anything worth keeping. Inventory is what leads to our personal spiritual awakening.

46, 8 We cannot make these changes alone. We need our Higher Power’s help, and the help of the Narcotics Anonymous Fellowship.

One of the greatest blessings in the NA 12 Steps is realizing our HP is always with us and always on our side! This is where “Never Alone” comes from. Some of us constantly pray, “God I need your help. I don’t have to power. Please help me.”

NA also means “Need Answers” which our Higher Power is willing to give us if we learn to listen to the still small quiet voice inside. We can also get answers by asking more experienced members to share their experience, strength and hope with us. The importance of a wide support network comes into bearing here. By building relationships with many members, we build a repository of strength and experience to draw upon in troubling times.

46.20 Do we really want to be rid of our old resentments, our angers, our fears? Do we really understand that they are a deadly poison in the heart of an addict?

Argue for your limitations and they are yours forever. Most all of us have a few complaints about things that bother us when we get clean. We will never be happy until we get rid of our resentments, anger and fear. Much of our concerns stem from our our inability to surrender and our lack of a belief in a loving, caring higher power. Every time we eliminate something that blocks our happiness, we make room for something good to take it’s place.

46, 22 Many of us cling to our fears, doubts, and self-loathing or hatred of others, because there is a certain distorted security in familiar pain.

These attributes have a way of hanging on for addicts. That is why NA is a day at a time program. Our personal list of NA basics are so important to the formation of “new habits.” How regularly we find ourselves thinking we have come a quite a ways in recovery only to realize we were thinking unkind thoughts about other members who were not going the way we wanted them to go! We are lucky if we catch ourselves before we begin to work a vengeance on our fellows. When we forgive others, we may find we are really forgiving ourselves!

47, 10 This is a Step of willingness. That is the spiritual principle of Step Six.

This principle is also what we find in Step Three. Without willingness what do we have? We must become willing and have a desire for us to get clean and to continue to stay clean. When we become ready to try a new way of life. Life has become easier rather than the pain of fighting life.

Willingness is a state of readiness to get into the solution, replacing depression, anxiety and self-pity.

47.13 Being human we will of course fall short.We still get mad and still feel hurt, especially if we are too hungry, angry ,lonely,tired, or too serious (H.A.L.T.S).

While our human condition is subject to limitations, when we learn and practice spiritual principles we stop running into obstacles that trip us up and prevent our human joy and freedom. As addicts who have experience hell on earth, happiness and comfort seem like impossible goals for us. HALTS gives us a simple way to identify where some of our negative feelings come from. We will catch ourselves thinking some negative stuff and realize it is only fatigue or hunger that tries to assign our internal feelings to some outside force. .

47. 22 The breakdown of old ideas and old ways seems to be beyond our conscious control. The only control of the situation we seem to have is a choice of acceptance.

Critical thinking is one of the abilities that comes with recovery. Critical thinking is not necessarily negative. It just holds up something like a thought, feeling or perception to the light of a new understanding. We can then decide to include or exclude items once we have had the opportunity to think it over. What we learn increases our ability to learn more. One of the “pay offs” of learning is we get to a point where we can talk about almost anything without fear or anger. By doing these things we are no longer chained or limited to our past low levels of understanding. We choose to accept only things that we have gone over in our hearts and minds.

47, 27 The tools we use to maintain our willingness are practice and prayer. How sincerely we work Step Six will be proportionate to our desire for change.

Every member of NA has experienced the desire for recovery before they got clean. Practice gives us a way to get better by repeating our efforts until we get it right. Prayer sets us in the mood to receive without putting up a fight what we are going after. Our efforts are based on our desire for recovery. This is what gives us the power we need to work all 12 Steps.

48, 12 Having decided we want God, as we understood Him, to relieve us of the useless or destructive aspects of our personalities, we have arrived at the Seventh Step.

This Step is the ‘game changer’ because we finally leave our defects behind. Because everyone who works the 12 Steps is human, we have to take apart the illusion we are in control by making an inventory list, sharing it with someone and becoming willing before we ask our higher power to remove our defects. Defects are one thing and the the ways we fall short of our goals are our shortcomings. Like changing a flat tire on a car, we first have to realize we have a flat, find the tools and a spare tire and then go through the process of correction. These things have to be done in a certain order. Addicts seem to try to do it all at once, like snapping their fingers.

48, 21 The key to this Step is an understanding of the humility. Humility is a part of staying clean, as food and water are to staying alive.

Ego is so pervasive and tricky, we can be really selfish and not even realize. Cultivating humility by praying to have our egos reduced to a level that gets the garbage taken out without demanding the whole world do it our way. Like most addicts when I first started with NA, some of the lingo threw me. Surrender to win? Let go and let God? Routinely expanding our positions and ideas until they become unworkable paints us into many corners. Different information about surrender and driving home the point that it is the losing battles we stop fighting helped me with surrender. Getting some new ideas and changing my belief allowed me to see where a higher power could cover bases I couldn’t reach because my human arm wasn’t long enough. Well, that actually began to make sense. And it goes.

I realize now that humility is giving God credit for the things God does and still be set on ready when something comes up I can do. When I meet or have contact with someone I feel is humble, they seems steady and solid. Competent. Yet they lack a certain presumption and arrogance that indicates they are self-bound and apt to overdo things. It limits my co-existence with them.

Humility is usually brought up along with patience and tolerance. All defects start out as virtues and go overboard into trying to trying to do things our way without bothering to hear what they are trying to say. Toning myself down, listening and thinking through makes a big difference, more in focus.

48, 30 It wasn’t until we made a real mess of our lives that we realized that we couldn’t do it alone.

Being clean clears our vision and increases our ability to hear what others are saying. It may be that the isolation that characterizes our disease comes from the many, many times we wake up when the drugs wear off and look around for all the things we have to answer for. Then, we go around saying, “Hey man. Sorry I did that, it was the dope not me.” Like the using gives us a smoke screen to avoid personal responsibility. Clean, we can string together events as they occur in our lives and see more vividly how one thing leads to all the rest. It may take awhile,

and that’s ok. Healing should not be a rush job. It takes time clean for recovery to penetrate past our surface issues into the depths of our being but the result is the workable, more complete and satisfying being we become.

49, 2 If the defects we have discovered are real and we have a chance to be rid of them, we would surely experience a sense of well being when we rid ourselves of them.

Recovery is not something imposed on us from the outside. That is probably one of the reasons NA works for us. It is an inside job. It is our desire for recovery that propels us and our desire to know ourselves better and discover what our real self is like. Having habitually done things a certain way makes them familiar and somewhat ‘normal’ at least to us. The more we get in touch with what ‘defective’ actually means, the more embarrassment sets in and more happy we are not to have to live that way anymore.

49, 10 However we want to handle it, we go all the way. Think of what we have to lose! As soon as we feel ourselves willing, we should go ahead and ask God to remove our shortcomings.

We gain momentum as relief replaced reluctance and we realize we are not going to miss those damn defects! Their removal makes from for effective habits and routines that allow our real selves to come out and take over. Our ego may want to jump in and wallow in the glory but the grace of God helps us remember when all this recovery stuff felt weird. In the present we experience the changes as improvements and not hindrances. We are free to go and see and do things that have long been beyond our reach. Surrender works because when we get up off our knees, our feet are finally on the ground!
