49, 22 We must puncture our egos and realize that we have much more work to do. When someone points out a shortcoming, our first reaction is one of defensiveness.

We need to realize that someone cares enough about us to show us our character defects and then find the willingness to take a look at them. It is only with awareness, acceptance and action that we have any hope of changing. We learn to pause instead of instantly react, think about our responses and what is the right thing to do in accordance with our growing understanding of spiritual principles and only then take action. It is this momentary pause before action that helps redirect efforts in a direction that is not only good for ourselves but often for those we love and care about.

49, 31 This is our road to spiritual growth, to change our character, day by day, to gradually, carefully and simply pull ourselves out of the isolation and loneliness of addiction into this mainstream of useful Fellowship.

Instead of following an academic approach to recovery subject to the ebbs and flows of trends within the treatment industry, we follow a sensible set of principled Steps that lead us reliably and predictably into exactly the things we need to work on to complete our fractured

personalities and gain the habits and associations needed to live our new lives. Some of our growth can be expressed in words and many are in the realm of feelings that do not fit into the little boxes of language. Our hugs and body language convey our feelings along with what words come to us. Finding out and participating with others going through similar changes keeps us rolling forward. We finally realize what it mean to be be never alone.

50, 10 The last five Steps of Narcotics Anonymous, the Eighth through the Twelfth, are the “get out and live” Steps.

There are many references and correlations within the Twelve Steps of NA. One to Three and Ten to Twelve match-up to open the doorway to recovery and to maintain your new life. The six Steps from Four to Nine directly address mechanisms that help us change. The quotation addresses the rock over from the first six to the last six. The changes within make the changes outside stick and help us work forward with our Program. It just seems to have worked out that the first Three lead us from surrender to a state of empowerment that is clearly miraculous. And the last Three represent an almost totally spiritual daily life that gives expression and increase of what is best in each of us and continues to help us change for the better and better. In between, there are three pairs of Steps that go together. Four and Five bring what’s bothering us out into the open. Six and Seven open the doorway to a new life by appealing to our Higher Power to remove defects that no longer have a place in our lives. It takes our willingness to allow do this for us without the injury that would come from having our defects forcibly taken away. As we grow what has been wrong with us becomes ever more visible and clear.

50, 28 We had to feel better internally before we could even bear to think about whom we had harmed and how we had harmed them, and exactly what was the way we perceived ourselves.

There is a lot of love and sensitivity built into our NA Twelve Steps. They come in a given order that helps insure we are ready for the Step we are working at each step forward. It is rare to find language fitting into the real lives of people in desperate trouble and hopeless states. And it is very supportive and caring that along with each Step we can see how others have gone through the same things. So, as we progress, we are also woven into a Fellowship of men and women just like us, all on the road to a new life clean.

51, 4 Our purpose is to achieve freedom from the guilt we have carried so far, with so much pain, so that we can look the world in the eye with neither aggressiveness nor fear.

When we are becoming new people, it makes us brave as our curiosity overcomes our negative expectations. We addicts are a curious bunch, always wanting to know how things work. It probably began as a way of feeling like we were gaining some control over life and all the

confusion that kept us racing in circles. When a person is less afraid they stop running and start asking questions.

51, 13 The thing that will make us better is to clean up our lives by relieving ourselves of guilt. The Eighth Steps is a mighty stride away from a lie dominated by guilt and remorse.

The willingness to admit fault and release the chains that have held us back from re- experiencing painful guilt and shame is real movement. Life no longer has to be the way it has been. It is one thing to think about freedom and it is quite another to walk down new roads in our lives. Keeping our wits about us and staying with the one day at a time approach to the new life is a real good idea. If we move too fast, we make it easy to take a fall.

51, 27 We make our list, or take it from our Fourth Step and add to it any more people we can think of and we face that list honestly and openly and examine our fault.

There is a simple way to look at this Step. There are stories like the one about a princess who could feel a stone beneath a bunch of mattresses. Another phrase refers to a burr under the saddle. If they didn’t bother us, they probably wouldn’t be on our Eighth Step list! When we reach way down and wiggle them loose, we have settled old issues and are free to find different ways to spend our time and energy. Putting closure on things from the past frees up our future. Clean is not only time, it is the freed up energy to find new ways to live without endlessly repeating what doesn’t work for us!

52, 6 A problem many of us seem to have with the Eighth Step and the admission of the harm we did is the belief we were victims, not victimizers, in our addiction.

The endless quest to look good and sound innocent avails us nothing here. Getting real and finding the source of our pain is worth the tiny jet of honesty it takes to really look at what we have been up to in active addiction and early recovery. But instead of increasing our feelings of doom, we experience elation and joy. Whatever it was, the main thing is to stop feeding the engines that produce such results. When we stagger out of a drug induced fog, we often get things backwards and fail to realize how much of a part we played in painful memories of the past.

52, 21 “The main thing this Step does for us is to build awareness that, little by little, we are gaining new attitudes about ourselves, and how we deal with other people.”

Many of us find a bonus in the Ninth Step when we avoid situations where amends might be needed at a later date for what is happening in the present. It is easier to get real and be honest in the present tense than to carry bad feelings in the past tense. And it seems to be a principle that when we move one way, we free ourselves in the other. So a little relief leads to more relief and more relief frees us from the shame and guilt that has been chasing us all along.

52, 25 We want to get free of our fear, but we don’t wish to do so at the price of anyone.

We do not want to create more difficulty and pain for others to push away our own embarrassment and disgust at the way we were living. That would only lead us back to feeling self-destructive about ourselves. Instead we move forward on a daily basis making amends as needed. Others see us as a different person. Without the fear, we new motivation from the good feelings that come with raising our heads and examining the roles we are playing to make sure we are not leaving damage behind us while we think we are going forward. Wherever possible we build out new lives on solid ground.

53, 5 When it gets to the point that we cannot hold the hurt any longer, amends will be made.

The Twelve Steps follow a natural sequence and move from one necessary focus to the next. The need for amends comes from inner pain, guilt and shame. Some part of us knows there is something that we and only we can straighten. Whether it involves feelings, money or things, by our amends brings order from the chaos. We feel better. Those who receive our amends feel better. Unanswered questions may produce disorder again and again for years without resolution. Putting closure on things allows everyone involved to go on with their lives. For example, at some point we took something that wasn’t our. The situation requires someone who ‘owned’ the stolen item, a manager, who will wonder who took the items (s). Other innocent parties may be fired, accused, sanctioned even though they did nothing wrong. This could happen at work, in a family household and the same paradigm or template may fit countless scenarios. The truth we share removes tensions, allows for restoration of whatever was taken, and everybody involved is off the hook so to speak.

53, 24 Pride, fear, and procrastination often seem as impossible barrier and stand in our way of progress and growth through the Ninth Step.

A thorn in our foot will keep hurting until it is removed. We may even fear the pain of pulling out the thorn and put off removal until it begins to fester and redden to the point of blood poisoning. One thing is sure: once the source of the pain is removed, the healing process begins. The same thing happens with various kinds of disorders stemming for our past behavior. Any form of meanness and wishing ill to others comes back on us to blindside us with new problems.

54, 18 The unconditional love we experience will rejuvenate our will to live and each positive move on our part will be matched by an unexpected opportunity.

Many members get clean, stay clean and begin to work the 12 Steps of NA only to get bogged down in details and forget the larger picture recovery brings to light. Recovery is about freedom and the help comes from many sources which have common denominators like those who help us share our feelings and courage. They respect what we are doing because they have gone or are going through the same things themselves.

54, 24 We as addicts, suffer from a problem deeper than the drugs we took. Because of this, we must live by spiritual principles. Step Ten and all the Steps are to be applied to every area of our lives.

Starting with some basic touchstones of recovery. we learn about how to view the stages of recovery. While one stage blends into the next and all stages are linked up, we clearly start out as newcomers who must learn how we get along in the Fellowship. This stage is not a permanent label confining us to a less-than status. When we experience some of the pink cloud and early lessening of pressure that comes from surrender and the joy of mutual support it can make us almost manic. We all know addiction is a limiting and life threatening disease and yet the good feelings are important to our learning and growing also. As go through these processes we often enjoy helping someone else who is just beginning recovery road.

54. 28 There is no area of our lives that the disease does not affect.The truth is that fear is present in every area of our lives. So, in our experience, the program can and must be worked continuously in every area of our lives.

Just like our blood feeds and cleans our entire body as a natural part of circulation, what we learn in one area is applied in many others. And where we find fault with ourselves, we find the same need for correction in many areas. Each benefits the whole on a continuous basis.

55.4 Step 10 is a continuation of a fearless is a continuation of a fearless and searching look within ourselves in order to repair disorders that fear, pride, jealousy, and other ruinous defects can cause.

It is worth mentioning that the disorders mentioned above involved keeping people at a distance and keeping us at a distance from them. The ongoing problems brought on by addictive disease feeds on itself. Constantly justifying our actions and reactions that caused us problems with people while continually committing the same actions. Our problems beget more problems. When we tell a newcomer to stop making any avoidable decisions, they generally do pretty good. It is when they start ‘deciding’ again that new upsets occur. Doing nothing is doing something. We find food, shelter and clothing. We are tender in recovery. Some of our seemingly large problems seem small and so the new problems are diminished as we grow comfortable dealing with them.

55, 12 Now that we are clean, we have recognized the advantage of getting our own house in order. We can recognize ourselves as the heart of the problem.

What a relief it was to discover that our lives had become quite unmanageable as a result of our drug use, this was very clear to see. What was really an eye opener, was that after we had been clean for a while, that every attempt at trying to control, life, or to change, or attempt to manipulate a situation, we began to feel the fear and that same feeling of unmanageability that

we experienced when we were using. This is when we discover that surrender is the key, and we are the heart of the problem.

We might recall the serenity prayer at this time, knowing that we cannot change another person, we can only change ourselves and pray for the wisdom to know the difference.

Part of the miracle of recovery is knowing that we can actively participate in our own recovery and cease to be the problem and become a part of the solution.

55, 16 Any thought that we are going to be perfect has to be smashed.

The idea that perfection for humans ignores our experience. If we are willing to surrender and do the best we can, that is enough more often than not. In ourselves, or in our friends, we pray for strength and guidance when overwhelmed by a demand or situation over which we are powerless.

Constantly striving for perfection and falling short can give us fears of not being good enough.

55, 18 The smart ones who argue a lot usually die. The open-minded ones get to live.

Perfection is a fear-based idea. It is harder for more intelligent people to surrender because in our world many things give way to intelligence. There are things that require sensitivity and savvy to understand or ‘get’ and these are sometimes workless, or are defined by special words that go beyond objects and labels. A loving, caring person is not especially respected in certain parts of our society, yet their value is beyond price.

55. 33 We honestly chalk up our achievements.Thus we get our house in order. We feel more room to grow.

We find that a power greater than ourselves is taking hold and allowing ourselves to be transformed into the person we always wanted to be and never knew how.

56 .25 In step ten we strive for genuine humility. In this humility we can better interact with others.

In other words, personal, daily inventory is a vaccination against insanity.

57.27 We have discussed the preventative side of tenth step.. The love was there all the time,waiting for us to accept it.

58, 10 The Eleventh Step helps us, in the face of a problem, to be aware of God. The underlying principle of this Step is God consciousness. We try not asking for specific things.

58, 15 A person who has realized their powerlessness and seen the vision the Higher Power has for them will see why we pray only for knowledge of his will for us and the power to carry it out.

Learning spirituality involves study, discussion and listening to what others have to say. Spiritual principles may be based in a Higher Consciousness but the nuts and bolts are learned just like we learn everything else. We hear others share or read something that strikes us in a meaningful way and we try it out to see if it will work for us. Mostly we are amazed to find simple sounding solutions have great power to heal and instruct.

58, 18 Our deepest longings and recurring images of the kind of people we’d like to be are only glimpses of God’s will for us.

Later in this paragraph, we read, “It is our own real dreams that come true.” To continue the idea that many people seem to know intuitively, God is in all of us. The idea that a supreme all knowing and all powerful creator lives within each of us seems overwhelming to begin with. But as a solution to all our living problems and the resolution to all our thinkings, it certainly fulfills the criteria.

58, 23 When we pray a remarkable thing happens; we find the means, the ways, and energies, to perform tasks for beyond our capabilities. By the surrender of our own power, we gain a far greater power that will see us through.

The truth of this statement bears out the efficacy of our spiritual power being ‘higher.’ The easy way to say this is that surrender and prayer gives us the ‘luck.’

58, 28 It is easy to slip back into our old ways. We have to learn to maintain our new lives on a spiritually sound basis to insure our continued growth and recover. God will not force his goodness on us, but we will receive it if we ask.

Essay starts.

58. 34 Many times, our efforts have produced in us feelings of peace and serenity that we have never known before.

Begin essay

58, 35 We know that in doing God’s will, our lives will be fulfilled.

It is one of the quietest yet strongest truths of NA what the Program takes us from insanity and futility to a spiritual awakening wherein we live by spiritual principles and look out for other people. Literally, our overflows into goodness not just for ourselves but for all who come in contact with us. There is a real basis for our sadness and despair when we come to NA seeking help. We have had plenty of failure and confusion. Gradually, as we work our Steps, go to meetings and hear others share, we are re-built into what we were meant to be. It is our dreams, yours and mine, that come true for us. The new understanding of a loving God, freedom from defects and amends with those we have harmed that come from the change inside each one of us.

59, 5 We become spiritually refreshed and are glad to be alive.

We don’t sugar coat reality or promise our members that there will be no problems along recovery road. What changes is the way we are inside and the way we react to the happenings in our lives. If you are not spiritually refreshed and glad to be alive, there is usually one or more of the basics that worked for you in the beginning that you have discontinued or forgotten to observe. The spiritual life keeps us pointed in a direction that feeds us helpful information, a new attitude and people who are there for us when we need them. By not reading literature, going to meetings, calling your sponsor and, oh yeah, not using, we lay ourselves open to attack without warning. That’s what a relapse is - an attack on our our new lives. Hopelessness and despair are always there, waiting around the corner, and come out when we run out of spiritual food and the opportunities that the Program brings to us.

59, 11 There are those of us who tried to stay clean without the benefit of “having had a spiritual awakening as a result of those steps,” and they are no longer with us.

The Program of Narcotics Anonymous is no joke. It is a set of principles we can live by with good results. Through the years we have seen many members put their program on hold while they took care of ‘more important things.’ Their relationship, their job, their pasttimes and hobbies. They failed to realize for us addicts working the NA Program is a life and death matter. Now, there are those who wind up the NA who are not addicts. A little controlled using convinced and made believers of them and they got back with the program. There are others who stuck with the Program and lived on to better things. The Program is designed to help those who suffer from the disease of addiction and want a new way of life.

59, 26 The first way in which we carry the message is by staying clean with the help of God and the Fellowship. Our new way of living speaks for itself better than our words ever could.

Essay starts.

60, 2 The message is meaningless unless we live it. If we do live it, we give it more meaning with our lives than any words can express.

Essay starts.

60, 5 Learning the art of helping others when it is appropriate, without creating resentments, is a marvelous benefit of the N.A. program.

Essay starts.

60, 7 Remarkably, the Twelve Steps guide us from a state of humiliation and despair to a state wherein we are able to act as instruments of God’s will.

60, 35 We don’t set ourselves up as God. That is why it is spiritual. We get to be an instrument of God’s grace in action.

61, 2 Those of us who do service work are the luckiest people on God’s earth! Those who have been in the pits of despair, now strive to help other people to find a new and better way to live.

Essay starts.

61, 25 We no longer wish to participate in the problem. We now serve God. In this manner of service, we renew our vow to turn our will and lives over to the care of God

The brightness and pain of active addiction does more that make us say, “Ouch.” Hey, it really hurts. With the pain comes the willingness. Most people realize there are ups and downs in life. If you have drifted away from the things that brought you freedom and relief in the beginning of recovery, you do not have to take a fall to get back with the program, just do what it says here: “Renew your vow to turn your will and live over to the care of God.” That’s all it takes. Just say the words to yourself a few times.

62, 20 Trusting others doesn’t come naturally to us, but we must learn to rust because an addict alone is in bad company. the needed strength wasn’t there until we found N.A., which showed us how to make faith work for us.

What a statement! Faith does work for us but we have to learn a little to get it right. Faith is evidence of things unseen. But the difference shows in how we make new choices and how we react to events and circumstances. Positive expectation may be a big part of it. Keeps our minds open to positive outcomes. If we let ourselves believe our situation is hopeless, it is hard to see good opportunities.

62, 30 Remember, we didn’t become addicted in one day. We can’t possibly solve all our problems at once, no matter how much energy we have. Take it easy! The only way we recover is to apply what we learn from each other, on a daily basis.

When we get away from the simple, true, proven program of NA, we can drift into endless complication. Keep it simple is more that just a neat phrase! The NA Program is assembled out of the combined experiences of men and women who found answers in the 12 Steps and work within the boundaries and principles of the 12 Traditions. To let our minds get fuzzy and drift away from what works for us, oftentimes the only thing that works for us, is insanity rejoined!

61, 2 Those of us who do service work are the luckiest people on God’s earth!

Service is an expression of gratitude. We are truly grateful for the wonderful gift of recovery. We consider it a privilege to give nameless selfless service to Narcotics Anonymous. Whether we help a newcomer, make coffee, sponsor someone, bring a meeting to a shut-in, serve as chairperson for a meeting, become a trusted servant for area, region, H&I, phone lines or other positions, we consider ourselves lucky. By working the Steps have learned the joys of living in the present in accordance with spiritual principles.

61, 34 Living just for today relieves the burden of the past and alleviates fear of the future.

We find guilt is a large part of the burden of our past. Working Steps 8 and 9 teaches us forgiveness and relieves us of this load. We have fears about the future and worry about things that have not yet happened. This tends to ruin our present. By living here now in the present, we can make the absolute most of this moment. We learn to live in the solution. When we live by spiritual principles, continually doing the right thing now, our past becomes pleasurable memories and the future looks golden. [used May 26]

62, 20 Trusting others doesn’t come naturally to us, but we must learn to trust because an addict alone is in bad company.

Essay starts.

62, 23 We had a lot of energy, but we channeled it into self-destruction.

We now have a choice and can choose to channel our energy into things that bring us ongoing happiness and joy. The more we do this, the more we feel good about our recovery. If we are not busy making these choices, probably get ungrateful and begin the old whining and ingratitude that characterized our active addiction. Eventually we will say, “I am clean but I haven’t changed any.” Start over today! Freedom means we don’t have to languish in our pain and misery. We do the best we can on a daily basis and that leads us into a happy destiny!

62, 32 The only way we recover is to apply what we learn from each other, on a daily basis. Our growth is a lifetime process.

The more we learn the more we are capable of learning. Generally, our next best move is right in front of us and we don’t recognize it because we dislike and distrust openness and honesty. When we try something new and it works for us, we become curious about checking out some other scary changes!

63.1 Addiction is physical,mental, and spiritual . Therefore,we believe that we must develop faith in a higher Power Before we can hope to recover from its destruction.

Begin essay

*150*

63, 3 When we have a strong faith in a Power greater than ourselves, and use that faith in our daily lives, that Power will be able to reach us and supply us with the strength and guidance that we need to recover.

Faith is visible in the changes we can see in our lives. Staying open to what other members share is a good practice because it gives us dozens of windows into the recovery experience of others. There is no end to what we can learn and practice. Sometimes it takes a few tries to get it right. Nobody recovers overnight. We practice what we learn and may have to wait a while to see the results. Hey, we waited for the dope man! Don’t give up before the miracle.

63, 29 Spiritual and religious is one of our most basic principles. Each of us are free to work out our own concept of God, or reject the concept of a God.

Like many other freedom, the power is in the right of choice. If our old ideas of a higher power did not produce the results we wanted, that is encouragement to go looking for a new understanding!

64, 9 Three “musts” are honesty, open-mindedness, and willingness to try. Agnostics and atheists generally start out by just talking to “Whatever’s there.”

This daily meditation stresses the importance of being open and experimental. There are ways of learning positive ways to investigate and learn more before committing ourselves. Because of our societal limitations, we may not know we are free to really take a look around, read books, ask others to share their experience before adopting or committing themselves to their own personal understanding of a higher power. When you have your own belief, you begin to capitalize higher power. A Higher Power of your own understanding is the key to ongoing recovery and spiritual growth.

64, 13 There is a spirit or an energy that can be felt in the meetings, and it is sometimes the newcomer’s first perception of God.

Recalling our feelings and impressions from our initial contact with NA helps us keep our minds open to new ideas and target what we really want and expect from recovery. That little lift in the chest and sitting up straight expresses the openness and attention to learn more about the NA 12 Steps and Traditions. We may not even have words for it yet, but it is this desire for self- betterment that drives all our recovery.

64.19 We no longer blame God and others for our own problems, and see that our problems have been of our own making.

Begin Essay

64.22 After we accept that we created our own hell and that there is a God that wants to help us, we begin to make progress in solving our problems.

Begin Essay:

64, 28 One way to develop our conscious contact with God is to make up a “gratitude list,” count our blessings and thank our Higher Power for them.

This kind of applied spirituality may be new to us. Our discomfort and disease has blotted out our view of reality, selecting only for the negative and unpleasant. That’s why our newfound hope is surprising. Active addiction removed our hope, bit by bit until there was nothing left.

65, 9 Rigorous honesty is the most important tool we have in learning to deal with the past and to live today.

Like sharpening a knife on a wet-stone, we ‘hone’ our daily habit patterns to re-route our lives into a form that we can live with without having to make amends later on. Honesty appears to have the same root word.

65.13 Our lack of fear and our new found faith serves as a firm foundation for courage in the future .

begin essay

65, 23 We have never before experienced gut-level honesty because we covered up our feelings by using.

Most often we were unaware of avoiding our true feelings because using distorted our feelings with our using. Adapting to this bizarre mish-mash of feeling became our daily view of the world. Recovery is re-adapting to the world as it is, so there is no need to run and hide they way we used to.

65, 33 Managing our own lives got us to the program of Narcotics Anonymous. What we knew about living when we got here had almost killed us.

Our ‘shared’ existence in NA factors in what has been learned and found workable in recovery by our fellow members. Human beings are programed to mimic the behavior of the crowd they spend their time with in everyday life. Living clean and practicing our spiritual principles opens doors and modify our addictive conditioning in ways we may have trouble seeing on a daily basis - but we can see and feel the results of the changes.

66, 2 Being open-minded allows us to hear something that might save our lives.

If this sounds pretty dramatic, it is. Most of us have developed defense mechanisms that keep us locked up in a narrow, defensive and lonely point of view. Our new found hope opens many doors for us, starting with believing change is possible. Hope is like a flame that warms us. Even if it is small in the beginning, the warmth makes a difference and feels good so we learned to trust our feelings and go with them as our life steadily improves. Sometimes it is hard for us to

see the old ways creeping in and don’t accurately notice we can begin to work our program in reverse - using our tools to justify and condone behaviour of self-righteousness and isolation.

66, 9 We no longer have to make fools of ourselves by standing up for some non-existent values.

Pretending to be more than we are is a fear-based behavior that seemed to protect us from negative judgements by others as to our conduct or behavior. Hiding from the truth is not necessary if our house is in order.

66, 18 Honesty, open-mindedness, and willingness to try, work hand in hand. The lack of one of these principles in our programs can kill us.

Many people who call themselves members may be under a false impression that addiction is not a disease that can kill us. Rocking chair recovery can appear to work for a while, but it may leave us without the tools and principles that comprise the NA Program. Many members rest on the wide-spread meetings and body of literature as if there is nothing left to do for the growth and continuance of NA. Nothing could be further from the truth. When we stop working the Program, the Program stops working! Without knowledge of who we are and where we come from we remain children.

66, 23 Remember too that H.O.W. are spiritual principles which mean that they can be relied on to get us out of trouble that dishonesty, closed-mindedness and unwillingness go us into.

Essay starts.

67, 7 We were in the depths of despair, degradation and lost in a destructive chaos. WE came in puking, sweating and shaking. Some of us stayed. Was it through our own merit? Wh think not! Our way got us here. Recovery was done through the help of others, the tools they shared with us, and a Higher Power.

It is oh so easy to forget just how bad it was and what it felt like to have no hope, no way out and no one to turn to. Almost all humans select for a softer, easier, way. The difference for us is that that way offers only the illusion of security because we put only a coat of paint over our old behavior and call it clean! A softer, easier, way can kill us because it offers no change in how we live our lives.

67, 20 We react angrily as recovering addicts if anyone tells us what to do. In N.A. meetings, We share what it was like in our practicing addiction, our suffering that brought us to the turning point and how we stay clean today.

One of the unspoken facts our our clean life and recovery is there are people in the rooms who do not share our enthusiasm, energy and willingness to commit to our Program. They may go further with their program and realize the stakes are their lives, their happiness and their dreams or lose all three. One general statement stands out clearly and that is the Program as we know it was built by people who lived it and wrote it down in the Basic Text. Those who have attempted to ‘own’ and take credit for our literature are almost completely in the dark as to how it was written and the spirituality involved! So, in our daily recovery we are free to embrace, learn and apply the deepest and most loving NA light we can find and apply to our lives.

68, 2 This force in the meetings isn’t just the people there, but also something within the people. We return to these meetings and use them like medicine in the form of unconditional love.

There is a strong spirituality in the love we share at our NA meetings. We express as best we can what we are doing in our day to day to lives and the way we use the principles of NA to deal with things that used to have us on the run. Hearing some of the hair-raising episodes that occur in the lives of our friends will automatically encourage us and change the way we do things. Sometimes it helps us in our daily living just to stop and get in that “NA state of mind” in the midst of whatever is going on. We focus better, we recall things we need to deal with life and we are more honest and considerate of others. As we succeed using NA principles, they become woven into the fabric of our lives.

68, 13 Finally, throughout our lives, we surround ourselves with fellow members who continue to let us know we can count on them. One of the advantages of the N.A. program is that is (it) places us in an intimate, regular contact with recovering addicts who can most understand and help us in our recovery.

As newcomers we, sometimes fearfully, share our problems and feel a deep kinship with our predecessors as they share their actual experience and solutions to our problems.

68, 31 To us, at first, it feels like insanity to give another person knowledge of our pain. As we become more closely involved with others we will learn to share our pain and it will lessen. Part of the horror of addiction is being cut off from this human experience. Our fears and guilt kept us from receiving the benefits afforded to every day people.

Essay starts.

69, 7 Being able to serve as an instrument of a loving God and participation in the recovery of others keeps a sense of wonder and gratitude in our lives. Giving comfort and encouragement to others encourages and comforts us.

Essay starts.

69, 18 If you want to change your life -- risk sharing! It is by taking risks we have almost lost our lives, by asking for help in the same way, we can change.

Essay starts.

69, 29 It is a way of life for the addicts who want to learn to live clean and have discovered one another. We share our mental and spiritual resources for the good of everyone.

Essay starts.

69, 33 Recovering addicts take great pleasure in helping other suffering addicts recover. Recovery as found in Narcotics Anonymous must come from within and no one can get clean for anyone else.

Essay starts.

70, 5 The Steps guide us into our place in society. We begin by straightening our internal disorder and obtaining release from the defects that prevent us from being all we should be.

Essay starts.

70, 11 We emphasize setting our own house in order and trying to do more that our part. We trust in our Higher Power to give us strength and to meet our needs.

Essay starts.

70, 16 If we’re not grateful for the things we’ve got, we won’t be grateful for the things we are yet to receive.

Essay starts.

70, 26 In the past we have relied on desperation to give us the strength to periodically re-order our lives. There is another way.

Essay starts.

70, 29 When we accept that we are responsible for our problems, we realize that we can be equally responsible for our solutions.

True freedom starts when we take full responsibility for our actions, the consequences of our actions, our feelings and our attitudes. We no longer are victims, either of others or of circumstances. We become free to “be here now” finding the solution to our problems without wasting time or effort on how we got here. Having the ability to live in the solution is a result of incorporating spiritual principles in every aspect of our lives.

70, 33 Being clean is abnormal to us, and we must learn how to live in an on-going manner. A great magic is found when we help others. As clean addicts in the Fellowship of Narcotics Anonymous, we help ourselves by helping others.

Essay starts.

71, 1 Our attention focuses on the solutions, and our old ideas break up and dissolve like icebergs in the tropics. When the compulsion to use is lifted from us, and we begin to think of others before ourselves, a true miracle begins. Working the steps, practicing the principles and using the tools, we begin to see ourselves in a new light.

Essay starts.

71, 9 We put spiritual living first and exercise patience, tolerance and humility in our daily lives. The further we get from the last pill, fix, drink or take, the more we see of our past and more we realize the miracle of the release from our disease of addiction.

Essay starts.

71, 18 Many of our dreams, forgotten and obscured by our addiction, return and help us regain the sense of wonder and excitement at the miracle of living clean. The old compulsions fade and the habits of mind associated with addiction weaken and are broken. A great many things become possible for us. Since we live clean and grow, we are able to take our place in the world.

Essay starts.

71, 21 In dealing with the other people in our lives, we develop a loving attitude. We forgive more easily, anger more slowly, expect less, and give more....

Essay starts.

71, 26 If we want to reap the benefits of staying clean, we find it necessary to take continual inventories of ourselves. Hidden fears and needs are still potential driving forces. Just because we don’t recognize fear or anger doesn’t mean that it isn’t influencing our lives. We found it important to examine places where we grew angry or our beliefs were tested.

In the spirit of reciprocity we must continually look inside to receive the benefits we are seeking for our lives. For so long we were living in darkness. In order to place love in our lives we need to objectively see the darkness that covers our spirit. This takes continual surveying and plotting new courses to steer our way to a more healthy lifestyle. Once seeing the darkness does not serve us, we replace it with with a loving attribute. If we desire different results, we learn to vary our approach to get different results.

We can do this as often as needed to find what works best for us in our creative freedom. When something we are experimenting with does not serve us well, we inventory again seeking another idea that may serve us better. Each avenue finds something else we may use for finding our freedom. We also may take each perspective we have found, mix and match to find what works best for us in the moment.

We find that it is important to remain flexible, remembering that the only constant is change and that nothing should ever be perceived as being set in stone

It takes continual searching and fearless inventory to replace the unhealthy attributes with the light we seek. To find light we needed to seek the light we had covered in darkness.

An inventory is a tool to take stock of our assets and liabilities. Seeing and hearing clearly gives us a chance to take a reading on where we are and what we are doing.

71, 35 Truth seems to be something that is impossible to understand until it becomes obvious. Truth is something we never suspected until we knew it. There are no exceptions--only incomplete truths. Everything we know about truth is subject to revision, especially what we know about truth.

Essay starts. When we close our eyes to the truth , we deny ourselves the new possibilities that come with our new way of life. Freedom comes from not just staying clean , but in our ability to surrender unconditionally. When we learn to just let go, we experience a freedom , many of us have never experienced. The only way to do this is not just for the day , but right now in the moment. It takes practice , but is attainable.

72, 5 Another desirable quality is love. We love the ambitious for they can inspire us; we love the failures for they can teach us; we love the the kings for they are but human; we love the meek for they are divine. We love the poor for they are so many. We love the rich for they are lonely. We love the young for the faith they hold; we love the old for the wisdom they share. We love the beautiful for their eyes of sadness’ we love the ugly for their souls of peace.

Essay starts.

72, 13 We think of love as a shield against the attacks of other people, and as a weapon to blast through walls of hate, and to open closed hearts and closed minds. We feel protected by our love, through adversity, discouragement, anger, and insecurity.

Essay starts.

72, 22 We forgive more easily, anger more slowly, expect less, and give more to our brothers and sisters.

These aspects of recovery are not to be forgotten or pushed aside. They are a few of the aspects of recovering addicts different from the way we were before.

72, 34 In the old days we knew, deep down inside, that we were faking it whenever we indulged in our delusions of grandeur and self-importance. Now, because we were beginning to love ourselves unashamedly, we can love other people more completely, because we no longer feel like we have anything to hide from anyone.

These are priceless blessings that money cannot buy. Many people live out their lifetimes without activating these aspects from the list of available potentials.

73, 4 Our egos used to control us in all kinds of subtle ways. For one thing, it seemed important for us to compete with others in almost all of our endeavors. Some of us even refused to try something that we might not be the best at. We watched other people closely , not with any concern for their wellbeing, but to check and see if we were measuring up to their standards.

With all this negative expectation, when we did well, we were only confused. Everyone we met was sized up in terms of how they could be useful to us or a threat to our self-esteem.

73, 11 Some of us had no idea who we were, or who we wanted to be. We only knew we didn’t want to be ourselves.

Essay starts.

73, 15 The Twelve Steps to recovery, that N.A. outlines for us, hold the answer for all of our ego-trips and insecurities. they seem to hold the only answers for us that we can really use. In living these Steps, we first begin to let go of old egotism. We then open up to a Higher Power, so that we will lose all fear of facing ourselves and of facing other people.

Essay starts.

73, 30 The Twelve Steps led us to a point of recovery that seemed to make the world change before our eyes, for the better.

Essay starts.

74, 4 We place our recovery first and our own petty desires and egos last.

Essay starts.

74, 10 In our past, we were usually irresponsible. After we face ourselves in the inventory Steps, and make amends to others in our amends steps, we can no longer allow other people the “pay our way” for us. We want to serve.

It is important to remind ourselves that we are making progress. We have been down so long we have to re-conditions ourselves to

74, 15 In our groups, we all need to open up first, selfishly, for our own recovery, and later candidly, for the inspiration of the newer members.

Essay starts.

74, 21 It isn’t easy, but, if we want to live happily, we have to learn to live ethically, and within our means, facing what we have and what we have not, with honest acceptance and gratitude.

Essay starts.

74, 23 Those of us who are consciously working and using the Twelve Steps to recovery in our lives are seldom bothered by gossip. Our lives are lived like “open books,” and really don’t feel like hiding anything, or judging anyone. WE no longer feel a need to put up a front, because we are doing the best we can.

Essay starts.

74, 32 We apply what we learn in the meetings to all our affairs, using these teachings as a basis for living, but returning, again and again to our groups, both to help and to be helped in our recovery from addictions.

75, 1 One of the simplest and most important parts of our whole recovery process is the concept of “live a day at a time.”

By living in the present, fully focused on what is happening now, we can be mindful of our actions and not simply react. We can take the time to figure out an appropriate response in accordance with spiritual principles. We are also going to notice how beautiful our world has become and take pleasure in it.

“A day at a time” is one of the great liberators for addicts in recovery because it removes the brick wall that confines us to things which do not work out. We can change and change back if we need to.

75, 7 We are experts at making ourselves so frustrated that we lose perspective completely.

Losing perspective means our thinking is off and we need to take a break.

75, 17 During our active using, we often lived way beyond our means, out of necessity. Unfortunately, not all of us lose our extravagance and greed easily, ever after we stop using.

Living within our means and practicing a little restraint just means we’ll pay the rent on time and our lights won’t be cut off next month. Later, it means more, much more. It means we will be free of financial worries that we had become accustomed to enduring. Many of us were addicted to a painful way of living.

76, 2 Humility is an honest self-acceptance, which leads to further acceptance of the conditions around us.

Essay starts.

76, 9 One of our greatest enemies is resentment. It has the power to kill. The only way to be rid of resentment is to develop humility, in the form of forgiveness. .

Essay starts

76, 25 Sometimes, the best revente we can have over a manipulative or unpleasant person is in not allowing them to “pull our strings” by making us react unkindly to them.

Essay starts.

76, 31 Remember, we can’t change other people. We can, through the program of Narcotics Anonymous, change ourselves.

Essay starts

77, 4 Alienation and isolation are symptoms of the mental part of our disease. To the practicing Addict, life is just a movement between connections and oblivion. Normal concerns are pushed aside, as the disease progresses.

78, 1-5 It all begins with our first admission and surrender. From that point, each addict is reminded that a day clean is a day won. At first we can little more than go to meetings.Probably we cannot even remember a single name, word or thought from our first meeting. What we do remember is the feeling we got.

79.1 That no matter what we have done or what course our addiction has taken, we can relax and enjoy the love that fills the room at every meeting which follows the Twelve Traditions.

Asking one to sponsor us can also seem insurmountable. Because of fear and a lack of understanding, we go to more meetings to find if we really desire this lifestyle.

79, 18 Freedom to change seems to come mainly after our acceptance of ourselves.

Essay Begins.

80, 8 We often miss what we are looking for because it isn’t hidden.

It may take us a while to surrender enough to get this, but lots of things in life are not hard. We make them hard by trying too hard. Relaxing and calming ourselves changes dramatically the way others see us. If we exhibit tension and fear of things going wrong, others pick up on that. They would much rather interact with someone who is straight up and carries no air of negative anticipation.

80, 34 The program doesn’t work when we adapt it to our life, we have to adapt our life to the program.

80, 26 The willingness to try new ideas and possible solutions will help open the door to our recovery.

81, 4 Personality change is a natural progression set in motion by our surrender to the program.

Essay starts.

81, 13 We may still, however, feel that we cannot have a happy life without drugs. WE may suffer from the fear of insanity and feel we have no escape from using others than an insane and depresses existence.

By staying clean and going to meetings, we stop many of the things that set us up for failure and confusion. Right away, we are able to do simple things, find a place to stay and something to eat. That mean old world out there does provide some niceties when the parallel behaviors that go with using drugs disappear because we are not crazy out of our minds so much.

81, 34 The essence of addiction is that it is easier to change our perception of reality than the reality we perceive.

Living clean switches all that around. Changing reality means the changes stay put and need less correction as we go along in our new lives. We may have some amends to make but that is infinitely better than endless feelings of guilt and shame and all the avoidance patterns that go with unmade amends.

82, 4 As the benefits of basic honesty in the world begin to roll in, we are ready to consider honesty at a deeper level. Self-honesty is being in touch with the way we really feel and the way we spend our time.

82, 11 The ego of the addict must be busted for him to have a chance at recovery. “Terminal hipness” and “fatal cool” are symptoms of the addictive personality.

82, 21 At least one meeting a day for ninety days seems to be a good guide for those who are going to any lengths.

Going to meetings, reading literature, calling your sponsor, asking for God’s help, don’t pick up - these are five recommendations that seem to work for all members regardless of age, sex, etc. It is astonishing how helpful these simple things give us a daily reprieve from the hell of active addiction! Only a sincere member of NA gets to participate in this wonderful thing we call simply “recovery.” The price of membership is high - it costs our self-will and ego. The idea we can handle it on our own can kill us. You are the one who decides if you are sincere or not. Identification with other NA members brings a special calm and new self appraisal since we begin to compare our feelings with others in the same boat we are.

83, 2. Guilt and worry keep us from living in the here and now. The denial of our disease, or reservations, keep us sick. We lack humility, clinging to our old ways.

Gaining humility takes time and a thorough working of Steps 6 and 7. Humility is a total acceptance of ourselves, both our good points and our character defects. We need to achieve such a total acceptance of ourselves that we don’t feel the need to justify ourselves, explain ourselves, defend ourselves or promote ourselves. We can just calmly state our truth and move on without further discussion or argument.

83, 13 Life then becomes for us what we always wished it to be -- a constant state of awakening.

Many of us used drugs to intensify our minds and this helped drive us crazy!

83, 20 Living clean each day at a time will reveal to us the things that truly come from within and give us better understanding over things that would interrupt our flow.

Without the drugs and addictive distractions, what we go through reconnects to reality and if we want to add or take away something, we have that power. Many members forget their power to ‘change gears.’ If something is bothering us, we can do something about it!

84, 1 Letting go of character defects should be done with love. It is important we think, to be gentle with ourselves when putting our ego to rest.

It is easy to get all worked up and create new problems by lashing out in anger, even if our intent is to change our ways for the better. We can attack others and do damage to ourselves without realizing. We need always to bear in mind our program works a day at a time and going slow and deep is better that quick and superficial.

84, 10 We had to reach out and to accept the love and understanding that the Fellowship had to offer.

Essay starts.

84, 32 It has been our experience that personal problems will be resolved when we are willing to accept responsibility for them.

Personality change used to be rare among addicts. Once an addict, always an addict used to be the way it was. Not, through the 12 Steps of NA, we have a new way to deal with our disease of addiction. We can follow a certain order and take our self-will and ego at its roots.

85, 19 N.A. reawakened old memories of what it felt like to be a member of the human family. Slowly, we opened up, reached our, warmed up, and let ourselves love and be loved. The original desire to be clean leads us to a desire to help others.

85, 25 The only way we keep from continuing a habit is not to take that first fix, pill, drink or toke.

This line was taken out of the material reviewed for the Basic Text, Narcotics Anonymous. The idea was to focus on the disease and not the venue. While this is still a good and valid idea, there is power in breaking habits what makes it important to avoid the context as well as the content. When we avoid the specific drugs we took, it may help us make it. The whole program revolves around changing our personalities and getting away from escape from reality.

Newcomers are still told to avoid people, places and things associated with their personal using patterns and that does include drugs in various forms.

85, 31 Abstinence is the basis of our program. Any mood or mind-altering chemical, prescription or not, is poison to our bodies.

There is literature available outside our approved material that goes right to the heart of the thinking behind taking drugs to ‘feel better’ or adjust our moods without changing the things that produce stress or bad feelings in our day to day lives. This is hardly an outside issue. There are plenty of people who may benefit from various treatments that usually include meds.They don’t have to call themselves NA members or follow our 12 Steps. There are also many members in good standing who both work our program and get the relief they seek from doing so. Folks have to find what works for them. Our role is supportive, not directive.

86, 3 If we clean our bodies by daily abstinence we should clean our minds of preconceptions based on past experience. It is those who stay clean when it seems like it isn’t worth it who make it.

86, 9 The bottom line of Narcotics Anonymous is staying clean. When we realize that we can’t use drugs in any form and live, we are ready to admit our powerlessness.

Relapse is not a subtle state to be in! The effects are immediate and devastating. If we are a little quieter and go to work on time, that may be a misleading benefit. Like the prohibition against taking the first drug in any form, the visible relapse comes later when we imagine we are using successfully. This vicious cycle must be avoided at all costs.

86, 22 We believe the solutions for the problem of having drug-fogged minds, sick bodies and tormented emotions is in a spiritual way of life.

Spiritual means learning about the things that affect how we feel, for better or worse. Anger, resentment, judgemental attitudes, allowing ourselves to focus on what is bothering us rather that looking for new ways of looking at things will generally put us in a bad mood to say the least. Focusing on what we have to be grateful for, seeking help from our sponsor or another member we trust, making a gratitude list and a whole lot of other different ways to process our feelings improve our spiritual condition and open the many doors that will lead us out of our misery and ingratitude. Many members are addicted to their powerlessness. Reading material on prayer and meditation can put us in touch with others seeking to improve their lives by following a spiritual path. Old experiences with religion and hokey non-sense may bar the door to new ideas and ways to improve our condition. Spiritual involves the quality of our life.

86, 12. It takes some of us a while to realize how unmanageable we were and are still. For others this is the only thing we can be sure.

The first step lets us know that we are powerless and our lives were unmanageable. It was easily evident that the lives that we had led brought us to places we hadn’t planned on going to: jails, institutions, near death, as well as witnessing our friends die. After getting and remaining clean, we experience the care, concern, and love from other addicts, that lived in just as an unmanageable life as we had. (love from other addicts whose lives had been as unmanageable as ours.) Our stories and experiences may have been different, however, the pain, shame, and woundedness that we lived with all feel the same .

88, 6 We came to this program for many different reasons. Those of us who stay, do so for the same reason--to stop using and stay clean.

Essay starts.

88, 19 Usually one of the first things we try to do when we’re working with a newcomer is to get them to a meeting. After all, that’s what worked for us.

An NA group has one primary purpose, and that is to carry this message to the addict who still suffers. This can only be done through our members sharing their honest experience, strength and hope. By watching newcomers who have raised their hands, or when we notice the scared addict sitting by him or herself in the back of the room. In a loving way , remembering their name or just simply introducing ourselves, “Hi, I am an addict named ____.

Meeting attendance helps the new member to identify with other recovering addicts and provides a sense of hope in the experience, strength and hope shared. It also provides an opportunity to be of service for our more experienced members when we “12th Step” newcomers by taking them to meetings.

This approach also helps our oldtimers to remain humble, putting ego aside and remembering that it is the group that is our most powerful vehicle for carrying the message and fosters the spirit of the therapeutic value of one addict helping another.

89, 1 What is it about our meetings that’s so special? Usually, about all we can say is that there is a feeling there, a feeling of strength and hope and love; an atmosphere of recovery. Our meetings are very special to most of us. They’re a place where we feel safe; a place where we fit in.

For many of us this is the first time we have felt that we fit in. Many addicts have felt different from everyone else all their lives and to finally feel we belong is an incredible feeling. We love it. We are greeted, told we will be loved until we learn to love ourselves and accepted just as we are. Later, we want to help the newcomer feel this way too.

89, 8 Groups of self-centered, self-willed, isolated individuals just can’t meet together peacefully and safely’ but we do. The reason that we can is that we have Twelve Traditions that help to keep our groups “safe” and free.

Often we forget how essential our Traditions are when it comes to basic stuff like the atmosphere of recovery in our meetings. Study and open discussion give us insight and a deeper understanding of how our freedom has to mesh with the freedoms of others. There is no doubt that our members go on binges of obsession on a single point or two and for the time can’t see a broader picture, even if others are trying to help them. Fortunately we have extensive training in dealing with nuts. Patience, humility and tolerance come easily to some of us. Loving and caring for others is not seen as an ongoing affair. Mostly, our members come out from obsession to gain information or the get to the bottom of something that bothers them.

90, 28 Most of us had never experienced the kind of attention and personal care that we found in the program. We are accepted and loved for what we are’ instead of “in spite” of what we are.

Because we have adapted our Steps and Traditions from another program, we have to remind ourselves we have taken a receipt from another program but also we have baked our own cake. We have expanded our program of recovery to include the many forms addictive disease can take. Once you take away the drugs, you just have a more efficient maniac. Addiction comes out in many ways. We get addicted to ideas, places, people, moods, activities: anything that qualifies for obsession or compulsion. Recovery is regaining our choice. Addiction is not having a say in what we do, when we do it or why we do it. We lose our rights to freedom when the addictive process is begun.

91, 8 In our addiction we consistently placed our personal welfare before anything else. Here we found that in the long run what’s best for the group was usually good for us. We chose to conform to the common good because that’s what worked for us.

We learned to put group purpose ahead of personal preference. Where there were conflicts, it was understood that we are free to re-consider our stand on issues. No one can make us do much of anything. We’ll leave the group if we are not happy there. We can even reconsider and go back later if we like. But for purposes of recovery, we all have to pull together if we are going to enjoy the miracle of recovery.

89, 22 The Twelve Traditions of N.A. are not suggested, and they are not negotiable. There are the rules that keep our fellowship alive and free.

90, 6 It is only through understanding and application that they have power.

We have all heard this line read at each NA meeting we attend. You can become a members when you say you are. You become a member in good standing when you stop using, go to meetings and work the NA 12 Steps. Members in good standing also become familiar with our NA 12 Traditions. Pulling together, learning about our group purpose and dealing with powerful personalities are extremely important as a floor plan for recovery to take place. All the Traditions interlock. You can consider a Tradition violation and with a little thought and study you can see where it affects the other eleven Traditions as well. The spirit of the Traditions brings us together with plenty of individual freedom along with the loving support of the group to pull on for what we need.
