91, 20 We found that we could no longer control our using, nor could we manage our own lives. This surrender was the starting point of our recovery, and is a primary point of unity for the Fellowship.

Without surrender we find it hard or impossible to admit our need for help or express our need to those who might have our answers.

92, 12 Without N.A. few of us would have survived, and fewer still would have found recovery.

We are still constructing NA as we know it. Our experienced members are often appalled at the members who seem to think we just make up things and do not realize we learned from those who have gone before. Our written literature helps our members get in touch with the reality of passing on experience that has proven itself in the lives of many addicts. Those who have a vested interest in NA want to continue growing and better carry a message of recovery based on the actual experience of our members. The scars of our addiction and the host of ideas carried in the minds and hearts of our members in different stages of recovery make an interesting mix. Keeping up appearances has to be balanced with the truth of inner experience. Financial issues and concerns need to balance with the love and concern we have for others. The things we gather in recovery should never take precedence over our need for our meetings and the others who walk our path.

92, 16 By nature we seem to be strong-willed, self-centered people, seeking self-gratification in the realms of money, power, and sex. As important part of our recovery is learning how to live

with these drives; how to realign our misguided instincts, how to stop acting our our insanities, how to disarm our self-destruct mechanisms, and how to rechannel our energies toward constructive ends.

As human beings we want to be productive, effective and well liked by the people we come in contact with. The problems come in when we are willing to use lies, deceit and thievery to achieve these ends. Honesty, openmindedness and the willingness to try seem impractical and sometimes, unworkable principles to apply to life on life’s terms.

92, 25 One of our sayings is “Our best ideas got us here.” This seems apt as we look back and see how many times our schemes and plans got us into trouble despite their original intent.

essay

93, 5 When we realized this had relates to our new group oriented way of life we often experienced a sort of gut-level panicky feeling.

Depending on people we are just getting to know can feel like risky business. in ‘real’ NA, we come to know down deep that those who are really working their 12 Steps have a vested interest in recovery and live the program like their very lives depended on their words and actions. Not everyone is able to jump into recovery this way. They hold back from habitual fear based on their experiences before recovery. NA recovery is not an everyday miracle. It is the result of faith, experience and positive intention.

93, 14 In working the Steps we need to come to depend on a Power greater than ourselves. WE We continue this relationship and utilize it for our Group purposes. If we each turned our will and our lives over to His care and seek to do his will, he will express Himself on a group level.

Sometimes things just go wrong. Maybe somebody though they were praying for strength and guidance but had a belief system that made reliance on God a risky business. How could we know. We can only to the best we can on an individual basis with the understanding that we will honor our commitment to serve and cover the shortfall if anything goes wrong. This is what it means to say “Our Program” and mean it!

94, 14 We’ve made a lot of bad decisions and pawned them off as Group conscience. This worries many of us. How can we really tell if our decisions are really Goup conscience or not, and how do we prevent painful mistakes?

Easy answer: we can’t! All we can do is work our program to the best of our ability. There are many choices involved with NA membership. We chose our friends and sponsors. We choose our home group. These choices sort out where we go and who we spend time with. This takes care of many problems in a quiet and natural manner. Egos, personal preferences, and honest mistakes insure we will have some problems. Hey, when we are clean, we can handle most

things that come up in a group so long as we follow our Traditions. One trouble is many of us don’t know when we are violating the letter or spirit of a Tradition. We stay away from the law of the land because outsiders, however well intended, cannot be expected to know our Program of understand our ways. We avoid affiliation for the same reason. When we are really focused on recovery and helping others, it takes up enough time and energy to keep us out of trouble. 94, 31 We have seen that we try to rest authority in the spiritual conscience of the group. In keeping with this, we make a special point of trying to prevent authoritarian leadership. We have learned that for our Fellowship, leadership by example and by self-less service works, and that direction and manipulation fails.

This is where clean time and experience comes into play. Without clean or experience, all we can offer is opinion and hopeful projections. There is a need to have those who have gone before us to share what they have seen work in practice and what leads to trouble..

95, 3 We choose not to have presidents, masters, chairmen or directors. Instead we have secretaries, treasurers, and representatives .These titles in themselves imply service rather than control.

Jailhouse lawyers, inexperienced persons and those who speak without thinking or praying, may miss the point here. Start with personal powerlessness. A surrendered person would want help and listen to what other members involved have to say. Someone with a tender ego may see everything in terms of what will make them important or look good. This is a distorted misperception. Egotistical perceptions make problems from the get-go. This is because we care about all our members and know that practice in decision making and group activities build up a person’s confidence and interpersonal exchanges. They move from attending a meeting to being a part of a group. None of this is by accident. Many of our group conscience processes do double duty by informing members at large while establishing and maintaining principled processes as well as spiritual principles. Good ideas that don’t work in real life and dismissed in favor of practical solutions. That is why so much of our literature is based on successful actions instead of great ideas.

95, 13 An atmosphere of recovery in our groups is one of our most precious assets; and we must guard it carefully lest we lose it to politics and personalities.

If each one of us prays to relinquish their ego and self-will, the atmosphere will resolve. Honest sharing and personal disclosure set the tone for other members to also follow suit.

95, 17 Those of us who have been involved in service for a long time or in getting a group started and keeping the doors open through the hard early days sometimes have a hard time letting go of the reins. Sometimes our egos get in the way, sometimes ungrounded fears get in the way, and sometimes the group gets in the way.

No one claims to completely understand the miracle of our our message and our recovery. Therefore there is no safe and sure way to make rules for members to obey blindly. We all need

to look up now and then to see how we are doing. Paying attention to the facial expressions of other members gives us the best way to determine when to pass the baton to someone else. If the moment passes, it can break the spirit of inter-personal sharing. The optimum is usually brief, genuine shares about what is happening in one’s life or a current concern, then sit back for others to share. Cross talk and a few members swapping comments leaves out the rest of the group and that too can interrupt the sharing. If you and I share our personal truth and experience, we leave it at that and pay attention to what others are sharing.

Someone observed that people generally share with other people who are at or near the same level of growth and development. This may be true. If it is, it might partly explain why people hold back or leave the room if someone is talking down to them or gives and unscheduled lecture. It disadvantages others and stops the open sharing of personal truth.

97, 2 Along with this they also accept that they do have special and valuable experiences which can benefit the group. They become a resource for the group; seeking neither to control the group not to set themselves apart. It is at this time that their long service truly contributes the most. They encourage us, inspire us, and teach us by example.

The material in the Grey Book leads up to this sentence enumerating the things that a person can go through as they progress in service. The ‘resource’ idea is crucially important. A group without someone to turn to in the advent of a log-jam is in trouble. This happens when everyone is getting confused and ready to set aside spiritual principles to get their way. Having a resource to get them back on the same page is a non-toxic exit to the disagreements.

97, 30 The newcomer is our lifeblood of N.A. and when one comes to us seeking help we welcome them with open arms.

The principle here is obvious. Evaluating the situation requires some common sense. Sometimes ‘qualified’ newcomers exhibit symptoms of mental disturbance that make it hard to determine if they are serious about recovery or dancing on air mentally. We actually are qualified to determine which is the case and we have always seen people transit into NA members if the disease and desire are there. Some people with mental illness benefit from the 12 Step process. Surrender, belief and capitulation open them to help in various forms. Sometimes it just takes time. Perhaps the important thing here is to remind us everyone in NA is a miracle and we were all in bad shape when we got here!

98, 11 Many of us could not visualize a life without drugs, let alone want it. Many uf us had reached the point in our addiction where we felt there was no hope for us, we only wanted a little relief.

The information we gain about addiction helps us stay clean as long as we are focused on adding to that information with our own personal experience. If we let ourselves drift into distraction and forget the basics of recovery, we can pave the way to relapse by dropping the new attitudes and living habits that helped us get clean in the first place.

99, 15 WE have also met members who are uncertain about where they really belong. We suggest that they ask themselves three questions: 1. What message do you carry? (What is the nature of your recovery and what have you recovered from?) 2. Who are you trying to carry this message to? 3, Where are you trying to carry this message?

Reviewing basic ideas gives us a chance to make sure we are on common ground with reality. We are great ones for charging off in directions composed of our own projections and negative expectations. Just because we run a definite risk of running off the road does not mean we have to run through a catalog of fear every time we get behind a wheel! Obsession can really mess up your perception of reality. The clean life requires a few adjustments!

99. 25 The choice of membership rests with the individual.. WE feel the ideal state for our fellowship exists when as addict can openly and freely come to an N.A. meeting; wherever and whenever they choose and leave just as freely if they want to. We realize that there is nothing we can do to make an addict stop using.

Without understanding and acceptance of this basic idea, the rest of our recovery process fails to ring true.

100, 2 The autonomy of our groups is one of our most precious possessions. This sounds great but what does it mean? What is it to be autonomous? Websters defines autonomous as “having the right or power of self government,” “undertaken or carried on without outside control,” “existing or capable of existing independently,“ “ responding, reacting or developing independently of the whole.”

There is a spirit that exists among individuals dedicated to a common cause or purpose. Common understanding and agreement results in unison of action and purpose that works to get the desired result. It takes freedom of belief and action to achieve this. Addicts who want to stay clean and enjoy a life in recovery should by all means be free to do so. A classical example of our God given ability to find ways to disagree is over where we say, “a half dozen or six.” Of course, it makes no real difference and with a little effort most people can see this but there of those who insist on their terminology and reject any variation out of hand. If getting clean, staying clean, and helping others is why we go to meetings, we stand on common group and are likely to be effective. Needless conflicts should not ruin our efforts to achieve our primary purpose.

100, 15 The answer, of course, is that these things are not N.A. They are services that we can utilize to help us in our recovery and further the primary purpose of our groups.

100, 18 Narcotics Anonymous is a Fellowship of men and women, addicts, meeting together in groups, and using a given set of spiritual principles to find freedom from addiction and a new way to live. All else is not N.A.

This simple statement has been made to appear disunifying and obscure enough to be removed by three members before the first publication of our Basic Text, Narcotics Anonymous. We hope through time and personal growth we will come to a high level of basic agreement. The importance of limiting perceived power in our service bodies is great. The huge majority need not be restricted to the level of a very few members. Even if they are gifted or full of noble sentiments, they do violence to the individual members and groups. If they have trouble seeing this simple point, it illustrates the importance of having a general and agreeable basic understanding in our written recovery material. Else readers will sooner or later take issue with offending items and some will leave NA. It is simple enough to write in an inclusive manner. Giving undue and excessive to trusted servants who surely may have problems understanding and maintaining proper boundaries for the sake of our common welfare. After all, most trusted servants have leeway to do their job and ask for help as needed. Whereas who takes up for the members if excesses occur or accumulate? Irreparable harm may go for years without correction. All because a few ‘powerful’ members took it upon themselves to disregard the deep consideration that went into the original they don’t quite understand.

100, 31 Once a group registers they are sent a starter kit. This contains many suggestions and is one of the way we share our experience to help the group. Whether or not they take our suggestion is their decision. In this starter kit is says that we must abide by the Twelve Traditions in order to call ourselves Narcotics Anonymous. This is also true, but these Traditions are part of the set of spiritual principles that are N.A. Without the Traditions, N.A. does not exist.

Just as a surveyor can get careless and mark his charts quarter of an inch off, it may make him a mile off down the line. Where great numbers of persons are concerned, great care must be exerted. For a few people to come in and re-write the work of very intense effort and review is a tremendous liability. Everyone today knows well the power of finance and corporate impropriety to degrade and destroy human considerations and values. Recover teaches us to take care and proper concern in all areas of our lives. Members who have a vested interest in the growth and continuance have only three ways of dealing with bureaucratic problems: correction, relapse or death. In time we hope for restitution of our original, attractive and edifying literature. We cannot permit statements and treatments of subjects in our approved literature that go against our Steps or Traditions. For instance, total abstinence and DRT or no surveillance and drug slip attendance. We have to be very careful and able to state the truth of our experience regardless of corporate or medical pressure to serve the views or issues of outside enterprizes.

101, 12 For us in Narcotics Anonymous autonomy is also creative freedom. It gives our groups the freedom to act on their own to establish their atmosphere of recovery, to serve their members, and to fulfill their primary purpose. It is this aspect of autonomy that makes it one of our most precious principles.

This does not give a group the license to violate the principles of the 12 Steps or Traditions. Among the important items of spiritual growth is the ability to sort out common sense. Almost all members visit other areas and travel to distant conventions or gatherings to see if members elsewhere work their Steps and practice the principles of NA the same way their members back home practice them.

102, 3 If we check to make sure that our actions are clearly within the bounds of our Traditions, if we don’t represent anyone but ourselves, if we don’t dictate to other groups or force anything upon them, and if we take the time to consider the consequences of our actions ahead of time, then I will be well.

Not one of us is perfect and the ability to make amends frees us to step out on faith and try something new or different. Still, anything new or untried. may or may not work out. In our daily lives, we make changes all the time. Before they put the curves at the end of aircraft wings, an airplane was said to be ‘off course’ over 90% of the time due to wind and turbulence! Self- correction is a real bonus that comes with our new freedom from the insanity of active addiction. Our lives find benefits from the principles of our 12 Steps and Traditions because principles are spiritual and universal. It takes time for all this to become a working part of our lives. When problems come up in our daily lives, we can think about our Program and sometimes find things we can do to help correct some situation that has gone wrong. There is no way to get ‘recovery’ without making mistakes and learning to apply the new tools in our lives.

102, 15 All Our groups can do is plant the seed for recovery and bring addicts together so that the magic of empathy, honesty, caring, sharing and service can do its thing.

This quote reminds us that our recovery is a God-given blessing far beyond what the mind of mankind can do. Recovery is a miraculous event. Those who work their Steps like their lives depend on it know what is meant by these words. The disease of addiction affects three levels: the physical, mental and spiritual. The physical is easy, we can feel it. We can feel the mental also but the idea that we are in control can become a serious roadblock. Spiritual knowledge is what grants us real recovery. Many, many members who work their program, go to meetings, read the literature and follow the proven NA Program in all ways but one miss out on the biggest blessing of all: relinquishment, freedom from our human frailty through a supernatural agency called God or Higher Power.

102, 26 The 12 Step of our personal program also says that we should carry the message to the addict who still suffers. This is no coincidence.

As we progress to the benefits of recovery, we recall how our surrender make such a big difference. Through helping others, we become a human plus sign. We offer a life giving solution to our situation of pain and endless suffering. Helping others gives us a mental, spiritual and even physical lift right away. We can see ourselves in others. We realize that those who help us do not feel grandiose or .better than us. It is actually the other way around. We are comforted because some of the words we use apply to us as well.

102, 30 For the newcomer this is how they find out about NA and how they stay clean; and for the members this reaffirms and clarifies what they have learned.

Sharing and caring are both active and positive expressions of our love for others.

103, 10 What would happen if our groups had other primary purposes? We feel our message would be diluted and then lost.

103, 19 In our groups we share our experience, strength and I hope and this is our message – that an addict, any addict, can stop using drugs; lose the desire to use again; and find a new way to live. Their message is hope and the promise of freedom.

103, 26 Our Fifth Tradition defines our primary purpose and our Sixth Tradition tells us some of the things we must do to preserve and protect this spiritual aim. This Tradition tells us that we ought never endorse, finance or lend the N.A. name to any outside enterprise. And then we are warned exactly what can happen if we ignore this advice.

104, 3 This Tradition is the basis for our policy of non affiliation and is extremely important to the continuation and growth of N.A. Unfortunately, this Tradition has also been a point of controversy within our Fellowship.

104.14 A direct endorsement is often used to try and persuade someone to do something.

Begin essay: Isn’t persuading someone to do something the same as manipulation. And isn’t manipulation a primary symptom of our disease of addiction. This is part of what Tradition six is about.

104, 21 The third thing warned against is lending N.A. name. This means letting someone use the name, Narcotics Anonymous, for something that is not Narcotics Anonymous. It also means letting an outsider mention or utilize our name for their own purposes.

106, 21 We who were unable to function as human beings now find anything is possible for us. Those dreams we gave up long ago can now become realities with God’s help.

109, 9 Spiritual principles are basic truths that do not change with time or place; they simply work in all cases.

109, 33 Our motivation is simple; this program was given freely to us by addicts who cared. We only do the same.

110, 4 These are the things we are and how our program works. They are a reality for us. We have our Steps; we have mobility; we really understand and care; and we are motivated by survival. All these things are a contradiction to traditional recovery approaches and to professionalism. The professional has no place in our fellowship; very nature prohibits this.

111, 1 This Tradition defines the way we run our Fellowship. A lot of confusion has occurred because of misinterpretations of our Ninth Tradition. Our members are addicts who have the desire to stop using, who want what we have to offer, and who have chosen to join us. Our meetings are a gathering of members for the purpose of staying clean.

111, 9 our steps in traditions are uniform and set in a specific order. They are numbered; they are not random and unstructured. Certainly they are organized, but this is not the organization of earned a tradition. For the purpose of this tradition, organize means having an administrative structure, and this implies management and control.

111, 16 N.A. should never be run by bureaucracy or management nor controlled by individuals within administrative structure. If we were to allow this, N.A. this would surely lose the best it has to offer and choke to death on our insanities.

111, 21 Even without this Tradition (9), organization such as this would be in opposition to our spiritual principles. A loving God as He may express himself in our group conscience would find no place within an administrative structure.

111, 28 Specialization and professionalism are the basis of any management scheme. Any administrative structure, but is very nature, eliminated the possibility of autonomy. An organized N.A. is a contradiction in terms and any attempt to force organization on us would destroy us.

111, 34 The Ninth Tradition goes on to define the nature of the things that we can do, outside N.A., to help N.A. It says that we may create service boards or committees directly responsible to those they serve.

7.24.15

112, 14 In order to achieve our spiritual aim, Narcotics Anonymous must be known and respected. No where is this more obvious than our history. N.A. I was founded 1953. For 20 years of Fellowship remain small and obscure. In the 1970s, society realized that addiction had become a worldwide condition and began to look for answers.

In making spiritual growth and active part of our lives, we too must be looking for answers on a daily basis. We need to repeat what works and discard what fails.

113, 23 We have learned the value of teaching bad example rather than direction. This is work for us and will utilize this principle while we work with other addicts. Are messages most obvious in our lives.

114, 17 Promotion is representing our sales is something we are not. In order to accomplish something we want, we don’t use promotion to encourage Alex to come to us and we don’t use promotions to make ourselves more acceptable. Our successes speak for themselves.

114, 32 Personal anonymity is really much more. It is a corner of freedom, and personal recovery.

115, 3 Each of us has her story, and around recovered. Individually, we’re powerless but as the fellowship we can achieve great things.

*300*

115, 6 The 12 Traditions of Narcotics Anonymous are even more inter-related than our Steps. They complement each other and are bound together by the principle of anonymity.

“Anonymity is the whole basis of the program; it is truly the foundation. In order to survive, we must set aside the differences we live by and become a part of a greater whole.” Recovery leads us back into the fabric of lot. To enjoy this new way of life we have to drop a lot of the personal preferences that in the end don’t mean much to us and they blocked the way to a happy destiny.

115, 11 Many of us have nowhere else to go if we could not trust our N.A. groups and members. We were no longer afraid to ask for help. How then did we lose our fear?

115, 16 The awakening of anonymity in each of us occurs when we’ve family give up trying to manager allies and begin to depend on a power greater than ourselves.

Thinking we are different from others, insisting on having our way, needless conflicts with others: all these are the prison bars of our active addiction. They keep us more or less in conflict with all the people we come in contact with in our day to day lives.

115, 25 Unity is the direct result of application of the principle of anonymity within the group and the fellowship.

116, 1 No single person (no personality) has authority. This is vested in a loving God to whom we have turned over our will and lives.

116, 7 We do not define our members. We only insist they have a desire to stop using. Nothing else should matter. This desire is the one crucial must of our program.

117, 26 Anonymity is everywhere in our Traditions and in our Fellowship. It is one of the basics of recovery. The principle of anonymity protects us from our defects of personality and character. Where anonymity exists, personalities and differences have no power. Anonymity in action makes it impossible for personalities to come before principals.

119, 1 For us, to use is to die. We have seen addicts come to our Fellowship, try our program, say clean for a period of time, only to drift gradually away.

120, 1 Basically, we lost our fare through the experience of sharing. You Share our fears with other addicts. The more we do this, the more our fear slips away.

Just one more just one more okay don’t do it don’t do it just

119, 1 For us to use is to die. We have seen addicts come to our fellowship, try our program, stay clean for a period of time only to drift away.

It is important to remember that the disease will undercut and derail our efforts to get with the NA program. Saying, “ I’m clean but I haven’t changed.” is a really sad statement because it is up to each individual member to surrender, admit unmanageability and begin to learn what has worked for others. We go looking for a Higher Power and find ways to go on from there. Strong egos can stop the recovery process before it has a chance. Surrender is the cure for egotism!

We attend funerals of fellow members and this brings home the importance of working a strong program and carrying a clear message of recovery. Anyone who has been in the program a while has seen a person nurse a futile resentment until it drives them out of the rooms.

What has changed besides not using? Are we collecting resentments or forgiveness where others seem to conflict with us?

This does not have to happen when we do the things that work for us, not just staying clean, but to use our sponsor, go to meetings regularly, find a homegroup that we attend and support, where we can feel safe and comfortable. By simple prayer, and discovering that we are experiencing true miracles, in our lives, we are becoming more comfortable in our own skin. We see that our obsessions have left us. Now we can believe and see that maybe this thing, called Narcotics Anonymous can, and is working for us.

119, 14 No longer comfortable with our using friends, we were not yet at home in meetings. Basically, we lost our fear through the experience of sharing. We share our fears with other addicts.

Essay starts.

120, 31 Addicts must patiently re- learn things forgotten and learn how to live. This is what Narcotics Anonymous is about.

Essay starts.

121, 11 If we are to continue abstinence we must be responsible and productive, not necessarily in terms of normal definitions, but in terms of spiritual principles. We must grow.

Each one of us is different and we have different requirements. The freedom NA offers allows us to take in and concentrate on the subject we need mort. As we grow a divine hand guides us to exactly what we need to be happier and more effective. We cannot always understand what is happening at the time, but clean time gives us a chance to review what happens and the results of our growth.

121, 26 Spirituality as a way of life for an addict seems a total contradiction. Many feel that they have found what they were searching for in drugs through spirituality. By working the Steps we are relieved of our obsession to use and many gratefully serve the Fellowship for this reason. Growth means change and we feel we must live on a spiritual basis in order to change.

121, 35 From self-centered, angry, frightened isolated people, we become loving, sharing, self- assured spiritual parts of a greater whole. We changed from hopelessly helpless to hopefully helping.

Our daily recovery offsets the gravity of our addictive disease to draw us into the same selfish and benighted condition we were before recovery with a suitable coat of paint to protect us from the notice of our fellow NA members. We want to look good. This is not a badness in us, it is simply our disease and all members admit they have it! Rationalization, justification and projection are tools we use to stay sick and avoid recovery.

122, 6 We remain honest to free ourselves from guilt. As we begin to function in society, our creative freedom helps us sort out priorities and do the most basic things first. Daily practice of the Twelve Step program enables us to change from what we were to what our Higher Power would have us become.

essay

122, 13 We cry easily and find it safe to cry and express love and share all our emotions in the atmosphere of recovery found at N.A. meetings. The steps become our framework of daily change. Continuous abstinence requires spiritual growth which leads to emotional recovery in our lives.

essay

122, 19 Each day we stay clean by practicing the Steps in our lives, our chances of relapse decrease. Each of us is only one drug away from a painful active addictions, but living our program through regular honest sharing in the Fellowship of Narcotics Anonymous increases our chances for uninterrupted clean time.

essay

122, 29 Complacency is the enemy of members with substantial clean time. We never fully recovery. Guilt, remorse, fear, lust and pride may all become unbearable if we fail to invest ourselves totally in the program, no matter how much clean time we have.

Write it on the wall! The idea that our good time drug use was fun will be like the call of the wild and at some point we will confuse ourselves enough to the point using seems like an option. This is amazing considering all we’ve been through. All we have to do is just remember what the last one did for us and after a few moments a miracle happens and we are restored to sanity!

123, 1 Meetings provide a safe place to share with others during this time. Many members who recoveries have blessed them tell us that they continue to attend meetings on a daily basis even after several years of clean time.

We never get so much clean time we lose our need for recovery meetings. Some of us are blessed with lives where we continuously talk, visit, host and travel within NA. Some of us can recall thinking, “This disease can kill me and all my dreams with it. I better get to more meetings!” only to look up from the highway and realize we are driving in the middle of the night to a three day NA convention in a favorite recovery community. Back home, we still get to meetings if we want to stay clean. Recovery is the shore and the meetings are our life boats.

123, 13 The experience gained with time insures our ongoing recovery. WE must use what we learn or we will lose it and probably relapse, no matter how long we have been clean.

essay

123, 25 The group we choose to be our home group will be like a spiritual savings account. The more we can invest in it through our care and sharing, the greater the dividends it will pay.

essay

123, 31 As we grow, we learn to overcome our tendency to run and hide our feelings. Learning to be totally honest about our feelings helps other people take us seriously.

Facts and personal self-disclosure ring true to the listener and when they see us let our guard down, they are better able to share honestly with us. All this honesty is good for us.

124, 1 Sharing our lives with others seems to keep us from feeling lonely. The Steps lead to the kind of honesty that can help us let others know and accept us.

essay

124, 5 We seek creativity through the Spirit. This spirit dwells within all of us. We need to understand that the spirit within each individual is the same. The action being that we tap the available resources of the spirit within ourselves. Some have tapped more than others. We need to create more soul searching within one another. This process is the creative action of the spirit. We listen to ourselves and others in order to recover and to stay on that path.

It is this sameness of spirit that makes NA a universal program. Our Steps and Principles have been applied and utilized in every part of the globe. Our original literature was written to include all people who might seek recovery in NA, regardless of their national language, history, religion or other factors that might hinder their approach to NA recovery.

124, 13 Lack of spiritual quality within our lives leads us down the path to relapse. This part of our recovery is the essence of the program. Spiritual maintenance is indispensable if recovery is what we’re after.

124, 15 . Diverting from the spirit in ourselves we utilize the negative consciousness that comes from within. Our life is the constant efforts of progressing in our spiritual consciousness.

Our attitudes to ourselves and others, learning, open mindedness, remaining teachable - all these and more affect our spiritual condition. Maintaining that condition is called recovery. Whenever and wherever we find ourselves being negative or critical of others, we have stepped back from that state of mind and soul that allows us to see past surfaces into what is really happening in the people and world around us. [we decided to drop this out of the quotes due to misspoken sentence.]

124, 19 Stagnation or just plain old character defects which we re-cultivate rather than let go of sends us back to where these defects were conceived.

124, 27 In our daily lives, we are subject to emotional, environmental, intellectual, mental and spiritual relapses, causing us to become defenseless against the physical relapse of addiction.

If we have worked our First Step, we know the connection between our using and our pain. Using will hurt like hell if we fall into one of the many traps it lays for us, all pretty and tasty

looking on top and steel jaws below. Want to feel lower than the sidewalk? You know the way. Want to feel joy and hopeful? These is a way called NA! Moved to May 31st

124, 31 As a disease, addictions is subject to relapse. Reverting to using is a physical relapse. Physical relapse is on a symptom. We are never forced into relapse. We are given a choice.

Many - if not every single one - of us have to get into the habit of praying and meditating, whatever level we are on. Contact with other members inside and outside our meetings keeps us on our toes recovery-wise. Honest sharing keeps us aware of the dangers of thinking we have it made. .

125, 1 When we were told in meetings we were flirting with relapse, we had to make a decision. We had reservations about anything which did not describe us to a tee and built these up until we thought we could use again.

What a disease! The stupid stuff we use to build long standing resentments against other members is incredible. We don’t have to worry about outside issues disrupting NA as long as the ties that bind us together remain strong enough. Most all of us have sponsors with much more clean time and recovery than we do and they all tell us of the painful times they have seen otherwise good members fight and carefully build resentments based on rumor and gossip that last for decades. Maybe someday we will be able to address all these issues but until then we have to pray for strength and guidance to not fall into the same traps ourselves!

How clear can you get? Unless we connect our using with our pain, we cannot work the First Step. The admission of our powerlessness is the basis for all recovery. The person who cannot ask for help is helpless. Those who ask for help get help. When we are learning about NA, we also learn to monitor our progress.

125, 5 Relapse is a sign we have had reservations in our program. We slighted our program and left loopholes in our daily lives. Aware of the pitfalls ahead, we trod blindly on in the belief we could make it on our own.

Although our addictive disease expresses itself in so many ways, it is surprising how much has to do with honesty, open-mindedness and the willingness to try. Perhaps it is the built-in memory of a painful past that continues to build up protective walls long after the need for them is gone.

125, 8 If we continue to fall back into the illusion that drugs would make life easier, we must not be ready yet. We believed that drugs would change us and forgot the changes would be fatal. Unless the illusion is shattered that we, in any way, can stop using on our own, we will use again.

Many people do not ever reach an understanding of why and how the NA program works. Try paying more attention to what others are saying with their words and their faces or body

language. Some will be attending every word spoken by a newcomer to our rooms because they came to the meeting to help others. What they share can tell you where they are at. After the meeting, crosstalk breaks the atmosphere of recovery, they get with the person and interact.

125, 14 We took the opportunity to avoid several of our responsibilities, missing meetings, skipping work, neglecting Twelve Step work, and most important, we stopped asking for help. Growth in the program stopped.

Other good stuff in this paragraph. The idea is that certain things we do in recovery can be written out and listed work together to help us stay clean and grow spiritually. If we stop doing one or more of these things, we put our whole recovery at risk.

Yes, we could coast forward for a little ways. But our spiritual deposits were soon matched by our spiritual withdrawals. The flow of miracles stopped coming. We need these miracles and it is sometimes hard for us to see where the prayers make a difference. Spiritual things are not of this world and so are difficult to quantify in terms like sight, sound, taste, color, etc. They are spiritual and beyond things of this world. Does someone need to spell this out for you? Is this not your experience? We see the difference in the way things work out for us. And the way others react to us when we are working the Program.

125, 22 When a resentment or any other emotional relapse occurs, failure to practice the Steps and result in physical relapse. Many of our newcomers have difficulty coming into the Fellowship because they do not understand we have a disease called “addiction.” We are doomed (spelling corrected) to see our past behavior as part of ourselves and not part of our disease.

essay

125, 28 As long as we preserve our clean time we enjoy the greatest possible advantage over our disease. For this we are grateful. The first thing to do is get clean. This makes the other stages of recovery possible. Learn that addiction is a disease. Try to forgive past behavior and realize it was caused by our disease. Go to meetings and study the program for your own personal growth.

This amazing paragraph is supremely important for it contains basic information that has worked for literally countless addicts all over the planet Earth. All faiths are welcome to NA! All nationalities, languages, national historical settings fit in with us. Addicts are the same wherever they come from and so is recovery!

125, 35 Obsessiveness is really common to all the addicts we have met or heard of. Our ego tells us we can do it on our own, then loneliness and paranoia return.

126, 3 There will be times, however, when we feel like getting off. We want to run, we feel lousy, we need to be reminded of where we came from and that it will be worse this time. This is when we need to Fellowship most.

Next

126, 11 We hate to be wrong. It is hard to believe that now in our recovery somehow self-will leads us to make decisions based on manipulation, ego, lust or false pride but it happens . . . often.

126, 14 Remember we’ve don’t recover overnight. When we realize that we have made a bad decision or bad judgment, we make an attempt to rationalize it. We become extreme in our self- righteous attempts to cover our tracks.

126, 27 We pray for willingness and humility and finally get honest about our mistake in judgment or bad decision. We tell those who were hurt that we were to blame, ask them to forgive us and make whatever amends necessary. Now we’re in the solution again.

126, 32 We think that the Tenth Step helps prevent relapse.

126, 34 There is something in our self-destructive personalities big cries for failure. It permeates our total being – “You do not deserve to succeed.” We have all experienced this and without the knowledge that we finally have earned the right to be responsible, respectable members of society. The manner in which we returned to our addiction hello is not important. What is essential to each of us is that we have the choice not to continue.

Next

127, 10 Those of us who have relapsed found the true progression the disease that plagues us. To know and come to understand that there is a Power greater than ourselves desiring to have a relationship with us, in spite of our faults, comforts us all.

127, 22 Some of us reach a point of complacency in recovery. If we stay at this level for long, the recovery process ceases and we begin to backslide. Clean tab in the program access insurance. If complacency is not acted upon the disease begins to manifest the parent symptoms in us. Denial returns along with obsession and compulsion.

128, 7 Meetings are a healing source. Writing about what we want, what we are asking for and what we get and sharing this with our sponsor or another trusted person helps us to work through those feelings, and letting other share with us about their experience gives us hope that it does get better.

128, 15 Attending meetings daily, living a day at a time, and reading the literature seems to send our mental attitude towards the positive. Willingness to try what has worked for others is vital.

128, 30 Progression of recovery is a continuous journey uphill with loving effort. Without love or effort we start the downhill run. The progression of the disease is an ongoing process, even during abstinence, no matter how long.

129, 4 Spirituality and sharing is the essence of their recovery . Keeping our illusion of reality rather than using the tools of the program and the people in the Fellowship will return us to isolation.

129, 15 To use is to die, often in more ways than one. We have found this program at the right time or we wouldn’t have found it at all. N.A. and its principles are here to help us achieve quality in our lives. If we were worthless we wouldn’t be alive.

129, 22 Left with a shadow of doubt, the mind of a newcomer and even the old-timer can prove to be this spark needed to set off the return of insanity and that first pill, fix, drink or toke.

130, 13 Life’s flow takes place in this manner: we come here powerless and the power we seek comes to us through other people in the Fellowship if we can only reach out for it.

130, 30 Slow down and lived life. A cynic is someone who has been very disappointed. He does not hear until the heart decides to listen, then he can accept help. It is not shameful to relapse. The real shame is in not coming back.

Next

131, 9 When someone returns the Fellowship after relapse, ways stress the importance of living just for today. The past is the past. We cannot change what is happening. What is important is to stay clean today.

131, 20 We all find the feeling we get from helping others motivates us to do better at around lives. If we are hurting, and most of us do from time to time, we learned asked for help.

131, 26 Recovery found the narcotics anonymous must come from within and no one gets clean for anyone but themselves.

8.06.15 124, 5 We seek creativity through the Spirit. This spirit dwells within all of us. We need to understand that the spirit within each individual is the same. The action being that we tap the available resources of the spirit within ourselves. Some have tapped more than others. We need

to create more soul searching within one another. This process is the creative action of the spirit. We listen to ourselves and others in order to recover and to stay on that path.124, 13 Lack of spiritual quality within our lives leads us down the path to relapse. This part of our recovery is the essence of the program.
